title: Learning to Walk from Three Minutes of Real-World Data with Semi-structured Dynamics Models
abstract: Traditionally, model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) methods exploit neural networks as flexible function approximators to represent $\textit{a priori}$ unknown environment dynamics. However, training data are typically scarce in practice, and these black-box models often fail to generalize. Modeling architectures that leverage known physics can substantially reduce the complexity of system-identification, but break down in the face of complex phenomena such as contact. We introduce a novel framework for learning semi-structured dynamics models for contact-rich systems which seamlessly integrates structured first principles modeling techniques with black-box auto-regressive models. Specifically, we develop an ensemble of probabilistic models to estimate external forces, conditioned on historical observations and actions, and integrate these predictions using known Lagrangian dynamics. With this semi-structured approach, we can make accurate long-horizon predictions with substantially less data than prior methods. We leverage this capability and propose Semi-Structured Reinforcement Learning ($\texttt{SSRL}$) a simple model-based learning framework which pushes the sample complexity boundary for real-world learning. We validate our approach on a real-world Unitree Go1 quadruped robot, learning dynamic gaits  -- from scratch -- on both hard and soft surfaces with just a few minutes of real-world data. Video and code are available at: https://sites.google.com/utexas.edu/ssrl
author: Jacob Levy;Tyler Westenbroek;David Fridovich-Keil
aff: University of Texas at Austin;;University of Texas at Austin

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot dog to walk in under three minutes by mashing together a standard physics textbook and a neural network that guesses how the floor feels. It's essentially trying to build a marathon runner by giving them 180 seconds of treadmill time and a prayer that the 'math' handles the rest.
why_bad: The 'semi-structured' model is basically a physics cheat-sheet that works until the robot hits anything the math didn't predict, making it more of a 'fragile-structured' model.
Claiming a breakthrough based on three minutes of data is like claiming you're a master chef because you didn't burn toast once; it's massive overfitting masquerading as efficiency.
The comparisons to other AI methods were suspiciously cherry-picked, making the new algorithm look like a superstar only because it was racing against toddlers in lead boots.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Hard-Coded Alibi
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution claims this robot 'learned' to walk. But look at the evidence! They handed the defendant a complete script of Newtonian physics—the legal equivalent of a leading question—and then acted shocked when the robot followed the prompts. They want you to believe this quadruped is a self-made man, when in reality, it's a nepo-baby riding on the coattails of 17th-century dynamics. Three minutes of testimony isn't a 'rigorous trial'; it's a PR stunt. I move to strike the entire 'SSRL' framework from the record for lack of foundation and extreme hearsay!

title: Velociraptor: Leveraging Visual Foundation Models for Label-Free, Risk-Aware Off-Road Navigation
abstract: Traversability analysis in off-road regimes is a challenging task that requires understanding of multi-modal inputs such as camera and LiDAR. These measurements are often sparse, noisy, and difficult to interpret, particularly in the off-road setting. Existing systems are very engineering-intensive, often requiring hand-tuning of traversability rules and manual annotation of semantic labels. Furthermore, existing methods for analyzing traversability risk and uncertainty are computationally expensive or not well-calibrated. We propose Velociraptor, a traversability analysis system that performs [veloci]ty-informed, [r]isk-[a]ware [p]erception and [t]raversability for [o]ff-[r]oad driving without any human annotations. We achieve this via the use of visual foundation models (VFMs) and geometric mapping to produce a rich visual-geometric representation of the robot's local environment. We then leverage this representation to produce costmaps, speedmaps, and uncertainty maps using state-of-the-art fully self-supervised techniques. Our approach enables intelligent high-speed off-road navigation with zero human annotation, and with about forty minutes of expert data, outperforms several geometric and semantic traversability baselines, both in offline and real-world robot trials across multiple challenging off-road sites.
author: Samuel Triest;Matthew Sivaprakasam;Shubhra Aich;David Fan;Wenshan Wang;Sebastian Scherer
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Jet Propulsion Laboratory;School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University;Near Earth Autonomy Inc.
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Jet Propulsion Laboratory;Near Earth Autonomy

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot to off-road by letting it watch 40 minutes of 'expert' driving and use massive, power-hungry AI models to guess which terrain is safe. It basically tries to 'vibe-check' a ditch for safety without a human ever explaining what a ditch actually is.
why_bad: The acronym 'Velociraptor' is a linguistic war crime that stretches the English language further than the robot's actual perception range.
Using massive Visual Foundation Models for real-time robotics is computationally delusional; the robot likely processes its surroundings at the speed of a tectonic plate.
The 'risk-awareness' is mathematically flimsy, essentially claiming the robot is 'confident' until it encounters a single bush that wasn't in its 40-minute training video.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a tech bro who thinks he’s a survivalist because he watched a few 10-minute YouTube clips of Bear Grylls while sitting in a heated garage. He’s taking a $100k electric SUV into the deep woods with no map, relying entirely on a spotty 5G connection to a cloud server that compares tree branches to a database of Pinterest photos. It’s a 'self-supervised' adventure where 'risk-aware' just means he’s brought a really expensive GoPro to record the moment he gets stuck in a creek he didn't recognize. We're disrupting the 'having actual experience' industry by replacing it with high-latency hallucinations and a cool-sounding name.

title: Theia: Distilling Diverse Vision Foundation Models for Robot Learning
abstract: Vision-based robot policy learning, which maps visual inputs to actions, necessitates a holistic understanding of diverse visual tasks beyond single-task needs like classification or segmentation. Inspired by this, we introduce Theia, a vision foundation model for robot learning that distills multiple off-the-shelf vision foundation models trained on varied vision tasks. Theia's rich visual representations encode diverse visual knowledge, enhancing downstream robot learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Theia outperforms its teacher models and prior robot learning models using less training data and smaller model sizes. Additionally, we quantify the quality of pre-trained visual representations and hypothesize that higher entropy in feature norm distributions leads to improved robot learning performance. Code, models, and demo are available at https://theia.theaiinstitute.com.
author: Jinghuan Shang;Karl Schmeckpeper;Brandon B. May;Maria Vittoria Minniti;Tarik Kelestemur;David Watkins;Laura Herlant
aff: Department of Computer Science, State University of New York, Stony Brook;The Robotics and AI Institute;;The AI Institute;Boston Dynamics AI Institute;;The Robotics and AI Institute
aff_unique_norm: State University of New York;Robotics and AI Institute;AI Institute;Boston Dynamics AI Institute

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'super-brain' for robots by blending several existing AI models into one compact version that supposedly does everything better. They also claimed that 'messier' data patterns are the secret sauce to making robots smarter, but the academic community wasn't exactly sold on the recipe.
why_bad: Lack of Novelty: The paper is essentially an expensive exercise in 'Model Smoothies,' where they blended existing tech without offering a truly new architectural breakthrough.

The 'Correlation vs. Causation' Trap: Their big theory about 'feature norm entropy' feels like a post-hoc justification—basically finding a random pattern and claiming it’s a universal law of physics.
Marginal Utility: Reviewers likely found that the complex distillation process didn't provide enough real-world improvement to justify the massive computational overhead required to train it.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You want to be the ultimate athlete, so instead of training, you buy the used sweatbands of a marathoner, a powerlifter, and a yoga instructor. You blend those sweatbands into a 'Performance Smoothie' and drink it, claiming you now possess 'distilled athleticism.' Our startup, Theia, is selling that smoothie. We’re telling investors that because our smoothie is smaller than three actual humans, it's 'more efficient,' and we have a white paper claiming that the funkier the smell (the 'entropy'), the faster you’ll run. The reviewers are the grumpy gym owners pointing out that you’re still just a guy who drank a dirty sock.

title: Dynamic 3D Gaussian Tracking for Graph-Based Neural Dynamics Modeling
abstract: Videos of robots interacting with objects encode rich information about the objects' dynamics. However, existing video prediction approaches typically do not explicitly account for the 3D information from videos, such as robot actions and objects' 3D states, limiting their use in real-world robotic applications. In this work, we introduce a framework to learn object dynamics directly from multi-view RGB videos by explicitly considering the robot's action trajectories and their effects on scene dynamics. We utilize the 3D Gaussian representation of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to train a particle-based dynamics model using Graph Neural Networks. This model operates on sparse control particles downsampled from the densely tracked 3D Gaussian reconstructions. By learning the neural dynamics model on offline robot interaction data, our method can predict object motions under varying initial configurations and unseen robot actions. The 3D transformations of Gaussians can be interpolated from the motions of control particles, enabling the rendering of predicted future object states and achieving action-conditioned video prediction. The dynamics model can also be applied to model-based planning frameworks for object manipulation tasks. We conduct experiments on various kinds of deformable materials, including ropes, clothes, and stuffed animals, demonstrating our framework's ability to model complex shapes and dynamics. Our project page is available at \url{https://gaussian-gbnd.github.io/}.
keywords: Dynamics Model;3D Gaussian Splatting;Action-Conditioned Video Prediction;Model-Based Planning
author: Mingtong Zhang;Kaifeng Zhang;Yunzhu Li
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots physics by turning video footage into a cloud of fuzzy 3D points and asking a neural network to guess where they’d float next. While it looks cool in a project demo, the system essentially tries to predict the future by oversimplifying complex objects into a handful of digital 'beads' that don't actually know how reality works.
why_bad: Temporal Drift: The 3D 'particles' have the attention span of a goldfish, losing their original shape and wandering off into the void after just a few seconds of movement.
Oversimplification: By condensing detailed objects into 'sparse control particles,' the model treats a delicate silk scarf and a heavy rope as the same collection of stiff, jittery dots.

Hallucinated Generalization: The authors claim it works on 'unseen actions,' but the model likely just memorized the training data and collapses the moment the robot does anything more complex than a basic nudge.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Vibrating Sweater
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution submits Exhibit A: a digital recreation of a robot folding a sweater. The defense calls this 'Dynamic Gaussian Tracking,' but let the record show that by frame forty, the sweater has developed the structural integrity of a cloud of bees. They took a perfectly good video, turned it into a thousand glowing marbles, and then asked a computer to 'predict' the physics. But look closely! The 'control particles' are perjury incarnate—they aren't following the laws of gravity; they're following a statistical hunch. When the robot arm moves, the sweater doesn't fold; it undergoes a localized existential crisis, vibrating into a different dimension because the math couldn't remember where the sleeve was supposed to be. This isn't a breakthrough in robotics, your Honor; it's a high-definition crime against Newtonian physics. I rest my case.

title: Teaching Robots with Show and Tell: Using Foundation Models to Synthesize Robot Policies from Language and Visual Demonstration
abstract: We introduce a modular, neuro-symbolic framework for teaching robots new skills through language and visual demonstration. Our approach, ShowTell, composes a mixture of foundation models to synthesize robot manipulation programs that are easy to interpret and generalize across a wide range of tasks and environments. ShowTell is designed to handle complex demonstrations involving high level logic such as loops and conditionals while being intuitive and natural for end-users. We validate this approach through a series of real-world robot experiments, showing that ShowTell out-performs a state-of-the-art baseline based on GPT4-V, on a variety of tasks, and that it is able to generalize to unseen environments and within category objects.
keywords: learning from demonstration;language model planning;neuro-symbolic reasoning
author: Michael Murray;Abhishek Gupta;Maya Cakmak
aff: University of Washington;University of Washington;University of Washington, Seattle

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that learns chores by watching a video and listening to a narrator, claiming it's smarter than just asking an AI for help. In reality, they duct-taped several existing AI models together and hoped the robot wouldn't get confused when asked to repeat a task.
why_bad: The 'neuro-symbolic' framework is mostly just a fragile chain of prompts that falls apart if the robot encounters a situation not explicitly covered in the training video.
The comparison against GPT-4V is a 'straw man' setup, using a poorly optimized baseline to make their own hacky solution look revolutionary by comparison.
The claim of 'generalization' is backed by a sample size so small it’s statistically indistinguishable from a series of lucky coincidences in a highly controlled lab.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Scripted Sous-Chef
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims this robot is a culinary genius because it can follow a recipe for toast. But look closer at Exhibit A! This isn't 'reasoning'; it's a glorified macro-recorder masquerading as an intellect. The prosecution has proven that when a slightly different loaf of bread—a sourdough, your honor!—was introduced, the defendant's system hallucinated a kitchen fire and tried to sauté the toaster. They're asking you to believe a parrot is a Shakespearean scholar just because it can squawk 'To be or not to be.' This isn't a breakthrough in robotics; it's a Rube Goldberg machine in a lab coat, and the 'logic' it follows is as thin as the paper this rejection is printed on!

title: Meta-Control: Automatic Model-based Control Synthesis for Heterogeneous Robot Skills
abstract: The requirements for real-world manipulation tasks are diverse and often conflicting; some tasks require precise motion while others require force compliance; some tasks require avoidance of certain regions while others require convergence to certain states. Satisfying these varied requirements with a fixed state-action representation and control strategy is challenging, impeding the development of a universal robotic foundation model. In this work, we propose Meta-Control, the first LLM-enabled automatic control synthesis approach that creates customized state representations and control strategies tailored to specific tasks. Our core insight is that a meta-control system can be built to automate the thought process that human experts use to design control systems. Specifically, human experts heavily use a model-based, hierarchical (from abstract to concrete) thought model, then compose various dynamic models and controllers together to form a control system. Meta-Control mimics the thought model and harnesses LLM's extensive control knowledge with Socrates' "art of midwifery" to automate the thought process. Meta-Control stands out for its fully model-based nature, allowing rigorous analysis, generalizability, robustness, efficient parameter tuning, and reliable real-time execution.
author: Tianhao Wei;Liqian Ma;Rui Chen;Weiye Zhao;Changliu Liu
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to turn ChatGPT into a senior robotics engineer that writes its own math and physics code for different robot tasks. Instead of humans manually tuning how a robot moves, they want an AI to 'philosophize' its way into a control strategy and hope the robot doesn't punch a hole through the floor.
why_bad: The 'Socratic' approach is essentially a fancy word for 'prompt engineering,' masking the fact that LLMs are notorious for hallucinating physics equations that don't actually work in the real world.
There is a massive gap between 'automated thought' and 'guaranteed stability'; the paper fails to prove the robot won't undergo a kinetic unscheduled disassembly when the LLM misses a decimal point.
The claim of being a 'universal foundation model' is overblown, as the system relies on pre-existing controller templates and just uses the LLM as a high-priced glorified configuration script.
roast.analogy_name: The Philosopher King of IKEA Furniture Assembly
roast.analogy_description: Imagine hiring a guy who has read every book on the history of architecture but has never held a screwdriver to build your new kitchen. He refuses to look at the manual, instead 'midwifing' the truth of the cabinet through deep thought and a Socratic dialogue with the hex key. He promises a 'robust and rigorous' result, but halfway through, he decides the laws of gravity are merely a suggestion and your spice rack is now a load-bearing pillar of salt. It's a cinematic thriller where the 'Art of Midwifery' meets 'Total Structural Collapse.'

title: BiGym: A Demo-Driven Mobile Bi-Manual Manipulation Benchmark
abstract: We introduce BiGym, a new benchmark and learning environment for mobile bi-manual demo-driven robotic manipulation. BiGym features 40 diverse tasks set in home environments, ranging from simple target reaching to complex kitchen cleaning. To capture the real-world performance accurately, we provide human-collected demonstrations for each task, reflecting the diverse modalities found in real-world robot trajectories. BiGym supports a variety of observations, including proprioceptive data and visual inputs such as RGB, and depth from 3 camera views. To validate the usability of BiGym, we thoroughly benchmark the state-of-the-art imitation learning algorithms and demo-driven reinforcement learning algorithms within the environment and discuss the future opportunities.
author: Nikita Chernyadev;Nicholas Backshall;Xiao Ma;Yunfan Lu;Younggyo Seo;Stephen James
aff: Dyson;;Dyson Robot Learning Lab;;Dyson;Dyson

roast.laymen_summary: We built a virtual house for two-armed robots to practice 40 different chores, using human recordings as a 'cheat sheet' for learning. It's essentially a high-stakes digital playground designed to prove that teaching a robot to clean a kitchen is way harder than just writing code.
why_bad: The 'Sim-to-Real' Abyss: The paper is strictly virtual, offering no evidence that a robot trained in this 'dream world' wouldn't immediately smash a real plate.
The Baseline Faceplant: Current AI models performed embarrassingly poorly on these tasks, leaving reviewers to wonder if the benchmark is a breakthrough or just an impossible headache.
Task Padding: While '40 tasks' sounds like a lot, the reviewers noted a lack of technical novelty, suggesting it's more of a data collection project than a scientific leap.
roast.analogy_name: Peloton for a Robot that Doesn't Exist
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a $2,000 VR headset specifically for robots to practice folding laundry. We’ve got 40 high-def videos of Steve from accounting doing the dishes, and we’re telling the AI, 'Just vibe with Steve's energy and figure it out.' It’s a bold, 'fake it till you make it' play where the AI is currently stuck in a perpetual 'buffering' state, and the actual hardware is still sitting in a box because the software is too busy hallucinating about virtual spatulas. It’s disruptive, it’s sleek, and it’s currently 100% incapable of actually clearing a table.

title: Robust Manipulation Primitive Learning via Domain Contraction
abstract: Contact-rich manipulation plays an important role in everyday life, but uncertain parameters pose significant challenges to model-based planning and control. To address this issue, domain adaptation and domain randomization have been proposed to learn robust policies. However, they either lose the generalization ability to diverse instances or perform conservatively due to neglecting instance-specific information. In this paper, we propose a bi-level approach to learn robust manipulation primitives, including parameter-augmented policy learning using multiple models with tensor approximation, and parameter-conditioned policy retrieval through domain contraction. This approach unifies domain randomization and domain adaptation, providing optimal behaviors while keeping generalization ability. We validate the proposed method on three contact-rich manipulation primitives: hitting, pushing, and reorientation. The experimental results showcase the superior performance of our approach in generating robust policies for instances with diverse physical parameters.
author: Teng Xue;Amirreza Razmjoo;Suhan Shetty;Sylvain Calinon
aff: Idiap Research Institute;;EPFL - EPF Lausanne;EPFL - EPF Lausanne

roast.laymen_summary: Robots usually suck at chores because they can't tell if a box is full of lead or feathers just by looking at it. This paper tries to fix that by making the robot perform a high-speed mental math session to guess the object's weight and slipperiness before it even finishes reaching for it.
why_bad: The 'bi-level approach' is essentially a mathematical Rube Goldberg machine that is way too computationally expensive for a robot that just needs to push a block.
The authors claim 'superior performance' but only tested it in a pristine simulation where gravity is a suggestion and friction never lies, leaving real-world hardware out in the cold.
The 'Domain Contraction' technique is basically a rebranded version of narrowing down a search window—a trick as old as your grandma's Rolodex, but dressed up in tensor approximation jewelry.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every car has a different set of brakes and the driveway is covered in a mystery liquid, one robot must learn to park. Standard robots either drive 1 MPH out of pure terror or floor it and hope for the best. Enter: The Bi-Level Contraction. Watch as our mechanical hero attempts to solve a 4th-dimensional Rubik's Cube just to figure out how hard to tap the brakes. It’s a high-stakes thriller where 'Tensor Approximation' meets 'Wait, why is the robot vibrating?' This summer, witness the complexity of a rocket launch applied to the task of pushing a juice box.
roast.tone_check: Lean, mean, and mathematically obscene.

title: Monocular Event-Based Vision for Obstacle Avoidance with a Quadrotor
abstract: We present the first static-obstacle avoidance method for quadrotors using just an onboard, monocular event camera. Quadrotors are capable of fast and agile flight in cluttered environments when piloted manually, but vision-based autonomous flight in unknown environments is difficult in part due to the sensor limitations of traditional onboard cameras. Event cameras, however, promise nearly zero motion blur and high dynamic range, but produce a very large volume of events under significant ego-motion and further lack a continuous-time sensor model in simulation, making direct sim-to-real transfer not possible. By leveraging depth prediction as a pretext task in our learning framework, we can pre-train a reactive obstacle avoidance events-to-control policy with approximated, simulated events and then fine-tune the perception component with limited events-and-depth real-world data to achieve obstacle avoidance in indoor and outdoor settings. We demonstrate this across two quadrotor-event camera platforms in multiple settings and find, contrary to traditional vision-based works, that low speeds (1m/s) make the task harder and more prone to collisions, while high speeds (5m/s) result in better event-based depth estimation and avoidance. We also find that success rates in outdoor scenes can be significantly higher than in certain indoor scenes.
keywords: event-based vision;learning for control;simulation-to-real transfer;aerial robotics
author: Anish Bhattacharya;Marco Cannici;Nishanth Rao;Yuezhan Tao;Vijay Kumar;Nikolai Matni;Davide Scaramuzza
aff: University of Pennsylvania;University of Zurich;;University of Pennsylvania;University of Pennsylvania;School of Engineering and Applied Science, University of Pennsylvania;

roast.laymen_summary: We equipped a drone with a specialized high-speed camera that only detects changes in light, then realized it works best when the drone is flying dangerously fast. It turns out that if the drone slows down to be careful, the camera stops seeing enough data and the whole thing basically goes blind and hits a wall.
why_bad: The sim-to-real gap is more like a canyon because the 'approximated' simulated events fail to capture the noisy, chaotic reality of actual hardware.
The finding that the system is more prone to collisions at low speeds (1m/s) makes it a practical nightmare for any real-world safety certification.
The 'pretext task' of depth estimation acts as a brittle middleman that doesn't necessarily translate into robust steering logic when the environment gets messy.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents a drone that follows the Keanu Reeves school of navigation: if this bus drops below 50 miles per hour, everyone dies. The researchers admit they trained their 'expert witness' in a digital fever dream that doesn't match reality, and now they have the audacity to claim that flying like a caffeinated maniac is a 'feature' rather than a total failure of the perception system. They are essentially arguing that their client isn't guilty of reckless driving because he's only capable of seeing the road while he's actively breaking the speed limit!

title: DiffuseLoco: Real-Time Legged Locomotion Control with Diffusion from Offline Datasets
abstract: Offline learning at scale has led to breakthroughs in computer vision, natural language processing, and robotic manipulation domains. However, scaling up learning for legged robot locomotion, especially with multiple skills in a single policy, presents significant challenges for prior online reinforcement learning (RL) methods. To address this challenge, we propose DiffuseLoco, a novel, scalable framework that leverages diffusion models to directly learn from offline multimodal datasets with a diverse set of locomotion skills. With design choices tailored for real-time control in dynamical systems, including receding horizon control and delayed inputs, DiffuseLoco is capable of reproducing multimodality in performing various locomotion skills, zero-shot transferred to real quadruped robots and deployed on edge computes. Through extensive real-world benchmarking, DiffuseLoco exhibits better stability and velocity tracking performance compared to prior RL and non-diffusion-based behavior cloning baselines. This work opens new possibilities for scaling up learning-based legged locomotion control through the scaling of large, expressive models and diverse offline datasets.
author: Xiaoyu Huang;Yufeng Chi;Ruofeng Wang;Zhongyu Li;Xue Bin Peng;Sophia Shao;Borivoje Nikolic;Koushil Sreenath
aff: University of California, Berkeley;;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Simon Fraser University;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley
position: PhD student;;MS student;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor;Full Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of California, Berkeley;Simon Fraser University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot dog to walk by having it binge-watch thousands of hours of 'how-to' videos instead of letting it learn from its own mistakes. We used the same fancy AI math behind DALL-E to generate leg movements, hoping the robot could 'hallucinate' a stable walking path in real-time.
why_bad: Diffusion models are notoriously slow, and trying to run them on a robot's tiny brain in real-time is like trying to play Cyberpunk 2077 on a digital watch.
The paper claims it's a breakthrough, but reviewers felt it’s mostly just 'fancy behavior cloning'—it memorizes the data rather than actually understanding how to stay upright.
Offline datasets are great until the robot hits a literal bump in the road that wasn't in the training videos, leading to a very expensive pile of scrap metal.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where style beats substance, one robot dog decides to learn mountaineering by looking at 10,000 filtered photos of Everest. Witness the cinematic tension as the robot pauses for 50 milliseconds to 'denoise' its concept of a rock. It has the artistic soul of a painter, but the reflexes of a stoned sloth. Coming this fall: it’s the high-budget blockbuster where the protagonist spends $50,000 on compute power just to trip over a curb that wasn't in the script.
roast.scientific_accuracy_check: Verified. The critique focuses on the inference latency of diffusion-based policies and the distribution shift inherent in offline imitation learning for high-frequency dynamical systems.

title: Generalized Animal Imitator: Agile Locomotion with Versatile Motion Prior
abstract: The agility of animals, particularly in complex activities such as running, turning, jumping, and backflipping, stands as an exemplar for robotic system design. Transferring this suite of behaviors to legged robotic systems introduces essential inquiries: How can a robot be trained to learn multiple locomotion behaviors simultaneously? How can the robot execute these tasks with a smooth transition? How to integrate these skills for wide-range applications? This paper introduces the Versatile Instructable Motion prior (VIM) – a Reinforcement Learning framework designed to incorporate a range of agile locomotion tasks suitable for advanced robotic applications. Our framework enables legged robots to learn diverse agile low-level skills by imitating animal motions and manually designed motions. Our Functionality reward guides the robot's ability to adopt varied skills, and our Stylization reward ensures that robot motions align with reference motions. Our evaluations of the VIM framework span both simulation environments and real-world deployment. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that allows a robot to concurrently learn diverse agile locomotion skills using a single learning-based controller in the real world.
author: Ruihan Yang;Zhuoqun Chen;Jianhan Ma;Chongyi Zheng;Yiyu Chen;Quan Nguyen;Xiaolong Wang
aff: University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;Princeton University;University of Southern California;University of Southern California;University of California, San Diego
position: PhD student;MS student;MS student;PhD student;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of California, San Diego;Princeton University;University of Southern California

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to build a 'master brain' for robot dogs so they can run, jump, and backflip just by mimicking videos of real animals. Instead of teaching each trick separately, they used a single AI model to help the robot switch between being a Greyhound and a gymnast on the fly.
why_bad: The 'first of its kind' claim is a stretch, as the core math is essentially just rebranding existing imitation learning techniques like DeepMimic for quadrupeds.
The balance between the 'Functionality' and 'Stylization' rewards is a delicate house of cards; the robot often chooses looking cool over actually staying upright.
The 'smooth transitions' between behaviors are poorly defined, relying more on the luck of the reinforcement learning seed than on a robust architectural innovation.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching an athlete who trains exclusively by watching YouTube highlights of Usain Bolt and Simone Biles. We call it 'VIM.' Instead of teaching the athlete biology or physics, we just scream 'Be the dog!' until they can do a backflip. It's disruptive, it’s cinematic, and it works perfectly—as long as you ignore the twenty takes where they fell over and the fact that we're basically just playing 'Follow the Leader' with a $50,000 piece of hardware. We’re not just moving; we’re performing, even if the performance occasionally ends in a heap of expensive scrap metal.

title: Language-guided Manipulator Motion Planning with Bounded Task Space
abstract: Language-based robot control is a powerful and versatile method to control a robot manipulator where large language models (LLMs) are used to reason about the environment. However, the generated robot motions by these controllers often lack safety and performance, resulting in jerky movements. In this work, a novel modular framework for zero-shot motion planning for manipulation tasks is developed. The modular components do not require any motion-planning-specific training. An LLM is combined with a vision model to create Python code that interacts with a novel path planner, which creates a piecewise linear reference path with bounds around the path that ensure safety. An optimization-based planner, the BoundMPC framework, is utilized to execute optimal, safe, and collision-free trajectories along the reference path. The effectiveness of the approach is shown on various everyday manipulation tasks in simulation and experiment, shown in the video at www.acin.tuwien.ac.at/42d2.
author: Thies Oelerich;Christian Hartl-Nesic;Andreas Kugi
aff: Technische Universität Wien;;Technische Universität Wien

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop robot arms from moving like caffeinated toddlers by using ChatGPT to write steering instructions for a safety-first math controller. It’s essentially an attempt to turn 'vague vibes' from an AI into a 'don't-cross-the-line' game for heavy machinery.
why_bad: The 'BoundMPC' framework is essentially a standard optimization trick with a fresh coat of paint, offering very little theoretical 'meat' for a top-tier robotics conference.
Relying on LLMs to generate Python code for real-time physical control is a house of cards; one hallucinated variable and your robot is punching a hole through the drywall.
The 'piecewise linear' pathing is fundamentally at odds with smooth motion; it’s like trying to drive a Ferrari through a series of sharp, jagged zig-zags and calling it 'optimized'.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the grand finals of the Interdisciplinary Relays! In the starting blocks, we have a Blind Poet (the LLM) who’s never touched a wrench but is shouting directions based on a photo he saw once. He’s yelling to a Translator (the Python code) who’s frantically scribbling a map using only straight lines and right angles. Finally, we have the Sprinter (the MPC), who is blindfolded and told he can’t leave the chalk lines drawn by the Translator. The starter pistol fires! The Poet screams 'Move gracefully!', the Translator draws a jagged lightning bolt, and the Sprinter is twitching violently trying to follow a 90-degree turn at full speed. It’s not 'motion planning,' folks—it’s a high-stakes game of Telephone where the prize is a broken actuator and a rejected paper!

title: Multi-Task Interactive Robot Fleet Learning with Visual World Models
abstract: Recent advancements in large-scale multi-task robot learning offer the potential for deploying robot fleets in household and industrial settings, enabling them to perform diverse tasks across various environments. However, AI-enabled robots often face challenges with generalization and robustness when exposed to real-world variability and uncertainty. We introduce Sirius-Fleet, a multi-task interactive robot fleet learning framework to address these challenges. Sirius-Fleet monitors robot performance during deployment and involves humans to correct the robot's actions when necessary. We employ a visual world model to predict the outcomes of future actions and build anomaly predictors to predict whether they will likely result in anomalies. As the robot autonomy improves, the anomaly predictors automatically adapt their prediction criteria, leading to fewer requests for human intervention and gradually reducing human workload over time. Evaluations on large-scale benchmarks demonstrate Sirius-Fleet's effectiveness in improving multi-task policy performance and monitoring accuracy. We demonstrate Sirius-Fleet's performance in both RoboCasa in simulation and Mutex in the real world, two diverse, large-scale multi-task benchmarks. More information is available on the project website: https://ut-austin-rpl.github.io/sirius-fleet
author: Huihan Liu;Yu Zhang;Vaarij Betala;Evan Zhang;James Liu;Crystal Ding;Yuke Zhu
aff: ;ShanghaiTech University;;;;;Computer Science Department, University of Texas, Austin
aff_unique_norm: ShanghaiTech University;University of Texas at Austin

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to make a fleet of robots that do chores by having them imagine what might go wrong and call a human for help when they get scared. It’s essentially a high-tech version of a student who constantly asks the teacher for the answer instead of actually studying for the final.
why_bad: The 'Visual World Model' is prone to 'hallucinating' outcomes, meaning the robot is basically making decisions based on a vivid but inaccurate daydream.
The claim of 'reduced human workload' is dubious since the system relies on humans to bail it out of every minor edge case in real-time.
Experimental results are heavily reliant on simulation (RoboCasa), which is like claiming you're a Formula 1 driver because you're good at Mario Kart.
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the Defense claims this robot fleet is 'autonomous,' but it’s actually a witness that hallucinates evidence. The 'Visual World Model' is a series of doctored Polaroids shown to the jury to predict a crime that hasn't happened yet. Every time the defendant encounters a rug it doesn't recognize, it calls for a mistrial and demands a human judge handle the sentencing. This isn't a breakthrough in AI; it's a supervised work-release program where the supervisors are doing 90% of the manual labor while the defendant takes the credit.

title: MOSAIC: Modular Foundation Models for Assistive and Interactive Cooking
abstract: We present MOSAIC, a modular architecture for coordinating multiple robots to (a) interact with users using natural language and (b) manipulate an open vocabulary of everyday objects. At several levels, MOSAIC employs modularity: it leverages multiple large-scale pre-trained models for high-level tasks like language and image recognition, while using streamlined modules designed for low-level task-specific control. This decomposition allows us to reap the complementary benefits of foundation models and precise, more specialized models, enabling our system to scale to complex tasks that involve coordinating multiple robots and humans. First, we unit-test individual modules with 180 episodes of visuomotor picking, 60 episodes of human motion forecasting, and 46 online user evaluations of the task planner. We then extensively evaluate MOSAIC with 60 end-to-end trials. We discuss crucial design decisions, limitations of the current system, and open challenges in this domain
author: Huaxiaoyue Wang;Kushal Kedia;Juntao Ren;Rahma Abdullah;Atiksh Bhardwaj;Angela Chao;Kelly Y Chen;Nathaniel Chin;Prithwish Dan;Xinyi Fan;Gonzalo Gonzalez-Pumariega;Aditya Kompella;Maximus Adrian Pace;Yash Sharma;Xiangwan Sun;Neha Sunkara;Sanjiban Choudhury

position: PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;MS student;MS student;Undergrad student;MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to build a robotic sous-chef by duct-taping fancy AI 'brains' to mechanical arms, hoping they could chat with humans and flip pancakes without causing a kitchen fire. It turns out that getting multiple specialized AIs to play nice together is mostly just a high-tech way to ensure the system fails in four different languages simultaneously.
why_bad: The evaluation metrics are statistically flimsy; running 60 trials is more of a 'lucky weekend in the lab' than a robust scientific proof of concept.
The paper suffers from 'Frankenstein Syndrome,' where gluing existing APIs like GPT-4 and CLIP together is mistaken for actual architectural innovation.
The 'error compounding' problem is ignored: if the vision module mistakes a cucumber for a finger, the rest of the sophisticated pipeline just efficiently proceeds to commit a crime.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Disjointed Sous-Chef
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, look at the defendant: a 'modular' chef. The Prosecution argues that this isn't a chef at all, but four toddlers in a trench coat. One toddler speaks French (the LLM), one is blind but feels shapes (the Vision module), and one is a caffeinated gymnast (the Controller). When asked to 'crack an egg,' the French toddler writes a poem about shells, the blind one mistakes the chef's watch for an egg, and the gymnast crushes it into the floorboards. The defense claims 'synergy,' but the judge—the reviewer—is looking at a kitchen that resembles a crime scene. Case dismissed for lack of evidence that these modules have ever actually spoken to one another without a subpoena.

title: Learning Visuotactile Estimation and Control for Non-prehensile Manipulation under Occlusions
abstract: Manipulation without grasping, known as non-prehensile manipulation, is essential for dexterous robots in contact-rich environments, but presents many challenges relating with underactuation, hybrid-dynamics, and frictional uncertainty. Additionally, object occlusions in a scenario of contact uncertainty and where the motion of the object evolves independently from the robot becomes a critical problem, which previous literature fails to address. We present a method for learning visuotactile state estimators and uncertainty-aware control policies for non-prehensile manipulation under occlusions, by leveraging diverse interaction data from privileged policies trained in simulation. We formulate the estimator within a Bayesian deep learning framework, to model its uncertainty, and then train uncertainty-aware control policies by incorporating the pre-learned estimator into the reinforcement learning (RL) loop, both of which lead to significantly improved estimator and policy performance. Therefore, unlike prior non-prehensile research that relies on complex external perception set-ups, our method successfully handles occlusions after sim-to-real transfer to robotic hardware with a simple onboard camera.
keywords: State Estimation;Reinforcement Learning with Tactile Sensing;Non-prehensile Manipulation
author: Juan Del Aguila Ferrandis;Joao Moura;Sethu Vijayakumar
aff: ;University of Edinburgh, University of Edinburgh;

title: What Matters in Range View 3D Object Detection
abstract: Lidar-based perception pipelines rely on 3D object detection models to interpret complex scenes. While multiple representations for lidar exist, the range view is enticing since it losslessly encodes the entire lidar sensor output. In this work, we achieve state-of-the-art amongst range view 3D object detection models without using multiple techniques proposed in past range view literature. We explore range view 3D object detection across two modern datasets with substantially different properties: Argoverse 2 and Waymo Open. Our investigation reveals key insights: (1) input feature dimensionality significantly influences the overall performance, (2) surprisingly, employing a classification loss grounded in 3D spatial proximity works as well or better compared to more elaborate IoU-based losses, and (3) addressing non-uniform lidar density via a straightforward range subsampling technique outperforms existing multi-resolution, range-conditioned networks. Our experiments reveal that techniques proposed in recent range view literature are not needed to achieve state-of-the-art performance. Combining the above findings, we establish a new state-of-the-art model for range view 3D object detection — improving AP by 2.2% on the Waymo Open dataset while maintaining a runtime of 10 Hz. We are the first to benchmark a range view model on the Argoverse 2 dataset and outperform strong voxel-based baselines. All models are multi-class and open-source. Code is available at https://github.com/benjaminrwilson/range-view-3d-detection.
author: Benjamin Wilson;Nicholas Autio Mitchell;Jhony Kaesemodel Pontes;James Hays
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;NVIDIA;Latitude AI;Georgia Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors discovered that self-driving car 'eyes' (LiDAR) were being overfed with complex math that actually made them slower and dumber. By stripping away the fancy algorithmic clutter and sticking to basic spatial common sense, they accidentally built a faster system that beats the over-engineered models.
why_bad: The 'contribution' is basically just deleting other people's complex features, which reviewers often mistake for a lack of effort rather than a stroke of genius.
Claiming a 2.2% improvement is statistically 'cool' but scientifically 'meh' when your main discovery is that simpler math is better.
The range subsampling technique is essentially a high-tech way of saying 'we just looked at fewer points,' which lacks the academic 'razzle-dazzle' required for top-tier acceptance.
roast.analogy_name: The Minimalist Boxer vs. The Gadget King
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the Main Event! In the red corner, we have 'The Over-Engineer,' wearing $5,000 sensors, heated boots, and a biometric-tracking headband. And in the blue corner, we have Benjamin 'Keep It Simple' Wilson, who showed up in cargo shorts and a t-shirt. The whistle blows, and Wilson just lands a straight jab! No spinning back-kicks, no cinematic slow-mo, just basic geometry to the jaw. The crowd—the 'Waymo Open' fans—are stunned because the simple jab is landing 2.2% more often! But the judges? They're shaking their heads. They wanted to see the high-tech gadgets in action, not a guy winning by simply standing in the right spot and throwing out his expensive equipment. It’s a technical knockout that the committee is refusing to call because it makes their fancy gear look like expensive paperweights!

title: OmniH2O: Universal and Dexterous Human-to-Humanoid Whole-Body Teleoperation and Learning
abstract: We present OmniH2O (Omni Human-to-Humanoid), a learning-based system for whole-body humanoid teleoperation and autonomy. Using kinematic pose as a universal control interface, OmniH2O enables various ways for a human to control a full-sized humanoid with dexterous hands, including using real-time teleoperation through VR headset, verbal instruction, and RGB camera. OmniH2O also enables full autonomy by learning from teleoperated demonstrations or integrating with frontier models such as GPT-4. OmniH2O demonstrates versatility and dexterity in various real-world whole-body tasks through teleoperation or autonomy, such as playing multiple sports, moving and manipulating objects, and interacting with humans. We develop an RL-based sim-to-real pipeline, which involves large-scale retargeting and augmentation of human motion datasets, learning a real-world deployable policy with sparse sensor input by imitating a privileged teacher policy, and reward designs to enhance robustness and stability. We release the first humanoid whole-body control dataset, OmniH2O-6, containing six everyday tasks, and demonstrate humanoid whole-body skill learning from teleoperated datasets. Videos at the anonymous website [https://anonymous-omni-h2o.github.io/](https://anonymous-omni-h2o.github.io/)
author: Tairan He;Zhengyi Luo;Xialin He;Wenli Xiao;Chong Zhang;Weinan Zhang;Kris M. Kitani;Changliu Liu;Guanya Shi
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Meta Platforms, Inc.;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Carnegie Mellon University;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;Shanghai Jiaotong University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Meta;Shanghai Jiao Tong University;ETH Zurich

roast.laymen_summary: This system lets you pilot a humanoid robot like a high-tech avatar using a VR headset or simple voice commands. It attempts to bridge the gap between human grace and robotic clunkiness by having the AI 'shadow' human movements across a handful of sports and chores.
why_bad: The 'Universal' claim is hilariously overblown; the system was tested on exactly six tasks, which is less 'omni-capable' and more 'occasionally functional.'

The reliance on a 'privileged teacher' in simulation means the robot learns in a world of perfect information, then has a total existential crisis when faced with the messy, noisy sensors of the real world.

The motion retargeting often produces 'uncanny valley' physics where the robot's center of mass is more of a suggestion than a law, leading to stability issues that are conveniently edited out of the highlight reel.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a 'Universal Pro-Athlete Simulator' that lets you play NBA-level basketball from your couch using a Wii Remote and a grainy Zoom feed. The pitch deck is gorgeous, but the reality is that you're just a guy in pajamas waving his arms while a $100,000 mannequin faceplants in a parking lot. It’s essentially a high-stakes marionette show where the strings are made of 'hope' and the AI 'brain' is just GPT-4 hallucinating what a knee joint does under pressure. We're disrupting the 'not-falling-over' industry by making falling over look like a deliberate data point.

title: Modeling Drivers’ Situational Awareness from Eye Gaze for Driving Assistance
abstract:
Intelligent driving assistance can alert drivers to objects in their environment; however, such systems require a model of drivers' situational awareness (SA) (what aspects of the scene they are already aware of) to avoid unnecessary alerts. 
Moreover, collecting the data to train such an SA model is challenging: 
being an internal human cognitive state, driver SA is difficult to measure, and non-verbal signals such as eye gaze are some of the only outward manifestations of it. Traditional methods to obtain SA labels rely on probes that result in sparse, intermittent SA labels unsuitable for modeling a dense, temporally correlated process via machine learning. We propose a novel interactive labeling protocol that captures dense, continuous SA labels and use it to collect an object-level SA dataset in a VR driving simulator. Our dataset comprises 20 unique drivers' SA labels, driving data, and gaze (over 320 minutes of driving) which will be made public.
Additionally, we train an SA model from this data, formulating the object-level driver SA prediction problem as a semantic segmentation problem. Our formulation allows all objects in a scene at a timestep to be processed simultaneously, leveraging global scene context and local gaze-object relationships together.
Our experiments show that this formulation leads to improved performance over common sense baselines and prior art on the SA prediction task.
author: Abhijat Biswas;Pranay Gupta;Shreeya Khurana;David Held;Henny Admoni
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop cars from nagging drivers by using eye-tracking to figure out what the driver had already noticed. Essentially, they built an AI meant to distinguish between 'I see that pedestrian' and 'I’m zoning out,' but it mostly just learned to guess where people look in a video game.
why_bad: The 'Looked but Failed to See' Problem: The model assumes that if your eyes pass over an object, your brain definitely registered it, ignoring the very human tendency to stare directly at a truck while thinking about a sandwich.
Simulator Syndrome: The data comes from 20 people in a VR headset, which lacks the 'actual threat of death' variable that tends to sharpen situational awareness in the real world.
The Tiny Data Trap: 320 minutes of driving is barely a commute in Los Angeles; training a complex neural network on such a small sample is like trying to learn French by reading one menu.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where your car won't stop screaming at you... ONE TEAM thinks they can read your mind by watching your pupils dilate. Coming this summer to a VR headset near you: 'THE GAZE-INATOR.' It knows where you're looking, but it has no idea if you're actually paying attention or just dissociating into the abyss. It’s a high-stakes thriller where the AI bets your life on the fact that 'staring' equals 'caring.' Rated R for Restricted Data and Ridiculous Assumptions.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized.

title: OPEN TEACH: A Versatile Teleoperation System for Robotic Manipulation
abstract: Open-sourced, user-friendly tools form the bedrock of scientific advancement across disciplines. The widespread adoption of data-driven learning has led to remarkable progress in multi-fingered dexterity, bimanual manipulation, and applications ranging from logistics to home robotics. However, existing data collection platforms are often proprietary, costly, or tailored to specific robotic morphologies. We present OPEN TEACH, a new teleoperation system leveraging VR headsets to immerse users in mixed reality for intuitive robot control. built on the affordable Meta Quest 3, which costs $500, OPEN TEACH enables real-time control of various robots, including multi-fingered hands, bimanual arms, and mobile manipulators, through an easy-to-use app. Using natural hand gestures and movements, users can manipulate robots at up to 90Hz with smooth visual feedback and interface widgets offering closeup environment views. We demonstrate the versatility of OPEN TEACH across 38 tasks on different robots. A comprehensive user study indicates significant improvement in teleoperation capability over the AnyTeleop framework. Further experiments exhibit that the collected data is compatible with policy learning on 10 dexterous and contact-rich manipulation tasks. Currently supporting Franka, xArm, Jaco, Allegro, and Hello Stretch platforms, OPEN TEACH is fully open-sourced to promote broader adoption. Videos are available at https://anon-open-teach.github.io/.
author: Aadhithya Iyer;Zhuoran Peng;Yinlong Dai;Irmak Guzey;Siddhant Haldar;Soumith Chintala;Lerrel Pinto
aff: New York University;New York University;New York University;New York University;New York University;Meta Facebook;New York University
position: MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;PhD student;PhD student;Researcher;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We spent $500 on a VR headset to turn expensive robots into high-stakes remote-controlled toys for humans to pilot. It’s an open-source manual for anyone who wants to replace autonomous AI with a grad student sweating inside a Meta Quest 3.
why_bad: Technical Novelty: The paper is essentially a glorified API wrapper for the Meta Quest SDK that any hobbyist with a Unity license could build in a weekend.
The Baseline Problem: Comparing the system only to 'AnyTeleop' is the academic equivalent of winning a gold medal in a race where you were the only one who didn't trip over the starting line.
Latency vs. Reality: Claiming a 90Hz refresh rate is cute, but it ignores the crushing jitter of real-world Wi-Fi that turns 'dexterous manipulation' into a robotic seizure.
roast.analogy_name: Avatar: The Way of the Wi-Fi
roast.analogy_description: In a world where robots are too incompetent to pick up a spoon, one team has a revolutionary idea: put a human in a plastic visor and make them do it instead. Witness the heart-pounding tension of a $30,000 robotic arm mimicking a human hand—with just enough lag to make every movement feel like a drunken fever dream. Coming this fall: it’s not Artificial Intelligence, it’s just a guy in a headset trying not to vomit while he remotely stack blocks in a windowless lab.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels nominal.

title: Multi-Strategy Deployment-Time Learning and Adaptation for Navigation under Uncertainty
abstract: We present an approach for performant point-goal navigation in unfamiliar partially-mapped environments. When deployed, our robot runs multiple strategies for deployment-time learning and visual domain adaptation in parallel and quickly selects the best-performing among them. Choosing between policies as they are learned or adapted between navigation trials requires continually updating estimates of their performance as they evolve. Leveraging recent work in model-based learning-informed planning under uncertainty, we determine lower bounds on the would-be performance of newly-updated policies on old trials without needing to re-deploy them. This information constrains and accelerates bandit-like policy selection, affording quick selection of the best-performing strategy shortly after it would start to yield good performance. We validate the effectiveness of our approach in simulated maze-like environments, showing improved navigation cost and cumulative regret versus existing baselines.
author: Abhishek Paudel;Xuesu Xiao;Gregory J. Stein
aff: George Mason University;George Mason University;George Mason University

roast.laymen_summary: We built a robot that tries several different ways to navigate a maze simultaneously and attempts to guess which method is best without actually finishing its chores. It uses theoretical math to predict how its new ideas would have handled old mistakes, avoiding the inconvenience of real-world testing.
why_bad: Running multiple learning strategies in parallel is a computational death sentence for any battery-powered robot that isn't tethered to a nuclear reactor.
The 'lower bound' performance estimates are essentially mathematical fan fiction that assumes simulation physics will behave perfectly in the messy real world.
Validating only in 'maze-like simulations' is the academic equivalent of claiming you can survive the Amazon because you're decent at Minecraft.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking competition where, instead of just making a soup, the chef starts five different pots using five different recipes at the exact same time. Halfway through, the chef stops tasting the food and starts using a calculator to predict how 'Pot C' would have tasted if they had used the salt from 'Pot A' ten minutes ago. It’s a chaotic, over-engineered nightmare that produces a lot of heat and very little dinner. The judges aren't impressed that you've 'mathematically proven' the soup isn't salty—they’re just wondering why the kitchen is on fire and where the actual food is.

title: GenSim2: Scaling Robot Data Generation with Multi-modal and Reasoning LLMs
abstract: Robotic simulation today remains challenging to scale up due to the human efforts required to create diverse simulation tasks and scenes. Simulation-trained policies also face scalability issues as many sim-to-real methods focus on a single task. To address these challenges, this work proposes GenSim2, a scalable framework that leverages coding LLMs with multi-modal and reasoning capabilities for complex and realistic simulation task creation, including long-horizon tasks with articulated objects. To automatically generate demonstration data for these tasks at scale, we propose planning and RL solvers that generalize within object categories. The pipeline can generate data for up to 100 articulated tasks with 200 objects and reduce the required human efforts. To utilize such data, we propose an effective multi-task language-conditioned policy architecture, dubbed proprioceptive point-cloud transformer (PPT), that learns from the generated demonstrations and exhibits strong sim-to-real zero-shot transfer. Combining the proposed pipeline and the policy architecture, we show a promising usage of GenSim2 that the generated data can be used for zero-shot transfer or co-train with real-world collected data, which enhances the policy performance by 20% compared with training exclusively on limited real data.
author: Pu Hua;Minghuan Liu;Annabella Macaluso;Yunfeng Lin;Weinan Zhang;Huazhe Xu;Lirui Wang

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to automate the boring job of building robot training simulations by letting an AI write the code for them. They then used this massive pile of synthetic 'hallucinated' practice data to help a real-world robot suck slightly less at basic chores.
why_bad: The 20% performance boost is compared to a 'limited' real-world dataset, which is essentially bragging about beating a runner who was wearing lead shoes.
The 'automated task generation' often produces repetitive or physically unrealistic scenarios that don't actually translate to the messy complexity of a real kitchen.
The paper relies heavily on the 'PPT' architecture without proving it’s actually an innovation rather than just a collection of current AI buzzwords glued together.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine trying to train a world-class chef by having them play a VR cooking game coded by someone who has only ever read descriptions of food. You’ve generated 100 'complex' recipes like 'digital boiling water' and 'low-poly omelet,' and because the chef practiced in this fake kitchen for a thousand hours, they are now slightly better than a person who has never seen a stove. You're serving a 20% better frozen dinner and calling it a Michelin star breakthrough, ignoring the fact that the 'chef' still doesn't know that real oil can catch fire.

title: DexGraspNet 2.0: Learning Generative Dexterous Grasping in Large-scale Synthetic Cluttered Scenes
abstract: Grasping in cluttered scenes remains highly challenging for dexterous hands due to the scarcity of data. To address this problem, we present a large-scale synthetic dataset, encompassing 1319 objects, 8270 scenes, and 426 million grasps. Beyond benchmarking, we also explore data-efficient learning strategies from grasping data. We reveal that the combination of a conditional generative model that focuses on local geometry and a grasp dataset that emphasizes complex scene variations is key to achieving effective generalization. Our proposed generative method outperforms all baselines in simulation experiments. Furthermore, it demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer through test-time depth restoration, attaining 91% real-world success rate, showcasing the robust potential of utilizing fully synthetic training data.
author: Jialiang Zhang;Haoran Liu;Danshi Li;XinQiang Yu;Haoran Geng;Yufei Ding;Jiayi Chen;He Wang
aff: Peking University;Peking University;New York University;Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences;Peking University;Peking University;Peking University;Peking University
position: Undergrad student;Undergrad student;MS student;MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor;PhD student
aff_unique_norm: Peking University;New York University;Chinese Academy of Sciences

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a massive digital playground with millions of virtual hands grabbing virtual trash to teach robots how to stop being clumsy. They claim that after enough simulation, the robot can finally pick up a real-world coffee mug without dropping it 91% of the time.
why_bad: The 'Brute Force' Offense: Critics felt the authors just threw 426 million data points at the problem to mask a lack of actual algorithmic innovation.
The Simulation Bubble: Reviewers suspected the 91% 'real-world' success rate was heavily cherry-picked and wouldn't hold up if the robot encountered a slightly shiny spoon or a dusty room.

The DLC Syndrome: The paper was seen as an incremental 'Version 2.0' that offered more data but very few new insights over their previous work, making it feel like an expensive map pack rather than a new game.
roast.analogy_name: The 'I Played 10,000 Hours of Surgeon Simulator' Defense
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution finds it laughable. The defendant claims to be a master of 'dexterous manipulation,' yet his entire CV consists of playing a high-res video game in his mother’s basement. You can't enter a 'Large-scale Synthetic Cluttered Scene' into evidence when that 'scene' is just a glorified Minecraft mod! My learned colleague argues that because the defendant successfully grabbed a digital pixel-apple 400 million times, he is qualified to perform open-heart surgery. But look at the witness stand: the moment we change the lighting or give him a translucent glass, he experiences a 'distribution shift' and freezes! This isn't a breakthrough in robotics; it’s a teenager claiming he can fly a Boeing 747 because he has the 'Gold Edition' of Flight Simulator. I move to strike this '91% success rate' from the record—it was performed in a controlled lab that’s more 'theatrical set' than 'real world'!

title: Leveraging Locality to Boost Sample Efficiency in Robotic Manipulation
abstract: Given the high cost of collecting robotic data in the real world, sample efficiency is a consistently compelling pursuit in robotics. In this paper, we introduce SGRv2, an imitation learning framework that enhances sample efficiency through improved visual and action representations. Central to the design of SGRv2 is the incorporation of a critical inductive bias—$\textit{action locality}$, which posits that robot's actions are predominantly influenced by the target object and its interactions with the local environment. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world settings demonstrate that action locality is essential for boosting sample efficiency. SGRv2 excels in RLBench tasks with keyframe control using merely 5 demonstrations and surpasses the RVT baseline in 23 of 26 tasks. Furthermore, when evaluated on ManiSkill2 and MimicGen using dense control, SGRv2's success rate is 2.54 times that of SGR. In real-world environments, with only eight demonstrations, SGRv2 can perform a variety of tasks at a markedly higher success rate compared to baseline models.
author: Tong Zhang;Yingdong Hu;Jiacheng You;Yang Gao
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized robots are slow learners, so they told their AI to stop overthinking and only pay attention to the stuff it’s actually touching. They claim this 'blinder' approach lets the robot learn a task from just five examples, assuming the rest of the world basically doesn't exist.
why_bad: The 'action locality' breakthrough is essentially just a fancy way of saying they cropped the camera feed and called it an inductive bias.
The model’s success is heavily padded by RLBench, a simulator where tasks are so sterile they make a hospital operating room look like a dumpster fire.
By ignoring the global environment, the robot becomes a specialized idiot that can pick up a block but will happily punch through a glass wall if it happens to be in the way.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're on a high-stakes cooking show, and your new apprentice claims they can master a 12-course meal after watching you cook it once. The catch? They insist on wearing a VR headset that only shows them the 6 inches directly around the frying pan. Sure, they can flip a scallop perfectly with zero practice, but they just stepped in the trash can and set their apron on fire because they've decided 'the rest of the kitchen' is irrelevant data. You haven't invented a more efficient chef; you've just invented a very fast way to cause a kitchen fire while looking at a scallop.
roast.tone_check: Dry, snarky, and 100% unimpressed by your sample efficiency.

title: RoboKoop: Efficient Control Conditioned Representations from Visual Input in Robotics using Koopman Operator
abstract: Developing agents that can perform complex control tasks from high-dimensional observations is a core ability of autonomous agents that requires underlying robust task control policies and adapting the underlying visual representations to the task. Most existing policies need a lot of training samples and treat this problem from the lens of two-stage learning with a controller learned on top of pre-trained vision models. We approach this problem from the lens of Koopman theory and learn visual representations from robotic agents conditioned on specific downstream tasks in the context of learning stabilizing control for the agent. We introduce a Contrastive Spectral Koopman Embedding network that allows us to learn efficient linearized visual representations from the agent's visual data in a high dimensional latent space and utilizes reinforcement learning to perform off-policy control on top of the extracted representations with a linear controller. Our method enhances stability and control in gradient dynamics over time, significantly outperforming existing approaches by improving efficiency and accuracy in learning task policies over extended horizons.
author: Hemant Kumawat;Biswadeep Chakraborty;Saibal Mukhopadhyay
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;Georgia Institute of Technology;Georgia Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach robots to handle complex tasks by simplifying messy camera footage into neat, straight-line math equations. It's an attempt to make robot brains more efficient by pretending the chaotic physical world follows the same simple rules as a high school geometry homework assignment.
why_bad: The Koopman operator assumes you can linearize complex reality, which falls apart the moment the robot actually touches something or encounters a 'non-smooth' event.
The 'Contrastive Spectral' architecture is a high-calorie buzzword salad that adds massive computational overhead for what amounts to a glorified smoothing filter.
Claims of 'outperforming existing approaches' are likely based on hyper-specific simulations where the environment is as predictable as a metronome.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re a VC. We come to you with a jetpack. But instead of expensive sensors and gyroscopes, our pilot just stares at an Excel spreadsheet. Why? Because we’ve decided that if you look at a hurricane through a dark enough lens, the wind moves in a perfectly straight line. We’re disrupting the 'actually having to see' market by projecting the entire universe into a 500-dimensional disco room where physics is just a suggestion. It’s elegant, it’s mathematical, and it works perfectly—as long as the pilot never actually turns the engine on.

title: SPIRE: Synergistic Planning, Imitation, and Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Manipulation
abstract: Robot learning has proven to be a general and effective technique for programming manipulators. Imitation learning is able to teach robots solely from human demonstrations but is bottlenecked by the capabilities of the demonstrations. Reinforcement learning uses exploration to discover better behaviors; however, the space of possible improvements can be too large to start from scratch. And for both techniques, the learning difficulty increases proportional to the length of the manipulation task. Accounting for this, we propose SPIRE, a system that first uses Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) to decompose tasks into smaller learning subproblems and second combines imitation and reinforcement learning to maximize their strengths. We develop novel strategies to train learning agents when deployed in the context of a planning system. We evaluate SPIRE on a suite of long-horizon and contact-rich robot manipulation problems. We find that SPIRE outperforms prior approaches that integrate imitation learning, reinforcement learning, and planning by 35% to 50% in average task performance, is 6 times more data efficient in the number of human demonstrations needed to train proficient agents, and learns to complete tasks nearly twice as efficiently. View https://sites.google.com/view/spire-corl-2024 for more details.
author: Zihan Zhou;Animesh Garg;Dieter Fox;Caelan Reed Garrett;Ajay Mandlekar
aff: Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto;NVIDIA;Department of Computer Science;NVIDIA;NVIDIA
aff_unique_dep: Department of Computer Science;NVIDIA Corporation;Department of Computer Science

roast.laymen_summary: SPIRE attempts to fix clumsy robots by giving them a GPS for tasks, a YouTube tutorial to copy, and the freedom to fail until they figure it out. It's essentially trying to teach a robot to make a sandwich by combining a recipe book, a cooking show, and trial-and-error all at once.
why_bad: It's an 'Algorithmic Frankenstein' that stitches together three existing techniques without contributing a genuinely novel 'secret sauce' to the field.
The hand-offs between the planning phase and the learning phase are notoriously brittle, likely falling apart if the environment changes by even a fraction.
The benchmarks are 'softball' setups; the authors claimed victory by comparing their high-powered hybrid against simplified versions of competitors that weren't designed for this specific marathon.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the Big Leagues, where SPIRE is stepping onto the field with a 1,000-page playbook, a VR headset playing Tom Brady highlights, and a personal trainer whispering in its ear! It’s trying to run a quadruple-reverse flea-flicker before it even knows how to snap the ball. The commentators are baffled: sure, it looks great in a choreographed practice against stationary pylons, but the moment a real defender shows up, this 'synergistic' strategy is going to look like a yard sale. It’s a classic case of bringing a rocket launcher to a knife fight and still missing the target!

title: SonicSense: Object Perception from In-Hand Acoustic Vibration
abstract: We introduce SonicSense, a holistic design of hardware and software to enable rich robot object perception through in-hand acoustic vibration sensing. While previous studies have shown promising results with acoustic sensing for object perception, current solutions are constrained to a handful of objects with simple geometries and homogeneous materials, single-finger sensing, and mixing training and testing on the same objects. SonicSense enables container inventory status differentiation, heterogeneous material prediction, 3D shape reconstruction, and object re-identification from a diverse set of 83 real-world objects. Our system employs a simple but effective heuristic exploration policy to interact with the objects as well as end-to-end learning-based algorithms to fuse vibration signals to infer object properties. Our framework underscores the significance of in-hand acoustic vibration sensing in advancing robot tactile perception.
keywords: Tactile Perception;Object State Estimation;Audio;Acoustic Vibration Sensing
author: Jiaxun Liu;Boyuan Chen
aff: Duke University;Duke University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to give robots 'super-hearing' by putting vibration sensors in their fingers so they can identify objects just by touching and tapping them. It's essentially a high-tech version of shaking a Christmas present to guess what's inside, but using complex algorithms to figure out if a container is full or what it's made of.
why_bad: Acoustic Vulnerability: The reviewers likely pointed out that the system would probably have a meltdown if someone sneezed in the same room, as ambient noise ruins vibration data.
Overfitting to the 'Playlist': With only 83 objects, the AI isn't learning the 'physics of sound'; it’s likely just memorizing the specific 'songs' of those exact items.
The 'Why Bother' Factor: The 3D reconstruction claims are scientifically flimsy, as the resulting models are often low-resolution blobs that a $20 camera could identify ten times faster.
roast.analogy_name: The Blindfolded Percussionist at the Grocery Store
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the 'Over-Engineering Open,' where our contender is trying to do a weekly grocery run while blindfolded, ear-plugged, and wearing oven mitts with microphones taped to them! He’s not looking at the labels; no, he’s flicking a jar of pickles and trying to calculate the liquid displacement based on the 'thwack' sound. It’s a bold strategy, folks! He’s spent six months practicing on these specific 83 jars, but the moment a child screams in Aisle 4, his 'sonar' thinks a gallon of milk is a chainsaw. He’s doing complex Fourier transforms to realize he’s holding a potato, while everyone else is just using their eyes. It’s a masterpiece of doing the absolute most to achieve the absolute least!

title: Action Space Design in Reinforcement Learning for Robot Motor Skills
abstract:
Practitioners often rely on intuition to select action spaces for learning. The choice can substantially impact final performance even when choosing among configuration-space representations such as joint position, velocity, and torque commands. We examine action space selection considering a wheeled-legged robot, a quadruped robot, and a simulated suite of locomotion, manipulation, and control tasks. 
We analyze the mechanisms by which action space can improve performance and conclude that the action space can influence learning performance substantially in a task-dependent way.
