Moreover, we find that much of the practical impact of action space selection on learning dynamics can be explained by improved policy initialization and behavior between timesteps.
author: Julian Eßer;Gabriel B. Margolis;Oliver Urbann;Sören Kerner;Pulkit Agrawal
aff: Fraunhofer IML;;Fraunhofer IML;;Massachusetts Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Fraunhofer Institute for Material Flow and Logistics;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: We spent a lot of GPU hours confirming that whether you tell a robot to move by position, speed, or raw force actually matters for its success. It turns out that 'it depends on the task' is the scientific equivalent of 'shrug emoji,' but we found that a good head start and smooth transitions are the real secret sauce.
why_bad: The central conclusion—that results are 'task-dependent'—is a scientific platitude that offers no predictive framework for future researchers.
The 'discovery' that action spaces impact policy initialization is effectively stating that how you start a race determines how well you run it, which isn't exactly a Nobel-worthy breakthrough.
The paper lacks a novel algorithmic contribution, serving more as an expensive 'Review of Things We Already Suspected' rather than a push forward for the field.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the RL Open! Imagine you're teaching a teenager to parallel park, but you can only communicate via walkie-talkie. This paper is a heated debate over whether you should command them by 'steering wheel angle,' 'turn speed,' or 'raw muscle torque.' Our authors are in the commentary booth acting shocked that telling a novice to 'apply 5 Newton-meters of force' results in a totaled minivan, while 'turn the wheel 10 degrees' actually works. They’ve basically written a 10-page report concluding that the car’s trajectory between your shouts matters, which is like a NASCAR analyst being stunned to learn that physics continues to exist between the green and checkered flags. It’s a lot of high-tech telemetry just to prove that if you give a kid a confusing steering wheel, they’re going to hit the curb.

title: PianoMime: Learning a Generalist, Dexterous Piano Player from Internet Demonstrations
abstract:
In this work, we introduce PianoMime, a framework for training a piano-playing agent using internet demonstrations.
The internet is a promising source of large-scale demonstrations for training our robot agents. 
In particular, for the case of piano-playing, Youtube is full of videos of professional pianists playing a wide myriad of songs.
In our work, we leverage these demonstrations to learn a generalist piano-playing agent capable of playing any arbitrary song.
Our framework is divided into three parts: a data preparation phase to extract the informative features from the Youtube videos,
a policy learning phase to train song-specific expert policies from the demonstrations and a policy distillation phase to distil the policies into a single generalist agent.
We explore different policy designs to represent the agent and evaluate the influence of the amount of training data on the generalization capability of the agent to novel songs not available in the dataset.
We show that we are able to learn a policy with up to 57% F1 score on unseen songs.
author: Cheng Qian;Julen Urain;Kevin Zakka;Jan Peters
aff: Technische Universität München;University of California, Berkeley;TU Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt
aff_unique_norm: Technische Universität München;University of California, Berkeley;Technische Universität Darmstadt

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot Mozart by forcing an AI to binge-watch YouTube piano tutorials and then 'distilling' that knowledge into a single brain. In the end, the robot could only hit the right notes about 57% of the time, which in the music world is less 'prodigy' and more 'toddler having a tantrum on the keys.'
why_bad: An F1 score of 57% on new songs is a polite way of saying the robot failed nearly every other note, making it essentially unlistenable.
Watching a video of a hand is not the same as understanding the physics of a piano; the model lacks the tactile feedback required for actual dexterity.
The 'generalist' agent is effectively a master of none, suffering from massive performance drops because it's trying to average out thousands of different playing styles into one blurry mess.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the kitchen! Our contestant today has never touched a stove, but they’ve watched three million hours of Gordon Ramsay TikToks. They aren't learning how to taste or season; they're just trying to mimic the exact blur of Gordon’s hands. When we ask them to whip up a simple omelet, they serve us a plate that is 57% eggshells and raw flour because they don't actually know what 'heat' is—they just know what a spatula looks like when it's moving. It’s a high-tech performance of a chef that results in a meal no one can actually eat.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 100% confidence and 57% accuracy.

title: What Makes Pre-Trained Visual Representations Successful for Robust Manipulation?
abstract: Inspired by the success of transfer learning in computer vision, roboticists have investigated visual pre-training as a means to improve the learning efficiency and generalization ability of policies learned from pixels. To that end, past work has favored large object interaction datasets, such as first-person videos of humans completing diverse tasks, in pursuit of manipulation-relevant features. Although this approach improves the efficiency of policy learning, it remains unclear how reliable these representations are in the presence of distribution shifts that arise commonly in robotic applications. Surprisingly, we find that visual representations designed for control tasks do not necessarily generalize under subtle changes in lighting and scene texture or the introduction of distractor objects. To understand what properties _do_ lead to robust representations, we compare the performance of 15 pre-trained vision models under different visual appearances. We find that emergent segmentation ability is a strong predictor of out-of-distribution generalization among ViT models. The rank order induced by this metric is more predictive than metrics that have previously guided generalization research within computer vision and machine learning, such as downstream ImageNet accuracy, in-domain accuracy, or shape-bias as evaluated by cue-conflict performance. We test this finding extensively on a suite of distribution shifts in ten tasks across two simulated manipulation environments. On the ALOHA setup, segmentation score predicts real-world performance after offline training with 50 demonstrations.
author: Kaylee Burns;Zach Witzel;Jubayer Ibn Hamid;Tianhe Yu;Chelsea Finn;Karol Hausman
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;;Google Brain;Google;

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers discovered that just because an AI is a genius at identifying cats on the internet doesn't mean it won't have a total meltdown if a robot tries to pick up a fork in slightly dim lighting. It turns out the 'secret sauce' for a robot not being useless isn't how many pictures it has seen, but whether it's good at mentally tracing the outlines of objects.
why_bad: The 'groundbreaking' conclusion that robots work better when they can actually see object boundaries is essentially the computer vision equivalent of discovering that water is wet.

While the paper claims an 'extensive' benchmark of 15 models, most are just minor variations of the same architecture, making the 'diversity' of the study feel like a buffet that only serves different shapes of potatoes.
The leap from simulated environments to the real-world ALOHA setup is backed by a sample size so small it barely qualifies as a case study, let alone a robust statistical proof.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you hire a world-class food photographer to actually work the line at a busy Sunday brunch. He’s got millions of followers because he knows exactly what a pancake should look like, but the moment the kitchen gets a little smoky or someone moves the spatula, he starts plating the dish soap. The researchers are shocked—shocked!—to find that 'looking' at pictures of food isn't the same as 'cooking' it. They finally realized that the only cooks who didn't burn the place down were the ones who actually knew where the pancake ended and the griddle began. It’s a tough day for the 'Visual Aesthetics' fans in the league; turns out, if you can't segment the eggs from the bacon, you're just a glorified tourist in the kitchen. Back to you in the booth, Jim!

title: Towards Open-World Grasping with Large Vision-Language Models
abstract:
The ability to grasp objects in-the-wild from open-ended language instructions constitutes a fundamental challenge in robotics.
An open-world grasping system should be able to combine high-level contextual with low-level physical-geometric reasoning in order to be applicable in arbitrary scenarios.
Recent works exploit the web-scale knowledge inherent in large language models (LLMs) to plan and reason in robotic context, but rely on external vision and action models to ground such knowledge into the environment and parameterize actuation.
This setup suffers from two major bottlenecks: a) the LLM's reasoning capacity is constrained by the quality of visual grounding, and b) LLMs do not contain low-level spatial understanding of the world, which is essential for grasping in contact-rich scenarios.
In this work we demonstrate that modern vision-language models (VLMs) are capable of tackling such limitations, as they are implicitly grounded and can jointly reason about semantics and geometry. 
We propose \texttt{OWG}, an open-world grasping pipeline that combines VLMs with segmentation and grasp synthesis models to unlock grounded world understanding in three stages: open-ended referring segmentation, grounded grasp planning and grasp ranking via contact reasoning, all of which can be applied zero-shot via suitable visual prompting mechanisms.
We conduct extensive evaluation in cluttered indoor scene datasets to showcase \texttt{OWG}'s robustness in grounding from open-ended language, as well as open-world robotic grasping experiments in both simulation and hardware that demonstrate superior performance compared to previous supervised and zero-shot LLM-based methods.
keywords: Foundation Models for Robotics;Open-World Grasping;Open-Ended23 Visual Grounding;Robot Planning
author: Georgios Tziafas;Hamidreza Kasaei
aff: University of Groningen;University of Groningen

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that can pick up any random object just by looking at it and 'thinking' about it using a massive AI brain. Unfortunately, knowing what a spatula looks like doesn't mean you have the hand-eye coordination to actually grab it without knocking over the entire kitchen.
why_bad: Architecture by Duct Tape: The system isn't a breakthrough; it's just three existing AI models stapled together, hoping the 'vibes' of a Vision-Language Model can magically solve physics.
The Simulation-to-Reality Gap: 'Superior performance' in a sterile digital sandbox means nothing when the real-world robot realizes it can't handle a shadow or a slightly slippery surface.
Computational Gluttony: Using a massive VLM to decide how to pinch a toy block is like using a supercomputer to calculate a 15% tip—it’s slow, expensive, and overkill for a simple mechanical task.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where intelligence is measured by tweets, one robot has read every playbook ever written. It can identify a blitz, critique the grass type, and explain the aerodynamics of a spiral in fourteen languages. But when the ball is hiked? The robot spends ten minutes 'reasoning' about the pigskin’s texture before accidentally punting its own teammate. It’s the ultimate Yelp critic: it has the most sophisticated opinions on dinner, but it still can't figure out how to use a fork without stabbing the table.

title: TOP-Nav: Legged Navigation Integrating Terrain, Obstacle and Proprioception Estimation
abstract: Legged navigation is typically examined within open-world, off-road, and challenging environments. In these scenarios, estimating external disturbances requires a complex synthesis of multi-modal information. This underlines a major limitation in existing works that primarily focus on avoiding obstacles. In this work, we propose TOP-Nav, a novel legged navigation framework that integrates a comprehensive path planner with Terrain awareness, Obstacle avoidance and close-loop Proprioception. TOP-Nav underscores the synergies between vision and proprioception in both path and motion planning. Within the path planner, we present a terrain estimator that enables the robot to select waypoints on terrains with higher traversability while effectively avoiding obstacles. In the motion planning level, we construct a proprioception advisor from the learning-based locomotion controller to provide motion evaluations for the path planner. Based on the close-loop motion feedback, we offer online corrections for the vision-based terrain and obstacle estimations. Consequently, TOP-Nav achieves open-world navigation that the robot can handle terrains or disturbances beyond the distribution of prior knowledge and overcomes constraints imposed by visual conditions. Building upon extensive experiments conducted in both simulation and real-world environments, TOP-Nav demonstrates superior performance in open-world navigation compared to existing methods.
author: Junli Ren;Yikai Liu;Yingru Dai;Junfeng Long;Guijin Wang
aff: University of Hong Kong;;;Shanghai AI Laboratory;Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University
aff_unique_norm: University of Hong Kong;Shanghai AI Laboratory;Tsinghua University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot dog that stops faceplanting by cross-referencing what its cameras see with how much its feet are actually slipping. It is essentially teaching a machine that when its eyes see 'cool grass' but its legs feel 'black ice,' it should probably stop trusting its eyes.
why_bad: The 'Proprioception Advisor' is essentially a rebranded feedback loop that adds significant computational bloat without proving it’s better than standard end-to-end reinforcement learning.
The 'open-world' claims are a stretch, as the experiments lack the chaotic edge cases—like tall grass or soft sand—that actually break current navigation stacks.
The paper fails to provide a rigorous ablation study, making it unclear if the 'superior performance' comes from the fancy new framework or just a very well-tuned PID controller.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where depth cameras are liars... and every pebble is a potential lawsuit... one robot must learn to trust its gut. Or at least its ankles. Witness the cinematic journey of a machine with the eyes of a hawk but the coordination of a Victorian orphan. He saw the marble floor. He felt the slip. And for the first time in robotic history, he decided to do something about it. Coming this summer: 'The Feedback Loop That Thought It Was An Architecture.'

title: Handling Long-Term Safety and Uncertainty in Safe Reinforcement Learning
abstract:
Safety is one of the key issues preventing the deployment of reinforcement learning techniques in real-world robots. While most approaches in the Safe Reinforcement Learning area do not require prior knowledge of constraints and robot kinematics and rely solely on data, it is often difficult to deploy them in complex real-world settings. Instead, model-based approaches that incorporate prior knowledge of the constraints and dynamics into the learning framework have proven capable of deploying the learning algorithm directly on the real robot.
Unfortunately, while an approximated model of the robot dynamics is often available, the safety constraints are task-specific and hard to obtain: they may be too complicated to encode analytically, too expensive to compute, or it may be difficult to envision a priori the long-term safety requirements. In this paper, we bridge this gap by extending the safe exploration method, ATACOM, with learnable constraints, with a particular focus on ensuring long-term safety and handling of uncertainty. Our approach is competitive or superior to state-of-the-art methods in final performance while maintaining safer behavior during training.
author: Jonas Günster;Puze Liu;Jan Peters;Davide Tateo
aff: Technische Universität Darmstadt;TU Darmstadt;TU Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that learns its own safety rules on the fly because hard-coding 'don't crash' is apparently too much work. They essentially slapped a 'best guess' statistical layer onto an existing algorithm and hoped it would stop the robot from self-destructing in the real world.
why_bad: The experimental environments are so laughably simple (1D and 2D) that they fail to prove the method can handle the 'complex real-world settings' mentioned in the abstract.
The technical novelty is paper-thin, mostly just bolting a standard Gaussian Process onto an existing framework called ATACOM without significant architectural innovation.
The 'safety' is an illusion; the math assumes the robot already has a rough idea of where danger is, which completely contradicts the paper's premise that constraints are 'hard to obtain.'
roast.analogy_name: The 'Trust Me, I’m a Chef' Flambé
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: AI Edition.' Our contestant claims they’ve invented a way to cook a five-course meal without ever reading a safety manual or using a meat thermometer. Instead, they took a 20-year-old microwave (ATACOM), threw in some 'statistical vibes' (Gaussian Processes) to guess when the oil might catch fire, and then 'proved' it worked by successfully boiling a single cup of lukewarm water. When the judges asked if it could handle a real stove, the contestant argued that water is basically a simplified version of a lobster thermidor. It’s not a revolution in kitchen safety; it’s just a guy hoping the smoke alarm has dead batteries.

title: Gaussian Splatting to Real World Flight Navigation Transfer with Liquid Networks
abstract:
Simulators are powerful tools for autonomous robot learning as they offer scalable data generation, flexible design, and optimization of trajectories. 
However, transferring behavior learned from simulation data into the real world proves to be difficult, usually mitigated with compute-heavy domain randomization methods or further model fine-tuning. We present a method to improve generalization and robustness to distribution shifts in sim-to-real visual quadrotor navigation tasks. To this end, we first build a simulator by integrating Gaussian Splatting with quadrotor flight dynamics, and then, train robust navigation policies using Liquid neural networks. In this way, we obtain a full-stack imitation learning protocol that combines advances in 3D Gaussian splatting radiance field rendering, crafty programming of expert demonstration training data, and the task understanding capabilities of Liquid networks. Through a series of quantitative flight tests, we demonstrate the robust transfer of navigation skills learned in a single simulation scene directly to the real world. We further show the ability to maintain performance beyond the training environment under drastic distribution and physical environment changes. Our learned Liquid policies, trained on single target maneuvers curated from a photorealistic simulated indoor flight only, generalize to multi-step hikes onboard a real hardware platform outdoors.
author: Alex Quach;Makram Chahine;Alexander Amini;Ramin Hasani;Daniela Rus
aff: Liquid AI;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a drone to fly by creating a hyper-realistic video game version of a room and using a 'flexible' AI brain called a Liquid Network. They claim that because the simulation looked pretty, the drone should magically know how to hike through the woods in real life without crashing into a tree.
why_bad: Architectural Buzzword Cohesion: The paper fails to explain why 'Liquid Networks' are specifically better for 'Gaussian Splatting' data than standard models, making it feel like a trendy tech mashup rather than a scientific breakthrough.

The 'One-Room' Generalization Fallacy: Training a robot in exactly one simulated indoor environment and expecting it to navigate the unpredictable outdoors is statistically over-optimistic, often masking massive overfitting.

Insufficient Comparative Baselines: The authors didn't rigorously prove that their fancy 'photorealistic' simulator actually performs better than much simpler, uglier simulations that have been used for years.
roast.analogy_name: The VR Basement Dweller vs. The Actual Sun
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where high-resolution textures are mistaken for physical laws. One drone spent its entire life inside a flawless, 4K digital rendering of a developer's living room, mastering the art of hovering over a virtual rug. It thought it was a god. It thought it was ready. But now, it must face the ultimate adversary: REALITY. Watch as 'Liquid AI' meets 'Solid Concrete.' Witness the terrifying moment a gust of wind—a concept never once mentioned in its training data—sends our hero into a recursive mid-life crisis. This summer, experience the sim-to-real gap like never before in: OVERFIT AND OUT OF CONTROL.

title: TLDR: Unsupervised Goal-Conditioned RL via Temporal Distance-Aware Representations
abstract: Unsupervised goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is a promising paradigm for developing diverse robotic skills without external supervision. However, existing unsupervised GCRL methods often struggle to cover a wide range of states in complex environments due to their limited exploration and sparse or noisy rewards for GCRL. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel unsupervised GCRL method that leverages TemporaL Distance-aware Representations (TLDR). Based on temporal distance, TLDR selects faraway goals to initiate exploration and computes intrinsic exploration rewards and goal-reaching rewards. Specifically, our exploration policy seeks states with large temporal distances (i.e. covering a large state space), while the goal-conditioned policy learns to minimize the temporal distance to the goal (i.e. reaching the goal). Our results in six simulated locomotion environments demonstrate that TLDR significantly outperforms prior unsupervised GCRL methods in achieving a wide range of states.
keywords: Unsupervised Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning;Temporal Distance-Aware Representations
author: Junik Bae;Kwanyoung Park;Youngwoon Lee
aff: Seoul National University;Seoul National University;University of California, Berkeley
aff_unique_norm: Seoul National University;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach robots how to explore by telling them to pick the most time-consuming goals possible and then sprint toward them. It's essentially 'The Floor is Lava' for algorithms, assuming that if a place takes a long time to reach, it must be worth visiting.
why_bad: The core idea of using 'temporal distance' is about as fresh as a three-week-old bagel, having been a staple of RL literature for years.
The method collapses the moment the environment gets noisy, as the robot mistakes random jitter for 'meaningful exploration distance.'
Testing exclusively in 'simulated locomotion' is the academic equivalent of bragging about being a pro athlete because you’re good at Wii Sports.
roast.analogy_name: The GPS with a 'Longest Route' Fetish
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are witnessing a spectacular display of logic where the driver decides that the best way to learn a city is to exclusively navigate to the furthest possible Starbucks. He’s not checking the map; he’s just checking his watch and assuming that if he's been driving for six hours, he must be doing something 'diverse.' The crowd is hushed as he hits a cul-de-sac for the tenth time, claiming it’s a 'novel state space.' The referees—the reviewers—are reaching for the red flag because, while he’s certainly burning gas, he hasn't actually learned how to park the car.

title: Genetic Algorithm for Curriculum Design in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: As the deployment of autonomous agents in real-world scenarios grows, so does the interest in their application to competitive environments with other robots. Self-play in Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables agents to develop competitive strategies. However, the complexity arising from multi-agent interactions and the tendency for RL agents to disrupt competitors' training introduce instability and a risk of overfitting. While traditional methods depend on costly Nash equilibrium approximations or random exploration for training scenario optimization, this can be inefficient in large search spaces often prevalent in multi-agent problems. However, related works in single-agent setups show that genetic algorithms perform better in large scenario spaces. Therefore, we propose using genetic algorithms to adaptively adjust environment parameters and opponent policies in a multi-agent context to find and synthesize coherent scenarios efficiently. We also introduce GenOpt Agent—a genetically optimized, open-loop agent executing scheduled actions. The open-loop aspect of GenOpt prevents RL agents from winning through adversarial perturbations, thereby fostering generalizable strategies. Also, GenOpt is genetically optimized without expert supervision, negating the need for expensive expert supervision to have meaningful opponents at the start of training. Our empirical studies indicate that this method surpasses several established baselines in two-player competitive settings with continuous action spaces, validating its effectiveness and stability in training.
author: Yeeho Song;Jeff Schneider
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: To stop robots from cheating while they learn to fight each other, the authors replaced the opponent with a predictable bot that follows a pre-set script. They used an evolution-style algorithm to tweak the rules of the game, hoping the AI would learn to be better without being bullied by a smarter rival.
why_bad: The 'GenOpt' agent is 'open-loop,' meaning it doesn't react to what the learner does; training against a predictable script isn't 'competitive AI,' it's just high-stakes pattern memorization.
Genetic Algorithms are notoriously sample-inefficient and computationally expensive, making the 'efficiency' claims feel like a polite fiction in any high-dimensional reality.
Beating 'established baselines' in simple two-player games is the academic equivalent of winning a local talent show—it doesn't mean your method scales to the complexity of the real world.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You want to train the next heavyweight boxing champion, but every time they spar, they just end up kicking each other in the shins until someone cries. Disruptive, right? Our solution: We replace the opponent with a Roomba that has a boxing glove taped to it. It moves in a pre-programmed, 'genetically optimized' zig-zag. Because the Roomba is literally incapable of reacting to your punches, your boxer can't 'cheat' by being mean—they just have to learn the Roomba's dance. It’s not 'fighting,' it’s a choreographed HIIT workout that we’re rebranding as an Elite Combat Curriculum. We’re currently seeking $5M in seed funding to disrupt the 'actually being good at things' industry.

title: Manipulate-Anything: Automating Real-World Robots using Vision-Language Models
abstract:
Large-scale endeavors like RT-1 and widespread community efforts such as Open-X-Embodiment have contributed to growing the scale of robot demonstration data. However, there is still an opportunity to improve the quality, quantity, and diversity of robot demonstration data. Although vision-language models have been shown to automatically generate demonstration data, their utility has been limited to environments with privileged state information, they require hand-designed skills, and are limited to interactions with few object instances. We propose Manipulate-Anything, a scalable automated generation method for real-world robotic manipulation.
Unlike prior work, our method can operate in real-world environments without any privileged state information, hand-designed skills, and can manipulate any static object. We evaluate our method using two setups. First, Manipulate-Anything successfully generates trajectories for all 5 real-world and 12 simulation tasks, significantly outperforming existing methods like VoxPoser. 
Second, Manipulate-Anything's demonstrations can train more robust behavior cloning policies than training with human demonstrations, or from data generated by VoxPoser and Code-As-Policies.
We believe Manipulate-Anything can be the scalable method for both generating data for robotics and solving novel tasks in a zero-shot setting.  Anonymous project page: manipulate-anything.github.io.
keywords: Robot Learning; Multimodal Large Language Model; Data Generation; Imitation Learning; Behavior Cloning
author: Jiafei Duan;Wentao Yuan;Wilbert Pumacay;Yi Ru Wang;Kiana Ehsani;Dieter Fox;Ranjay Krishna
aff: NVIDIA;University of Washington, Seattle;Universidad Nacional de Ingeniería;University of Washington;Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence;Department of Computer Science;University of Washington
aff_unique_norm: NVIDIA;University of Washington;Universidad Nacional de Ingeniería;Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence;Unknown Institution

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim they've built a robot that can figure out how to move any object just by looking at it, effectively firing human teachers. In reality, it's an AI-driven guess-and-check system that tries to replace actual physical understanding with high-level descriptions from a Vision-Language Model.
why_bad: The 'Anything' in the title is doing some heavy lifting, given the evaluation only covers five real-world tasks in a highly controlled environment.
The system relies on 'sampling-based planning' with a Vision-Language Model, which is a fancy way of saying it’s excruciatingly slow and computationally expensive for simple movements.
The benchmarks against human demonstrations are misleading because the 'human' data used for comparison was likely low-quality or intentionally hamstrung to make the AI look like a prodigy.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Hallucinating Handyman
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims their client is a master carpenter who can build 'anything' without a manual. But when we look at the evidence, we see a man who stares at a picture of a chair for ten minutes, pokes a piece of wood with a hammer, and calls it 'zero-shot furniture assembly.' The prosecution moves to strike the 'Manipulate-Anything' claim from the record; you cannot claim to have mastered the laws of physics when your primary strategy is asking a chatbot where the screwdriver goes and praying the table doesn't collapse before the camera stops recording.

title: Provably Safe Online Multi-Agent Navigation in Unknown Environments
abstract: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) provide safety guarantees for multi-agent navigation. However, traditional approaches require full knowledge of the environment (e.g., obstacle positions and shapes) to formulate CBFs and hence, are not applicable in unknown environments. This paper overcomes this issue by proposing an Online Exploration-based Control Lyapunov Barrier Function (OE-CLBF) controller. It estimates the unknown environment by learning its corresponding CBF with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) in an online manner, using local neighborhood information, and leverages the latter to generate actions for safe navigation. To reduce the computation incurred by the online SVM training, we use an Imitation Learning (IL) framework to predict the importance of neighboring agents with Graph Attention Networks (GATs), and train the SVM only with information received from neighbors of high `value'. The OE-CLBF allows for decentralized deployment, and importantly, provides provable safety guarantees that we derive in this paper. Experiments corroborate theoretical findings and demonstrate superior performance w.r.t. state-of-the-art baselines in a variety of unknown environments.
keywords: Decentralized Multi-Agent Navigation;Unknown Environment;Support Vector Machine;Graph Attention Learning;Control Barrier Function
author: Zhan Gao;Guang Yang;Jasmine Bayrooti;Amanda Prorok
aff: ;University of Cambridge;University of Cambridge;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop a swarm of robots from crashing into things in new places by making them learn the 'rules of the room' on the fly. To keep the robots' brains from melting under the pressure, they added a filter that lets them ignore 'unimportant' neighbors while they navigate.
why_bad: The 'provable safety' claim is total fantasy because it assumes the sensors are perfect and the math updates instantly, which never happens in the messy real world.
Stacking a Graph Attention Network on top of an online Support Vector Machine creates a 'computational sandwich' that is far too heavy to run in real-time on actual robot hardware.
The Imitation Learning pruning is a safety paradox: you're training the robot to ignore data to save time, which is exactly how you 'accidentally' ignore the one obstacle that ends your mission.
roast.analogy_name: The High-Stakes 'Trust Me, I’m a Genius' Blindfold Dash
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the Arena of Chaos! In the red corner, we have a robot trying to sprint through a dark, moving obstacle course. Instead of a map, it’s carrying a whiteboard and trying to solve multivariable calculus equations to 'prove' it won't hit anything. But wait—it’s getting overwhelmed! So, it decides to start a secondary internal monologue to rank which of its teammates are 'cool enough' to listen to before it finishes the math. It's a bold strategy, Cotton! It’s basically trying to win a game of dodgeball by first peer-reviewing the trajectory of the ball before deciding to move. Ouch! It just took a 10mph hit to the chassis because it was too busy 'calculating its importance' to notice the wall. The refs are shaking their heads—you can't call it 'provably safe' when your brain is buffering during a collision!

title: Learning Visual Parkour from Generated Images
abstract: Fast and accurate physics simulation is an essential component of robot learning, where robots can explore failure scenarios that are difficult to produce in the real world and learn from unlimited on-policy data. Yet, it remains challenging to incorporate RGB-color perception into the sim-to-real pipeline that matches the real world in its richness and realism. In this work, we train a robot dog in simulation for visual parkour. We propose a way to use generative models to synthesize diverse and physically accurate image sequences of the scene from the robot's ego-centric perspective. We present demonstrations of zero-shot transfer to the RGB-only observations of the real world on a robot equipped with a low-cost, off-the-shelf color camera.
author: Alan Yu;Ge Yang;Ran Choi;Yajvan Ravan;John Leonard;Phillip Isola
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot dog parkour by showing it a bunch of AI-generated hallucinations instead of training it in the actual world. It turns out that a robot trained on 'dream logic' struggles when reality doesn't come with a prompt engineer.
why_bad: The generative model produces 'physically accurate' images that are actually just fancy textures masking a lack of underlying spatial understanding.
Temporal flickering in the AI-generated video sequences gives the robot a literal headache, causing it to lose track of obstacles between frames.
The 'zero-shot' transfer is more of a 'zero-chance' transfer because the model hasn't accounted for real-world lighting variables like 'the sun moves'.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where gravity is a suggestion and reality is rendered by a GPU on its fourth espresso... one robot dog must learn to jump. He didn't train in the gym. He didn't train on the streets. He watched ten thousand hours of AI-generated fever dreams and now he’s ready to face his greatest nemesis: a slightly uneven sidewalk. Coming this summer: A machine that can calculate a backflip in a void but is emotionally destroyed by a shadow. Witness the spectacular failure of a dog that knows exactly what a parkour course looks like, but has no idea where his own feet are.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels nominal.

title: ClutterGen: A Cluttered Scene Generator for Robot Learning
abstract: We introduce ClutterGen, a physically compliant simulation scene generator capable of producing highly diverse, cluttered, and stable scenes for robot learning. Generating such scenes is challenging as each object must adhere to physical laws like gravity and collision. As the number of objects increases, finding valid poses becomes more difficult, necessitating significant human engineering effort, which limits the diversity of the scenes. To overcome these challenges, we propose a reinforcement learning method that can be trained with physics-based reward signals provided by the simulator. Our experiments demonstrate that ClutterGen can generate cluttered object layouts with up to ten objects on confined table surfaces. Additionally, our policy design explicitly encourages the diversity of the generated scenes for open-ended generation. Our real-world robot results show that ClutterGen can be directly used for clutter rearrangement and stable placement policy training.
author: Yinsen Jia;Boyuan Chen
aff: Duke University;Duke University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built an AI that learns how to create messy piles of stuff on a table so that robots can practice cleaning them up. It uses a complex learning system to make sure the objects don't accidentally phase through each other like ghosts or defy gravity.
why_bad: The 'clutter' is limited to a whopping ten objects, which is less of a 'complex scene' and more of a 'slightly disorganized desk of a minimalist.'
Using a high-powered Reinforcement Learning policy to place objects on a table is a massive over-calculation; most researchers just let physics run for three seconds and call it a day.
The claim of 'open-ended generation' is a stretch when the system struggles to scale beyond the complexity of a Happy Meal toy set.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where robots are baffled by a stack of books... ONE TEAM spends thousands of dollars in electricity to solve a problem a toddler solves every morning. WITNESS the heart-pounding action of an AI meticulously placing A COFFEE MUG next to A STAPLER. Can the robot handle TEN WHOLE ITEMS? Or will the simulation collapse under the weight of its own over-engineering? Coming this summer: THE CLUTTER-INATOR. It’s expensive, it’s unnecessary, and it’s coming to a table near you... as long as that table is very small and doesn't have more than ten things on it.
roast.tone_check: Scientific snark level: Optimal.

title: I Can Tell What I am Doing: Toward Real-World Natural Language Grounding of Robot Experiences
abstract: Understanding robot behaviors and experiences through natural language is crucial for developing intelligent and transparent robotic systems. Recent advancement in large language models (LLMs) makes it possible to translate complex, multi-modal robotic experiences into coherent, human-readable narratives. However, grounding real-world robot experiences into natural language is challenging due to many reasons, such as multi-modal nature of data, differing sample rates, and data volume. We introduce RONAR, an LLM-based system that generates natural language narrations from robot experiences, aiding in behavior announcement, failure analysis, and human interaction to recover failure. Evaluated across various scenarios, RONAR outperforms state-of-the-art methods and improves failure recovery efficiency. Our contributions include a multi-modal framework for robot experience narration, a comprehensive real-robot dataset, and empirical evidence of RONAR's effectiveness in enhancing user experience in system transparency and failure analysis.
author: Zihan Wang;Brian Liang;Varad Dhat;Zander Brumbaugh;Nick Walker;Ranjay Krishna;Maya Cakmak
aff: University of Washington;University of Washington;University of Washington;Department of Computer Science;University of Washington;University of Washington;University of Washington, Seattle
position: PhD student;Undergrad student;MS student;MS student;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to give robots a 'voice' by using AI to translate their sensor data into a running commentary of what they're doing. The goal was to make it easier for humans to understand why a robot is failing, essentially turning a confusing machine error into a polite 'Oops, I tripped over the rug' text message.
why_bad: The 'Prompt-Engineering' Trap: The paper is largely a wrapper for existing Large Language Models (LLMs) rather than a fundamental breakthrough in robotic control or perception.
Superficial Grounding: Reviewers likely found that the robot isn't actually 'understanding' its environment; it's just slapping descriptive labels on data patterns it doesn't truly comprehend.
The Utility Gap: While the robot can now eloquently describe its own failures, the system doesn't actually provide a mechanism to prevent those failures from happening in the first place.
roast.analogy_name: The Sportscaster for a Burning Kitchen
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Iron Chef: LLM Edition.' Imagine a contestant who is absolutely terrible at cooking—we're talking salt in the coffee and setting the salad on fire. Instead of learning how to use a knife, this chef hires a world-class sports commentator to stand next to them. As the chef accidentally tosses a lobster into a blender, the commentator says in a silky-smooth voice, 'Notice the bold, avant-garde approach to crustacean processing.' The judges (the reviewers) aren't impressed that the disaster is being narrated beautifully; they’re just wondering why the kitchen is still on fire and why the chef thinks a play-by-play makes the food taste better. You haven't fixed the cooking; you've just made the failure more poetic.
roast.tone_check: Scientific snark level: Optimal.

title: RP1M: A Large-Scale Motion Dataset for Piano Playing with Bi-Manual Dexterous Robot Hands
abstract: Endowing robot hands with human-level dexterity is a long-lasting research objective. Bi-manual robot piano playing constitutes a task that combines challenges from dynamic tasks, such as generating fast while precise motions, with slower but contact-rich manipulation problems. Although reinforcement learning based approaches have shown promising results in single-task performance, these methods struggle in a multi-song setting. Our work aims to close this gap and, thereby, enable imitation learning approaches for robot piano playing at scale. To this end, we introduce the Robot Piano 1 Million (RP1M) dataset, containing bi-manual robot piano playing motion data of more than one million trajectories. We formulate finger placements as an optimal transport problem, thus, enabling automatic annotation of vast amounts of unlabeled songs. Benchmarking existing imitation learning approaches shows that such approaches reach state-of-the-art robot piano playing performance by leveraging RP1M.
keywords: Bi-manual dexterous robot hands;dataset for robot piano playing;imitation learning;robot learning at scale
author: Yi Zhao;Le Chen;Jan Schneider;Quankai Gao;Juho Kannala;Bernhard Schölkopf;Joni Pajarinen;Dieter Büchler
aff: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;University of Southern California;Aalto University;;Aalto University;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, Max-Planck Institute
position: Intern;;PhD student;PhD student;Associate Professor;;Assistant Professor;Group Leader
aff_unique_norm: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;University of Southern California;Aalto University

roast.laymen_summary: We built a massive library of 1 million robot finger-wiggling videos to teach machines how to play the piano without losing their minds. By using a fancy math shortcut to guess which finger goes on which key, we're hoping robots can finally stop being one-hit wonders and learn a full setlist.
why_bad: The '1 million trajectories' are likely synthetic junk generated by an algorithm, meaning the robot is learning from a computer hallucination rather than real-world physics.
Using 'Optimal Transport' for finger placement is massive mathematical overkill—it's like using a supercomputer to calculate the trajectory of a spoon to your mouth.
The 'state-of-the-art' performance is a bit of a circular brag; they’re basically saying their AI is the best at playing with the specific toys they just built.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Virtual Olympics, folks! Today we're watching a rookie who has spent ten thousand hours practicing his 'optimal finger placement' on a plastic Guitar Hero controller. He’s got the data! He’s got the high score! He’s even used a specialized algorithm to map his wrist movements to the beat! But there's a problem, Jim—the moment you put a real, heavy, wooden guitar in his hands, he's going to realize that 'optimal transport' doesn't mean squat when you have to actually fight gravity and friction. It's a gold medal performance in a stadium that doesn't exist!

title: APRICOT: Active Preference Learning and Constraint-Aware Task Planning with LLMs
abstract:
Home robots performing personalized tasks must adeptly balance user preferences with environmental affordances.
We focus on organization tasks within constrained spaces, such as arranging items into a refrigerator, where preferences for placement collide with physical limitations.
The robot must infer user preferences based on a small set of demonstrations, which is easier for users to provide than extensively defining all their requirements.
While recent works use Large Language Models (LLMs) to learn preferences from user demonstrations, they encounter two fundamental challenges.
First, there is inherent ambiguity in interpreting user actions, as multiple preferences can often explain a single observed behavior.
Second, not all user preferences are practically feasible due to geometric constraints in the environment.
To address these challenges, we introduce APRICOT, a novel approach that merges LLM-based Bayesian active preference learning with constraint-aware task planning. 
APRICOT refines its generated preferences by actively querying the user and dynamically adapts its plan to respect environmental constraints.
We evaluate APRICOT on a dataset of diverse organization tasks and demonstrate its effectiveness in real-world scenarios, showing significant improvements in both preference satisfaction and plan feasibility.
keywords: Active Preference Learning;Task Planning;Large Language Models
author: Huaxiaoyue Wang;Nathaniel Chin;Gonzalo Gonzalez-Pumariega;Xiangwan Sun;Neha Sunkara;Maximus Adrian Pace;Jeannette Bohg;Sanjiban Choudhury
aff: Cornell University;Cornell University;Cornell University;Cornell University;Cornell University;Cornell University;Stanford University;Cornell University
position: PhD student;MS student;MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that organizes your fridge by guessing your 'vibe,' but it gets confused because it can't tell if you put the milk on the top shelf because you're tall or because you're just messy. To fix this, the robot basically pestered people with follow-up questions to make sure their aesthetic preferences didn't violate the literal laws of physics.
why_bad: The 'Active Learning' component is essentially a glorified, high-latency game of 20 Questions that would likely result in the user throwing the robot out of a window before the fridge is actually organized.

The evaluation relies on 'diverse organization tasks' that are likely simulated toy environments, ignoring the chaotic, sticky reality of a fridge that contains more than three perfectly clean Tupperware containers.

The integration of LLMs and task planners is less of a 'novel framework' and more of a fragile software bridge where the LLM hallucinates a preference and the planner spends all its time explaining why a 2-liter bottle won't fit in a 2-inch gap.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Neurotic Interior Decorator
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, I present to you the defendant: a robot that claims to be a 'constrained task planner' but acts like a high-maintenance intern. The prosecution alleges that when asked to put away the groceries, the defendant spent forty-five minutes asking the homeowner if the placement of the Grey Poupon was a 'deeply held personal value' or a 'statistical anomaly.' When finally told to just put the milk away, the defendant entered a 'constraint-aware' fugue state, arguing that the shelf height was an 'environmental affordance violation' instead of simply moving the shelf. It’s a classic case of over-analysis paralysis—the defense calls it 'Bayesian Active Learning,' but the court recognizes it as an expensive way to keep the milk sitting on the counter until it spoils.

title: Trajectory Improvement and Reward Learning from Comparative Language Feedback
abstract: Learning from human feedback has gained traction in fields like robotics and natural language processing in recent years. While prior works mostly rely on human feedback in the form of comparisons, language is a preferable modality that provides more informative insights into user preferences. In this work, we aim to incorporate comparative language feedback to iteratively improve robot trajectories and to learn reward functions that encode human preferences. To achieve this goal, we learn a shared latent space that integrates trajectory data and language feedback, and subsequently leverage the learned latent space to improve trajectories and learn human preferences. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to incorporate comparative language feedback into reward learning. Our simulation experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the learned latent space and the success of our learning algorithms. We also conduct human subject studies that show our reward learning algorithm achieves a 23.9% higher subjective score on average and is 11.3% more time-efficient compared to preference-based reward learning, underscoring the superior performance of our method. Our website is at https://liralab.usc.edu/comparative-language-feedback/.
keywords: Learning from Human Language Feedback;Reward Learning;Human-Robot Interaction
author: Zhaojing Yang;Miru Jun;Jeremy Tien;Stuart Russell;Anca Dragan;Erdem Biyik
aff: University of Southern California;University of Southern California;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of Southern California
position: MS student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Full Professor;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Southern California;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots to understand nuanced verbal feedback, like 'swing smoother,' instead of just relying on simple 'better or worse' clicks. They created a digital translator meant to turn human sass into mathematical instructions for better robot movements.
why_bad: The 'shared latent space' is effectively a black-box blender that attempts to map vague words to precise physics, often resulting in a 'hallucinated' middle ground that works in simulation but would likely fail in a messy kitchen.

A 11.3% improvement in time efficiency is statistically underwhelming; in the world of human-subject studies, that's often the difference between a participant who had their morning coffee and one who didn't.
The paper claims to be 'the first' to do this, conveniently sidestepping a mountain of existing research on language-conditioned reinforcement learning that does nearly the exact same thing.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the AI Invitational! Our driver, 'The Bot,' is trying to parallel park, but he's blindfolded. In the passenger seat, we have a 'Human Coach' who isn't allowed to touch the wheel—they can only say things like, 'Make it feel more like a Sunday morning.' The USC pit crew claims they've built a 'Latent Space Translator' to turn that poetry into a perfect reverse-park. But wait—the Bot is just drifting aimlessly while the Coach gets frustrated! The judges are looking at the 23% 'Subjective Score' boost and calling foul, because 'feeling better' about a robot doesn't mean it’s not about to hit a curb. It’s a lot of fancy footwork for a car that’s still idling in the driveway!

title: ACE: A Cross-platform and visual-Exoskeletons System for Low-Cost Dexterous Teleoperation
abstract:
Bimanual robotic manipulation with dexterous hands has a large potential workability and a wide workspace as it follows the most natural human workflow.
Learning from human demonstrations has proven highly effective for learning a dexterous manipulation policy. To collect such data, teleoperation serves as a straightforward and efficient way to do so.
However, a cost-effective and easy-to-use teleoperation system is lacking for anthropomorphic robot hands.
To fill the deficiency, we developed \our, a cross-platform visual-exoskeleton system for low-cost dexterous teleoperation. 
Our system employs a hand-facing camera to capture 3D hand poses and an exoskeleton mounted on a base that can be easily carried on users' backs. ACE captures both the hand root end-effector and hand pose in real-time and enables cross-platform operations. 
We evaluate the key system parameters compared with previous teleoperation systems and show clear advantages of \our.
We then showcase the desktop and mobile versions of our system on six different robot platforms (including humanoid-hands, arm-hands, arm-gripper, and quadruped-gripper systems), and demonstrate the effectiveness of learning three difficult real-world tasks through the collected demonstration on two of them.
keywords: Teleopration System; Hardware; Imitation Learning; Robot Learning; Exoskeletons
author: Shiqi Yang;Minghuan Liu;Yuzhe Qin;Runyu Ding;Jialong Li;Xuxin Cheng;Ruihan Yang;Sha Yi;Xiaolong Wang
aff: University of California, San Diego;Shanghai Jiaotong University;University of California, San Diego;Electrical and Electronic Engineering, University of Hong Kong;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, San Diego
position: MS student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;MS student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of California, San Diego;Shanghai Jiao Tong University;University of Hong Kong;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: We built a budget-friendly robot puppet rig that lets you control mechanical hands by wearing a backpack and waving your arms at a camera. It’s supposed to be a 'one-size-fits-all' solution for teaching robots how to move, but it mostly proves that 'cheap' and 'precise' are still not on speaking terms.
why_bad: Methodological Thinness: The authors claimed 'cross-platform' mastery across six robots but only actually showed real results for two, which is the academic equivalent of saying you're fluent in six languages because you can order a beer in all of them.

Hardware Jank: Using a 'low-cost' camera-exoskeleton hybrid usually means the data is noisier than a heavy metal concert, making high-precision dexterity tasks nearly impossible to reproduce reliably.

The 'Ease-of-Use' Lie: Describing a system that requires a custom-built backpack and a specific camera-to-hand mounting rig as 'easy-to-use' ignores the thirty minutes of calibration and sweat involved in just getting the thing to turn on.
roast.analogy_name: The $20 Universal Remote for a Spaceship
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup pitching a universal remote that can control a TV, a blender, and a Boeing 747. The catch? You have to wear a 15-pound harness, and half the time the 'Volume Up' button accidentally deploys the landing gear. Sure, it's 'low-cost' and 'cross-platform,' but by the time you've finished calibrating the vest, the pilot has already landed the plane manually. It’s a solution in search of a problem that doesn't mind a little bit of lag-induced catastrophe.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 99% confidence intervals.

title: Tokenize the World into Object-level Knowledge to Address Long-tail Events in Autonomous Driving
abstract: The autonomous driving industry is increasingly adopting end-to-end learning from sensory inputs to minimize human biases in system design. Traditional end-to-end driving models, however, suffer from long-tail events due to rare or unseen inputs within their training distributions. To address this, we propose TOKEN, a novel Multi-Modal Large Language Model (MM-LLM) that tokenizes the world into object-level knowledge, enabling better utilization of LLM’s reasoning capabilities to enhance autonomous vehicle planning in long-tail scenarios. TOKEN effectively alleviates data scarcity and inefficient tokenization by producing condensed and semantically enriched representations of the scene. Our results demonstrate that TOKEN excels in grounding, reasoning, and planning capabilities, outperforming existing frameworks with a 27% reduction in trajectory L2 error and a 39% decrease in collision rates in long-tail scenarios. Additionally, our work highlights the importance of representation alignment and structured reasoning in sparking the common-sense reasoning capabilities of MM-LLMs for effective planning.
author: Thomas Tian;Boyi Li;Xinshuo Weng;Yuxiao Chen;Edward Schmerling;Yue Wang;Boris Ivanovic;Marco Pavone
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;Stanford University;California Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: University of California, Berkeley;NVIDIA;Stanford University;California Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to give self-driving cars 'common sense' by making them translate the road into a list of words so a giant AI brain (like ChatGPT) can think about them. They hope that by 'reasoning' through weird situations—like a sofa falling off a truck—the car won't panic, but instead chat its way to a safer driving path.
why_bad: The 'Reasoning' Latency: Large Language Models are notoriously slow, meaning the car might finish 'reasoning' about the obstacle approximately three seconds after it has already crashed into it.

Over-engineered Object Detection: Critics likely noted that 'tokenizing the world into object-level knowledge' is just a fancy way of saying 'labeling boxes,' a task we've been doing for a decade without needing a massive, power-hungry LLM to overcomplicate the math.

Simulation vs. Reality Gap: A 39% reduction in crashes in a virtual simulator is great, but LLMs are famous for 'hallucinating' facts; if the car hallucinates that a stop sign is actually a decorative red octagon, the 'common sense' reasoning becomes a liability.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re hurtling down the highway at 70 mph when a mattress flies off the car in front of you. Instead of a quick reflex, our system pauses to ask, 'What is the essence of a mattress in a socio-dynamic traffic context?' We’ve replaced split-second physics with a five-paragraph essay. It’s a revolutionary pivot from 'not dying' to 'contemplating the road.' Sure, you might hit the mattress, but your car will be able to explain the ethical implications of the collision in perfect, grammatically correct prose. It’s not a safety feature; it’s a vibe.

title: Autonomous Interactive Correction MLLM for Robust Robotic Manipulation
abstract: The ability to reflect on and correct failures is crucial for robotic systems to interact stably with real-life objects. Observing the generalization and reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), previous approaches have aimed to utilize these models to enhance robotic systems accordingly. However, these methods typically focus on high-level planning corrections using an additional MLLM, with limited utilization of failed samples to correct low-level contact poses which is particularly prone to occur during articulated object manipulation. To address this gap, we propose an Autonomous Interactive Correction (AIC) MLLM, which makes use of previous low-level interaction experiences to correct SE(3) pose predictions for articulated object. Specifically, AIC MLLM is initially fine-tuned to acquire both pose prediction and feedback prompt comprehension abilities. We design two types of prompt instructions for interactions with objects: 1) visual masks to highlight unmovable parts for position correction, and 2) textual descriptions to indicate potential directions for rotation correction. During inference, a Feedback Information Extraction module is introduced to recognize the failure cause, allowing AIC MLLM to adaptively correct the pose prediction using the corresponding prompts. To further enhance manipulation stability, we devise a Test Time Adaptation strategy that enables AIC MLLM to better adapt to the current scene configuration. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted in both simulated and real-world environments to evaluate the proposed method. The results demonstrate that our AIC MLLM can efficiently correct failure samples by leveraging interaction experience prompts.
author: Chuyan Xiong;Chengyu Shen;Xiaoqi Li;Kaichen Zhou;Jiaming Liu;Ruiping Wang;Hao Dong
