title: Learning Performance-oriented Control Barrier Functions Under Complex Safety Constraints and Limited Actuation
abstract: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) offer an elegant framework for constraining nonlinear control system dynamics to an invariant subset of a pre-specified safe set. However, finding a CBF that simultaneously promotes performance by maximizing the resulting control invariant set while accommodating complex safety constraints, especially in high relative degree systems with actuation constraints, remains a significant challenge. In this work, we propose a novel self-supervised learning framework that holistically addresses these hurdles. Given a Boolean composition of multiple state constraints defining the safe set, our approach begins by constructing a smooth function whose zero superlevel set provides an inner approximation of the safe set. This function is then used with a smooth neural network to parameterize the CBF candidate. Finally, we design a physics-informed training loss function based on a Hamilton-Jacobi Partial Differential Equation (PDE) to train the PINN-CBF and enlarge the volume of the induced control invariant set. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a 2D double integrator (DI) system and a 7D fixed-wing aircraft system (F16).
keywords: Control Barrier Functions;Safety;Hamilton-Jacobi Partial Differential Equation
author: Lakshmideepakreddy Manda;Shaoru Chen;Mahyar Fazlyab
aff: Whiting School of Engineering;Microsoft Research;Johns Hopkins University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a digital safety guardrail for robots that keeps them from crashing without making them move like a cautious grandma. They used a neural network to guess the 'safe zone' because doing the actual math for a fighter jet is too hard for traditional methods.
why_bad: The 'physics-informed' training is essentially a fancy coat of paint on a neural network that offers no actual mathematical guarantee the plane won't hit a wall.
Using a smooth approximation for complex safety boundaries is just 'rounding down' the danger until the math looks pretty, which is a great way to ignore real-world jagged edges.
The 7D F16 simulation is a classic case of 'it works on my machine'; the computational cost of solving these PDEs usually explodes the moment you add a hint of real-world turbulence.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a race car driver trying to speed through a narrow mountain pass at midnight. Instead of installing actual steel barriers, the team decides to project a 'performance-oriented' hologram of a guardrail based on a dream the lead engineer had about gravity. It looks sleek, it doesn't weigh the car down, and it theoretically shows the safest path. But the moment the driver clips a corner, they'll realize that 'learning-based safety' is just a high-tech way of saying there's nothing actually stopping you from plummeting into the ravine. Great for the highlight reel, lethal for the pilot.

title: TidyBot++: An Open-Source Holonomic Mobile Manipulator for Robot Learning
abstract: Exploiting the promise of recent advances in imitation learning for mobile manipulation will require the collection of large numbers of human-guided demonstrations. This paper proposes an open-source design for an inexpensive, robust, and flexible mobile manipulator that can support arbitrary arms, enabling a wide range of real-world household mobile manipulation tasks. Crucially, our design uses powered casters to enable the mobile base to be fully holonomic, able to control all planar degrees of freedom independently and simultaneously. This feature makes the base more maneuverable and simplifies many mobile manipulation tasks, eliminating the kinematic constraints that create complex and time-consuming motions in nonholonomic bases. We equip our robot with an intuitive mobile phone teleoperation interface to enable easy data acquisition for imitation learning. In our experiments, we use this interface to collect data and show that the resulting learned policies can successfully perform a variety of common household mobile manipulation tasks.
author: Jimmy Wu;William Chong;Robert Holmberg;Aaditya Prasad;Yihuai Gao;Oussama Khatib;Shuran Song;Szymon Rusinkiewicz;Jeannette Bohg
aff: Princeton University;;;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Princeton University;Stanford University
position: PhD student;;;Undergrad student;PhD student;Full Professor;Assistant Professor;Professor;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a budget-friendly robot on wheels that moves in any direction like a frantic crab, all controlled by a smartphone app. The goal was to let humans play a high-stakes game of 'Remote Control Butler' to teach the machine how to tidy up your living room without it requiring a PhD to operate.
why_bad: Incremental Hardware Novelty: Using powered casters to achieve holonomic motion is a well-worn trick in robotics, making the 'TidyBot++' feel more like a weekend DIY project than a peer-reviewed breakthrough.

The 'iPhone-to-Robot' Bottleneck: While teleoperating with a phone is convenient, it lacks the precision haptics needed for complex manipulation, likely resulting in a dataset full of 'clumsy toddler' movements.

Evaluation Surface-Level: The experiments show the robot doing 'household tasks,' but critics noted a lack of rigorous benchmarking against existing platforms that don't require building a custom base from scratch.
roast.analogy_name: The Drift-King Dishwasher on a Budget
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to the Main Event! TidyBot++ enters the arena looking like a shopping cart that’s been hitting the gym, boasting a 'holonomic' chassis that promises to out-maneuver a cat on a laser pointer! The driver is on the sidelines, frantically swiping on an iPhone like they're playing a laggy game of Temple Run, trying to get this hunk of metal to pick up a laundry basket! It’s a bold strategy, Cotton—trying to solve the world’s hardest AI problems with hardware held together by open-source dreams and a prayer. But wait! The judges are looking at the replay, and they’ve seen this move before! It’s flashy, it’s sideways, but it’s just not enough to get the gold when the competition is already flying drones through keyholes! Back to the lab for this rookie!

title: Adaptive Language-Guided Abstraction from Contrastive Explanations
abstract:
Many approaches to robot learning begin by inferring a reward function from a set of human demonstrations.
To learn a good reward, it is necessary to determine which features of the environment are relevant before determining how these features should be used to compute reward.
In particularly complex, high-dimensional environments, human demonstrators often struggle to fully specify their desired behavior from a small number of demonstrations.
End-to-end reward learning methods (e.g., using deep networks or program synthesis techniques) often yield brittle reward functions that are sensitive to spurious state features.
By contrast, humans can often generalizably learn from a small number of demonstrations by incorporating strong priors about what features of a demonstration are likely meaningful for a task of interest. 
How do we build robots that leverage this kind of background knowledge when learning from new demonstrations?
This paper describes a method named ALGAE which alternates between using language models to iteratively identify human-meaningful features needed to explain demonstrated behavior, then standard inverse reinforcement learning techniques to assign weights to these features.
Experiments across a variety of both simulated and real-world robot environments show that ALGAElearns generalizable reward functions defined on interpretable features using only small numbers of demonstrations.
Importantly, ALGAE can recognize when features are missing, then extract and define those features without any human input -- making it possible to quickly and efficiently acquire rich representations of user behavior.
author: Andi Peng;Belinda Z. Li;Ilia Sucholutsky;Nishanth Kumar;Julie Shah;Jacob Andreas;Andreea Bobu
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Princeton University;The AI Institute;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Microsoft;The AI Institute;Massachusetts Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Princeton University;AI Institute;Microsoft

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to stop robots from being social morons by using an AI chatbot to guess what a human actually cares about during a task. Instead of the robot guessing purely based on math, it uses 'common sense' from the internet to write a cheat sheet of what it thinks it saw.
why_bad: The claim of 'no human input' is a blatant hallucination, given that the system relies entirely on LLMs trained on the entire human-written internet.
The acronym ALGAE is a self-fulfilling prophecy for a paper that is essentially bottom-tier pond scum in terms of actual algorithmic novelty.
The 'interpretable features' often amount to the LLM making up poetic descriptions for sensor data that the robot's hardware can't actually verify or track reliably.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a mime trying to teach a toddler how to bake a cake using only gestures. The toddler is an idiot, so you hire a 'Deep Learning Consultant' to sit in the corner, watch your hand waves, and write a 10-page screenplay about the 'Emotional Resonance of Flour.' Our startup, ALGAE, is disrupting the kitchen-toddler space by replacing actual sensing with high-end fan fiction. We aren't just teaching the robot to bake; we're teaching it to hallucinate the *vibe* of baking. Series A funding is currently pending a successful demonstration where the robot doesn't mistake the cat for a loaf of sourdough.

title: Avoid Everything: Model-Free Collision Avoidance with Expert-Guided Fine-Tuning
abstract: The world is full of clutter. In order to operate effectively in uncontrolled, real world spaces, robots must navigate safely by executing tasks around obstacles while in proximity to hazards. Creating safe movement for robotic manipulators remains a long-standing challenge in robotics, particularly in environments with partial observability. In partially observed settings, classical techniques often fail. Learned end-to-end motion policies can infer correct solutions in these settings, but are as-yet unable to produce reliably safe movement when close to obstacles. In this work, we introduce Avoid Everything, a novel end-to-end system for generating collision-free motion toward a target, even targets close to obstacles. Avoid Everything consists of two parts: 1) Motion Policy Transformer (M$\pi$Former), a transformer architecture for end-to-end joint space control from point clouds, trained on over 1,000,000 expert trajectories and 2) a fine-tuning procedure we call Refining on Optimized Policy Experts (ROPE), which uses optimization to provide demonstrations of safe behavior in challenging states. With these techniques, we are able to successfully solve over 63% of reaching problems that caused the previous state of the art method to fail, resulting in an overall success rate of over 91\% in challenging manipulation settings.
author: Adam Fishman;Aaron Walsman;Mohak Bhardwaj;Wentao Yuan;Balakumar Sundaralingam;Byron Boots;Dieter Fox
aff: University of Washington;Harvard University;;University of Washington, Seattle;NVIDIA;;Department of Computer Science

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop robot arms from smashing into things by feeding a massive AI model a million examples of humans doing it right. They added a special 'safety leash' called ROPE to fix the robot's mistakes, but it still manages to crash into its environment nearly 10% of the time.
why_bad: A 9% failure rate in collision avoidance isn't a 'high success rate'; it’s an insurance liability that would get a human operator fired on their first day.
The ROPE fine-tuning method is essentially a patch for a leaky boat, using optimization to 'cheat' the answers rather than teaching the robot actual spatial reasoning.
The model relies on point clouds and transformers for real-time control, which is computationally equivalent to trying to solve a Rubik's Cube while sprinting through a minefield.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Point Cloud
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the defense claims this robot is a 'safe navigator,' yet Exhibit A shows it obliterating a Ming vase because the lighting changed. The prosecution argues that 'ROPE' isn't a breakthrough—it's a witness who memorized a script but starts sweating the moment the cross-examination moves two inches to the left. You cannot claim 91% reliability when the missing 9% involves a robotic arm committing aggravated battery against a lab table. I move to strike this entire architecture from the record; this isn't machine learning, it's a defendant pleading the fifth on every difficult corner.

title: Learning a Distributed Hierarchical Locomotion Controller for Embodied Cooperation
abstract: In this work, we propose a distributed hierarchical locomotion control strategy for whole-body cooperation and demonstrate the potential for migration into large numbers of agents. Our method utilizes a hierarchical structure to break down complex tasks into smaller, manageable sub-tasks. By incorporating spatiotemporal continuity features, we establish the sequential logic necessary for causal inference and cooperative behaviour in sequential tasks, thereby facilitating efficient and coordinated control strategies. Through training within this framework, we demonstrate enhanced adaptability and cooperation, leading to superior performance in task completion compared to the original methods. Moreover, we construct a set of environments as the benchmark for embodied cooperation.
author: Chuye Hong;Kangyao Huang;Huaping Liu
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a group of robots how to move in sync by giving them a corporate-style chain of command and a sense of timing. Basically, it’s an attempt to stop a robot swarm from tripping over their own feet while trying to move a heavy sofa together.
why_bad: The 'benchmarks' are a home-cooked sandbox specifically designed to make this specific algorithm look like a genius, ignoring standard industry tests.
The 'hierarchical structure' is essentially over-engineered bureaucracy that adds massive computational overhead for tasks a simple script could solve.
Claims of 'causal inference' are a massive reach; the robots aren't actually understanding cause and effect, they're just over-fitting to a very specific set of movements.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup where every time you want to take a step, your left foot has to file a Jira ticket with a 'Central Locomotion Manager,' which then waits for a 'Spatiotemporal Sync' from your right foot before approving the movement. It sounds like 'disruptive synergy' in a pitch deck, but in practice, you're just a guy standing in the lobby vibrating while your competitors—who just used basic physics—are already at the bar. We’ve successfully turned the simple act of walking into a series of expensive, slow-moving board meetings.

title: Non-rigid Relative Placement through 3D Dense Diffusion
abstract: The task of "relative placement" is to predict the placement of one object in relation to another, e.g. placing a mug on a mug rack. Recent methods for relative placement have made tremendous progress towards data-efficient learning for robot manipulation; using explicit object-centric geometric reasoning, these approaches enable generalization to unseen task variations from a small number of demonstrations. State-of-the-art works in this area, however, have yet to represent deformable transformations, despite the ubiquity of non-rigid bodies in real world settings. As a first step towards bridging this gap, we propose "cross-displacement" - an extension of the principles of relative placement to geometric relationships between deformable objects - and present a novel vision-based method to learn cross-displacement for a non-rigid task through dense diffusion. To this end, we demonstrate our method's ability to generalize to unseen object instances, out-of-distribution scene configurations, and multimodal goals on a highly deformable cloth-hanging task beyond the scope of prior works.
author: Eric Cai;Octavian Donca;Ben Eisner;David Held
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots how to handle floppy, squishy things like laundry by predicting exactly how one object should sit on another. They used 'diffusion'—the same tech behind AI art—to help the robot guess the best way to drape a cloth over a rack without it sliding off.
why_bad: The 'cross-displacement' concept is essentially a fancy rebranding of existing geometry problems, adding vocabulary but not necessarily solving the core physics.
Using dense diffusion for real-time manipulation is like trying to win a drag race in a car that has to stop and render a 4K movie before every gear shift; it’s far too slow for practical robotics.
The experimental 'generalization' was limited to a single cloth-hanging task, which is the academic equivalent of claiming you’re a Michelin-star chef because you successfully microwaved a burrito.
roast.analogy_name: The Fabric of Reality: Slow-Motion Sock Saga
roast.analogy_description: In a world where industrial robots can build cars but are defeated by a pair of yoga pants, one algorithm dares to overthink everything. Witness the high-stakes drama of a $100,000 robot arm standing perfectly still for ten minutes while its 'dense diffusion' brain hallucinates every possible way a towel could fold. It’s a cinematic journey of over-engineering where the math is beautiful, the physics are crunchy, and the laundry still isn't done. Coming soon to a lab where 'real-time' means 'see you next Tuesday.'

title: CtRL-Sim: Reactive and Controllable Driving Agents with Offline Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Evaluating autonomous vehicle stacks (AVs) in simulation typically involves replaying driving logs from real-world recorded traffic. However, agents replayed from offline data are not reactive and hard to intuitively control. Existing approaches address these challenges by proposing methods that rely on heuristics or generative models of real-world data but these approaches either lack realism or necessitate costly iterative sampling procedures to control the generated behaviours. In this work, we take an alternative approach and propose CtRL-Sim, a method that leverages return-conditioned offline reinforcement learning to efficiently generate reactive and controllable traffic agents. Specifically, we process real-world driving data through a physics-enhanced Nocturne simulator to generate a diverse offline reinforcement learning dataset, annotated with various reward terms. We then train a return-conditioned multi-agent behaviour model that allows for fine-grained manipulation of agent behaviours by modifying the desired returns for the various reward components. This capability enables the generation of a wide range of driving behaviours beyond the scope of the initial dataset, including adversarial behaviours. We demonstrate that CtRL-Sim can generate diverse and realistic safety-critical scenarios while providing fine-grained control over agent behaviours.
author: Luke Rowe;Roger Girgis;Anthony Gosselin;Bruno Carrez;Florian Golemo;Felix Heide;Liam Paull;Christopher Pal
aff: Université de Montréal;Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute;Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms, University of Montreal, Université de Montréal;Mila;Mila;Algolux;;Polytechnique Montreal

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to stop simulated self-driving cars from acting like brain-dead ghosts that just follow a pre-recorded script. They built a system where you can essentially use a 'mood slider' to force digital drivers to be aggressive, reckless, or safe on command.
why_bad: The 'physics-enhanced' Nocturne simulator is essentially a 2D flatland, making claims of 'real-world realism' feel like calling a stick figure a portrait.
The 'fine-grained control' is largely a result of reward-shaping, which often leads to the AI 'cheating' the physics to hit its goals rather than driving naturally.
The method struggles to prove that these generated scenarios are actually useful for training real cars, rather than just being high-tech digital hallucinations.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Remote-Controlled Witness
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a defense attorney presenting a crime scene reconstruction where the actors are puppets. The attorney claims the 'simulation' is revolutionary because he can turn a dial on the puppets' backs to make them act 'guilty' or 'innocent.' The Judge—a weary peer reviewer—bangs his gavel. 'Counselor,' he sighs, 'you haven't proven how a real human behaves; you've just shown us that if you pull the 'Aggression' string, the puppet hits the wall. This isn't a breakthrough in behavioral science; it’s just a very expensive way to play with dolls in front of a jury that expects actual data.'

title: HYPERmotion: Learning Hybrid Behavior Planning for Autonomous Loco-manipulation
abstract: Enabling robots to autonomously perform hybrid motions in diverse environments can be beneficial for long-horizon tasks such as material handling, household chores, and work assistance. This requires extensive exploitation of intrinsic motion capabilities, extraction of affordances from rich environmental information, and planning of physical interaction behaviors. Despite recent progress has demonstrated impressive humanoid whole-body control abilities, they struggle to achieve versatility and adaptability for new tasks. In this work, we propose HYPERmotion, a framework that learns, selects and plans behaviors based on tasks in different scenarios. We combine reinforcement learning with whole-body optimization to generate motion for 38 actuated joints and create a motion library to store the learned skills. We apply the planning and reasoning features of the large language models (LLMs) to complex loco-manipulation tasks, constructing a hierarchical task graph that comprises a series of primitive behaviors to bridge lower-level execution with higher-level planning. By leveraging the interaction of distilled spatial geometry and 2D observation with a visual language model (VLM) to ground knowledge into a robotic morphology selector to choose appropriate actions in single- or dual-arm, legged or wheeled locomotion.  Experiments in simulation and real-world show that learned motions can efficiently adapt to new tasks, demonstrating high autonomy from free-text commands in unstructured scenes. Videos and website: hy-motion.github.io//
author: Jin Wang;Rui Dai;Weijie Wang;Luca Rossini;Francesco Ruscelli;Nikos Tsagarakis
aff: Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia;Università degli Studi di Genova, Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia;Università degli Studi di Genova, Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia;;;
aff_unique_norm: Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia;Università degli Studi di Genova

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'Swiss Army Knife' robot that can walk, roll, and grab things by letting a ChatGPT-style AI act as the middle manager for 38 different motorized joints. It’s an attempt to turn vague text commands into complex physical actions using every expensive AI buzzword available in 2024.
why_bad: The 'Kitchen Sink' Approach: By shoving LLMs, VLMs, RL, and whole-body optimization into one paper, the authors created a 'Frankenstein’s Monster' where it's impossible to tell if the system is actually smart or just lucky.

The Reasoning Hallucination: Using a Large Language Model to 'plan' physical loco-manipulation is like asking a novelist to fly a helicopter; it can describe the physics beautifully while still crashing into a building.

Lack of Ablation Rigor: The paper fails to prove that this massive, over-engineered hierarchy is actually better than simpler methods, making the '38 actuated joints' feel more like a hardware flex than a functional breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where a simple rolling robot isn't enough... comes a machine with more joints than a yoga retreat and a brain powered by a chatbot that’s read too much Nietzsche. Witness THE HYPERMOTION. It doesn't just open a door; it 'extracts affordances' and 'distills spatial geometry' while having an internal debate about whether it should use its legs or its wheels. It’s got the coordination of a drunk giraffe and the ego of a Silicon Valley CEO. Coming this summer to an unstructured environment near you: watch it attempt to pick up a soda can using a 12-step hierarchical task graph while its vision model confuses a shadow for a structural obstacle. It’s over-engineered. It’s over-complex. It’s... HYPERmotion: The Movie.

title: ANAVI: Audio Noise Awareness using Visual of Indoor environments for NAVIgation
abstract:
We propose Audio Noise Awareness using Visuals of Indoors for NAVIgation for quieter robot path planning.  While humans are naturally aware of the noise they make and its impact on those around them, robots currently lack this awareness. 
A key challenge in achieving audio awareness for robots is estimating how loud will the robot’s actions be at a listener’s location? Since sound depends upon the geometry and material composition of rooms, we train the robot to passively perceive loudness using visual observations of indoor environments. To this end, we generate data on how loud an `impulse' sounds at different listener locations in simulated homes, and train our Acoustic Noise Predictor (ANP). Next, we collect acoustic profiles corresponding to different actions for navigation. Unifying ANP with action acoustics, we demonstrate experiments with wheeled (Hello Robot Stretch) and legged (Unitree Go2) robots so that these robots adhere to the noise constraints of the environment.  All simulated and real-world data, code and model checkpoints is released at https://anavi-corl24.github.io/.
author: Vidhi Jain;Rishi Veerapaneni;Yonatan Bisk
aff: Google;;Meta

roast.laymen_summary: This research tries to give robots 'social ears' by teaching them to look at a room and guess how much their metallic clanking will annoy you. It essentially attempts to stop a robot from being the mechanical equivalent of a roommate who decides to vacuum at 2:00 AM.
why_bad: The 'Sim-to-Real' gap: Predicting acoustics from a photo is a guessing game that falls apart the moment a robot encounters a material it hasn't seen in a simulator.
The 'Physics Overkill' problem: Training a complex neural network to predict sound propagation often yields results that a basic geometry formula could have solved in half the time.
Hardware limitations: Knowing you're loud doesn't make you quiet; telling a 15kg quadraped to 'tread softly' on hardwood floors is like asking a bulldozer to tip-toe.
roast.analogy_description: We’re disrupting the noise-pollution space with a revolutionary wearable for clumsy people. Imagine a pair of smart glasses that stares at your surroundings and whispers, 'Hey, that floor is marble, maybe don't wear your tap shoes.' It doesn't actually provide quieter shoes, and it ignores the fact that you’re still carrying a leaf blower, but it gives you a very sophisticated visual heatmap of exactly how much everyone in the building hates you. It’s not 'avoiding noise'—it’s 'disruptive acoustic mindfulness' for the socially illiterate machine.

title: SLR: Learning Quadruped Locomotion without Privileged Information
abstract: Traditional reinforcement learning control for quadruped robots often relies on privileged information, demanding meticulous selection and precise estimation, thereby imposing constraints on the development process. This work proposes a Self-learning Latent Representation (SLR) method, which achieves high-performance control policy learning without the need for privileged information. To enhance the credibility of our proposed method's evaluation, SLR is compared with open-source code repositories of state-of-the-art algorithms, retaining the original authors' configuration parameters. Across four repositories, SLR consistently outperforms the reference results. Ultimately, the trained policy and encoder empower the quadruped robot to navigate steps, climb stairs, ascend rocks, and traverse various challenging terrains.
author: Shiyi Chen;Zeyu Wan;Shiyang Yan;Chun Zhang;Weiyi Zhang;Qiang Li;Debing Zhang;Fasih Ud Din Farrukh
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Shenzhen Technology University;Tsinghua University ;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University
position: MS student;MS student;MS student;PhD student;Full Professor;Postdoc;Full Professor;Instructor

roast.laymen_summary: We built a robot dog that learns to climb stairs and rocks without using a digital 'cheat sheet' of the environment that usually helps AI robots find their footing. We claim our method beats everyone else's existing tech, even though we just downloaded their code and didn't bother to tune it for a fair comparison.
why_bad: The 'state-of-the-art' comparison is rigged; keeping the original authors' default parameters instead of optimizing them is the research equivalent of racing a Ferrari against a Tesla that’s stuck in 'Valet Mode.'
The paper fails to provide a rigorous ablation study, leaving us to wonder if the success comes from the new architecture or just a lucky set of random seeds and some aggressive reward shaping.

The central claim of avoiding 'privileged information' isn't the breakthrough they think it is; the field moved on to similar self-supervised representations years ago, making this feel like 'Yesterday's News' rebranded as a 'Breaking Scoop.'
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Blindfolded Bloodhound
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims their 'Self-learning' dog is a prodigy because it navigated a literal obstacle course without looking at a map. But look at the evidence! They 'proved' this by comparing their dog to the neighborhood champions... while those champions were still locked in their travel crates. You can’t claim your client is the fastest sprinter in history just because they beat a world-record holder who was forced to run in soggy flip-flops and a parka. This isn't a scientific breakthrough; it's a choreographed hit job on the competition's default settings. Your Honor, I move to strike this entire testimony until the 'expert' learns how to conduct a fair trial!

title: Goal-Reaching Policy Learning from Non-Expert Observations via Effective Subgoal Guidance
abstract: In this work, we address the challenging problem of long-horizon goal-reaching policy learning from non-expert, action-free observation data. Unlike fully labeled expert data, our data is more accessible and avoids the costly process of action labeling. Additionally, compared to online learning, which often involves aimless exploration, our data provides useful guidance for more efficient exploration. To achieve our goal, we propose a novel subgoal guidance learning strategy. The motivation behind this strategy is that long-horizon goals offer limited guidance for efficient exploration and accurate state transition. We develop a diffusion strategy-based high-level policy to generate reasonable subgoals as waypoints, preferring states that more easily lead to the final goal. Additionally, we learn state-goal value functions to encourage efficient subgoal reaching. These two components naturally integrate into the off-policy actor-critic framework, enabling efficient goal attainment through informative exploration. We evaluate our method on complex robotic navigation and manipulation tasks, demonstrating a significant performance advantage over existing methods. Our ablation study further shows that our method is robust to observation data with various corruptions.
author: RenMing Huang;Shaochong Liu;Yunqiang Pei;Peng Wang;Guoqing Wang;Yang Yang;Heng Tao Shen
aff: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;Tongji University
position: MS student;MS student;PhD student;Full Professor;Full Professor;Full Professor;Full Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China;Tongji University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach robots to finish complex tasks by showing them videos of amateurs doing it, hoping the robot could figure out the middle steps itself. It turns out that asking an AI to 'fill in the blanks' between random snapshots is much harder when the AI doesn't even know which buttons to press to move its own arms.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The paper essentially bolts a trendy diffusion model onto a standard hierarchical framework, which is the academic equivalent of putting a spoiler on a minivan and calling it a supercar.
Computational Gluttony: Using a massive generative model just to pick a single 'subgoal' is wildly inefficient, creating a 'solution' that takes longer to think than the robot takes to fail.

Theoretical Hand-Waving: The authors claim it works with 'corrupted' and 'non-expert' data, but they fail to prove the robot isn't just getting lucky or relying on hidden biases in the test environment.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Hallucinating Sherpa
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution presents Exhibit A: a mountain climber who has never seen a map, but has looked at a few Polaroids of the summit taken by a drunk hiker. The defendant claims that by 'imagining' beautiful basecamps—or 'subgoals'—they can magically navigate a blizzard. But look at the transcript! The 'high-level policy' is just a fancy storyteller hallucinating a path, while the 'actor' is at the bottom of the cliff because no one actually explained how to use a literal rope. You can't win a trial by showing the jury a picture of a 'Not Guilty' verdict and expecting the law to assemble itself. This isn't a breakthrough; it's a daydreamer trying to 'vibe' their way up Everest.

title: Generalizing End-To-End Autonomous Driving In Real-World Environments Using Zero-Shot LLMs
abstract: Traditional autonomous driving methods adopt modular design, decomposing tasks into sub-tasks, including perception, prediction, planning, and control. In contrast, end-to-end autonomous driving directly outputs actions from raw sensor data, avoiding error accumulation. However, training an end-to-end model requires a comprehensive dataset. Without adequate data, the end-to-end model exhibits poor generalization capabilities. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have been applied to enhance the generalization property of end-to-end driving models. Most studies explore LLMs in an open-loop manner, where the output actions are compared to those of experts without direct activation in the real world. Other studies in closed-loop settings examine their results in simulated environments. In comparison, this paper proposes an efficient architecture that integrates multimodal LLMs into end-to-end real-world driving models in a closed-loop setting. The LLM periodically takes raw sensor data to generate high-level driving instructions. In our architecture, LLMs can effectively guide the end-to-end model, even at a slower rate than the raw sensor data, because updates aren't needed every time frame. This architecture relaxes the trade-off between the latency and inference quality of the LLM. It also allows us to choose a wide variety of LLMs to improve high-level driving instructions and minimize fine-tuning costs. Consequently, our architecture reduces the data collection requirements because the LLMs do not directly output actions, and we only need to train a simple imitation learning model to output actions. In our experiments, the training data for the end-to-end model in a real-world environment consists of only simple obstacle configurations with one traffic cone, while the test environment is more complex and contains different types of obstacles. Experiments show that the proposed architecture enhances the generalization capabilities of the end-to-end model even without fine-tuning the LLM.
author: Zeyu Dong;Yimin Zhu;Yansong Li;Kevin Mahon;Yu Sun
aff: State University of New York at Stony Brook;, State University of New York at Stony Brook;University of Illinois Chicago;Sunrise AI Tech;Sunrise Technology Inc.
aff_unique_norm: State University of New York at Stony Brook;University of Illinois at Chicago;Sunrise AI Tech;Sunrise Technology Inc.

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to use a slow-moving AI brain—like the one behind ChatGPT—to bark high-level orders at a tiny, fast-acting 'intern' model that actually steers the car. They claim that because the big brain 'knows' things about the world, the car can navigate complex city streets despite only being trained on a single traffic cone.
why_bad: Safety Latency: Relying on a slow LLM to give 'periodic' advice while moving at 60mph is like getting a 'turn left' text message three seconds after you've already driven into a lake.
Generalization Leap: Training a model on exactly one traffic cone and assuming it can handle a bustling intersection is the peak of academic optimism, bordering on delusion.
The 'Blind Leading the Blind' Problem: If the LLM hallucinates or misinterprets a raw sensor frame, the simple imitation model lacks the 'common sense' to do anything other than obediently crash.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the Executive Chef is locked in a basement with no windows and gets a grainy Polaroid of the stove once every ten minutes. He has to text instructions to a toddler in the kitchen who has only ever been taught how to touch a single piece of bread. The Chef texts 'Sauté the aromatics'—a great high-level instruction—but the toddler is currently holding a live lobster and a blowtorch. By the time the Chef's next 'high-level' text arrives to correct the course, the kitchen is a smoking crater. Sure, the Chef 'understands' fine dining, but that doesn't mean the toddler can execute a Beef Wellington using zero-shot intuition.

title: Body Transformer: Leveraging Robot Embodiment for Policy Learning
abstract: In recent years, the transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for machine learning algorithms applied to natural language processing and computer vision. Despite notable evidence of successful deployment of this architecture in the context of robot learning, we claim that vanilla transformers do not fully exploit the structure of the robot learning problem. We propose Body Transformer (BoT), an architecture that exploits the robot embodiment by providing an inductive bias that guides the learning process. We represent the robot body as a graph of sensors and actuators, and rely on masked attention to pool information through the architecture. The resulting architecture outperforms the vanilla transformer, as well as the classical multilayer perceptron, with respect to task completion, scaling properties, and computational efficiency when representing either imitation or reinforcement learning policies.
keywords: Robot Learning;Graph Neural Networks;Imitation Learning;Reinforcement Learning
author: Carmelo Sferrazza;Dun-Ming Huang;Fangchen Liu;Jongmin Lee;Pieter Abbeel
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Covariant

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to make robots smarter by hard-coding a map of their limbs directly into the AI’s brain so it knows where its 'arm' ends and its 'leg' begins. It turns out that micromanaging a robot's self-awareness is just a fancy way of making it less flexible when things actually get complicated.
why_bad: The 'Inductive Bias' is actually a straightjacket: By forcing the AI to focus on the robot's physical layout, they limited its ability to learn complex patterns that don't fit into a neat anatomical graph.

The 'Been There, Done That' Problem: Reviewers pointed out that treating a robot body as a graph isn't a revolution; it’s basically just putting a trendy 'Transformer' sticker on a decade-old Graph Neural Network concept.

Weak Baselines and Toy Tasks: The performance gains were mostly seen in basic simulations, failing to prove that this extra complexity would actually survive the messy, unpredictable physics of the real world.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where AI is supposed to be an autonomous prodigy, one team of scientists decided to treat it like a toddler in a bubble-wrap suit. *Cue dramatic synth music:* Meet the Body Transformer—the only AI architecture that refuses to let the robot move its left pinky without first consulting a 40-page anatomical manual. While 'Vanilla' AI is out there learning to parkour by sheer intuition, BoT is stuck in the corner, overthinking its elbow-to-wrist connectivity. It’s a high-stakes drama of micromanagement where 'structure' is the hero, 'flexibility' is the villain, and the reviewers are the cold-hearted critics demanding to know why the robot is still overcomplicating a simple walk to the fridge.
roast.tone_check: Lean, mean, and scientifically grounded.

title: Implicit Grasp Diffusion: Bridging the Gap between Dense Prediction and Sampling-based Grasping
abstract: There are two dominant approaches in modern robot grasp planning: dense prediction and sampling-based methods. Dense prediction calculates viable grasps across the robot’s view but is limited to predicting one grasp per voxel. Sampling-based methods, on the other hand, encode multi-modal grasp distributions, allowing for different grasp approaches at a point. However, these methods rely on a global latent representation, which struggles to represent the entire field of view, resulting in coarse grasps. To address this, we introduce \emph{Implicit Grasp Diffusion} (IGD), which combines the strengths of both methods by using implicit neural representations to extract detailed local features and sampling grasps from diffusion models conditioned on these features. Evaluations on clutter removal tasks in both simulated and real-world environments show that IGD delivers high accuracy, noise resilience, and multi-modal grasp pose capabilities.
author: Pinhao Song;Pengteng Li;Renaud Detry
aff: KU Leuven;;

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to give a robot arm 'artistic intuition' by using the same AI tech behind image generators to help it figure out how to grab objects. Instead of just pointing at a spot, the robot now 'dreams' up the perfect hand angle for every tiny pixel it sees.
why_bad: The inference speed is a disaster; the robot spends so much time 'thinking' through its diffusion steps that the object might as well have evolved into a new species by the time it moves.
The novelty is basically 'AI LEGOs'—taking two existing popular methods and smashing them together without proving why the extra complexity is actually necessary.
The real-world evaluations were suspiciously cherry-picked, featuring a 'cluttered' environment that looks suspiciously like a neatly organized minimalist's desk.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Robotics Edition.' Our chef today wants to flip a pancake. Instead of just using a spatula, he insists on laser-scanning every bubble on the surface (dense prediction) while simultaneously running a thousand-step cloud simulation to 'imagine' the most aesthetic flip possible (diffusion). It’s technically impressive, but by the time the robot actually moves its arm, the pancake is a charcoal brick and the customers have all gone home to eat at McDonald's. It's a five-star solution for a one-star problem.

title: Neural Attention Field: Emerging Point Relevance in 3D Scenes for One-Shot Dexterous Grasping
abstract: One-shot transfer of dexterous grasps to novel scenes with object and context variations has been a challenging problem. While distilled feature fields from large vision models have enabled semantic correspondences across 3D scenes, their features are point-based and restricted to object surfaces, limiting their capability of modeling complex semantic feature distributions for hand-object interactions. In this work, we propose the *neural attention field* for representing semantic-aware dense feature fields in the 3D space by modeling inter-point relevance instead of individual point features. Core to it is a transformer decoder that computes the cross-attention between any 3D query point with all the scene points, and provides the query point feature with an attention-based aggregation. We further propose a self-supervised framework for training the transformer decoder from only a few 3D pointclouds without hand demonstrations. Post-training, the attention field can be applied to novel scenes for semantics-aware dexterous grasping from one-shot demonstration. Experiments show that our method provides better optimization landscapes by encouraging the end-effector to focus on task-relevant scene regions, resulting in significant improvements in success rates on real robots compared with the feature-field-based methods.
keywords: Desterous Grasping;One-Shot Manipulation;Distilled Feature Field;Neural Implicit Field;Self-Supervised Learning
author: Qianxu Wang;Congyue Deng;Tyler Ga Wei Lum;Yuanpei Chen;Yaodong Yang;Jeannette Bohg;Yixin Zhu;Leonidas Guibas
aff: Peking University;Stanford University;Stanford University;PsiRobot;Peking University;Stanford University;Peking University;Stanford University
position: Undergrad student;PhD student;PhD student;Researcher;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor;Full Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of just looking at an object's surface, this robot tries to understand a 3D scene by calculating how every single point in space relates to every other point. It's essentially trying to teach a robot to grab a mug by making it meditate on the 'vibe' of the entire room first.
why_bad: The 'Neural Attention Field' is a massive computational over-complication for tasks that basic geometry solved decades ago.
The 'one-shot' promise is misleading because the model requires heavy self-supervised pre-training on specific data before it can do its 'one' trick.
The experimental results lack the statistical rigor to prove this isn't just a lucky simulation run dressed up in Transformer-decoder buzzwords.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where picking up a spoon is a four-dimensional puzzle... one machine refuses to just use its eyes. Witness the silicon-brained madness of a robot that won't touch your car keys until it has mathematically reconciled the relationship between the keychain and the dust bunny in the corner. Coming this summer: A machine that spends 40 minutes contemplating the 'essence' of a spatula, only to drop it the moment a shadow moves. It’s not just grasping; it’s overthinking. This time, it’s computationally expensive.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized.

title: Trust the PRoC3S: Solving Long-Horizon Robotics Problems with LLMs and Constraint Satisfaction
abstract: Recent developments in pretrained large language models (LLMs) applied to robotics have demonstrated their capacity for sequencing a set of discrete skills to achieve open-ended goals in simple robotic tasks. In this paper, we examine the topic of LLM planning for a set of *continuously parameterized* skills whose execution must avoid violations of a set of kinematic, geometric, and physical constraints. We prompt the LLM to output code for a function with open parameters, which, together with environmental constraints, can be viewed as a Continuous Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CCSP). This CCSP can be solved through sampling or optimization to find a skill sequence and continuous parameter settings that achieve the goal while avoiding constraint violations. Additionally, we consider cases where the LLM proposes unsatisfiable CCSPs, such as those that are kinematically infeasible, dynamically unstable, or lead to collisions, and re-prompt the LLM to form a new CCSP accordingly. Experiments across simulated and real-world domains demonstrate that our proposed strategy, \OursNoSpace, is capable of solving a wide range of complex manipulation tasks with realistic constraints much more efficiently and effectively than existing baselines.
keywords: LLMs for planning;task and motion planning;constraint satisfaction
author: Aidan Curtis;Nishanth Kumar;Jing Cao;Tomás Lozano-Pérez;Leslie Pack Kaelbling
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;The AI Institute;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make robots smarter by letting a chatbot write the instructions, then using a math filter to make sure the robot didn't accidentally punch a hole through a wall. When the chatbot suggested something physically impossible, they just told it to 'try again' until the math finally checked out.
why_bad: The 'Trial and Error' Fallacy: Relying on re-prompting an LLM when it fails is computationally expensive and essentially just waiting for the AI to get a 'lucky' guess that satisfies the physics solver.

The Math is Doing the Heavy Lifting: Reviewers likely noted that the actual robotics work is done by the Constraint Satisfaction solver, while the LLM is just a glorified, occasionally hallucinating scriptwriter.

Real-World Latency: The system is too slow for dynamic environments; by the time the LLM and the solver agree on how to pick up a falling glass, the glass has already been shards on the floor for three minutes.
roast.analogy_description: [Deep Movie Trailer Voice] In a world where physics is law, one robot decided to take advice... from a poet. Witness the cinematic chaos of 'The PRoC3S.' Watch as a trillion-parameter brain tries to explain 'gravity' to a titanium arm that just wants to grab a juice box. See the high-stakes drama as the math-based babysitter rejects the AI's 40th attempt to reach through a solid table. It’s a pulse-pounding thriller where the hero spends 90% of the movie waiting for a server response. Coming this fall: Trust the PRoC3S—where 'I think I can' meets 'I definitely can't because of the laws of thermodynamics.'

title: 3D-ViTac: Learning Fine-Grained Manipulation with Visuo-Tactile Sensing
abstract: Tactile and visual perception are both crucial for humans to perform fine-grained interactions with their environment. Developing similar multi-modal sensing capabilities for robots can significantly enhance and expand their manipulation skills. This paper introduces **3D-ViTac**, a multi-modal sensing and learning system designed for dexterous bimanual manipulation. Our system features tactile sensors equipped with dense sensing units, each covering an area of 3$mm^2$. These sensors are low-cost and flexible, providing detailed and extensive coverage of physical contacts, effectively complementing visual information. To integrate tactile and visual data, we fuse them into a unified 3D representation space that preserves their 3D structures and spatial relationships. The multi-modal representation can then be coupled with diffusion policies for imitation learning. Through concrete hardware experiments, we demonstrate that even low-cost robots can perform precise manipulations and significantly outperform vision-only policies, particularly in safe interactions with fragile items and executing long-horizon tasks involving in-hand manipulation. Our project page is available at https://binghao-huang.github.io/3D-ViTac/.
author: Binghao Huang;Yixuan Wang;Xinyi Yang;Yiyue Luo;Yunzhu Li
aff: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;Zhejiang University;Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, Electrical Engineering & Computer Science;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
position: PhD student;MS student;Undergrad student;PhD student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Zhejiang University;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: We glued cheap, DIY pressure sensors to robot hands so they can 'feel' objects while looking at them, theoretically preventing them from crushing eggs. The system mashes 3D camera data and finger-touch data into one digital map to help the robot perform delicate chores.
why_bad: Calibration Chaos: Fusing 3D vision with tactile data requires surgical precision; if the camera or 'skin' shifts by a millimeter, the robot’s internal map becomes a hallucinogenic mess.

The Generalization Gap: While the robot can handle the specific items on the project page, the 'diffusion policy' approach often results in a glorified puppet that memorizes paths rather than understanding the physics of touch.
Lack of Novelty: Adding a tactile sensor to an existing vision model is the academic equivalent of putting a new case on an iPhone and calling it a revolutionary breakthrough in telecommunications.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a 'revolutionary' way to do surgery by wearing thick gardening gloves covered in sandpaper while looking through a laggy GoPro. Our MVP, Sensi-Bot 3000, solves the 'I'm a clumsy robot' problem by over-engineering a $20,000 solution for a problem a pair of tweezers could solve. We’re not just picking up fragile items; we’re 'leveraging multi-modal spatial synergies' to ensure that when we eventually drop the egg, we have high-resolution data on exactly how hard we squeezed it. It’s not a failure; it’s a high-fidelity haptic learning opportunity. Seed round is closing fast—invest in the future of expensive fumbling.

title: Learning Granular Media Avalanche Behavior for Indirectly Manipulating Obstacles on a Granular Slope
abstract: Legged robot locomotion on sand slopes is challenging due to the complex dynamics of granular media and how the lack of solid surfaces can hinder locomotion. A promising strategy, inspired by ghost crabs and other organisms in nature, is to strategically interact with rocks, debris, and other obstacles to facilitate movement. To provide legged robots with this ability, we present a novel approach that leverages avalanche dynamics to indirectly manipulate objects on a granular slope. We use a Vision Transformer (ViT) to process image representations of granular dynamics and robot excavation actions. The ViT predicts object movement, which we use to determine which leg excavation action to execute. We collect training data from 100 real physical trials and, at test time, deploy our trained model in novel settings. Experimental results suggest that our model can accurately predict object movements and achieve a success rate ≥ 80% in a variety of manipulation tasks with up to four obstacles, and can also generalize to objects with different physics properties. To our knowledge, this is the first paper to leverage granular media avalanche dynamics to indirectly manipulate objects on granular slopes. Supplementary material is available at https://sites.google.com/view/grain-corl2024/home.
author: Haodi Hu;Feifei Qian;Daniel Seita
aff: University of Southern California;University of Southern California;University of Southern California

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to teach a robot to act like a construction worker having a tantrum by kicking sand to trigger mini-landslides that move obstacles out of its way. While it sounds high-tech, it’s essentially using a massive AI brain to predict which way a pile of dirt will collapse when you poke it.
why_bad: Data starvation: Training a data-hungry Vision Transformer on only 100 trials is like trying to learn the entire works of Shakespeare by reading three fortune cookies.
Over-engineered gravity: Using a complex neural network to predict that 'stuff slides down a hill' is a massive computational overkill for basic Newtonian physics.
The 'Laboratory Pet' problem: The system is so finely tuned to one specific sandbox that it would likely have a total meltdown if the sand got slightly damp or the rocks changed shape.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Extreme Culinary Over-Engineering,' where our chef is attempting to move a single garnish across a plate by blowing through a straw at a pile of salt. He’s wearing $3,000 augmented reality goggles to calculate the 'granular trajectory' of every salt crystal, but he’s only practiced this four times and the kitchen windows are closed to prevent a breeze. He calls it 'Indirect Ingredient Displacement via Crystalline Fluidity,' but the judges are pointing out that he could have just used a spoon, and his fancy goggles are mostly just guessing that things fall when pushed. It’s a spectacular way to make a mess while claiming you’ve reinvented the fork.

title: Gentle Manipulation of Tree Branches: A Contact-Aware Policy Learning Approach
abstract: Learning to interact with deformable tree branches with minimal damage is challenging due to their intricate geometry and inscrutable dynamics. Furthermore, traditional vision-based modelling systems suffer from implicit occlusions in dense foliage, severely changing lighting conditions, and limited field of view, in addition to having a significant computational burden preventing real-time deployment.In this work, we simulate a procedural forest with realistic, self-similar branching structures derived from a parametric L-system model, actuated with crude spring abstractions, mirroring real-world variations with domain randomisation over the morphological and dynamic attributes. We then train a novel Proprioceptive Contact-Aware Policy (PCAP) for a reach task using reinforcement learning, aided by a whole-arm contact detection classifier and reward engineering, without external vision, tactile, or torque sensing. The agent deploys novel strategies to evade and mitigate contact impact, favouring a reactive exploration of the task space. Finally, we demonstrate that the learned behavioural patterns can be transferred zero-shot from simulation to real, allowing the arm to navigate around real branches with unseen topology and variable occlusions while minimising the contact forces and expected ruptures.
author: Jay Jacob;Shizhe Cai;Paulo Vinicius Koerich Borges;Tirthankar Bandyopadhyay;Fabio Ramos
aff: ;University of Sydney;CSIRO;, CSIRO;NVIDIA
aff_unique_norm: University of Sydney;Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation;CSIRO;NVIDIA

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot arm how to navigate through thick bushes without snapping twigs by essentially blindfolding it and telling it to 'feel the vibes.' Instead of using cameras that get confused by leaves, the robot relies on its own internal motor strain to guess when it’s bumping into something and then wiggles away.
why_bad: The simulation uses 'crude springs' to model wood, which is like trying to learn surgery by practicing on a bowl of overcooked spaghetti.
Relying solely on internal motor data for 'contact detection' is incredibly noisy, meaning the robot can't distinguish between a stiff breeze and a structural branch until something snaps.
The 'novel strategy' of reactive exploration is just a glorified version of 'bump into things until you stop hitting them,' which isn't 'gentle' so much as it is 'cautiously clumsy.'
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Hell’s Kitchen: Robotics Edition!' Today, our chef is attempting to whisk a delicate egg-white foam, but there's a catch: he’s blindfolded, wearing thick winter parkas, and the kitchen is filled with hanging crystal chandeliers. Instead of looking at the bowl, he’s just swinging his arms and hoping the resistance in his shoulder joints tells him he hasn't smashed a light fixture yet. He practiced this by waving his arms in a room full of hanging rubber bands and now insists he’s ready for a Michelin-star bistro. He calls it 'Contact-Aware Policy Learning'; Gordon Ramsay calls it 'flailing around like a muppet while the soufflé collapses in fear.' It’s technically impressive that he hasn't broken a window yet, but he still hasn't actually managed to whisk the eggs.

title: RoboEXP: Action-Conditioned Scene Graph via Interactive Exploration for Robotic Manipulation
abstract:
We introduce the novel task of interactive scene exploration, wherein robots autonomously explore environments and produce an action-conditioned scene graph (ACSG) that captures the structure of the underlying environment. The ACSG accounts for both low-level information (geometry and semantics) and high-level information (action-conditioned relationships between different entities) in the scene. To this end, we present the Robotic Exploration (RoboEXP) system, which incorporates the Large Multimodal Model (LMM) and an explicit memory design to enhance our system's capabilities. The robot reasons about what and how to explore an object, accumulating new information through the interaction process and incrementally constructing the ACSG.
Leveraging the constructed ACSG, we illustrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our RoboEXP system in facilitating a wide range of real-world manipulation tasks involving rigid, articulated objects, nested objects, and deformable objects. Project Page: https://jianghanxiao.github.io/roboexp-web/
keywords: Action-Conditioned Scene Graph;Foundation Models for Robotics;Scene Exploration;Robotic Manipulation
author: Hanxiao Jiang;Binghao Huang;Ruihai Wu;Zhuoran Li;Shubham Garg;Hooshang Nayyeri;Shenlong Wang;Yunzhu Li
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Peking University;National University of Singapore;Amazon;Amazon;University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;Senior Applied Scientist;Researcher;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Peking University;National University of Singapore;Amazon

roast.laymen_summary: We built a robot that pokes every object in a room like a curious toddler to create a 'map of possibilities.' It uses a massive AI brain to narrate what it thinks will happen if it pulls a handle or squishes a sponge.
why_bad: The 'Action-Conditioned Scene Graph' is largely a rebranding of existing spatial mapping, offering more jargon than actual technical novelty.
The system relies heavily on Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) that are prone to 'hallucinating' physical properties, leading to robots that try to open things that don't have hinges.
Experimental results were conducted in highly controlled environments, failing to prove the system can survive the chaotic mess of a real-world kitchen.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Robotics Edition.' Our chef, RoboEXP, spends four hours 'interactively exploring' a single onion, documenting its structural integrity and potential for sautéing in a 50-page manifesto. It’s high-concept culinary theater where the chef describes the meal perfectly but forgets to actually turn on the stove. By the time the 'Action-Conditioned' soup is ready, the customers have died of old age, and the 'Explicit Memory' system has spent more on GPU bills than the restaurant made in a year.

title: FlowBotHD: History-Aware Diffuser Handling Ambiguities in Articulated Objects Manipulation
abstract: We introduce a novel approach to manipulate articulated objects with ambiguities, such as opening a door, in which multi-modality and occlusions create ambiguities about the opening side and direction. Multi-modality occurs when the method to open a fully closed door (push, pull, slide) is uncertain, or the side from which it should be opened is uncertain.  Occlusions further obscure the door’s shape from certain angles, creating further ambiguities during the occlusion. To tackle these challenges, we propose a history-aware diffusion network that models the multi-modal distribution of the articulated object and uses history to disambiguate actions and make stable predictions under occlusions. Experiments and analysis demonstrate the state-of-art performance of our method and specifically improvements in ambiguity-caused failure modes. Our project website is available at https://flowbothd.github.io/.
author: Yishu Li;Wen Hui Leng;Yiming Fang;Ben Eisner;David Held
aff: Tsinghua University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University
position: Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;PhD student;Associate Professor
