title: VIRL: Self-Supervised Visual Graph Inverse Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Learning dense reward functions from unlabeled videos for reinforcement learning exhibits scalability due to the vast diversity and quantity of video resources. Recent works use visual features or graph abstractions in videos to measure task progress as rewards, which either deteriorate in unseen domains or capture spatial information while overlooking visual details. We propose $\textbf{V}$isual-Graph $\textbf{I}$nverse $\textbf{R}$einforcement $\textbf{L}$earning (VIRL), a self-supervised method that synergizes low-level visual features and high-level graph abstractions from frames to graph representations for reward learning. VIRL utilizes a visual encoder that extracts object-wise features for graph nodes and a graph encoder that derives properties from graphs constructed from detected objects in each frame. The encoded representations are enforced to align videos temporally and reconstruct in-scene objects. The pretrained visual graph encoder is then utilized to construct a dense reward function for policy learning by measuring latent distances between current frames and the goal frame. Our empirical evaluation on the X-MAGICAL and Robot Visual Pusher benchmark demonstrates that VIRL effectively handles tasks necessitating both granular visual attention and broader global feature consideration, and exhibits robust generalization to $\textit{extrapolation}$ tasks and domains not seen in demonstrations. Our policy for the robotic task also achieves the highest success rate in real-world robot experiments.
keywords: Inverse Reinforcement Learning;Learning from Video;Graph Network
author: Lei Huang;Weijia Cai;Zihan Zhu;Chen Feng;Helge Rhodin;Zhengbo Zou
aff: University of British Columbia;University of British Columbia;;New York University;;Columbia University
aff_unique_norm: University of British Columbia;New York University;Columbia University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach robots how to perform tasks by having them watch videos and build complex mental maps of how objects relate to each other. In reality, they built a very expensive 'spot the difference' engine that struggles to understand the actual physics of the tasks it's watching.
why_bad: The 'self-supervised' claim is a stretch because the system relies heavily on pre-trained object detectors, essentially smuggling in human knowledge through the back door.
Using latent distances as a reward function is notoriously brittle; the robot often prioritizes looking like it’s succeeding over actually accomplishing the goal.
The complexity of the dual-encoder graph architecture offers marginal gains over simpler methods, suggesting the authors brought a bazooka to a knife fight and still missed the target.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: AI Edition.' Our contestant is trying to win a Michelin star by watching silent, grainy CCTV footage of a chef making a soufflé. Instead of learning to cook, he’s meticulously mapping the X-Y coordinates of every copper pot and the trajectory of the whisk. He thinks that if he matches the 'vibe' of the kitchen layout and keeps his elbows at the same angle as the pro, a cake will magically appear. He’s spent millions on a 3D-mapped kitchen grid, but he still doesn't realize the oven needs to be turned on. It’s a visual masterpiece of raw eggs and confused ambition.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with the cold precision of a rejected NeurIPS reviewer.

title: Scaling Safe Multi-Agent Control for Signal Temporal Logic Specifications
abstract: Existing methods for safe multi-agent control using logic specifications like Signal Temporal Logic (STL) often face scalability issues. This is because they rely either on single-agent perspectives or on Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP)-based planners, which are complex to optimize. These methods have proven to be computationally expensive and inefficient when dealing with a large number of agents. To address these limitations, we present a new scalable approach to multi-agent control in this setting. Our method treats the relationships between agents using a graph structure rather than in terms of a single-agent perspective. Moreover, it combines a multi-agent collision avoidance controller with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) based planner, models the system in a decentralized fashion, and trains on STL-based objectives to generate safe and efficient plans for multiple agents, thereby optimizing the satisfaction of complex temporal specifications while also facilitating multi-agent collision avoidance. Our experiments show that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods that use a state-of-the-art MILP-based planner in terms of scalability and performance.
keywords: Multi-Robot Systems;Path Planning for Multiple Mobile Robots or Agents;Collision Avoidance;Hybrid Logical/Dynamical Planning and Verification;Deep Learning Methods
author: Joe Eappen;Zikang Xiong;Dipam Patel;Aniket Bera;Suresh Jagannathan
aff: Purdue University;Purdue University;Purdue University;University of Maryland, College Park;
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Adjunct Associate Professor;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach a swarm of robots how to follow complex, time-sensitive rules without crashing into each other, a task that usually makes computers move at the speed of a dial-up modem. They swapped out rigorous math for a 'best guess' neural network to speed things up, but they basically sacrificed the robots' ability to actually guarantee they won't mess up.
why_bad: The authors compared their 'fast and loose' neural network to a 'slow and perfect' math solver, which is like bragging that a bicycle is better than a freight train because it's easier to park.
Signal Temporal Logic is built on mathematical guarantees, but by using a GNN, the authors turned those 'ironclad rules' into 'vague suggestions' that the robots might ignore if the math gets hard.

The experiments likely cherry-picked simple scenarios where 'not hitting each other' was the only real challenge, ignoring the complex temporal constraints that make the problem worth solving in the first place.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're pitching an app that coordinates a 100-person brunch where everyone has hyper-specific allergies and 'Signal Temporal' seating requirements—like 'I must sit near the window, but only after Dave arrives and before the mimosas run out.' The old way involved a head waiter spending three hours solving a giant logic puzzle while the eggs got cold. Your 'disruptive' solution? Fire the waiter, give everyone a walkie-talkie, and tell them to 'vibe it out' using a decentralized social graph. Sure, everyone sits down in thirty seconds, but the gluten-intolerant guy is eating a bagel in the bathroom and the mimosas never actually arrived. You’ve scaled the seating process, but you’ve fundamentally failed the brunch.

title: Learning H-Infinity Locomotion Control
abstract: Stable locomotion in precipitous environments is an essential task for quadruped robots, requiring the ability to resist various external disturbances. Recent neural policies enhance robustness against disturbances by learning to resist external forces sampled from a fixed distribution in the simulated environment. However, the force generation process doesn’t consider the robot’s current state, making it difficult to identify the most effective direction and magnitude that can push the robot to the most unstable but recoverable state. Thus, challenging cases in the buffer are insufficient to optimize robustness. In this paper, we propose to model the robust locomotion learning process as an adversarial interaction between the locomotion policy and a learnable disturbance that is conditioned on the robot state to generate appropriate external forces. To make the joint optimization stable, our novel $H_{\infty}$ constraint mandates the bound of the ratio between the cost and the intensity of the external forces. We verify the robustness of our approach in both simulated environments and real-world deployment, on quadrupedal locomotion tasks and a more challenging task where the quadruped performs locomotion merely on hind legs. Training and deployment code will be made public.
author: Junfeng Long;Wenye Yu;Quanyi Li;ZiRui Wang;Dahua Lin;Jiangmiao Pang
aff: Shanghai AI Laboratory;Shanghai Jiaotong University;University of Edinburgh;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Shanghai AI Laboratory ;Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory
aff_unique_norm: Shanghai AI Laboratory;Shanghai Jiao Tong University;University of Edinburgh;Chinese University of Hong Kong;Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of letting a robot dog wander around getting randomly bumped, the authors built a 'bully AI' designed to find the robot's weakest spots and shove it as hard as possible. To keep this digital hazing from becoming a total disaster, they used a math-heavy 'speed limit' called H-Infinity to ensure the bully doesn't push harder than the robot can reasonably learn to handle.
why_bad: The 'bully' AI is basically just an adversarial reinforcement learning setup, a concept so well-trodden it has its own retirement home in AI research.

Using H-Infinity as a constraint on a neural network sounds fancy, but without a rigorous mathematical proof, it's basically just 'math-washing' a heuristic that could have been a simple clipping function.
The 'hind leg' walking task is a flashy gimmick that doesn't actually prove the robustness of the control logic compared to existing, less-complex baselines.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Overzealous Prosecutor
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the Defendant (our robot dog) is trying to prove it's a 'Good Boy' who can stay upright under pressure. The Prosecution (the learnable disturbance) isn't just presenting evidence; they are a shapeshifting legal genius specifically hunting for the exact sentence that will make the Defendant crumble. Enter the Judge (the H-Infinity constraint), who constantly bangs the gavel yelling, 'Objection! You can't harass the witness into a total nervous breakdown!' The defense argues this high-stakes drama makes the Defendant stronger, but the Jury (the reviewers) is unimpressed. They’ve seen this 'adversarial' legal strategy in every episode of Law & Order: RL, and they're pretty sure the Judge's new 'H-Infinity' rule is just a fancy way of telling the lawyers to stop shouting so loud.

title: Gameplay Filters: Robust Zero-Shot Safety through Adversarial Imagination
abstract: Despite the impressive recent advances in learning-based robot control, ensuring robustness to out-of-distribution conditions remains an open challenge. Safety filters can, in principle, keep arbitrary control policies from incurring catastrophic failures by overriding unsafe actions, but existing solutions for complex (e.g., legged) robot dynamics do not span the full motion envelope and instead rely on local, reduced-order models. These filters tend to overly restrict agility and can still fail when perturbed away from nominal conditions. This paper presents the gameplay filter, a new class of predictive safety filter that continually plays out hypothetical matches between its simulation-trained safety strategy and a virtual adversary co-trained to invoke worst-case events and sim-to-real error, and precludes actions that would cause failures down the line. We demonstrate the scalability and robustness of the approach with a first-of-its-kind full-order safety filter for (36-D) quadrupedal dynamics. Physical experiments on two different quadruped platforms demonstrate the superior zero-shot effectiveness of the gameplay filter under large perturbations such as tugging and unmodeled terrain. Experiment videos and open-source software are available online: https://saferobotics.org/research/gameplay-filter
author: Duy Phuong Nguyen;Kai-Chieh Hsu;Wenhao Yu;Jie Tan;Jaime Fernández Fisac
aff: Princeton University;Princeton University;Google;Google;Princeton University
position: PhD student;PhD student;Software Engineer;Research Scientist;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a 'safety filter' that makes a robot play a mental game of chess against an imaginary bully trying to trip it in real-time. By constantly simulating the worst-case scenario, the robot theoretically avoids any move that could lead to it ending up as a pile of expensive scrap metal.
why_bad: The 'adversarial imagination' is often too pessimistic, turning a high-performance quadruped into a timid toddler that refuses to move because it's over-analyzing every hypothetical pebble.
Running full 36-dimensional physics simulations in a loop is computationally suicidal; the robot spends more energy 'thinking' about falling than it does actually walking.

The 'zero-shot' effectiveness is mostly a mirage because the virtual adversary is only as creative as the programmers who built it, meaning it fails spectacularly when faced with real-world physics it didn't rehearse for.
roast.analogy_description: We’re disrupting the locomotion space by giving your hardware a perpetual existential crisis. Imagine an Uber driver who refuses to turn left because they’ve simulated 40,000 scenarios where a meteor hits the car at that exact junction—that’s the Gameplay Filter. We’ve successfully replaced 'fluid movement' with 'analysis paralysis,' ensuring your $70,000 robot stays perfectly safe by essentially staying in bed. It’s not a bug, it’s a high-fidelity avoidance of reality. Pivot your career into 'Aggressive Loitering' today!

title: A3VLM: Actionable Articulation-Aware Vision Language Model
abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) for robotics have received significant attention in recent years. As a VLM can understand robot observations and perform complex visual reasoning, it is regarded as a potential universal solution for general robotics challenges such as manipulation and navigation. However, previous robotics VLMs such as RT-1, RT-2, and ManipLLM have focused on directly learning robot actions. Such approaches require collecting a significant amount of robot interaction data, which is extremely costly in the real world. Thus, we propose A3VLM, an object-centric, actionable, articulation-aware vision language model. A3VLM focuses on the articulation structure and action affordances of objects. Its representation is robot-agnostic and can be translated into robot actions using simple action primitives. Extensive experiments in both simulation benchmarks and real-world settings demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of A3VLM.
author: Siyuan Huang;Haonan Chang;Yuhan Liu;Yimeng Zhu;Hao Dong;Abdeslam Boularias;Peng Gao;Hongsheng Li
aff: Shanghai Jiaotong University;Rutgers, New Brunswick;Rutgers University;Yuandao AI;Peking University;, Rutgers University;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;shanghai ai lab
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Researcher;Assistant Professor;Associate Professor;Associate Professor;Researcher
aff_unique_norm: Shanghai Jiao Tong University;Rutgers University;Yuandao AI;Peking University;Chinese University of Hong Kong;Shanghai AI Lab

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot brain that studies how objects move—like knowing a drawer slides out—instead of practicing the actual physical movements. They hoped that by understanding the 'vibes' of a handle or a hinge, any robot could magically finish the job without specific training.
why_bad: The 'action primitives' are a massive hand-wave that ignores the grueling reality of contact physics and torque control.
Being 'robot-agnostic' is just a fancy way of saying the model doesn't actually know how to move a specific arm in the real world.
The experimental results likely relied on 'cherry-picked' simulation successes that would crumble the moment a real robot encountered a sticky door hinge.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show contestant who refuses to actually touch a stove. Instead, they spend the whole episode pointing at a picture of a whisk and explaining, 'This is a mechanical agitation device designed for fluid aeration.' When the judges ask for a soufflé, the contestant hands them the whisk's instruction manual and says the meal is 'chef-agnostic.' Sure, they technically explained how the tools work, but the kitchen is still empty, the judges are starving, and the 'chef' is getting sent home for failing to actually cook the damn food.

title: Dreaming to Assist: Learning to Align with Human Objectives for Shared Control in High-Speed Racing
abstract: Tight coordination is required for effective human-robot teams in domains involving fast dynamics and tactical decisions, such as multi-car racing.  In such settings, robot teammates must react to cues of a human teammate's tactical objective to assist in a way that is consistent with the objective (e.g., navigating left or right around an obstacle). To address this challenge, we present _Dream2Assist_,  a framework that combines a rich world model able to infer human objectives and value functions, and an assistive agent that provides appropriate expert assistance to a given human teammate. Our approach builds on a recurrent state space model to explicitly infer human intents, enabling the assistive agent to select actions that align with the human and enabling a fluid teaming interaction. We demonstrate our approach in a high-speed racing domain with a population of synthetic human drivers pursuing mutually exclusive objectives, such as "stay-behind" and "overtake". We show that the combined human-robot team, when blending its actions with those of the human, outperforms synthetic humans alone and several baseline assistance strategies, and that intent-conditioning enables adherence to human preferences during task execution, leading to improved performance while satisfying the human's objective.
author: Jonathan DeCastro;Andrew Silva;Deepak Gopinath;Emily Sumner;Thomas Matrai Balch;Laporsha Dees;Guy Rosman
aff: Toyota Research Institute;Toyota Research Institute;;;Toyota Research Institute;;Toyota Research Institute

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built an AI co-pilot that tries to read your mind during a high-speed race to help you steer better. Unfortunately, they only tested it on predictable computer scripts instead of actual, chaotic humans, making the 'mind-reading' part fairly pointless.
why_bad: The 'human' subjects were actually just simple algorithms, meaning the AI was essentially 'aligning' with a calculator rather than a person.
Using a complex world model to decide whether to turn left or right is the computational equivalent of using a chainsaw to cut a grape.
The paper fails to prove the AI is actually collaborating; it’s more likely the AI is just overriding the driver and playing the game by itself.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Psychic Carjacker
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, the defendant claims this AI is a 'supportive partner,' but the evidence suggests a hostile takeover. Your Honor, the system spent its entire development phase 'learning' the intentions of a cardboard cutout, yet it now claims to be an expert on the human soul. When the AI 'assists' by forcing the steering wheel to the left, it isn't honoring a partnership—it's committing a carjacking under the guise of intuition. I move to strike this 'synthetic' data from the record; you cannot claim to understand the driver if you've never actually met a person who wasn't made of lines of code.
roast.tone_check: Dry, snarky, and scientifically accurate.

title: EscIRL: Evolving Self-Contrastive IRL for Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving
abstract: While deep neural networks (DNN) and inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) have both been commonly used in autonomous driving to predict trajectories through learning from expert demonstrations, DNN-based methods suffer from data-scarcity, while IRL-based approaches often struggle with generalizability, making both hard to apply to new driving scenarios. To address these issues, we introduce EscIRL, a novel decoupled bi-level training framework that iteratively learns robust reward models from only a few mixed-scenario demonstrations. At the inner level, EscIRL introduces a self-contrastive IRL module that learns a spectrum of specialized reward functions by contrasting demonstrations across different scenarios. At the outer level, ESCIRL employs an evolving loop that iteratively refines the contrastive sets, ensuring global convergence. Experiments on two multi-scenario datasets, CitySim and INTERACTION, demonstrate the effectiveness of EscIRL, outperforming state-of-the-art DNN and IRL-based methods by 41.3% on average. Notably, we show that EscIRL achieves superior generalizability compared to DNN-based approaches while requiring only a small fraction of the data, effectively addressing data-scarcity constraints. All code and data are available at https://github.com/SiyueWang-CiDi/EscIRL.
author: Siyue Wang;Zhaorun Chen;Zhuokai Zhao;Chaoli Mao;Yiyang Zhou;Jiayu He;Albert Sibo Hu
aff: CIDI Intelligent Driving(Chengdu) Technology Co., Ltd.;University of Chicago;University of Chicago;CIDI;Xi'an Jiaotong University;cidi-lab;CiDi
aff_unique_norm: CIDI Intelligent Driving Technology Co., Ltd.;University of Chicago;Center for International Development and Integration;Xi'an Jiao Tong University;cidi-lab;CiDi

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach self-driving cars to predict traffic by having the AI compare a few driving videos and highlight the differences, rather than feeding it the entire internet. It’s essentially an attempt to build a master driver that learns from snippets of 'good' and 'bad' driving instead of memorizing every road on Earth.
why_bad: The 'bi-level evolving loop' is a hyperparameter nightmare that likely requires more manual tuning than the manual data labeling it claims to replace.
A 41.3% performance leap over SOTA on INTERACTION usually indicates the baselines were either poorly optimized or the 'evolving loop' inadvertently overfit to the test scenarios.
The 'self-contrastive' mechanism lacks a theoretical guarantee, making the 'global convergence' claim feel more like optimistic branding than mathematical reality.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a scout trying to predict exactly how shoppers move through a grocery store. Instead of watching thousands of hours of CCTV, our scout watches three people for five minutes and then spends the rest of the day staring in a mirror, comparing how a person buys milk versus how they buy kale. He calls this 'evolving self-contrastive insights.' When he predicts the next shopper will moonwalk to the frozen peas with 41% better accuracy, the store manager points out he's just hallucinating patterns because he's too cheap to buy more footage. He’s technically 'learning,' but if a toddler enters the store, his 'global convergence' model is going to result in a multi-cart pileup in Aisle 4.

title: Transferable Tactile Transformers for Representation Learning Across Diverse Sensors and Tasks
abstract: This paper presents T3: Transferable Tactile Transformers, a framework for tactile representation learning that scales across multi-sensors and multi-tasks.T3 is designed to overcome the contemporary issue that camera-based tactile sensing is extremely heterogeneous, i.e. sensors are built into different form factors, and existing datasets were collected for disparate tasks. T3 captures the shared latent information across different sensor-task pairings by constructing a shared trunk transformer with sensor-specific encoders and task-specific decoders. The pre-training of T3utilizes a novel Foundation Tactile (FoTa) dataset, which is aggregated from several open-sourced datasets and it contains over 3 million data points gathered from 13 sensors and 11 tasks. FoTa is the largest and most diverse dataset in tactile sensing to date and it is made publicly available in a unified format. Across various sensors and tasks, experiments show that T3 pre-trained with FoTa achieved zero-shot transferability in certain sensor-task pairings, can be further fine-tuned with small amounts of domain-specific data, and its performance scales with bigger network sizes. T3 is also effective as a tactile encoder for long horizon contact-rich manipulation. Results from sub-millimeter multi-pin electronics insertion tasks show that T3 achieved a task success rate 25% higher than that of policies trained with tactile encoders trained from scratch, or 53% higher than without tactile sensing. Data, code, and model checkpoints are open-sourced at https://t3.alanz.info.
keywords: Tactile Sensing;Representation Learning;Heterogeneous Learning;Robot Manipulation;Robot Learning
author: Jialiang Zhao;Yuxiang Ma;Lirui Wang;Edward Adelson
aff: Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'universal translator' for robot touch sensors so different robotic fingers could share the same brain. They mashed together 13 different types of sensor data into one giant model, hoping it would magically understand how to feel things it's never touched before.
why_bad: The 'Zero-Shot' claims are doing a lot of heavy lifting for a model that only works on 'certain' sensor pairings, which is academic speak for 'it mostly failed on the hard stuff.'
The architecture is essentially a standard Transformer with different hats on; it doesn't actually solve the fundamental physics problem of sensors having wildly different shapes and sensitivities.
Aggregating 11 old datasets into one 'new' foundation dataset is less of a scientific breakthrough and more of a data-hoarding exercise that lacks original, high-quality tactile supervision.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a universal remote that promises to control every device in your house, from your Tesla to your toaster. You get it home and realize it only works on three specific brands of VCR, and even then, the 'Play' button accidentally triggers your sprinklers 47% of the time. Sure, it’s 'technically' better than having no remote at all, and they’ve collected a massive database of infrared codes from the local dump, but you’re still basically just poking buttons in the dark and hoping the TV doesn't explode.

title: Bimanual Dexterity for Complex Tasks
abstract: To train generalist robot policies, machine learning methods often require a substantial amount of expert human teleoperation data. An ideal robot for humans collecting data is one that closely mimics them: bimanual arms and dexterous hands. However, creating such a bimanual teleoperation system with over 50 DoF is a significant challenge.  To address this, we introduce Bidex, an extremely dexterous, low-cost, low-latency and portable bimanual dexterous teleoperation system which relies on motion capture gloves and teacher arms.   We compare Bidex to a Vision Pro teleoperation system and a SteamVR system and find Bidex to produce better quality data for more complex tasks at a faster rate. Additionally, we show Bidex operating a mobile bimanual robot for in the wild tasks. Please refer to https://bidex-teleop.github.io for video results and instructions to recreate Bidex.  The robot hands (5k USD) and teleoperation system (7k USD) is readily reproducible and can be used on many robot arms including two xArms ($16k USD).
author: Kenneth Shaw;Yulong Li;Jiahui Yang;Mohan Kumar Srirama;Ray Liu;Haoyu Xiong;Russell Mendonca;Deepak Pathak
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University;;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University
position: PhD student;MS student;;Researcher;;MS student;PhD student;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a $28,000 high-tech puppet suit so humans can remotely control a robot with 50 moving parts, essentially playing the world's most expensive game of 'Operation.' They claim it's better than using a VR headset, which is like saying a custom-tuned racing rig is better for driving than a Nintendo Wii wheel.
why_bad: The 'low-cost' claim is a total reach; calling a $28,000 hardware stack 'affordable' is academic gaslighting for any lab without a DARPA-sized budget.
It’s a 'system paper' that lacks algorithmic meat, functioning more like a very expensive IKEA assembly guide than a scientific breakthrough.
The evaluation is a classic 'straw man' setup, comparing their specialized motion-capture rig to off-the-shelf gaming hardware like SteamVR to make their results look artificially impressive.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a Silicon Valley startup pitching a 'revolutionary bimanual dining interface' that costs $30,000 and requires you to wear haptic gloves just to eat ramen. It’s 'disruptive' because it’s slightly more precise than using a pair of $1 plastic forks, but it ignores the fact that the real goal was supposed to be teaching the robot how to eat by itself. We’re not disruptors; we’re just building the world’s most over-engineered marionette because we can’t figure out how to give the puppet a brain of its own. It’s peak 'VC-funded solution in search of a problem' energy.

title: LeLaN: Learning A Language-Conditioned Navigation Policy from In-the-Wild Video
abstract: We present our method, LeLaN, which uses action-free egocentric data to learn robust language-conditioned object navigation. By leveraging the knowledge of large vision and language models and grounding this knowledge using pre-trained segmentation and depth estimation models, we can label in-the-wild data from a variety of indoor and outdoor environments with diverse instructions that capture a range of objects with varied granularity and noise in their descriptions. Leveraging this method to label over 50 hours of data collected in indoor and outdoor environments, including robot observations, YouTube video tours, and human-collected walking data allows us to train a policy that can outperform state-of-the-art methods on the zero-shot object navigation task in both success rate and precision.
author: Noriaki Hirose;Catherine Glossop;Ajay Sridhar;Oier Mees;Sergey Levine
aff: Toyota Central R&D Labs., Inc;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department, University of California, Berkeley;Google
aff_unique_norm: Toyota Central R&D Labs., Inc;University of California, Berkeley;Google

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots how to find objects by making them binge-watch thousands of hours of YouTube house tours and random GoPro footage. They used other AI models to automatically guess what was in the videos, hoping the robot would magically learn to navigate your living room without ever being told how to actually move its wheels.
why_bad: The 'action-free' data problem: Watching a video of a human walking through a house doesn't teach a robot physics; it's like trying to learn surgery by watching 'Grey's Anatomy'—you see the organs, but you have no idea how to hold the scalpel.

The 'hallucination loop': Using VLMs to label 'in-the-wild' data means they're training a model on the noisy, often incorrect guesses of another model, essentially creating a massive game of AI 'Telephone' where a trash can eventually gets labeled as a 'designer ottoman.'

Zero-shot skepticism: The paper claims to beat the state-of-the-art without specific training, but reviewers suspect the 'robustness' is just the model memorizing what a generic IKEA-furnished apartment looks like rather than actually understanding spatial navigation.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You want to train the world's best Uber driver, but gas is expensive and driving is hard. Our disruptor solution? We make a teenager watch 50 hours of 'Grand Theft Auto' gameplay and 'Van Life' vlogs on 2x speed. We don't give them a steering wheel; we just show them pictures of a steering wheel and ask ChatGPT to describe the 'vibe' of a left turn. We’re pitching this as 'Zero-Shot Commuting.' Sure, the car is currently upside down in a Starbucks drive-thru, but look at how accurately it identified that the latte was 'granularity-diverse' before impact. We're not crashing; we're just navigating with 'creative noise'!

title: Multi-Transmotion: Pre-trained Model for Human Motion Prediction
abstract: The ability of intelligent systems to predict human behaviors is essential, particularly in fields such as autonomous vehicle navigation and social robotics. However, the intricacies of human motion have precluded the development of a standardized dataset and model for human motion prediction, thereby hindering the establishment of pre-trained models. In this paper, we address these limitations by integrating multiple datasets, encompassing both trajectory and 3D pose keypoints, to further propose a pre-trained model for human motion prediction. We merge seven distinct datasets across varying modalities and standardize their formats. To facilitate multimodal pre-training, we introduce Multi-Transmotion, an innovative transformer-based model capable of cross-modality pre-training. Additionally, we devise a novel masking strategy to learn rich representations. Our methodology demonstrates competitive performance across various datasets on several downstream tasks, including trajectory prediction in the NBA and JTA datasets, as well as pose prediction in the AMASS and 3DPW datasets. The code will be made available upon publication.
author: Yang Gao;Po-Chien Luan;Alexandre Alahi
aff: EPFL - EPF Lausanne;EPFL - EPF Lausanne;EPFL

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'universal brain' for human movement by smashing together wildly different data, like 3D body scans and dots moving on a basketball court. They hoped that by feeding this chaotic salad into a standard AI model, it would magically learn to predict exactly where you’re walking or how you’re dancing.
why_bad: The 'Input Identity Crisis': Mixing 3D skeletal poses with 2D GPS trajectories is like trying to teach a student biology and geometry at the same time using the same textbook—the physics and scales simply don't translate.
The 'Participation Trophy' Results: Claiming 'competitive performance' is academic speak for 'we spent six months and $10,000 in cloud credits to be slightly worse than existing specialized models.'
Lack of Architectural Soul: The 'innovative' masking strategy is essentially a retread of techniques used in language models since 2018, offering nothing truly new to the field of spatial dynamics.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a chef on a reality show trying to create a 'Universal Cuisine.' He takes a delicate Japanese sashimi, a greasy Chicago deep-dish pizza, and a bowl of spicy Thai curry, throws them all into a high-speed industrial blender, and serves the resulting grey sludge in a wine glass. He calls it 'Multi-Taste Fusion' and claims it's the future of dining because it contains all the food groups. The judges are horrified because, while it technically contains 'everything,' it has lost the texture, flavor, and purpose of the individual ingredients. It’s an over-engineered smoothie that no one asked for and doesn't taste as good as a simple slice of pizza.

title: Guided Reinforcement Learning for Robust Multi-Contact Loco-Manipulation
abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown remarkable proficiency in developing robust control policies for contact-rich applications. However, it typically requires meticulous Markov Decision Process (MDP) designing tailored to each task and robotic platform. This work addresses this challenge by creating a systematic approach to behavior synthesis and control for multi-contact loco-manipulation.
We define a task-independent MDP formulation to learn robust RL policies using a single demonstration (per task) generated from a fast model-based trajectory optimization method. Our framework is validated on diverse real-world tasks, such as navigating spring-loaded doors and manipulating heavy dishwashers. The learned behaviors can handle dynamic uncertainties and external disturbances, showcasing recovery maneuvers, such as re-grasping objects during execution. Finally, we successfully transfer the policies to a real robot, demonstrating the approach's practical viability.
author: Jean Pierre Sleiman;Mayank Mittal;Marco Hutter
aff: ETHZ - ETH Zurich;NVIDIA;ETHZ - ETH Zurich

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot how to walk and move heavy stuff at the same time by giving it exactly one 'how-to' video to copy. While it managed to open a dishwasher without exploding, it’s basically just a very expensive toddler following a very specific set of instructions.
why_bad: The 'task-independent' claim is a stretch; the model relies so heavily on a pre-computed demo that it's more like a fancy copycat than a truly flexible brain.
The 'robustness' is questionable because if the initial model-based guess is slightly off, the reinforcement learning agent just inherits the errors and fails with more confidence.
Real-world transfer results are 'anecdotal' at best, featuring a suspiciously low number of trials that suggest the robot might fail 9 out of 10 times off-camera.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a driver who has never seen a road before, so you give them a single recorded GPS track of a professional racer driving to a grocery store. Our driver is 'robust' because if they hit a pothole, they can technically steer back onto that one specific line, but if you ask them to go to a different store or—God forbid—the road is slightly wet, they’re toast. They aren't 'learning to drive'; they’re just aggressively tracing a drawing someone else made, and the reviewers aren't buying that this makes them the next Lewis Hamilton of dishwashers.

title: Learning to Look: Seeking Information for Decision Making via Policy Factorization
abstract: Many robot manipulation tasks require active or interactive exploration behavior in order to be performed successfully. Such tasks are ubiquitous in embodied domains, where agents must actively search for the information necessary for each stage of a task, e.g., moving the head of the robot to find information relevant to manipulation, or in multi-robot domains, where one scout robot may search for the information that another robot needs to make informed decisions. We identify these tasks with a new type of problem, factorized Contextual Markov Decision Processes, and propose DISaM, a dual-policy solution composed of an information-seeking policy that explores the environment to find the relevant contextual information and an information-receiving policy that exploits the context to achieve the manipulation goal. This factorization allows us to train both policies separately, using the information-receiving one to provide reward to train the information-seeking policy. At test time, the dual agent balances exploration and exploitation based on the uncertainty the manipulation policy has on what the next best action is. We demonstrate the capabilities of our dual policy solution in five manipulation tasks that require information-seeking behaviors, both in simulation and in the real-world, where DISaM significantly outperforms existing methods. More information at https://robin-lab.cs.utexas.edu/learning2look/.
author: Shivin Dass;Jiaheng Hu;Ben Abbatematteo;Peter Stone;Roberto Martín-Martín
aff: University of Texas at Austin;University of Texas, Austin;University of Texas at Austin;Brown University;University of Texas at Austin
position: PhD student;Full Professor;Assistant Professor;PhD student;PhD student
aff_unique_norm: University of Texas at Austin;Brown University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to fix clumsy robots by splitting their brains into two parts: one scout to find necessary information and one worker to actually do the job. The worker rewards the scout for finding the right clues, theoretically creating a 'look before you leap' mentality for machines.
why_bad: The 'Factorized Contextual MDP' is mostly just a rebranding of hierarchical reinforcement learning with a fancier, more expensive coat of paint.
The uncertainty-based switching mechanism is notoriously brittle, often causing the robot to enter a 'paralysis by analysis' loop when sensors get slightly noisy.
The separation of policies assumes the worker can accurately judge the scout's performance, which is like asking a blind man to grade a photography contest.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Robotics Edition.' We’ve got two chefs. One is the 'Searcher' who can only find ingredients but can’t cook a grilled cheese to save his life. The other is the 'Executioner' who is a Michelin-star chef but is literally blindfolded and can’t move his feet. The paper argues that as long as the blind guy shouts how 'uncertain' he feels about the location of the salt, the Searcher will eventually point a flashlight at it. In reality, the Searcher spends twenty minutes staring at a whisk while the Executioner stands perfectly still because his 'uncertainty' hasn't reached the magic threshold to tell the other guy to move the light. It’s a brilliant way to spend $50,000 on hardware to make a bowl of cereal.

title: DriveVLM: The Convergence of Autonomous Driving and Large Vision-Language Models
abstract: A primary hurdle of autonomous driving in urban environments is understanding complex and long-tail scenarios, such as challenging road conditions and delicate human behaviors. We introduce DriveVLM, an autonomous driving system leveraging Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for enhanced scene understanding and planning capabilities. DriveVLM integrates a unique combination of reasoning modules for scene description, scene analysis, and hierarchical planning. Furthermore, recognizing the limitations of VLMs in spatial reasoning and heavy computational requirements, we propose DriveVLM-Dual, a hybrid system that synergizes the strengths of DriveVLM with the traditional autonomous driving pipeline. Experiments on both the nuScenes dataset and our SUP-AD dataset demonstrate the efficacy of DriveVLM and DriveVLM-Dual in handling complex and unpredictable driving conditions. Finally, we deploy the DriveVLM-Dual on a production vehicle, verifying it is effective in real-world autonomous driving environments.
author: Xiaoyu Tian;Junru Gu;Bailin Li;Yicheng Liu;Yang Wang;Zhiyong Zhao;Kun Zhan;Peng Jia;XianPeng Lang;Hang Zhao
aff: IIIS, Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Li Auto;Tsinghua University;LiAuto;LiAuto;Li Auto;LiAuto;Tsinghua University;Li Auto
position: PhD student;PhD student;Software Engineer;PhD student;AI Architect;Engneering;Researcher;Vice President;Assistant Professor;Researcher

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to give self-driving cars a massive 'brain' that can chat about the road like a human, but it turned out to be too slow and spatially confused to actually drive. To fix it, they essentially duct-taped a regular computer back onto the 'smart' brain so the car wouldn't crash while the AI was busy writing an essay about a stop sign.
why_bad: The 'Vision-Language Model' has the reflexes of a tectonic plate; by the time it finishes describing a pedestrian, that pedestrian is already part of the grill.
VLMs are notoriously 'math-blind' and struggle with depth, meaning the car can identify a truck perfectly but has no idea if it's ten feet away or on a different continent.
The computational cost is absurd—you shouldn't need a literal server farm in your trunk just to navigate a suburban cul-de-sac without hitting a mailbox.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Chatty Chauffeur vs. The Laws of Physics
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom drama where the defendant is an Uber driver who, instead of hitting the brakes, spent five seconds describing the 'vibrant crimson hue and octagonal majesty' of a stop sign to his passengers. The Prosecution points out that while his 'scene understanding' is poetic, he is currently sitting in the middle of a pile-up. The Defense argues they’ve solved the problem with a 'Dual System'—which is just a polite way of saying they hired a second, much faster driver to sit in the passenger seat and scream 'BRAKE!' whenever the first one gets too lost in thought. The Judge (the reviewer) is unimpressed, noting that the 'smart' driver is basically just a very expensive, very slow narrator who adds nothing but overhead to a job that requires millisecond reflexes, not a literary critique of traffic patterns.

title: JointMotion: Joint Self-Supervision for Joint Motion Prediction
abstract: We present JointMotion, a self-supervised pre-training method for joint motion prediction in self-driving vehicles. Our method jointly optimizes a scene-level objective connecting motion and environments, and an instance-level objective to refine learned representations. Scene-level representations are learned via non-contrastive similarity learning of past motion sequences and environment context. At the instance level, we use masked autoencoding to refine multimodal polyline representations. We complement this with an adaptive pre-training decoder that enables JointMotion to generalize across different environment representations, fusion mechanisms, and dataset characteristics. Notably, our method reduces the joint final displacement error of Wayformer, HPTR, and Scene Transformer models by 3%, 8%, and 12%, respectively; and enables transfer learning between the Waymo Open Motion and the Argoverse 2 Motion Forecasting datasets.
keywords: Self-supervised learning;representation learning;multimodal pre-training;motion prediction;data-efficient learning
author: Royden Wagner;Omer Sahin Tas;Marvin Klemp;Carlos Fernandez
aff: Karlsruhe Institute of Technology;FZI Research Center for Information Technology;Karlsruhe Institute of Technology;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie
aff_unique_norm: Karlsruhe Institute of Technology;FZI Research Center for Information Technology;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to teach self-driving cars to predict traffic patterns by making them play a high-tech version of 'fill-in-the-blanks' with map data and car movements. It’s essentially a pre-training bootcamp where the AI stares at traffic footage until it learns the 'vibe' of the road before it’s even allowed to touch a steering wheel.
why_bad: The 'joint' architecture is a linguistic nightmare that masks a lack of theoretical novelty; repeating a word four times in the title doesn't make the math more profound.
A 3% improvement on Wayformer is less of a 'breakthrough' and more of a 'statistical hiccup' that likely wouldn't survive a different random seed or a slightly cloudier day in the simulator.
The 'adaptive decoder' is essentially a fancy band-aid for the fact that the model struggles to generalize between different sensor setups without significant hand-holding.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: We’re disrupting the valet industry. Instead of hiring drivers who know how to park, we hire 'Visionary Path-Feelers' who spend six months looking at Polaroids of parking lots with the corners cut off. We tell them, 'Guess where the curb is, it’s self-supervised learning!' Then, we brag to investors that our guys are 3% less likely to hit a pillar than a distracted teenager. We don't actually teach them to drive—we just teach them to 'manifest' the trajectory of a Tesla. It’s not a failure to park; it’s a 'multimodal representation refinement' of the concrete wall. We're currently seeking $50M in Series A to prove that staring at a map of Pittsburgh helps you navigate a mall parking lot in Dubai. Scaling is a vibe, bro.

title: LiDARGrid: Self-supervised 3D Opacity Grid from LiDAR for Scene Forecasting
abstract: Timely capturing the dense geometry of the surrounding scene with unlabeled LiDAR data is valuable but under-explored for mobile robotic applications. Its value lies in the huge amount of such unlabeled data, enabling self-supervised learning for various downstream tasks. Current dynamic 3D scene reconstruction approaches however heavily rely on data annotations to tackle the moving objects in the scene. In response, we present LiDARGrid, a 3D opacity grid representation instantly derived from LiDAR points, which captures the dense 3D scene and facilitates scene forecasting. Our method features a novel self-supervised neural volume densification procedure based on an autoencoder and differentiable volume rendering. Leveraging this representation, self-supervised scene forecasting can be performed. Our method is trained on NuScenes dataset for autonomous driving, and is evaluated by predicting future point clouds using the scene forecasting. It notably outperforms state-of-the-art methods in point cloud forecasting in all performance metrics. Beyond scene forecasting, our representation excels in supporting additional tasks such as moving region detection and depth completion, as shown by experiments.
keywords: 3D perception;lidar;opacity grid;occupancy grid;neural rendering;self-supervised learning;mobile robot;autonomous driving
author: Chuanyu Pan;Aolin Xu

roast.laymen_summary: We built a system that takes messy laser scans from self-driving cars and uses AI to 'fill in the blanks' and predict where objects will move next without any human help. It's essentially trying to give a car a psychic intuition for 3D space by making it daydream about what the gaps between laser points look like.
why_bad: Derivative Architecture: Reviewers noted that sticking an autoencoder onto differentiable volume rendering is basically just 'NeRF for LiDAR,' which is the research equivalent of putting a spoiler on a Prius and calling it a race car.

Computational Obesity: The 'instant' claims are dubious; the heavy neural volume densification process likely runs at the speed of a tectonic plate, making it useless for a car actually trying to avoid a collision in real-time.

The 'SOTA' Mirage: The paper claims to crush the competition, but critics pointed out that the benchmarks used were either outdated or avoided the newest, more robust baseline models that don't rely on 'opacity grid' gimmicks.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Hallucinating Eyewitness
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense presents 'LiDARGrid' as a star witness who can predict a car crash before it happens. But under cross-examination, it's clear this witness didn't actually see the street—they saw three flickers of light in a dark alley and 'filled in the rest' using their overactive imagination. The prosecution argues that while the witness is certainly creative, calling a hallucinated 3D grid 'evidence' is a slap in the face to physics. Furthermore, this witness takes six hours to answer a 'yes or no' question about a moving truck. Objection, Your Honor: we can't let a self-driving car navigate a school zone based on the 'intuition' of a GPU that's currently overheating trying to render a single curb!

title: Autonomous Improvement of Instruction Following Skills via Foundation Models
abstract: Intelligent robots capable of improving from autonomously collected experience have the potential to transform robot learning: instead of collecting costly teleoperated demonstration data, large-scale deployment of fleets of robots can quickly collect larger quantities of autonomous data useful for training better robot policies. However, autonomous improvement requires solving two key problems: (i) fully automating a scalable data collection procedure that can collect diverse and semantically meaningful robot data and (ii) learning from non-optimal, autonomous data with no human annotations. To this end, we propose a novel approach that addresses these challenges, allowing instruction following policies to improve from autonomously collected data without human supervision. Our framework leverages vision-language models to collect and evaluate semantically meaningful experiences in new environments, and then utilizes a decomposition of instruction following tasks into (semantic) language-conditioned image generation and (non-semantic) goal reaching, which makes it significantly more practical to improve from this autonomously collected data without any human annotations. We carry out extensive experiments in the real world to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, and find that in a suite of unseen environments, the robot policy can be improved significantly with autonomously collected data. We open-source the code for our semantic autonomous improvement pipeline, as well as our autonomous dataset of 25K trajectories collected across five tabletop environments: https://soar-autonomous-improvement.github.io
keywords: Autonomous Improvement;Instruction Following Skills;Scaled Data Collection
author: Zhiyuan Zhou;Pranav Atreya;Abraham Lee;Homer Rich Walke;Oier Mees;Sergey Levine
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department, University of California, Berkeley;Google
position: PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;PhD student;Postdoc;Research Scientist

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to build a robot that learns by playing with its toys alone instead of requiring a human to remote-control it like a high-stakes RC car. They used 'AI brains' to let the robot dream up its own goals and then grade its own homework, hoping it would eventually figure out how to be useful.
why_bad: The 'Self-Correction' Trap: The Vision-Language Model used to grade the robot is often just as hallucination-prone as the robot itself, leading to a 'blind leading the blind' scenario where the bot thinks it succeeded just by being in the same zip code as the goal.

The Dream-to-Reality Gap: Breaking tasks into 'imagine a picture of success' and 'get there' sounds cool until the robot tries to reach for an object that only exists in its grainy, AI-generated imagination.

Quantity Over Quality: While 25,000 trajectories sound impressive on a CV, most of that data is the robotic equivalent of a toddler knocking over a cup of juice—statistically significant but functionally useless for complex tasks.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a rookie quarterback who refuses to listen to his coach and instead decides to learn football by watching grainy TikTok highlights and then practicing alone in his basement. He’s out there throwing the ball into a laundry basket, and every time he hits the rim, his 'internal hype man'—a Vision-Language Model—screams that he's basically the next Patrick Mahomes. He’s racked up 25,000 'reps,' but none of them involved a real defense or even a regulation-sized field. When he finally steps onto the turf, he’s shocked that 'imagining a touchdown' doesn’t stop a 300-pound lineman from pancaking him into the grass. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, let’s see if it pays off—spoiler: he’s still in the basement.

title: Rate-Informed Discovery via Bayesian Adaptive Multifidelity Sampling
abstract: Ensuring the safety of autonomous vehicles (AVs) requires both accurate estimation of their performance and efficient discovery of potential failure cases. This paper introduces Bayesian adaptive multifidelity sampling (BAMS), which leverages the power of adaptive Bayesian sampling to achieve efficient discovery while simultaneously estimating the rate of adverse events. BAMS prioritizes exploration of regions with potentially low performance, leading to the identification of novel and critical scenarios that traditional methods might miss. Using real-world AV data we demonstrate that BAMS discovers 10 times as many issues as Monte Carlo (MC) and importance sampling (IS) baselines, while at the same time generating rate estimates with variances 15 and 6 times narrower than MC and IS baselines respectively.
author: Aman Sinha;Payam Nikdel;Supratik Paul;Shimon Whiteson
aff: Princeton University;Waymo;Waymo;University of Oxford

roast.laymen_summary: This paper claims to have built a high-tech math compass that finds self-driving car glitches much faster than just guessing randomly. It tries to do two things at once: find the worst-case crashes and calculate exactly how often they'll happen without actually driving the car.
why_bad: The authors benchmarked their method against Monte Carlo sampling, which is the academic equivalent of bragging that your Ferrari is faster than a tricycle.
The 'multifidelity' approach assumes low-quality simulations are actually useful, which is like trying to learn brain surgery by playing a game of Operation.
The paper ignores the 'curse of dimensionality,' essentially solving a 2D toy problem and claiming it will work in the chaotic, infinite-dimensional hellscape of a real city intersection.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a chef on a reality show tasked with finding every rotten blueberry in a warehouse. Instead of just looking at the fruit, you build a $50,000 'Bayesian Berry-Scanner' that smells the air and predicts rot based on the humidity of the floor. You brag to the judges that you found ten times more moldy berries than a blindfolded intern, while also providing a 'variance-informed' estimate of the warehouse's total rot. The judges chop you because your machine took six hours to calibrate, cost more than the warehouse, and failed to notice the entire back wall was on fire.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 95% confidence intervals.

title: InstructNav: Zero-shot System for Generic Instruction Navigation in Unexplored Environment
abstract: Enabling robots to navigate following diverse language instructions in unexplored environments is an attractive goal for human-robot interaction. However, this goal is challenging because different navigation tasks require different strategies. The scarcity of instruction navigation data hinders training an instruction navigation model with varied strategies. Therefore, previous methods are all constrained to one specific type of navigation instruction. In this work, we propose InstructNav, a generic instruction navigation system. InstructNav makes the first endeavor to handle various instruction navigation tasks without any navigation training or pre-built maps. To reach this goal, we introduce Dynamic Chain-of-Navigation (DCoN) to unify the planning process for different types of navigation instructions. Furthermore, we propose Multi-sourced Value Maps to model key elements in instruction navigation so that linguistic DCoN planning can be converted into robot actionable trajectories. With InstructNav, we complete the R2R-CE task in a zero-shot way for the first time and outperform many task-training methods. Besides, InstructNav also surpasses the previous SOTA method by 10.48% on the zero-shot Habitat ObjNav and by 86.34% on demand-driven navigation DDN. Real robot experiments on diverse indoor scenes further demonstrate our method's robustness in coping with the environment and instruction variations.
author: Yuxing Long;Wenzhe Cai;Hongcheng Wang;Guanqi Zhan;Hao Dong
aff: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications;Southeast University;Peking University;University of Oxford;Peking University
position: MS student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications;Southeast University;Peking University;University of Oxford

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a robot that navigates unfamiliar houses by 'thinking out loud' using a language model instead of actually training it on maps. It basically treats a Roomba like a philosophy major, asking it to ponder the meaning of 'go to the kitchen' before every single step.
why_bad: The 'Dynamic Chain-of-Navigation' is essentially a glorified prompt-engineering trick that likely suffers from massive latency—your robot might find the sofa, but only after it spends five minutes 'reasoning' at every doorway.
The zero-shot claims are mathematically impressive but practically fragile; the system likely crumbles the moment the lighting changes or the LLM decides 'near the TV' means 'inside the fireplace.'

Real-world robustness is overstated because the 'Multi-sourced Value Maps' rely on pre-trained vision models that often hallucinate objects in the messy, unstructured environments the paper claims to master.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Philosophizing Pizza Guy
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents a 'revolutionary' delivery driver who refuses to use a GPS. Instead, he stands at every street corner reciting a 'Chain-of-Navigation,' debating the socio-economic implications of a 'Left Turn' while your pepperoni pizza grows cold. The defense calls this 'Zero-Shot Intelligence,' but we call it a 'Total Lack of Direction.' This driver claims to outperform seasoned locals, yet he’s never actually driven a mile without stopping to consult a linguistic oracle. Your Honor, this isn't a navigation system; it’s a GPS with a theater degree that takes ten minutes to decide if a hallway is a hallway or a 'transitionary spatial concept.' I move to strike this 'InstructNav' from the record for crimes against efficiency!

title: Bridging the gap between Learning-to-plan, Motion Primitives and Safe Reinforcement Learning
abstract:
Trajectory planning under kinodynamic constraints is fundamental for advanced robotics applications that require dexterous, reactive, and rapid skills in complex environments. These constraints, which may represent task, safety, or actuator limitations, are essential for ensuring the proper functioning of robotic platforms and preventing unexpected behaviors. Recent advances in kinodynamic planning demonstrate that learning-to-plan techniques can generate complex and reactive motions under intricate constraints. However, these techniques necessitate the analytical modeling of both the robot and the entire task, a limiting assumption when systems are extremely complex or when constructing accurate task models is prohibitive.
This paper addresses this limitation by combining learning-to-plan methods with reinforcement learning, resulting in a novel integration of black-box learning of motion primitives and optimization. We evaluate our approach against state-of-the-art safe reinforcement learning methods, showing that our technique, particularly when exploiting task structure, outperforms baseline methods in challenging scenarios such as planning to hit in robot air hockey. This work demonstrates the potential of our integrated approach to enhance the performance and safety of robots operating under complex kinodynamic constraints.
author: Piotr Kicki;Davide Tateo;Puze Liu;Jonas Günster;Jan Peters;Krzysztof Walas
aff: IDEAS NCBR Sp.;Technische Universität Darmstadt;TU Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt;TU Darmstadt;Technical University of Poznan
aff_unique_norm: IDEAS NCBR;Technische Universität Darmstadt;Technical University of Poznan

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot to play air hockey by smashing together 'thinking ahead' math and 'trial-and-error' learning so it doesn't break itself or the table. It’s basically an attempt to give a robot both reflexes and a plan without having to write down every single law of physics first.
why_bad: Architectural Over-Engineering: It attempts to weld three massive, distinct frameworks into a 'Frankenstein's Monster' that is likely a nightmare to tune and impossible to replicate.

The 'Baseline' Bait-and-Switch: The authors claim victory over 'Safe RL' methods, but conveniently ignore that a standard, 20-year-old control equation could probably play air hockey just as well without the computational drama.
The Black-Box Cop-out: Labeling the core movement logic as 'black-box learning' is academic shorthand for 'we aren't quite sure how it's doing what it's doing, so please don't ask for the math.'
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Robotics Edition.' Our chef today tried to 'bridge the gap' between classical French culinary techniques and a frozen 3 a.m. snack. They used a $5,000 immersion circulator to heat a pepperoni Hot Pocket, claiming the 'optimized thermal trajectory' and 'black-box crust technology' revolutionize the snacking experience. But when the judges bit in, it was still frozen in the middle and scalding on the edges. Sure, the plating looked great on Instagram (that's your air hockey demo), but you don't need a PhD and a supercomputer to heat up a snack. You just made a simple task incredibly expensive and slightly more confusing for everyone involved. Bone appétit!

title: ThinkGrasp: A Vision-Language System for Strategic Part Grasping in Clutter
abstract: Robotic grasping in cluttered environments remains a significant challenge due to occlusions and complex object arrangements. We have developed ThinkGrasp, a plug-and-play vision-language grasping system that makes use of GPT-4o's advanced contextual reasoning for grasping strategies. ThinkGrasp can effectively identify and generate grasp poses for target objects, even when they are heavily obstructed or nearly invisible, by using goal-oriented language to guide the removal of obstructing objects. This approach progressively uncovers the target object and ultimately grasps it with a few steps and a high success rate. In both simulated and real experiments, ThinkGrasp achieved a high success rate and significantly outperformed state-of-the-art methods in heavily cluttered environments or with diverse unseen objects, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.
author: Yaoyao Qian;Xupeng Zhu;Ondrej Biza;Shuo Jiang;Linfeng Zhao;Haojie Huang;Yu Qi;Robert Platt
aff: Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;;Meta;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University
position: MS student;PhD student;PhD student;;ML PhD Intern;PhD student;PhD student;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to solve the 'messy toy box' problem by giving a robot arm a direct line to ChatGPT so it could talk its way through moving junk out of the way. It’s essentially a very expensive mechanical claw that has to 'meditate' on a pile of clutter before deciding to move a stapler.
why_bad: Systemic Latency: Using GPT-4o as a brain means the robot has the reaction time of a sloth on a conference call while it waits for cloud processing.
Lack of Physical Common Sense: The model relies on language logic rather than actual physics, leading to 'strategic' moves that might look good on paper but fail in the real world.
Methodological Thinness: The 'plug-and-play' approach is often code for 'we didn't actually build a new AI model, we just wrote a clever prompt for someone else's.'
roast.analogy_name: The Long-Distance Relationship with a Toaster
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where your robot arm is too insecure to move a banana without asking a server in California for permission. Witness the high-stakes drama of a machine that can identify a hidden screwdriver, but only after a 10-second existential crisis and three API calls. It’s 'Wall-E' meets 'The IT Crowd'—see the action, feel the lag, and pray the Wi-Fi doesn't go down before it drops your car keys into the garbage disposal. This summer, the smartest thing in the room... is actually just a very long extension cord plugged into a chatbot.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 99.9% confidence.

title: Enhancing Visual Domain Robustness in Behaviour Cloning via Saliency-Guided Augmentation
abstract: In vision-based behaviour cloning (BC), traditional image-level augmentation methods such as pixel shifting enhance in-domain performance but often struggle with visual domain shifts, including distractors, occlusion, and changes in lighting and backgrounds. Conversely, superimposition-based augmentation, proven effective in computer vision, improves model generalisability by blending training images and out-of-domain images. Despite its potential, the applicability of these methods to vision-based BC remains unclear due to the unique challenges posed by BC demonstrations; specifically, preserving task-critical scene semantics, spatial-temporal relationships, and agent-target interactions is crucial. To address this, we introduce RoboSaGA, a context-aware approach that dynamically adjusts augmentation intensity per pixel based on input saliency derived from the policy. This method ensures aggressive augmentation within task-trivial areas without compromising task-critical information. Furthermore, RoboSaGA seamlessly integrates into existing network architectures without the need for structural changes or additional learning objectives. Our empirical evaluations across both simulated and real-world settings demonstrate that RoboSaGA not only maintains in-domain performance but significantly improves resilience to distractors and background variations.
author: Zheyu Zhuang;RUIYU WANG;Nils Ingelhag;Ville Kyrki;Danica Kragic
aff: KTH Royal Institute of Technology;KTH Royal Institute of Technology;;Aalto University;KTH
aff_unique_norm: KTH Royal Institute of Technology;Aalto University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop robots from getting distracted by shiny backgrounds by blurring out everything the robot wasn't already looking at. It’s basically digital blinders designed to keep the AI focused on the task instead of the wallpaper.
why_bad: The 'novel' method is essentially just a fancy version of Masked Autoencoders or CutMix that the vision community finished playing with three years ago.
It relies on the robot's own 'saliency'—meaning if the robot is already looking at the wrong thing, this method just helps it focus even harder on its own mistake.
The experimental results are 'cherry-picked' at best, failing to prove it works any better than just turning up the brightness or adding a bit of random noise.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Deep Learning Bowl! Here we see RoboSaGA taking the field. The play-call? They’ve given the quarterback special goggles that blur out the cheerleaders and the hecklers so he only sees the receiver. Bold move, Cotton! But wait—the refs are blowing the whistle. Why? Because the quarterback is the one *choosing* what to blur. He’s staring at a hot dog vendor in row four, blurring out his own offensive line, and getting sacked into the next epoch! The commentators are calling it 'incremental' because we’ve seen people wear glasses before, and these ones don't even have a prescription. It's a fumble on the 5-yard line of innovation!

title: Get a Grip: Multi-Finger Grasp Evaluation at Scale Enables Robust Sim-to-Real Transfer
abstract: This work explores conditions under which multi-finger grasping algorithms can attain robust sim-to-real transfer. While numerous large datasets facilitate learning *generative* models for multi-finger grasping at scale, reliable real-world dexterous grasping remains challenging, with most methods degrading when deployed on hardware. An alternate strategy is to use *discriminative* grasp evaluation models for grasp selection and refinement, conditioned on real-world sensor measurements. This paradigm has produced state-of-the-art results for vision-based parallel-jaw grasping, but remains unproven in the multi-finger setting. In this work, we find that existing datasets and methods have been insufficient for training discriminitive models for multi-finger grasping. To train grasp evaluators at scale, datasets must provide on the order of millions of grasps, including both positive *and negative examples*, with corresponding visual data resembling measurements at inference time. To that end, we release a new, open-source dataset of 3.5M grasps on 4.3K objects annotated with RGB images, point clouds, and trained NeRFs. Leveraging this dataset, we train vision-based grasp evaluators that outperform both analytic and generative modeling-based baselines on extensive simulated and real-world trials across a diverse range of objects. We show via numerous ablations that the key factor for performance is indeed the evaluator, and that its quality degrades as the dataset shrinks, demonstrating the importance of our new dataset. Project website at: https://sites.google.com/view/get-a-grip-dataset.
author: Tyler Ga Wei Lum;Albert H. Li;Preston Culbertson;Krishnan Srinivasan;Aaron Ames;Mac Schwager;Jeannette Bohg
aff: Stanford University;;California Institute of Technology;Stanford University;California Institute of Technology;Stanford University;Stanford University
position: PhD student;;Postdoc;PhD student;Bren Professor;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop robots from being butter-fingered by showing them 3.5 million examples of what a bad grip looks like. Instead of teaching the robot how to move, we built a massive 'swipe left or right' app for hand positions so it can hopefully pick the one that doesn't end in shattered glass.
why_bad: The 'innovation' is essentially just a massive data dump, proving once again that if you throw enough GPUs at a problem, it eventually looks like science.
The reviewers weren't impressed by porting a 'parallel-jaw' logic to multi-finger hands, noting it's like putting a fresh coat of paint on a 2015 methodology.
Real-world 'robustness' claims are shaky because the model relies on high-quality NeRFs and point clouds that your average warehouse robot definitely doesn't have the patience to render.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Multi-Million Dollar Butter-Finger
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution asserts that my client is a master of dexterity because they have viewed 3.5 million photos of people dropping their car keys. We are in a courtroom of robotics, yet the defense presents no new 'laws' of motion, only a massive filing cabinet filled with 'Exhibit 4,000,201: A Very Bad Way to Hold a Taco.' The jury finds that while you have successfully documented failure at an industrial scale, you haven't actually taught the robot how to dance; you've just taught it to recognize when it's about to trip over its own mechanical feet. Objections sustained: this isn't a breakthrough, it's just a very expensive 'Don't Do This' manual.

title: MaIL: Improving Imitation Learning with Selective State Space Models
abstract: This work introduces Mamba Imitation Learning (MaIL), a novel imitation learning (IL) architecture that offers a computationally efficient alternative to state-of-the-art (SoTA) Transformer policies. Transformer-based policies have achieved remarkable results due to their ability in handling human-recorded data with inherently non-Markovian behavior. However, their high performance comes with the drawback of large models that complicate effective training. While state space models (SSMs) have been known for their efficiency, they were not able to match the performance of Transformers. Mamba significantly improves the performance of SSMs and rivals against Transformers, positioning it as an appealing alternative for IL policies. MaIL leverages Mamba as a backbone and introduces a formalism that allows using Mamba in the encoder-decoder structure. This formalism makes it a versatile architecture that can be used as a standalone policy or as part of a more advanced architecture, such as a diffuser in the diffusion process. Extensive evaluations on the LIBERO IL benchmark and three real robot experiments show that MaIL: i) outperforms Transformers in all LIBERO tasks, ii) achieves good performance even with small datasets, iii) is able to effectively process multi-modal sensory inputs, iv) is more robust to input noise compared to Transformers.
author: Xiaogang Jia;Qian Wang;Atalay Donat;Bowen Xing;Ge Li;Hongyi Zhou;Onur Celik;Denis Blessing;Rudolf Lioutikov;Gerhard Neumann

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to replace the robot's massive, power-hungry 'Transformer' brain with a sleek, trendy new model called 'Mamba' to see if it could learn chores faster. While it’s technically more efficient, the reviewers felt like the team just swapped the engine of a car without actually redesigning the vehicle.
why_bad: Architectural Deja Vu: Swapping Transformers for Mamba has become the 'low-hanging fruit' of 2024 research, offering minimal conceptual novelty beyond basic substitution.
Benchmark Cherry-Picking: Claiming victory on the LIBERO benchmark is less impressive when the comparisons ignore the most recent, highly-optimized versions of diffusion policies.
The Rule of Three: Conducting only three real-robot experiments is considered statistically anecdotal rather than a rigorous proof of robotic reliability.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where AI models are too 'THICC' to run on a toaster... one team has a plan. [BWAAA-RUM]. They’re stripping out the heavy Transformer parts and installing a Selective State Space turbocharger. It’s MaIL: The Mamba Imitation Learner. It’s leaner! It’s meaner! It’s slightly faster at picking up a plastic cup! But when the Critics’ Guild saw the premiere, they realized the plot was just a reboot of last year's movie with a different engine sound. Coming this fall: a high-speed chase through a benchmark that everyone already finished.

title: Legolas: Deep Leg-Inertial Odometry
abstract:
Estimating odometry, where an accumulating position and rotation is tracked, has critical applications in many areas of robotics as a form of state estimation such as in SLAM, navigation, and controls. During deployment of a legged robot, a vision system's tracking can easily get lost. Instead, using only the onboard leg and inertial sensor for odometry is a promising alternative. Previous methods in estimating leg-inertial odometry require analytical modeling or collecting high-quality real-world trajectories to train a model. Analytical modeling is specific to each robot, requires manual fine-tuning, and doesn't always capture real-world phenomena such as slippage. Previous work learning legged odometry still relies on collecting real-world data, this has been shown to not perform well out of distribution. In this work, we show that it is possible to estimate the odometry of a legged robot without any analytical modeling or real-world data collection. In this paper, we present Legolas, the first method that accurately estimates odometry in a purely data-driven fashion for quadruped robots. We deploy our method on two real-world quadruped robots in both indoor and outdoor environments. In the indoor scenes, our proposed method accomplishes a relative pose error that is 73% less than an analytical filtering-based approach and 87.5% less than a real-world behavioral cloning approach.
More results are available at: learned-odom.github.io
author: Justin Wasserman;Ananye Agarwal;Rishabh Jangir;Girish Chowdhary;Deepak Pathak;Abhinav Gupta
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, San Diego;University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University
position: PhD student;PhD student;MS student;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor;Full Professor
