aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, San Diego

roast.laymen_summary: We taught a robot dog to navigate 'blind' by sensing its own leg movements and balance, similar to how you find the bathroom in the middle of the night without turning on the lights. To save time, we trained its brain entirely inside a physics simulator and hoped it wouldn't freak out when it touched actual dirt.
why_bad: The Sim-to-Real Delusion: Relying 100% on synthetic training data is a bold move that usually ignores the messy reality of friction, pebbles, and the fact that physics engines are basically just fancy liars.

The 'Grandpa' Baseline: Comparing a modern deep-learning model to a 'basic filtering approach' is like bragging that your smartphone is faster than a 1980s calculator; it's a low bar that hides a lack of true innovation.

Odometer Drift Amnesia: The paper glosses over the fact that without a camera or GPS, these 'inertial' guesses eventually turn into a game of 'Where's Waldo' where the robot thinks it's in the kitchen when it's actually in the bushes.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: We’ve got a world-class athlete who has never actually been outside. We put them in a high-fidelity VR headset of a treadmill for six months and then dropped them blindfolded into the middle of a forest, telling them to 'feel the vibes' of the ground to find their way home. It’s a 'data-driven' disruption of the vision industry! Why use expensive eyes when you can just guess where you are based on how much your knees hurt? It's not 'lost,' it's just 'experiencing a high relative pose error' in a new zip code.

title: DextrAH-G: Pixels-to-Action Dexterous Arm-Hand Grasping with Geometric Fabrics
abstract: A pivotal challenge in robotics is achieving fast, safe, and robust dexterous grasping across a diverse range of objects, an important goal within industrial applications. However, existing methods often have very limited speed, dexterity, and generality, along with limited or no hardware safety guarantees. In this work, we introduce DextrAH-G, a depth-based dexterous grasping policy trained entirely in simulation that combines reinforcement learning, geometric fabrics, and teacher-student distillation. We address key challenges in joint arm-hand policy learning, such as high-dimensional observation and action spaces, the sim2real gap, collision avoidance, and hardware constraints. DextrAH-G enables a 23 motor arm-hand robot to safely and continuously grasp and transport a large variety of objects at high speed using multi-modal inputs including depth images, allowing generalization across object geometry. Videos at https://sites.google.com/view/dextrah-g.
keywords: Dexterous Grasping;Geometric Fabrics;Reinforcement Learning;Teacher-Student Distillation;Sim-to-Real Transfer
author: Tyler Ga Wei Lum;Martin Matak;Viktor Makoviychuk;Ankur Handa;Arthur Allshire;Tucker Hermans;Nathan D. Ratliff;Karl Van Wyk
aff: Stanford University;University of Utah;NVIDIA;Imperial College London;University of Toronto;University of Utah;NVIDIA;
position: PhD student;PhD student;Senior Research Scientist;Research Scientist;Undergrad student;Associate Professor;Researcher;
aff_unique_norm: Stanford University;University of Utah;NVIDIA;Imperial College London;University of Toronto

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to teach a 23-jointed robotic claw to pick up random junk by letting it play a high-stakes video game version of itself. They added a mathematical 'safety blanket' called Geometric Fabrics to ensure the robot doesn't punch itself or the user while trying to look busy.
why_bad: The 'Geometric Fabrics' are essentially a fancy math tax that makes the robot move with the agonizing hesitation of a teenager driving for the first time.
The 'Teacher-Student Distillation' is academic jargon for admitting the smart model was too slow to actually run, so they settled for a dumber, faster version that guesses.
The sim-to-real generalization claims are optimistic, as the system likely hallucinates the second it encounters a reflective surface or a slightly different shade of grey than the simulation.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where simple tongs would suffice, one team spent a fortune to build the ultimate over-thinker. Witness the cinematic tension as a 23-motor monstrosity calculates the geometric curvature of a rubber ducky in 4D space, only to move with the speed of a tectonic plate. It’s 'The Matrix' meets a 'Safety First' seminar—where the hero spends three hours planning how to touch a stapler without causing a workplace insurance claim. Rated R for Realistic Latency.

title: Let Occ Flow: Self-Supervised 3D Occupancy Flow Prediction
abstract: Accurate perception of the dynamic environment is a fundamental task for autonomous driving and robot systems. This paper introduces Let Occ Flow, the first self-supervised work for joint 3D occupancy and occupancy flow prediction using only camera inputs, eliminating the need for 3D annotations. Utilizing TPV for unified scene representation and deformable attention layers for feature aggregation, our approach incorporates a novel attention-based temporal fusion module to capture dynamic object dependencies, followed by a 3D refine module for fine-gained volumetric representation. Besides, our method extends differentiable rendering to 3D volumetric flow fields, leveraging zero-shot 2D segmentation and optical flow cues for dynamic decomposition and motion optimization. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and KITTI datasets demonstrate the competitive performance of our approach over prior state-of-the-art methods.
author: Yili Liu;Linzhan Mou;Xuan Yu;Chenrui Han;Sitong Mao;Rong Xiong;Yue Wang
aff: Zhejiang University;;Zhejiang University;Zhejiang University;The Hong Kong Polytechnic University;Zhejiang University;Zhejiang University
position: MS student;;PhD student;MS student;PhD student;Full Professor;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a self-driving car to imagine a 3D world and predict where everyone is moving using only flat camera images and zero human help. It’s essentially trying to reconstruct a 3D crime scene using only a single Polaroid and a lot of 'trust me, bro' math.
why_bad: Over-engineered Complexity: The model is a 'word salad' of TPV, deformable attention, and differentiable rendering that looks more like a Rube Goldberg machine than a functional neural network.
The 'Competitive' Cop-out: Claiming performance is 'competitive' usually means the model is slower than a snail and barely matches benchmarks set two years ago.
Self-Supervision Hallucinations: By relying on zero-shot 2D cues to guess 3D motion, the system likely produces 'ghost objects' that appear and disappear whenever the lighting changes.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: AI Edition.' Our chef today is trying to recreate a 5-course Gordon Ramsay tasting menu, but here’s the twist: they aren't allowed to taste the food or even see the ingredients. Instead, they’re looking at a grainy 2D photo of the trash can behind the restaurant (our 'optical flow cues') and trying to 'self-supervise' their way to a Michelin star. They’ve thrown in a '3D refine module'—which is just a fancy way of saying they’re putting a sprig of parsley on a pile of raw flour—and they’re shocked that the critics (the reviewers) aren't impressed by a soufflé that’s mostly 'unified scene representation' and zero actual substance. It’s a bold strategy, but you can't cook a steak just by staring at a picture of a cow and vibing.

title: Pre-emptive Action Revision by Environmental Feedback for Embodied Instruction Following Agents
abstract: When we, humans, perform a task, we consider changes in environments such as objects' arrangement due to interactions with objects and other reasons; e.g., when we find a mug to clean, if it is already clean, we skip cleaning it. But even the state-of-the-art embodied agents often ignore changed environments when performing a task, leading to failure to complete the task, executing unnecessary actions, or fixing the mistake after it was made. Here, we propose Pre-emptive Action Revision by Environmental feeDback (PRED) that allows an embodied agent to revise their action in response to the perceived environmental status before it makes mistakes. We empirically validate PRED and observe that it outperforms the prior art on two challenging benchmarks in the virtual environment, TEACh and ALFRED, by noticeable margins in most metrics, including unseen success rates, with shorter execution time, implying an efficiently behaved agent. Furthermore, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method with real robot experiments.
author: Jinyeon Kim;Cheolhong Min;Byeonghwi Kim;Jonghyun Choi
aff: Yonsei University;Yonsei University;Seoul National University;Yonsei University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to teach robots to stop being mindless automatons that try to wash a dish that is already clean. It introduces a 'look before you leap' feature so agents can update their plans when they notice the world isn't exactly how they expected it to be.
why_bad: The 'novel' contribution is essentially a glorified 'if-then' statement disguised under high-level academic jargon like 'Pre-emptive Action Revision.'
The benchmarks used (ALFRED and TEACh) are notoriously easy to 'game' by just slowing down the agent, making the reported 'efficiency' gains statistically suspicious.
The real-world robot experiments lacked sufficient trials to prove the system wouldn't crumble the moment a human moved a chair in a non-laboratory setting.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a Silicon Valley startup raising $20M for a 'Context-Aware Illumination Interface.' In reality, it’s just a guy who looks at a lamp before flipping the switch to see if it’s already glowing. The paper pitches this as 'Environmental Feedback Integration,' but for anyone who has ever programmed a VCR, it’s just basic error-handling. It’s like claiming you’ve revolutionized driving because you taught a car not to park in a spot that’s already occupied by a dumpster.

title: Online Transfer and Adaptation of Tactile Skill: A Teleoperation Framework
abstract: This paper presents a teleoperation framework designed for online learning and adaptation of tactile skills, which provides an intuitive interface without need for physical access to execution robot. The proposed tele-teaching approach utilizes periodical Dynamical Movement Primitives (DMP) and Recursive Least Square (RLS) for generating tactile skills. An autonomy allocation strategy, guided by the learning confidence and operator intention, ensures a smooth transition between human demonstration to autonomous robot operation. Our experimental results with two 7 Degree of Freedom (DoF) Franka Panda robot demonstrates that the tele-teaching framework facilitates online motion and force learning and adaptation within a few iterations.
keywords: Learning from Demonstration;Online Adaptation;Tactile Skill;Teleoperation;Autonomy Allocation
author: Xiao Chen;Tianle Ni;Kübra Karacan;Hamid Sadeghian;Sami Haddadin
aff: Technische Universität München;Technische Universität München;Technische Universität München;;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a system where a human 'remotes in' to teach a robot how to touch and move objects delicately without being in the same room. They basically created a high-tech puppet show where the robot tries to take over the strings once it 'feels' like it's seen enough.
why_bad: The 'learning confidence' metric is essentially a mathematical 'trust me bro,' lacking any rigorous proof that the robot won't accidentally pulverize the object the moment the human lets go.
Using Periodic Dynamical Movement Primitives (DMP) is a fancy way of saying the robot is great at repetitive scrubbing but fails miserably at any task that isn't a rhythmic loop.
The 'online adaptation' is suspiciously fast because the model is too shallow to actually understand physics; it’s just over-fitting to a few minutes of jerky human movement and calling it 'mastery'.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that lets you cook dinner at your house using a robot arm controlled by a Michelin-star chef in Paris. Sounds great, right? Except our 'AI' doesn't actually know what salt is. It just records the chef’s arm shaking a tin and then tries to replicate the 'vibes' of the movement. If the chef stops to sneeze, the robot assumes sneezing is a core part of the recipe and incorporates a violent jerk into your hollandaise. We call this 'Autonomy Allocation,' but in reality, it's just a $70,000 arm waiting for a human to tell it how not to be a paperweight. It's disrupting the 'having a clue' industry.

title: ViPER: Visibility-based Pursuit-Evasion via Reinforcement Learning
abstract: In visibility-based pursuit-evasion tasks, a team of mobile pursuer robots with limited sensing capabilities is tasked with detecting all evaders in a multiply-connected planar environment, whose map may or may not be known to pursuers beforehand. This requires tight coordination among multiple agents to ensure that the omniscient and potentially arbitrarily fast evaders are guaranteed to be detected by the pursuers. Whereas existing methods typically rely on a relatively large team of agents to clear the environment, we propose ViPER, a neural solution that leverages a graph attention network to learn a coordinated yet distributed policy via multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). We experimentally demonstrate that ViPER significantly outperforms other state-of-the-art non-learning planners, showcasing its emergent coordinated behaviors and adaptability to more challenging scenarios and various team sizes, and finally deploy its learned policies on hardware in an aerial search task.
author: Yizhuo Wang;Yuhong Cao;Jimmy Chiun;Subhadeep Koley;Mandy Pham;Guillaume Adrien Sartoretti
aff: National University of Singapore;National University of Singapore;;Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology, Shibpur;University of California, Berkeley;National University of Singapore
position: PhD student;PhD student;;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: National University of Singapore;Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: We taught a squad of drones to play the world's highest-stakes game of hide-and-seek against an invisible, cheating opponent that knows their every move. Instead of using old-school math, we gave the robots a 'collective brain' and let them figure out how to corner the ghost through trial and error.
why_bad: The paper claims 'guaranteed detection,' but reinforcement learning is famously allergic to mathematical guarantees and usually just hopes for the best.
The 'emergent coordination' is likely just the agents memorizing the specific maps they were trained on, failing the second they see a new hallway.
The authors benchmarked their shiny new AI against basic algorithms from the 90s, which is like bragging your Tesla beat a horse in a drag race.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that wants to replace professional exorcists with a pack of golden retrievers wearing GoPro headsets. Instead of using a map or common sense, we just let the dogs bark at each other via a 'Graph Attention Network' until the ghost gets bored and leaves. We're disrupting the 'not-getting-murdered-by-poltergeists' vertical by replacing boring geometric proofs with 'vibes' and 400,000 hours of simulated failure. Sure, the ghost is an omniscient interdimensional being, but our dogs have *synergy*.

title: SoftManiSim: A Fast Simulation Framework for Multi-Segment Continuum Manipulators Tailored for Robot Learning
abstract:
This paper introduces SoftManiSim, a novel simulation framework for multi-segment continuum manipulators. Existing continuum robot simulators often rely on simplifying assumptions, such as constant curvature bending or ignoring contact forces, to meet real-time simulation and training demands. To bridge this gap, we propose a robust and rapid mathematical model for continuum robots at the core of SoftManiSim, ensuring precise and adaptable simulations. The framework can integrate with various rigid-body robots, increasing its utility across different robotic platforms. SoftManiSim supports parallel operations for simultaneous simulations of multiple robots and generates synthetic data essential for training deep reinforcement learning models. This capability enhances the development and optimization of control strategies in dynamic environments. Extensive simulations validate the framework's effectiveness, demonstrating its capabilities in handling complex robotic interactions and tasks. We also present real robot validation to showcase the simulator's practical applicability and accuracy in real-world settings. To our knowledge, SoftManiSim is the first open-source real-time simulator capable of modeling continuum robot behavior under dynamic point/distributed loading. It enables rapid deployment in reinforcement learning and machine learning applications. 
This simulation framework can be downloaded from https://github.com/MohammadKasaei/SoftManiSim.
author: Mohammadreza Kasaei;Hamidreza Kasaei;Mohsen Khadem
aff: ;University of Groningen;Edinburgh University, University of Edinburgh

roast.laymen_summary: We made a high-speed video game for robot tentacles so they can practice grabbing things without needing a physical body. It’s designed to let AI 'brains' train thousands of times faster than real life, hopefully teaching bendy robots not to be complete klutzes.
why_bad: The 'novel' mathematical model is essentially just a spicy version of 20-year-old rod theory that ignores the messy, non-linear reality of actual soft materials.
The 'real-time' performance claims are likely achieved by cutting so many corners that the simulation is more 'CGI movie' than 'accurate physics engine.'
The sim-to-real validation is suspiciously thin, suggesting the robot works great in the digital void but would likely have a nervous breakdown when touching a real-world object.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re pitching a revolutionary VR flight simulator for birds. You tell investors it’s 'hyper-realistic' and 'tailored for avian learning,' but your physics engine assumes all birds are perfectly round, hollow, and flying in a vacuum. You’re promising to revolutionize how pigeons navigate urban environments, but you’ve actually just built a high-speed version of Flappy Bird that crashes if a breeze hits it. It’s great for generating 'synthetic data,' sure, but once the AI bird tries to land on a real power line, it’s going to realize your simulation forgot that gravity and wind exist.

title: Verification of Neural Control Barrier Functions with Symbolic Derivative Bounds Propagation
abstract: Control barrier functions (CBFs) are important in safety-critical systems and robot control applications. Neural networks have been used to parameterize and synthesize CBFs with bounded control input for complex systems. However, it is still challenging to verify pre-trained neural networks CBFs (neural CBFs) in an efficient symbolic manner. To this end, we propose a new efficient verification framework for ReLU-based neural CBFs through symbolic derivative bound propagation by combining the linearly bounded nonlinear dynamic system and the gradient bounds of neural CBFs. Specifically, with Heaviside step function form for derivatives of activation functions, we show that the symbolic bounds can be propagated through the inner product of neural CBF Jacobian and nonlinear system dynamics. Through extensive experiments on different robot dynamics, our results outperform the interval arithmetic-based baselines in verified rate and verification time along the CBF boundary, validating the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method with different model complexity. The code can be found at https://github.com/intelligent-control-lab/verify-neural-CBF.
keywords: Learning for control;control barrier function;formal verification
author: Hanjiang Hu;Yujie Yang;Tianhao Wei;Changliu Liu
aff: School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University;Tsinghua University;Carnegie Mellon University;Carnegie Mellon University

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a math-based 'safety cage' to stop AI-controlled robots from crashing, using a technique that tracks the steepest possible turns the AI might take. While they claim it's faster and better than existing methods, it’s basically trying to prove a black box is safe by drawing boxes around its possible mistakes.
why_bad: The 'complex robot dynamics' tested were essentially the academic equivalent of a tricycle, failing to prove it works on real-world machinery.
The mathematical bounds are so conservative that the robot would likely be too paralyzed by 'safety' to actually move.
The methodology is a marginal incremental tweak on existing interval arithmetic that doesn't justify the extra computational overhead.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where your contractor builds your house out of 'Neural Networks'—a material no one understands and that occasionally turns into liquid—one team has a plan. Witness the heart-pounding drama of THE SYMBOLIC BOUND. They aren't going to fix the walls; they’re going to use a special laser-pointer to prove the house *probably* won't collapse, provided nobody sneezes or walks on the second floor. It’s a race against time, logic, and reviewers who noticed the laser-pointer is running out of batteries. This summer, safety is guaranteed... as long as you stay in the hallway.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark levels at 94%.

title: Fleet Supervisor Allocation: A Submodular Maximization Approach
abstract:
In real-world scenarios, the data collected by robots in diverse and unpredictable environments is crucial for enhancing their perception and decision-making models. This data is predominantly collected under human supervision, particularly through imitation learning (IL), where robots learn complex tasks by observing human supervisors. However, the deployment of multiple robots and supervisors to accelerate the learning process often leads to data redundancy and inefficiencies, especially as the scale of robot fleets increases. Moreover, the reliance on teleoperation for supervision introduces additional challenges due to potential network connectivity issues. 
To address these issues in data collection, we introduce an Adaptive Submodular Allocation policy, ASA, designed for efficient human supervision allocation within multi-robot systems under uncertain connectivity conditions. Our approach reduces data redundancy by balancing the informativeness and diversity of data collection, and is capable of accommodating connectivity variances. We evaluate the effectiveness of ASA in simulations with 100 robots across four different environments and various network settings, including a real-world teleoperation scenario over a 5G network. We train and test our policy, ASA, and state-of-the-art policies utilizing NVIDIA's Isaac Gym. Our results show that ASA enhances the return on human effort by up to $3.37\times$, outperforming current baselines in all simulated scenarios and providing robustness against connectivity disruptions.
author: Oguzhan Akcin;Ahmet Ege Tanriverdi;Kaan Kale;Sandeep P. Chinchali
aff: The University of Texas at Austin;Bogazici University;Bogazici University;University of Texas at Austin
aff_unique_norm: University of Texas at Austin;Bogazici University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to solve the problem of 100 robots screaming for a single human's help at the same time over crappy Wi-Fi. It uses a 'fancy' math filter to ensure the human only helps the robots that are actually learning something new, rather than babysitting robots that are all making the same mistake.
why_bad: The 'submodular maximization' approach is essentially a glorified greedy algorithm dressed in a tuxedo to hide its lack of theoretical novelty.
Testing a '100-robot fleet' in the sterile vacuum of NVIDIA’s Isaac Gym simulation is a far cry from the messy reality of physical hardware and actual signal interference.
The reported 3.37x performance boost smells like cherry-picked data compared against 'state-of-the-art' baselines that were likely tuned to fail.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where 100 toddlers are trying to learn origami, but there is only one teacher... and that teacher is trapped inside a 1998 webcam. One algorithm must decide which toddler gets the 'Supervision' before the Wi-Fi drops and the house burns down. Witness the high-stakes math of deciding which child is the most 'informative' to save. This summer: The ASA Protocol. Because in space, no one can hear your 5G signal drop.

title: Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning with Hybrid Action Space for Free Gait Motion Planning of Hexapod Robots
abstract: Legged robots are able to overcome challenging terrains through diverse gaits formed by contact sequences. However, environments characterized by discrete footholds present significant challenges. In this paper, we tackle the problem of free gait motion planning for hexapod robots walking in randomly generated plum blossom pile environments. Specifically, we first address the complexity of multi-leg coordination in discrete environments by treating each leg of the hexapod robot as an individual agent. Then, we propose the Hybrid action space Multi-Agent Soft Actor Critic (Hybrid-MASAC) algorithm capable of handling both discrete and continuous actions. Finally, we present an integrated free gait motion planning method based on Hybrid-MASAC, streamlining gait, Center of Mass (COM), and foothold sequences planning into a single model. Comparative and ablation experiments in both of the simulated and real plum blossom pile environments demonstrate the feasibility and efficiency of our method.
keywords: Free Gait;Hexapod Robot;Hybrid Action Space;Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
author: Huiqiao Fu;Kaiqiang Tang;Peng Li;Guizhou Deng;Chunlin Chen
aff: Nanjing University;Nanjing University;Institute of Software, Chinese Academy of Sciences;;Nanjing University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a six-legged robot to walk across a field of narrow poles by giving each leg its own individual brain and a complex list of 'if-then' rules. While they claim it's a breakthrough in coordination, it’s essentially trying to get six toddlers to dance a synchronized ballet while they're all wearing noise-canceling headphones.
why_bad: The 'Independent Leg' Fallacy: Treating each leg as a separate agent creates a massive communication overhead that makes the robot move with the grace of a glitching video game character.
Algorithmic Overkill: Using a Hybrid-MASAC architecture for a walking task is the research equivalent of using a SpaceX Falcon 9 to deliver a pizza—unnecessarily expensive and prone to exploding.

The Fragile Environment Trap: The system is 'overfit' to the specific plum blossom pile setup, meaning the robot would likely suffer a total nervous breakdown if it encountered a pebble or a slight incline.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Six-Headed Defendant
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, look at the accused! The defense calls this 'Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning,' but I call it a blatant conspiracy to commit inefficiency. They’ve put six bickering lawyers inside one mechanical suit and asked them to cross a minefield. Your Honor, the prosecution has evidence that these legs aren't 'coordinating'; they are locked in a perpetual hung jury, debating the 'Hybrid Action Space' of a single step while the robot falls face-first into the dirt. This isn't a motion plan; it’s a chaotic legal deposition where the Center of Mass is being sued for lack of direction. I move for a mistrial on the grounds that common sense was never subpoenaed!

title: Modeling the Real World with High-Density Visual Particle Dynamics
abstract: We present High-Density Visual Particle Dynamics (HD-VPD), a  learned world model that can emulate the physical dynamics of real scenes by processing massive latent point clouds containing 100K+ particles. To enable efficiency at this scale, we introduce a novel family of Point Cloud Transformers (PCTs) called Interlacers leveraging intertwined linear-attention Performer layers and graph-based neighbour attention layers. We demonstrate the capabilities of HD-VPD by modeling the dynamics of high degree-of-freedom bi-manual robots with two RGB-D cameras. Compared to the previous graph neural network approach, our Interlacer dynamics is twice as fast with the same prediction quality, and can achieve higher quality using 4x as many particles. We illustrate how HD-VPD can evaluate motion plan quality with robotic box pushing and can grasping tasks. See videos and particle dynamics rendered by HD-VPD at https://sites.google.com/view/hd-vpd.
keywords: point clouds;particle dynamics;world models for control;Performers
author: William F Whitney;Jake Varley;Deepali Jain;Krzysztof Marcin Choromanski;Sumeet Singh;Vikas Sindhwani
aff: Google DeepMind;Google;Google;Google Brain Robotics & Columbia University;Google Brain Robotics;Google
position: Researcher;Engineer;Researcher;research scientist & adjunct assistant professor;Researcher;Senior Staff Research Scientist

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a simulator that imagines the entire world as a swarm of 100,000 tiny digital dots to help robots predict what happens when they touch stuff. It uses a fancy 'Interlacer' math trick to process these dots faster, theoretically letting a robot 'see' physics before it actually makes a mess.
why_bad: Incremental Architectural Novelty: The 'Interlacer' is essentially just a sandwich of two existing techniques (Performers and GNNs) without a convincing theoretical reason why this specific combo is the 'secret sauce' rather than just more compute.

The 'So What' Baseline Problem: Being twice as fast as a standard Graph Neural Network is like being the fastest runner in a retirement home—GNNs are notoriously slow at scale, so beating them doesn't prove this is actually 'efficient.'

Physics Hallucinations: While the dots look pretty, the paper fails to prove the model respects actual conservation laws, meaning the robot might be 'predicting' a world where boxes occasionally turn into ghosts or teleport through fingers.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where every object is made of a hundred thousand microscopic, vibrating marbles. ONE ROBOT... must learn to push a cardboard box without causing a glitch in the Matrix. Witness the 'Interlacer'—a technology so complex it uses two different types of math just to decide which marble is touching which. See the breathtaking 100K particle resolution! Ignore the fact that it still struggles with basic grasping! It’s 'Pointillism: The Reckoning'—coming soon to a rejection pile near you. Rated R for 'Redundant Computation'.

title: Real-to-Sim Grasp: Rethinking the Gap between Simulation and Real World in Grasp Detection
abstract: For 6-DoF grasp detection, simulated data is expandable to train more powerful model, but it faces the challenge of the large gap between simulation and real world. Previous works bridge this gap with a sim-to-real way. However, this way explicitly or implicitly forces the simulated data to adapt to the noisy real data when training grasp detectors, where the positional drift and structural distortion within the camera noise will harm the grasp learning. In this work, we propose a Real-to-Sim framework for 6-DoF Grasp detection, named R2SGrasp, with the key insight of bridging this gap in a real-to-sim way, which directly bypasses the camera noise in grasp detector training through an inference-time real-to-sim adaption. To achieve this real-to-sim adaptation, our R2SGrasp designs the Real-to-Sim Data Repairer (R2SRepairer) to mitigate the camera noise of real depth maps in data-level, and the Real-to-Sim Feature Enhancer (R2SEnhancer) to enhance real features with precise simulated geometric primitives in feature-level. To endow our framework with the generalization ability, we construct a large-scale simulated dataset cost-efficiently to train our grasp detector, which includes 64,000 RGB-D images with 14.4 million grasp annotations. Sufficient experiments show that R2SGrasp is powerful and our real-to-sim perspective is effective. The real-world experiments further show great generalization ability of R2SGrasp. Project page is available on https://isee-laboratory.github.io/R2SGrasp.
author: Jia-Feng Cai;Zibo Chen;Xiao-Ming Wu;Jian-Jian Jiang;Yi-Lin Wei;Wei-Shi Zheng
aff: SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;Macquarie University;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY;SUN YAT-SEN UNIVERSITY
position: MS student;MS student;Intern;MS student;Full Professor;PhD student

roast.laymen_summary: Most robot researchers try to make simulated training data look as messy as the real world, but this team decided to do the opposite by trying to make the real world look as perfect as a simulation. They built a digital 'Botox' filter to smooth out grainy camera footage so the robot can pretend it's still in a pristine virtual environment while trying to grab stuff.
why_bad: The 'Real-to-Sim' approach is essentially just fancy image denoising rebranded as a paradigm shift, lacking the theoretical novelty required for a top-tier venue.
The 'Repairer' and 'Enhancer' modules add significant computational overhead during inference, making the robot's reaction time about as fast as a sloth on a Sunday afternoon.

The evaluation metrics are suspiciously focused on their own custom dataset, conveniently side-stepping direct comparisons with industry-standard Sim-to-Real benchmarks that would likely expose the method's fragility.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: AI Edition.' Imagine a chef who only learned to cook in a high-end IKEA catalog where every surface is sterile and the lighting is perfect. When he's dropped into a real, grimy New York diner, he panics because the steam is blurring his vision. Instead of learning to cook through the steam like a pro, the authors suggest he wears a pair of $3,000 AR goggles that digitally replace the greasy, flickering kitchen with a 4K Pixar render of a kitchen. Sure, the goggles make the stove look 'cleaner,' but the chef is now so busy processing the digital 'repair' of his vision that he doesn't realize the actual burger is already on fire. It’s a brilliant way to make a simple task incredibly complicated while ignoring the fact that, eventually, you just have to learn to work in the smoke.

title: Open-TeleVision: Teleoperation with Immersive Active Visual Feedback
abstract: Teleoperation serves as a powerful method for collecting on-robot data essential for robot learning from demonstrations. The intuitiveness and ease of use of the teleoperation system are crucial for ensuring high-quality, diverse, and scalable data. To achieve this, we propose an immersive teleoperation system $\textbf{Open-TeleVision}$ that allows operators to actively perceive the robot's surroundings in a stereoscopic manner. Additionally, the system mirrors the operator's arm and hand movements on the robot, creating an immersive experience as if the operator's mind is transmitted to a robot embodiment. We validate the effectiveness of our system by collecting data and training imitation learning policies on four long-horizon, precise tasks (can sorting, can insertion, folding, and unloading) for 2 different humanoid robots and deploy them in the real world. The entire system will be open-sourced.
author: Xuxin Cheng;Jialong Li;Shiqi Yang;Ge Yang;Xiaolong Wang
aff: University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;University of California, San Diego
aff_unique_norm: University of California, San Diego;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: This system lets humans wear a VR headset to 'pilot' robots like mechanical avatars, seeing in 3D while the robot mimics their arm and hand gestures. The goal is to make it easier for humans to teach robots how to do chores by literally showing them the ropes in first-person.
why_bad: Incremental Innovation: Reviewers likely felt that sticking a VR headset on a human to control a robot is a well-worn trope that lacked a significant 'lightbulb' moment in algorithmic novelty.
Hardware Bottlenecks: The system relies heavily on specific, expensive setups, making the 'Open' part of the title feel like 'Open, provided you have a spare humanoid and a high-end server rack.'
Evaluation Gaps: While folding towels and sorting cans is cute, the paper failed to prove the system could handle complex, unpredictable environments beyond its own highly controlled lab playground.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Iron Chef: Remote Control Edition!' Open-TeleVision is like trying to win a Michelin star by wearing a GoPro and using two 6-foot-long grabber tools to cook a delicate soufflé from the parking lot. You're telling the judges you've revolutionized cooking because you can finally see the eggs in 3D, but the judges are pointing out that you're still just a guy with fancy tongs and the soufflé is collapsing because your 'immersive' connection has the lag of a cross-continental Zoom call. You didn't invent a new way to bake; you just bought a more expensive remote, and the kitchen is still a mess.

title: Learning Long-Horizon Action Dependencies in Sampling-Based Bilevel Planning
abstract: Autonomous robots will need the ability to make task and motion plans that involve long sequences of actions, e.g. to prepare a meal. One challenge is that the feasibility of actions late in the plan may depend on much earlier actions. This issue is exacerbated if these dependencies exist at a purely geometric level, making them difficult to express for a task planner. Backtracking is a common technique to resolve such geometric dependencies, but its time complexity limits its applicability to short-horizon dependencies. We propose an approach to account for these dependencies by learning a search heuristic for task and motion planning. We evaluate our approach on five quasi-static simulated domains and show a substantial improvement in success rate  over the baselines.
keywords: task and motion planning;long-horizon;learning for planning
author: Bartłomiej Cieślar;Leslie Pack Kaelbling;Tomás Lozano-Pérez;Jorge Mendez-Mendez
aff: Imperial College London;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Massachusetts Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: Imperial College London;Massachusetts Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot that if it puts a pot in the wrong spot now, it won't be able to reach the stove ten minutes later. They used a fancy AI 'guessing' tool to help the robot stop wasting time trying every single wrong move until it finds a right one.
why_bad: The 'quasi-static simulated domains' are glorified toy boxes that don't account for real-world physics or objects that actually move.
The learning heuristic is likely just memorizing the specific layouts of the training rooms rather than actually 'understanding' spatial logic.
The paper fails to prove the system can handle a scenario it hasn't seen before, making it a very expensive way to solve the same five puzzles.
roast.analogy_name: The Butterfly Effect: Kitchen Nightmare Edition
roast.analogy_description: In a world where placing a spoon at a 45-degree angle makes it impossible to ever open the fridge again, one robot tries to beat the odds. It’s spent thousands of hours in a digital trance, learning that the secret to a three-course meal is... never moving the wrong way once. But when a real human leaves a sock on the floor, the system collapses. Coming this fall: *Overfit & Over-Engineered*—it’s not a plan, it’s a prayer with a neural network.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized for peer-review catharsis.

title: Scaling Robot Policy Learning via Zero-Shot Labeling with Foundation Models
abstract:
A central challenge towards developing robots that can relate human language to their perception and actions is the scarcity of natural language annotations in diverse robot datasets. Moreover, robot policies that follow natural language instructions are typically trained on either templated language or expensive human-labeled instructions, hindering their scalability. 
To this end, we introduce NILS: Natural language Instruction Labeling for Scalability. NILS automatically labels uncurated, long-horizon robot data at scale in a zero-shot manner without any human intervention.
NILS combines pre-trained vision-language foundation models in a sophisticated, carefully considered manner in order to detect objects in a scene, detect object-centric changes, segment tasks from 
large datasets of unlabelled interaction data and ultimately label behavior datasets.
Evaluations on BridgeV2 and a kitchen play dataset show that NILS is able to autonomously annotate diverse robot demonstrations of unlabeled and unstructured datasets, while alleviating several shortcomings of crowdsourced human annotations.
author: Nils Blank;Moritz Reuss;Marcel Rühle;Ömer Erdinç Yağmurlu;Fabian Wenzel;Oier Mees;Rudolf Lioutikov
aff: Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department, University of California, Berkeley;Karlsruher Institut für Technologie
position: PhD student;PhD student;MS student;MS student;MS student;Postdoc;Tenure-Track Professor
aff_unique_norm: Karlsruher Institut für Technologie;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to skip the boring task of paying humans to label robot videos by letting big 'Foundation Models' (like GPT-4 for eyes) do it instead. The goal was to create an infinite supply of training data by having one AI guess what another AI is doing in a video, hopefully teaching robots to understand human commands without us lifting a finger.
why_bad: The 'Blind Leading the Blind' Problem: Foundation models often misidentify objects or actions in low-quality robot footage, meaning the robot is essentially learning from a teacher who is hallucinating half the curriculum.

Temporal Illiteracy: While the models are great at looking at a single photo, they struggle to understand the 'process' of a task, often labeling the middle of a motion as the end or missing the crucial physical nuances that make a robot actually successful.

Circular Knowledge: The paper fails to prove that this 'sophisticated' labeling actually makes the robot better at new things, rather than just reinforcing the existing biases and mistakes already present in the pre-trained models they started with.
roast.analogy_description: [Deep, gravelly movie trailer voice] IN A WORLD where interns are too expensive... ONE ROBOT ARM must learn to cook. But there is no teacher. There is only... THE NARRATOR: An AI who has read a million recipes but has never actually seen a spatula. WATCH as the Narrator watches a silent, blurry video of a robot flailing and calls it 'Masterfully Seasoning a Steak.' FEEL THE TENSION as the robot tries to replicate the 'masterpiece' by following instructions written by a bot that's currently tripping on its own training data. It’s a feedback loop of errors. It’s a recipe for a kitchen fire. Coming this summer: THE BLIND LEADING THE BLIND.

title: In-Flight Attitude Control of a Quadruped using Deep Reinforcement Learning
abstract: We present the development and real world demonstration of an in-flight attitude control law for a small low-cost quadruped with a five-bar-linkage leg design using only its legs as reaction masses. The control law is trained using deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and specifically through Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in the NVIDIA Omniverse Isaac Sim simulator with a GPU-accelerated DRL pipeline. To demonstrate the policy, a small quadruped is designed, constructed, and evaluated both on a rotating pole test setup and in free fall. During a free fall of 0.7 seconds, the quadruped follows commanded attitude steps of 45 degrees in all principal axes, and achieves an average base angular velocity of 110 degrees per second during large attitude reference steps.
author: Tarek El-Agroudi;Finn Gross Maurer;Jørgen Anker Olsen;Kostas Alexis
aff: Norwegian University of Science and Technology;Norwegian University of Science and Technology;Norwegian University of Science and Technology;Norwegian University of Science and Technology
aff_unique_norm: Norwegian University of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: We spent thousands of hours in a high-tech simulation teaching a budget robot dog to wiggle its legs fast enough to change its orientation while falling. Essentially, it’s a mechanical cat imitation that uses complex math to avoid a humiliating face-plant during a sub-one-second drop.
why_bad: The 0.7-second test window is so short that the robot barely has time to 'control' anything before physics takes over and ends the experiment.
Using spindly robot legs as 'reaction masses' is incredibly inefficient, akin to trying to steer a kayak by waving your hands in the air.
The sim-to-real gap is glaring; the policy works perfectly in the digital 'Matrix' but looks suspiciously like a frantic seizure when gravity actually kicks in.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where gravity is an unforgiving mistress, one budget quadruped is about to face its greatest fear: the floor. [BASS DROP] He wasn't born with a tail. He wasn't built with flywheels. But he has... Deep Reinforcement Learning. Watch as our hero spends an eternity in a GPU-accelerated purgatory just to learn how to flail his limbs with 'attitude.' It’s a 0.7-second race against destiny where 'advanced control laws' look a lot like a toddler falling off a bunk bed. This summer, witness the triumph of math over common sense in: THE GINGERLY YEET.
roast.tone_check: Expertly snarky, scientifically grounded.

title: NOD-TAMP: Generalizable Long-Horizon Planning with Neural Object Descriptors
abstract: Solving complex manipulation tasks in household and factory settings remains challenging due to long-horizon reasoning, fine-grained interactions, and broad object and scene diversity. Learning skills from demonstrations can be an effective strategy, but such methods often have limited generalizability beyond training data and struggle to solve long-horizon tasks. To overcome this, we propose to synergistically combine two paradigms: Neural Object Descriptors (NODs) that produce generalizable object-centric features and Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) frameworks that chain short-horizon skills to solve multi-step tasks. We introduce NOD-TAMP, a TAMP-based framework that extracts short manipulation trajectories from a handful of human demonstrations, adapts these trajectories using NOD features, and composes them to solve broad long-horizon, contact-rich tasks. NOD-TAMP solves existing manipulation benchmarks with a handful of demonstrations and significantly outperforms prior NOD-based approaches on new tabletop manipulation tasks that require diverse generalization. Finally, we deploy NOD-TAMP on a number of real-world tasks, including tool-use and high-precision insertion. For more details, please visit https://nodtamp.github.io/.
author: Shuo Cheng;Caelan Reed Garrett;Ajay Mandlekar;Danfei Xu
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;NVIDIA

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that learns chores by watching a few human videos and then using a 'connect-the-dots' map to stitch those actions together. It's essentially trying to give a robot common sense by forcing it to memorize a few play-by-play highlights and hoping it can improvise during the actual game.
why_bad: Lack of Novelty: The paper essentially glues two existing tools together (NODs and TAMP) without providing a significant 'secret sauce' or theoretical breakthrough that justifies the merger.

The Generalization Gap: While it claims to handle 'diverse' tasks, the system is still heavily tethered to specific human demonstrations, making it more of a fancy mimic than a truly flexible thinker.
Weak Baselines: The evaluation compared the system against outdated or strawman versions of AI, failing to prove it can outrun the current state-of-the-art heavyweights in the field.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Scripted Sous-Chef
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution will prove that the defendant, NOD-TAMP, is not the 'generalizable genius' they claim to be. They walked into a kitchen, watched a master chef flip exactly three pancakes, and now demand a Michelin star. When the defense says 'long-horizon planning,' they really mean 'following a rigid recipe'—and God help us all if the spatula is moved two inches to the left. They’ve essentially stapled a dictionary to a GPS and called it a novelist. Your Honor, this isn't innovation; it's just high-budget plagiarism with a robot arm, and it crumbles the moment it encounters a task that wasn't in the original script!

title: MimicTouch: Leveraging Multi-modal Human Tactile Demonstrations for Contact-rich Manipulation
abstract: Tactile sensing is critical to fine-grained, contact-rich manipulation tasks, such as insertion and assembly. Prior research has shown the possibility of learning tactile-guided policy from teleoperated demonstration data. However, to provide the demonstration, human users often rely on visual feedback to control the robot. This creates a gap between the sensing modality used for controlling the robot (visual) and the modality of interest (tactile). To bridge this gap, we introduce "MimicTouch'', a novel framework for learning policies directly from demonstrations provided by human users with their hands. The key innovations are i) a human tactile data collection system which collects multi-modal tactile dataset for learning human's tactile-guided control strategy, ii) an imitation learning-based framework for learning human's tactile-guided control strategy through such data, and iii) an online residual RL framework to bridge the embodiment gap between the human hand and the robot gripper. Through comprehensive experiments, we highlight the efficacy of utilizing human's tactile-guided control strategy to resolve contact-rich manipulation tasks. The project website is at https://sites.google.com/view/MimicTouch.
keywords: Tactile Sensing;Learning from Human;Data Collection;Imitation Learning;Reinforcement Learning
author: Kelin Yu;Yunhai Han;Qixian Wang;Vaibhav Saxena;Danfei Xu;Ye Zhao
aff: Georgia Institute of Technology;Georgia Institute of Technology;;Georgia Institute of Technology;NVIDIA;Georgia Institute of Technology
position: MS student;PhD student;;PhD student;Research Scientist;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to teach robots how to handle delicate tasks by letting them 'feel' the world through human tactile data instead of just watching videos. Since a robot gripper isn't a human hand, they added a secondary AI system to translate those human 'vibes' into machine movements.
why_bad: The 'embodiment gap' is massive; human fingers have thousands of sensors while a robot claw is essentially a pair of glorified pliers, making the data transfer nearly impossible.
The 'Residual RL' component is doing the heavy lifting, essentially acting as a digital crutch that fixes the mistakes the imitation learning shouldn't have made in the first place.
The hardware setup is so hyper-specific and complex that it’s more of a bespoke art installation than a scalable scientific framework.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where robots have the grace of a forklift, one team has a radical idea: record a world-class pianist’s finger movements and upload them... into a lobster. *Inception-style BWAAAH sound.* Watch as the lobster tries to play Mozart with its giant, meaty claws, while a scientist off-camera desperately uses a remote control to stop it from snapping the ivory keys. It’s a high-stakes drama of 'Sensory Mismatch' where the feeling is right, but the fingers are literally seafood.

title: ReMix: Optimizing Data Mixtures for Large Scale Imitation Learning
abstract: Increasingly large robotics datasets are being collected to train larger foundation models in robotics. However, despite the fact that data selection has been of utmost importance to scaling in vision and natural language processing (NLP), little work in robotics has questioned what data such models should actually be trained on. In this work we investigate how to weigh different subsets or ``domains'' of robotics datasets during pre-training to maximize worst-case performance across all possible downstream domains using distributionally robust optimization (DRO). Unlike in NLP, we find that these methods are hard to apply out of the box due to varying action spaces and dynamics across robots. Our method, ReMix, employs early stopping and action normalization and discretization to counteract these issues. Through extensive experimentation on both the Bridge and OpenX datasets, we demonstrate that data curation can have an outsized impact on downstream performance. Specifically, domain weights learned by ReMix outperform uniform weights by over 40\% on average and human-selected weights by over 20\% on datasets used to train the RT-X models.
author: Joey Hejna;Chethan Anand Bhateja;Yichen Jiang;Karl Pertsch;Dorsa Sadigh
aff: ;University of California, Berkeley;;Stanford University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to stop robots from learning bad habits by selectively choosing which parts of a massive, messy dataset to actually pay attention to. Instead of treating every robot video as equally important, they used a mathematical filter to prioritize the data that actually helps the AI survive real-world chaos.
why_bad: The 'breakthrough' relies heavily on action discretization, a common trick that simplifies the math but often results in robots that move with the fluid grace of a stop-motion nightmare.
Beating 'uniform weighting' is an embarrassingly low bar; it's the academic equivalent of bragging that your new diet is healthier than eating literal garbage.
The method effectively treats the fundamental physical differences between robots as a 'noise' problem that can be solved with a spreadsheet rather than addressing the actual robotics.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where one robot is a master sushi chef and the other is a literal toaster, one team of scientists thought they could blend their brains into a single super-genius. But when the chef uses grams and the toaster uses 'settings 1 through 5,' the result isn't a Michelin star—it's a kitchen fire. This summer, watch as 'ReMix' tries to save the meal by throwing away the burnt toast halfway through and pretending everyone is using the same measuring cup. It’s not 'Artificial Intelligence'... it's just a very expensive way to pick the least-worst leftovers.

title: DexCatch: Learning to Catch Arbitrary Objects with Dexterous Hands
abstract: Achieving human-like dexterous manipulation remains a crucial area of research in robotics. Current research focuses on improving the success rate of pick-and-place tasks. Compared with pick-and-place, throwing-catching behavior has the potential to increase the speed of transporting objects to their destination. However, dynamic dexterous manipulation poses a major challenge for stable control due to a large number of dynamic contacts. In this paper, we propose a Learning-based framework for Throwing-Catching tasks using dexterous hands (LTC). Our method, LTC, achieves a 73% success rate across 45 scenarios (diverse hand poses and objects), and the learned policies demonstrate strong zero-shot transfer performance on unseen objects. Additionally, in tasks where the object in hand faces sideways, an extremely unstable scenario due to the lack of support from the palm, all baselines fail, while our method still achieves a success rate of over 60%.
author: Fengbo Lan;Shengjie Wang;Yunzhe Zhang;Haotian Xu;Oluwatosin OluwaPelumi Oseni;Ziye Zhang;Yang Gao;Tao Zhang
aff: ;Tsinghua University;;Tsinghua University;;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to turn a high-tech robot hand into a Major League catcher so we can throw objects across the room instead of walking. While it handles 'sideways' objects better than previous bots, it still fumbles its catch nearly 30% of the time, making it less of an athlete and more of a clumsy toddler.
why_bad: A 73% success rate is statistically impressive for a weather forecast, but for a robot catching a fragile object, it’s just an expensive way to break your stuff.
Testing on only 45 scenarios is the scientific equivalent of saying you're a world-class chef because you can cook exactly three types of eggs.
The 'zero-shot transfer' claims are suspiciously bold given that the framework likely collapses the moment it encounters a shape that wasn't in the researchers' curated simulation.
roast.analogy_name: The Trial of the Butterfingered Butler
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense would have you believe that their client is a master of 'dexterous manipulation,' yet we have evidence of him dropping three out of every ten dinner plates! The prosecution argues that a 73% success rate isn't a 'breakthrough'—it’s a liability. The defendant claims he can catch 'arbitrary objects,' but when cross-examined on his performance with a sideways-facing saucer, he admitted to failing 40% of the time. Members of the jury, would you hire a butler who zero-shot transfers your heirloom Ming vase directly onto the hardwood floor? I move to strike this entire paper from the record on the grounds of gross over-optimism!

title: RiEMann: Near Real-Time SE(3)-Equivariant Robot Manipulation without Point Cloud Segmentation
abstract: We present RiEMann, an end-to-end near Real-time SE(3)-Equivariant Robot Manipulation imitation learning framework from scene point cloud input. Compared to previous methods that rely on descriptor field matching, RiEMann directly predicts the target actions for manipulation without any object segmentation. RiEMann can efficiently train the visuomotor policy from scratch with 5 to 10 demonstrations for a manipulation task, generalizes to unseen SE(3) transformations and instances of target objects, resists visual interference of distracting objects, and follows the near real-time pose change of the target object. The scalable SE(3)-equivariant action space of RiEMann supports both pick-and-place tasks and articulated object manipulation tasks. In simulation and real-world 6-DOF robot manipulation experiments, we test RiEMann on 5 categories of manipulation tasks with a total of 25 variants and show that RiEMann outperforms baselines in both task success rates and SE(3) geodesic distance errors (reduced by 68.6%), and achieves 5.4 frames per second (fps) network inference speed.
author: Chongkai Gao;Zhengrong Xue;Shuying Deng;Tianhai Liang;Siqi Yang;Lin Shao;Huazhe Xu
aff: National University of Singapore;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen;Tsinghua University;National University of Singapore;Tsinghua University
position: PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: National University of Singapore;Tsinghua University;Harbin Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to teach robot arms how to pick up and move objects by looking at a messy 'cloud' of 3D points without needing to manually label what's what. By using fancy geometry math, the researchers claim the robot can understand an object even if it's flipped or rotated, requiring only a handful of human demonstrations to learn a new trick.
why_bad: The authors claim 'near real-time' performance at 5.4 FPS, which in robotics terms is basically 'calculating the meaning of life' while the object has already moved three inches away.
Relying on SE(3)-equivariance is a massive mathematical flex that often crumbles when the 'target object' isn't a perfectly rigid, simple shape—like trying to use a compass to navigate a ball pit.

The 'no segmentation' approach sounds great until the robot tries to pick up a coffee mug but accidentally grabs the table it's sitting on because it can't tell where the ceramics end and the wood begins.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a goalie who has spent his entire life studying the physics of a soccer ball from every possible angle—he’s a geometry god, folks! But here’s the kicker: his brain only refreshes five times a second. The striker kicks the ball, and our goalie is still processing the beautiful SE(3) symmetry of the initial rotation while the ball is already vibrating the back of the net. He’s got the most efficient training camp in the league, needing only ten practice shots to 'get it,' but in a live game, he’s playing in stop-motion. He’s mathematically elegant, sure, but he’s got the reaction time of a sloth on a Sunday afternoon. It's a bold strategy, Cotton, let's see if it pays—oh, wait, he just dropped the trophy because he couldn't segment it from his own glove.

title: Continuously Improving Mobile Manipulation with Autonomous Real-World RL
abstract: We present a fully autonomous real-world RL framework for mobile manipulation that can learn policies without extensive instrumentation or human supervision. This is enabled by 1) task-relevant autonomy, which guides exploration towards object interactions and prevents stagnation near goal states, 2) efficient policy learning by leveraging basic task knowledge in behavior priors, and 3) formulating generic rewards that combine human-interpretable semantic information with low-level, fine-grained observations. We demonstrate that our approach allows Spot robots to continually improve their performance on a set of four challenging mobile manipulation tasks, obtaining an average success rate of 80% across tasks, a 3-4 times improvement over existing approaches. Videos can be found at https://continual-mobile-manip.github.io/.
author: Russell Mendonca;Emmanuel Panov;Bernadette Bucher;Jiuguang Wang;Deepak Pathak
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;;Boston Dynamics AI Institute;;Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Boston Dynamics AI Institute

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot dog to pick up objects on its own by giving it a 'behavioral cheat sheet' and letting it practice in the real world. While it eventually succeeded 80% of the time, it basically required a highly specific set of rules and 'generic' rewards that are actually just fancy ways of saying the researchers held its hand the whole time.
why_bad: The 'autonomous' label is misleading because the system relies on heavy-handed behavior priors—essentially hard-coding the 'soul' out of the reinforcement learning process.
The reward function is a fragile, hand-tuned mess of semantic data and low-level sensors that likely won't work if you change the room's lighting or move a chair.
An 80% success rate in a controlled lab setting is the academic equivalent of a 'C- minus'; in the real world, that 20% failure rate means your robot is eventually going to drive through a drywall.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the GPS-Guided Explorer
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the defense claims their client 'discovered' the path to the objective through 'autonomous exploration.' Yet, we find a GPS unit—labeled 'Behavior Priors'—stashed in the defendant's pocket, programmed with the exact coordinates of every turn. You call this Reinforcement Learning; I call it following a map and claiming you're Magellan. Furthermore, the prosecution submits that an 80% success rate for a 70-pound mechanical beast is not a breakthrough—it is a public safety hazard. If a witness only tells the truth 80% of the time, we call it perjury; when your robot fails 20% of the time, we call it a lawsuit. I move to strike this 'fully autonomous' claim from the record until the defendant can find the kitchen without a pre-written script and a dozen 'generic' participation trophies.

title: Mobility VLA: Multimodal Instruction Navigation with Long-Context VLMs and Topological Graphs
abstract: An elusive goal in navigation research is to build an intelligent agent that can understand multimodal instructions including natural language and image, and perform useful navigation. To achieve this, we study a widely useful category of navigation tasks we call Multimodal Instruction Navigation with demonstration Tours (MINT), in which the environment prior is provided through a previously recorded demonstration video. Recent advances in Vision Language Models (VLMs) have shown a promising path in achieving this goal as it demonstrates capabilities in perceiving and reasoning about multimodal inputs. However, VLMs are typically trained to predict textual output and it is an open research question about how to best utilize them in navigation. To solve MINT, we present Mobility VLA, a hierarchical Vision-Language-Action (VLA) navigation policy that combines the environment understanding and common sense reasoning power of long-context VLMs and a robust low-level navigation policy based on topological graphs. The high-level policy consists of a long-context VLM that takes the demonstration tour video and the multimodal user instruction as input to find the goal frame in the tour video. Next, a low-level policy uses the goal frame and an offline constructed topological graph to generate robot actions at every timestep. We evaluated Mobility VLA in a 836$m^2$ real world environment and show that Mobility VLA has a high end-to-end success rates on previously unsolved multimodal instructions such as ``Where should I return this?'' while holding a plastic bin.
author: Zhuo Xu;Hao-Tien Lewis Chiang;Zipeng Fu;Mithun George Jacob;Tingnan Zhang;Tsang-Wei Edward Lee;Wenhao Yu;Connor Schenck;David Rendleman;Dhruv Shah;Fei Xia;Jasmine Hsu;Jonathan Hoech;Pete Florence;Sean Kirmani;Sumeet Singh;Vikas Sindhwani;Carolina Parada;Chelsea Finn;Peng Xu;Sergey Levine;Jie Tan
aff: Google DeepMind;Google Deepmind;Stanford University;;Google;;Google;;;UC Berkeley;Google;New York University;;Google;Google DeepMind;Google Brain Robotics;Google;Google DeepMind;Google;Google;Google;Google
position: Researcher;Researcher;PhD student;;Software Engineer;;Software Engineer;;;PhD student;Researcher;MS student;;Research Scientist;Researcher;Researcher;Senior Staff Research Scientist;Principal Researcher;Research Scientist;Researcher;Research Scientist;Research Scientist
aff_unique_norm: Google;DeepMind;Stanford University;University of California, Berkeley;New York University

roast.laymen_summary: We built a robot that watches a 'house tour' video so it can figure out where to put things based on what you're holding. It uses a giant AI brain to pick a specific frame from that video and then follows a digital breadcrumb trail to reach that spot.
why_bad: The system isn't actually 'intelligent' at navigation; it's just a glorified search engine that matches your current view to a video you already filmed for it.
The 'demonstration tour' requirement is a massive crutch—the robot is effectively helpless in any room where a human hasn't already held its hand and recorded a walkthrough.

The paper follows the 'Lego-block' research trope: slapping a trendy Large Language Model onto an old-school mapping technique without fixing the lag or improving how the two actually talk to each other.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where robots can't find the fridge without a 4K walkthrough, one machine dares to... look at a video you just took. Witness the pulse-pounding action of a $100,000 AI playing a high-stakes game of 'I Spy.' It sees you holding a dirty plate, scrolls frantically through its own 'Kitchen Tour' vlog to find the dishwasher, and then slowly bumps its way there like a toddler following a string. It’s not spatial awareness—it's just a very expensive way to retrace your own steps. Coming this summer: The Ghost of Google Maps Past.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: T$^2$SQNet: A Recognition Model for Manipulating Partially Observed Transparent Tableware Objects
abstract: Recognizing and manipulating transparent tableware from partial view RGB image observations is made challenging by the difficulty in obtaining reliable depth measurements of transparent objects.  In this paper we present the Transparent Tableware SuperQuadric Network (T$^2$SQNet), a neural network model that leverages a family of newly extended deformable superquadrics to produce low-dimensional, instance-wise and accurate 3D geometric representations of transparent objects from partial views.  As a byproduct and contribution of independent interest, we also present TablewareNet, a publicly available toolset of seven parametrized shapes based on our extended deformable superquadrics, that can be used to generate new datasets of tableware objects of diverse shapes and sizes. Experiments with T$^2$SQNet trained with TablewareNet show that T$^2$SQNet outperforms existing methods in recognizing transparent objects, in some cases by significant margins, and can be effectively used in robotic applications like decluttering and target retrieval.
author: Young Hun Kim;Seungyeon Kim;Yonghyeon Lee;Frank C. Park
aff: Seoul National University;Seoul National University;Korea Institute for Advanced Study;Seoul National University
aff_unique_norm: Seoul National University;Korea Institute for Advanced Study

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach robots how to see invisible glass objects by guessing their 3D shapes using stretchy, mathematical blobs called superquadrics. While the robot is great at imagining what a bowl should look like from a tiny glimpse, it still struggles when the 'invisible' object doesn't fit its pre-programmed mold.
why_bad: Superquadrics are essentially 'geometric potatoes'—they are too low-dimensional to accurately represent complex tableware like wine glass stems or coffee mug handles.

The model relies heavily on TablewareNet, a synthetic dataset of its own making, which often leads to 'sim-to-real' failure where the robot panics the moment it sees a real-world fingerprint or a glare.

The paper claims 'significant margins' over existing methods, but fails to acknowledge that the bar for 'seeing transparent things' is currently so low that beating it isn't necessarily a breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're trying to identify ghosts in a dark room using nothing but a handful of Play-Doh. Our startup, T$^2$SQNet, doesn't actually 'see' the ghost; it just throws a lump of mathematical clay at the air and hopes it sticks in the shape of a salad bowl. We’re pitching a future where your robot butler doesn't smash your crystal—not because it actually sees the glass, but because it’s really good at imagining 'blobs' where the glass might be. It’s not a 'vision failure,' it’s a 'minimalist geometric hallucination' for the modern kitchen.

title: Promptable Closed-loop Traffic Simulation
abstract: Simulation stands as a cornerstone for safe and efficient autonomous driving development. At its core a simulation system ought to produce realistic, reactive, and controllable traffic patterns. In this paper, we propose ProSim, a multimodal promptable closed-loop traffic simulation framework. ProSim allows the user to give a complex set of numerical, categorical or textual prompts to instruct each agent’s behavior and intention. ProSim then rolls out a traffic scenario in a closed-loop manner, modeling each agent’s interaction with other traffic participants. Our experiments show that ProSim achieves high prompt controllability given different user prompts, while reaching competitive performance on the Waymo Sim Agents Challenge when no prompt is given. To support research on promptable traffic simulation, we create ProSim-Instruct-520k, a multimodal prompt-scenario paired driving dataset with over 10M text prompts for over 520k real-world driving scenarios. We will release data, benchmark, and labeling tools of ProSim-Instruct-520k upon publication.
author: Shuhan Tan;Boris Ivanovic;Yuxiao Chen;Boyi Li;Xinshuo Weng;Yulong Cao;Philipp Kraehenbuehl;Marco Pavone
aff: NVIDIA;NVIDIA;University of California, Berkeley;NVIDIA;NVIDIA;Apple;Stanford University;California Institute of Technology
aff_unique_norm: NVIDIA;University of California, Berkeley;Apple;Stanford University;California Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'SimCity' for self-driving cars where you can tell the AI drivers to behave by texting them instructions like 'drive aggressively.' While it sounds like a digital puppet show, the goal was to create realistic traffic chaos to test how robot cars handle human unpredictability.
why_bad: The '10 million prompts' are likely just repetitive, auto-generated templates that offer zero linguistic diversity, making the 'multimodal' claim feel like a marketing stretch.
The agents suffer from 'instructional amnesia,' where they follow a prompt for three seconds before reverting to standard, boring AI pathfinding or simply driving into a digital ditch.
The paper fails to prove that prompting a car to 'be reckless' actually produces realistic reckless behavior rather than just random, physics-defying jittering.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a coach trying to run a complex flea-flicker play by yelling 'do sports things!' through a megaphone from the nosebleed section. ProSim is essentially trying to coordinate a 50-car pileup via SMS. It looks great in the pre-game warmups when no one is moving, but the moment the whistle blows and real-world physics kick in, the agents have the situational awareness of a Roomba on an ice rink. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, let’s see if telling a digital sedan to 'drive like a jerk' actually helps a Tesla navigate a four-way stop.

title: ManiWAV: Learning Robot Manipulation from In-the-Wild Audio-Visual Data
abstract: Audio signals provide rich information for the robot interaction and object properties through contact. These information can surprisingly ease the learning of contact-rich robot manipulation skills, especially when the visual information alone is ambiguous or incomplete. However, the usage of audio data in robot manipulation has been constrained to teleoperated demonstrations collected by either attaching a microphone to the robot or object, which significantly limits its usage in robot learning pipelines. In this work, we introduce ManiWAV: an 'ear-in-hand' data collection device to collect in-the-wild human demonstrations with synchronous audio and visual feedback, and a corresponding policy interface to learn robot manipulation policy directly from the demonstrations. We demonstrate the capabilities of our system through four contact-rich manipulation tasks that require either passively sensing the contact events and modes, or actively sensing the object surface materials and states. In addition, we show that our system can generalize to unseen in-the-wild environments, by learning from diverse in-the-wild human demonstrations. All data, code, and policy will be public.
author: Zeyi Liu;Cheng Chi;Eric Cousineau;Naveen Kuppuswamy;Benjamin Burchfiel;Shuran Song
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Toyota Research Institute;Toyota Research Institute;Dexterous Manipulation Group, Toyota Research Institute;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers essentially taped a microphone to a human’s wrist to prove that robots can learn chores better if they can 'hear' what they're doing. They hoped that by recording the sounds of scraping and clicking, a robot could finally figure out how to handle objects without fumbling them like a toddler in the dark.
why_bad: The 'novel' hardware is basically just a microphone on a stick, failing to justify why this couldn't be done with existing off-the-shelf sensors.
The human-to-robot transfer is shaky; human hands have different acoustics and movement patterns than metal grippers, making the 'ear-in-hand' data misleading for the actual robot.
The paper fails to prove the audio is actually doing the heavy lifting, leaving reviewers wondering if the robot is just using its eyes while the 'WAV' part is just expensive background noise.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a quarterback trying to lead a game-winning drive, but instead of practicing with his team, he’s just wearing AirPods and listening to a podcast of Tom Brady describing what a 'spiral' sounds like. The ManiWAV team is out here on the sidelines, screaming that the 'crunch' of the turf is the secret to a touchdown, while the robot is getting sacked because it doesn't actually know how to use its hands. It's a classic rookie mistake: you've got a highlight reel with a great soundtrack, but when the clock is ticking and the pressure is on, the robot is just standing there listening to the wind while the ball bounces off its head. Absolute goal-line fumble on the technical execution!

title: Splat-MOVER: Multi-Stage, Open-Vocabulary Robotic Manipulation via Editable Gaussian Splatting
abstract:
We present Splat-MOVER, a modular robotics stack for open-vocabulary
robotic manipulation, which leverages the editability of Gaussian Splatting (GSplat)
scene representations to enable multi-stage manipulation tasks. Splat-MOVER
consists of: (i) ASK-Splat, a GSplat representation that distills semantic and grasp
affordance features into the 3D scene. ASK-Splat enables geometric, semantic,
and affordance understanding of 3D scenes, which is critical for many robotics
tasks; (ii) SEE-Splat, a real-time scene-editing module using 3D semantic masking
and infilling to visualize the motions of objects that result from robot interactions
in the real-world. SEE-Splat creates a “digital twin” of the evolving environment
throughout the manipulation task; and (iii) Grasp-Splat, a grasp generation module
that uses ASK-Splat and SEE-Splat to propose affordance-aligned candidate grasps
for open-world objects. ASK-Splat is trained in real-time from RGB images
in a brief scanning phase prior to operation, while SEE-Splat and Grasp-Splat
run in real-time during operation. We demonstrate the superior performance of
Splat-MOVER in hardware experiments on a Kinova robot compared to two recent
baselines in four single-stage, open-vocabulary manipulation tasks. In addition, we
demonstrate Splat-MOVER in four multi-stage manipulation tasks, using the edited
scene to reflect changes due to prior manipulation stages, which is not possible
with existing baselines. Video demonstrations and the code for the project are 
available at https://splatmover.github.io.
