author: Olaolu Shorinwa;Johnathan Tucker;Aliyah Smith;Aiden Swann;Timothy Chen;Roya Firoozi;Monroe David Kennedy;Mac Schwager
aff: Stanford University;;;Stanford University;Stanford University;;Stanford University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to give robots 'imagination' by using a high-tech 3D photo technique called Gaussian Splatting to visualize tasks before doing them. The goal was to let the robot 'photoshop' its environment in real-time to predict where objects should go, rather than just bumping into things blindly.
why_bad: The 'real-time' claim is a mathematical fantasy; the computational overhead of re-rendering 3D splats makes the robot move with the urgency of a tectonic plate.
The 'digital twin' editing lacks physical grounding, meaning the robot 'imagines' moving an object without understanding pesky concepts like gravity, friction, or not clipping through solid matter.
The benchmarks are suspiciously narrow, comparing the system against outdated baselines that make a basic robotic arm look like a breakthrough in artificial intelligence.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the chef spends three hours using a LIDAR scanner to create a 4K 3D model of a frozen burrito. Before heating it up, they use a 'digital twin' simulation to 'visualize' the cheese melting, carefully 'inpainting' the steam for aesthetic effect. It looks incredible on the monitor, but the audience is starving because the 'real-time' rendering takes longer than actual molecular gastronomy. By the time the robot reaches for the microwave, the software crashes because it didn't realize the burrito's wrapper reflects light differently than the simulation predicted. It's a $50,000 solution to a problem that a hungry college student solves with a paper towel and 90 seconds.

title: Accelerating Visual Sparse-Reward Learning with Latent Nearest-Demonstration-Guided Explorations
abstract: Recent progress in deep reinforcement learning (RL) and computer vision enables artificial agents to solve complex tasks, including locomotion, manipulation, and video games from high-dimensional pixel observations. However, RL usually relies on domain-specific reward functions for sufficient learning signals, requiring expert knowledge. While vision-based agents could learn skills from only sparse rewards, exploration challenges arise. We present Latent Nearest-demonstration-guided Exploration (LaNE), a novel and efficient method to solve sparse-reward robot manipulation tasks from image observations and a few demonstrations. First, LaNE builds on the pre-trained DINOv2 feature extractor to learn an embedding space for forward prediction. Next, it rewards the agent for exploring near the demos, quantified by quadratic control costs in the embedding space. Finally, LaNE optimizes the policy for the augmented rewards with RL. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency in Robosuite simulation and enables under-an-hour RL training from scratch on a Franka Panda robot, using only a few demonstrations.
author: Ruihan Zhao;ufuk topcu;Sandeep P. Chinchali;Mariano Phielipp
aff: University of Texas at Austin;University of Texas, Austin;Intel Labs;University of Texas at Austin

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop a robot from aimlessly flailing its arms by showing it a few 'how-to' videos and rewarding it for staying close to those examples. It uses a massive pre-trained vision model to 'see,' making it learn tasks in under an hour instead of the usual eternity.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just gluing a pre-trained DINOv2 model to an existing imitation learning setup and calling it a breakthrough.
The 'Nearest-Demonstration' metric is a fancy way of saying the robot is a copycat that likely falls apart the moment the lighting changes or the table moves two inches.
The experimental baselines are suspiciously weak, making the 'state-of-the-art' claims feel like beating a toddler at a 100-meter dash.
roast.analogy_name: The Karaoke Mimic: Path to Mediocrity
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where robots are too dumb to find a door handle, one arm tries to win it all by watching a TikTok tutorial once. It doesn't actually understand 'physics' or 'objects'—it's just playing a high-stakes game of 'Follow the Leader' in a hallucinated math dimension. He's got the moves, he's got the DINOv2 vision, but if the demo isn't perfect, he's just a 50-thousand-dollar paperweight doing a bad cover version of 'Picking Up a Block.' Coming this fall to a rejected folder near you.

title: Bootstrapping Reinforcement Learning with Imitation for Vision-Based Agile Flight
abstract:
Learning visuomotor policies for agile quadrotor flight presents significant difficulties, primarily from inefficient policy exploration caused by high-dimensional visual inputs and the need for precise and low-latency control.
To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach that combines the performance of Reinforcement Learning (RL) and the sample efficiency of Imitation Learning (IL) in the task of vision-based autonomous drone racing.
While RL provides a framework for learning high-performance controllers through trial and error, it faces challenges with sample efficiency and computational demands due to the high dimensionality of visual inputs.
Conversely, IL efficiently learns from visual expert demonstrations, but it remains limited by the expert's performance and state distribution.
To overcome these limitations, our policy learning framework integrates the strengths of both approaches.
Our framework contains three phases: training a teacher policy using RL with privileged state information, distilling it into a student policy via IL, and adaptive fine-tuning via RL.
Testing in both simulated and real-world scenarios shows our approach can not only learn in scenarios where RL from scratch fails but also outperforms existing IL methods in both robustness and performance, successfully navigating a quadrotor through a race course using only visual information.
author: Jiaxu Xing;Angel Romero;Leonard Bauersfeld;Davide Scaramuzza
aff: Department of Informatics, University of Zurich, University of Zurich;;University of Zurich;

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a drone to fly like a pro by first letting a 'cheating' computer program learn with full knowledge of the world, then forcing a camera-equipped drone to copy its moves. It's essentially a high-tech version of 'monkey see, monkey do' where the monkey starts with a cheat sheet and ends with a participation trophy.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The 'three-phase' framework is essentially a rebranded greatest-hits collection of existing techniques from 2018, offering nothing fundamentally new to the field.

Weak Baselines: The authors conveniently compared their 'breakthrough' against outdated methods rather than the current state-of-the-art, which is the academic equivalent of winning a race against a toddler.

Sim-to-Real Handwaving: The real-world tests were conducted in a highly sterilized environment, failing to prove the system wouldn't have a nervous breakdown the moment it encountered a stray shadow or a light breeze.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Robot Kitchen.' Today, our contestant is trying to bake a Michelin-star soufflé. Instead of learning to crack an egg, they’re watching a video of Gordon Ramsay—except Gordon has X-ray vision and can see through the oven door (that’s the privileged teacher). Our contestant then tries to mimic Gordon's hand gestures while blindfolded and holding a camera (the student). Finally, they toss a pinch of salt on the charred remains and call it 'adaptive fine-tuning.' The judges are sending it back because they've seen this exact frozen dinner at every academic buffet for the last five years, and the 'secret sauce' is just store-bought ketchup.

title: Sim-to-Real Transfer via 3D Feature Fields for Vision-and-Language Navigation
abstract: Vision-and-language navigation (VLN) enables the agent to navigate to a remote location in 3D environments following the natural language instruction. In this field, the agent is usually trained and evaluated in the navigation simulators, lacking effective approaches for sim-to-real transfer. The VLN agents with only a monocular camera exhibit extremely limited performance, while the mainstream VLN models trained with panoramic observation, perform better but are difficult to deploy on most monocular robots. For this case, we propose a sim-to-real transfer approach to endow the monocular robots with panoramic traversability perception and panoramic semantic understanding, thus smoothly transferring the high-performance panoramic VLN models to the common monocular robots. In this work, the semantic traversable map is proposed to predict agent-centric navigable waypoints, and the novel view representations of these navigable waypoints are predicted through the 3D feature fields. These methods broaden the limited field of view of the monocular robots and significantly improve navigation performance in the real world. Our VLN system outperforms previous SOTA monocular VLN methods in R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks within the simulation environments and is also validated in real-world environments, providing a practical and high-performance solution for real-world VLN.
keywords: Vision-and-Language Navigation;3D Feature Fields;Semantic Traversable Map
author: Zihan Wang;Xiangyang Li;Jiahao Yang;Yeqi Liu;Shuqiang Jiang
aff: Chinese Academy of Sciences;Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences;;;Institute of Computing Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

roast.laymen_summary: We built a system that lets a robot with a single cheap camera pretend it has eyes in the back of its head so it can follow verbal directions. It uses complex 3D math to 'hallucinate' the parts of the room it can’t actually see, hoping it won't run into a wall in the process.
why_bad: The 3D feature field generation is computationally bloated, likely turning the robot's onboard processor into a space heater before it even finds the door.
The 'Sim-to-Real' bridge is held together by hope, as the model relies heavily on perfect simulation data that disintegrates the moment it encounters a real-world shadow or a messy carpet.
It attempts to solve the 'limited field of view' problem by inventing a complex hallucination engine instead of just putting a $20 wide-angle lens on the camera.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a quarterback trying to play the Super Bowl while looking through a cardboard toilet paper roll. Instead of just turning his head, he’s using a high-powered supercomputer in his helmet to 'mathematically predict' where the defenders are based on the smell of the grass and the sound of the crowd. He looks like a genius in the pre-game VR simulation, but the second a real linebacker moves two inches off-script, he’s getting sacked into the stone age. It’s a world-class solution to a problem he created for himself by refusing to take the cardboard roll off his face.

title: Jacta: A Versatile Planner for Learning Dexterous and Whole-body Manipulation
abstract:
Robotic manipulation is challenging due to discontinuous dynamics, as well as high-dimensional state and action spaces. Data-driven approaches that succeed in manipulation tasks require large amounts of data and expert demonstrations, typically from humans. Existing planners are restricted to specific systems and often depend on specialized algorithms for using demonstrations. Therefore, we introduce a flexible motion planner tailored to dexterous and whole-body manipulation tasks. Our planner creates readily usable demonstrations for reinforcement learning algorithms, eliminating the need for additional training pipeline complexities. With this approach, we can efficiently learn policies for complex manipulation tasks, where traditional reinforcement learning alone only makes little progress. Furthermore, we demonstrate that learned policies are transferable to real robotic systems for solving complex dexterous manipulation tasks.

Project website: https://jacta-manipulation.github.io/
author: Jan Bruedigam;Ali Adeeb Abbas;Maks Sorokin;Kuan Fang;Brandon Hung;Maya Guru;Stefan Georg Sosnowski;Jiuguang Wang;Sandra Hirche;Simon Le Cleac'h
aff: Technische Universität München;;;;;;;;Technical University Munich;
aff_unique_norm: Technische Universität München;Technical University of Munich

roast.laymen_summary: Most robots are as clumsy as a toddler on roller skates unless a human spends months holding their hand. Jacta tries to fix this by letting the robot 'dream up' its own training videos, theoretically teaching itself how to use its hands without needing a human babysitter.
why_bad: The 'Versatile' label is doing a lot of heavy lifting for a system that mostly succeeds on narrow, carefully curated tasks.
The comparison benchmarks are 'strawmen'—they compared their shiny new tool against basic algorithms known to fail, rather than the actual industry leaders.
The jump from computer simulation to the real world is more of a stumble, with limited evidence that these 'skills' work outside of a controlled lab setting.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Forged Hall Pass
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense claims their client is a 'revolutionary self-taught scholar.' In reality, the defendant didn't learn the curriculum; they just built a very expensive machine to forge hall passes directly to the graduation ceremony. When the prosecution asked the defendant to solve a problem not found in their 'cheat sheet' demonstrations, the system collapsed like a house of cards. Furthermore, comparing this 'planner' to a basic Reinforcement Learning algorithm is like a professional athlete bragging about winning a race against a mannequin—it’s not a victory; it’s a lack of competition. We move to strike this 'versatility' claim from the record until the defendant can prove they can handle a screwdriver in a room that hasn't been perfectly stage-managed.

title: Twisting Lids Off with Two Hands
abstract: Manipulating objects with two multi-fingered hands has been a long-standing challenge in robotics, due to the contact-rich nature of many manipulation tasks and the complexity inherent in coordinating a high-dimensional bimanual system. In this work, we share novel insights into physical modeling, real-time perception, and reward design that enable policies trained in simulation using deep reinforcement learning (RL) to be effectively and efficiently transferred to the real world. Specifically, we consider the problem of twisting lids of various bottle-like objects with two hands, demonstrating policies with generalization capabilities across a diverse set of unseen objects as well as dynamic and dexterous behaviors. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first sim-to-real RL system that enables such capabilities on bimanual multi-fingered hands.
author: Toru Lin;Zhao-Heng Yin;Haozhi Qi;Pieter Abbeel;Jitendra Malik
aff: ;;University of California, Berkeley;Covariant;University of California, Berkeley
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 193 (char 837)

title: Structured Bayesian Meta-Learning for Data-Efficient Visual-Tactile Model Estimation
abstract: Estimating visual-tactile models of deformable objects is challenging because vision suffers from occlusion, while touch data is sparse and noisy.  We propose a novel data-efficient method for dense heterogeneous model estimation by leveraging experience from diverse training objects.  The method is based on Bayesian Meta-Learning (BML), which can mitigate overfitting high-capacity visual-tactile models by meta-learning an informed prior and naturally achieves few-shot online estimation via posterior estimation.  However, BML requires a shared parametric model across tasks but visual-tactile models for diverse objects have different parameter spaces.  To address this issue, we introduce Structured Bayesian Meta-Learning (SBML) that incorporates heterogeneous physics models, enabling learning from training objects with varying appearances and geometries.  SBML performs zero-shot vision-only prediction of deformable model parameters and few-shot adaptation after a handful of touches.  Experiments show that in two classes of heterogeneous objects, namely plants and shoes, SBML outperforms existing approaches in force and torque prediction accuracy in zero- and few-shot settings.
author: Shaoxiong Yao;Yifan Zhu;Kris Hauser
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign;Yale University
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;University of Illinois;Yale University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot how to handle squishy objects by having it 'guess' their texture based on past experiences with totally different items. Essentially, it looks at a shoe, remembers a fern it once poked, and hopes for the best until it actually touches the leather.
why_bad: The 'physics-based' model is likely just three glorified spring constants masquerading as a fundamental law of nature.
The dataset consists of plants and shoes—two categories with zero mechanical overlap—making the 'generalized learning' claim feel like a stretch.
Zero-shot prediction is basically just a high-tech way of saying the robot is making an educated guess based on the color green.
roast.analogy_name: The Blindfolded Gardener in a Foot Locker
roast.analogy_description: In a world where vision is blurry and fingers are numb, one robot must face its greatest challenge: poking a succulent without killing it. Witness the high-stakes drama as a machine looks at a Cactus, remembers a Reebok, and tries to calculate the 'squish factor' using math that barely passed high school physics. It’s a race against noise, occlusion, and common sense. Coming this summer: 'The Guess-timator'—where the physics are made up and the torque doesn't matter.

title: CoViS-Net: A Cooperative Visual Spatial Foundation Model for Multi-Robot Applications
abstract:
Autonomous robot operation in unstructured environments is often underpinned by spatial understanding through vision. Systems composed of multiple concurrently operating robots additionally require access to frequent, accurate and reliable pose estimates. Classical vision-based methods to regress relative pose are commonly computationally expensive (precluding real-time applications), and often lack data-derived priors for resolving ambiguities. In this work, we propose CoViS-Net, a cooperative, multi-robot visual spatial foundation model that learns spatial priors from data, enabling pose estimation as well as general spatial comprehension. Our model is fully decentralized, platform-agnostic, executable in real-time using onboard compute, and does not require existing networking infrastructure. CoViS-Net provides relative pose estimates and a local bird's-eye-view (BEV) representation, even without camera overlap between robots, and can predict BEV representations of unseen regions. We demonstrate its use in a multi-robot formation control task across various real-world settings. We provide supplementary material online and will open source our trained model in due course.
https://sites.google.com/view/covis-net
author: Jan Blumenkamp;Steven Morad;Jennifer Gielis;Amanda Prorok
aff: University of Cambridge;University of Cambridge;;

roast.laymen_summary: We built a system that lets a pack of robots guess where they are relative to each other by imagining what’s around them. It’s essentially high-tech 'trust me, bro' for drones that can’t actually see each other.
why_bad: The 'Foundation Model' label is a massive overreach for a system that only functions in highly specific, familiar environments.
The claim of working without camera overlap relies on 'spatial priors' that are essentially just lucky guesses when the robots hit a new room.
Evaluation was limited to basic formation flying, failing to prove the system can handle the actual chaos of an 'unstructured' real-world environment.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the AI Olympics, where CoViS-Net is attempting the 'Invisible Waltz.' The performers are claiming they can maintain a perfect formation while blindfolded, insisting they can 'sense' the stage layout through the power of collective imagination. But watch the replay: they aren't actually navigating; they’re just vibrating in unison and hoping they don't hit a wall. It’s a bold strategy to replace actual sensors with 'vibes,' but in this league, 'hallucinating the floor' usually ends with a pile of scrap metal and a very disappointed coach. They’ve got the 'foundation' of a sandcastle at high tide—pretty to look at, but structurally nonexistent.

title: Safe Bayesian Optimization for the Control of High-Dimensional Embodied Systems
abstract: Learning to move is a primary goal for animals and robots, where ensuring safety is often important when optimizing control policies on the embodied systems. For complex tasks such as the control of human or humanoid control, the high-dimensional parameter space adds complexity to the safe optimization effort. Current safe exploration algorithms exhibit inefficiency and may even become infeasible with large high-dimensional input spaces. Furthermore, existing high-dimensional constrained optimization methods neglect safety in the search process. In this paper, we propose High-dimensional Safe Bayesian Optimization with local optimistic exploration (HdSafeBO), a novel approach designed to handle high-dimensional sampling problems under probabilistic safety constraints. We introduce a local optimistic strategy to efficiently and safely optimize the objective function, providing a probabilistic safety guarantee and a cumulative safety violation bound. Through the use of isometric embedding, HdSafeBO addresses problems ranging from a few hundred to several thousand dimensions while maintaining safety guarantees. To our knowledge, HdSafeBO is the first algorithm capable of optimizing the control of high-dimensional musculoskeletal systems with high safety probability. We also demonstrate the real-world applicability of HdSafeBO through its use in the safe online optimization of neural stimulation-induced human motion control.
author: Yunyue Wei;Zeji Yi;Hongda Li;Saraswati Soedarmadji;Yanan Sui
aff: Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;California Institute of Technology;Tsinghua University
position: PhD student;MS student;PhD student;Undergrad student;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach robots and humans how to move using a remote control with 10,000 buttons without accidentally breaking any bones in the process. Our solution was to build a 'safety-first' AI that basically refuses to push any button it hasn't already obsessively googled first.
why_bad: The 'safety guarantee' relies on Gaussian Process math that is notorious for being a fragile pinky-swear when faced with messy, non-linear real-world physics.
Using isometric embedding is essentially 'computational squinting'—it simplifies the problem so much that you lose the very nuances required to actually control a complex musculoskeletal system.
The 'local optimistic exploration' is a fancy academic euphemism for being too scared to leave the starting line, meaning the AI never actually learns the cool moves it was built for.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where gravity is the enemy and every joint is a lawsuit waiting to happen... one team of researchers decided to teach a humanoid to dance. But there's a catch: the dancer is wearing a suit of armor made of fragile glass and the stage is a minefield. Watch as our protagonist, HdSafeBO, attempts to perform 'The Nutcracker' by only moving its left eyebrow and vibrating slightly, because anything else would violate its 'probabilistic safety constraints.' It’s a high-stakes thriller where the hero’s greatest power is his overwhelming fear of doing anything interesting. Coming this fall: The math is high-dimensional, but the ambition is strictly low-impact.
roast.tone_check: Lean, snarky, and computationally cynical.

title: Bi-Level Motion Imitation for Humanoid Robots
abstract: Imitation learning from human motion capture (MoCap) data provides a promising way to train humanoid robots. However, due to differences in morphology, such as varying degrees of joint freedom and force limits, exact replication of human behaviors may not be feasible for humanoid robots. Consequently, incorporating physically infeasible MoCap data in training datasets can adversely affect the performance of the robot policy. To address this issue, we propose a bi-level optimization-based imitation learning framework that alternates between optimizing both the robot policy and the target MoCap data. Specifically, we first develop a generative latent dynamics model using a novel self-consistent auto-encoder, which learns sparse and structured motion representations while capturing desired motion patterns in the dataset. The dynamics model is then utilized to generate reference motions while the latent representation regularizes the bi-level motion imitation process. Simulations conducted with a realistic model of a humanoid robot demonstrate that our method enhances the robot policy by modifying reference motions to be physically consistent.
author: Wenshuai Zhao;Yi Zhao;Joni Pajarinen;Michael Muehlebach
aff: Aalto University;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;Aalto University;Max-Planck Institute
aff_unique_norm: Aalto University;Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems;Max-Planck-Gesellschaft zur Förderung der Wissenschaften e.V.

roast.laymen_summary: Humanoid robots are terrible at copying human movements because their metal joints don't bend like our fleshy ones, making standard motion data physically impossible for them to follow. The researchers tried to build an AI filter that 'translates' human moves into robot-friendly instructions so the machine doesn't accidentally snap its own servos trying to do the Macarena.
why_bad: The 'bi-level optimization' approach is a computational nightmare that runs slower than a dial-up modem, making it impractical for any real-time application.
The 'novel self-consistent auto-encoder' is essentially a collection of 2018's favorite buzzwords rebranded as a breakthrough without offering any actual architectural innovation.
The paper relies entirely on 'realistic simulations,' which is academic shorthand for 'it works in a frictionless vacuum but the real robot would face-plant immediately.'
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're on a high-stakes cooking show where the challenge is to bake a five-tier French wedding cake, but your only ingredient is sawdust and your only tool is a chainsaw. This paper is the equivalent of writing a 20-page manual trying to 'optimize' the cake recipe so the chainsaw doesn't spray flour everywhere. You've built a 'Generative Latent Flour Model' to convince the chainsaw it's a whisk, but at the end of the day, you aren't making a cake—you're just making a very expensive, mathematically-validated pile of wet wood.

title: Context-Aware Replanning with Pre-Explored Semantic Map for Object Navigation
abstract: Pre-explored Semantic Map, constructed through prior exploration using visual language models (VLMs), has proven effective as a foundational element for training-free robotic applications. However, existing approaches assume the map's accuracy and do not provide effective mechanisms for revising decisions based on incorrect maps. This work introduces Context-Aware Replanning (CARe),, which estimates map uncertainty through confidence scores and multi-view consistency, enabling the agent to revise erroneous decisions stemming from inaccurate maps without additional labels. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method using two modern map backbones, VLMaps and OpenMask3D, and show significant improvements in performance on object navigation tasks.
author: Po-Chen Ko;Hung-Ting Su;CY Chen;Jia-Fong Yeh;Min Sun;Winston H. Hsu
aff: National Taiwan University;National Taiwan University;Sony Group Corporation;National Tsing Hua University;National Taiwan University;National Taiwan University
aff_unique_norm: National Taiwan University;Sony Group Corporation;National Tsing Hua University

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach robots that their internal maps are often hallucinating nonsense and that they shouldn't just blindly follow them. It's essentially a 'fact-checker' for robots that are too stubborn to admit they're lost in a kitchen.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially a glorified set of 'if-then' statements masquerading as a sophisticated uncertainty model.
The 'significant improvements' only show up if you squint really hard and ignore that the robot still moves with the speed of a tectonic plate.
It relies on pre-explored maps, which is like saying you've invented a great way to find your keys as long as you already know exactly where they were yesterday.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Robotics Bowl! Our agent is currently mid-play, staring at a microwave and convinced it's a bookshelf because its pre-loaded map says so. The authors call in the 'CARe' play—it’s like a backseat driver shouting 'Are you sure about that?' every five seconds. The robot pauses, runs a 'consistency check' that takes longer than a commercial break, and decides to maybe, possibly, turn left. It’s not 'context-aware navigation'; it’s a high-stakes game of 'I Think I'm Lost' played at 2 frames per second. The crowd is silent because they've seen this 'novel' uncertainty math in three other papers this season!

title: MBC: Multi-Brain Collaborative Control for Quadruped Robots
abstract: In the field of locomotion task of quadruped robots, Blind Policy and Perceptive Policy each have their own advantages and limitations. The Blind Policy relies on preset sensor information and algorithms, suitable for known and structured environments, but it lacks adaptability in complex or unknown environments. The Perceptive Policy uses visual sensors to obtain detailed environmental information, allowing it to adapt to complex terrains, but its effectiveness is limited under occluded conditions, especially when perception fails. Unlike the Blind Policy, the Perceptive Policy is not as robust under these conditions. To address these challenges, we propose a MBC:Multi-Brain collaborative system that incorporates the concepts of Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning and introduces collaboration between the Blind Policy and the Perceptive Policy. By applying this multi-policy collaborative model to a quadruped robot, the robot can maintain stable locomotion even when the perceptual system is impaired or observational data is incomplete. Our simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that this system significantly improves the robot's passability and robustness against perception failures in complex environments, validating the effectiveness of multi-policy collaboration in enhancing robotic motion performance.
author: Hang Liu;Yi Cheng;Rankun Li;Xiaowen Hu;Linqi Ye;Houde Liu
aff: University of Michigan - Ann Arbor;Tsinghua University;Shanghai University;Shanghai University;Tsinghua University;Shanghai University
position: MS student;MS student;China;Associate Professor;Full Professor;MS student

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make a robot dog smarter by giving it two 'brains'—one that feels the ground and one that looks at it—so it doesn't trip when its cameras get confused. It’s essentially a very expensive way to teach a machine how to walk in the dark without bumping into the coffee table.
why_bad: Rebranding standard sensor fusion as 'Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning' is academic clickbait that doesn't hide the lack of algorithmic novelty.
The 'Multi-Brain' approach introduces massive computational latency, effectively making the robot overthink its own footsteps until it lags into a wall.
The paper fails to compare the system against a single, well-trained policy, leaving reviewers to suspect that two brains are actually dumber than one.
roast.analogy_name: The Blind Gymnast and the Nervous Backseat Driver
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where a blindfolded parkour expert and a panicked tourist are forced to share the same pair of legs. One knows exactly how to flip, but has no idea where the cliff is; the other can see the cliff, but has never moved a muscle in their life. Together, they must navigate a rocky wasteland while screaming at each other through a walkie-talkie. It’s a high-stakes coordination nightmare where the 'Perceptive Brain' loses its mind the moment it gets dusty, and the 'Blind Brain' just wants to keep sprinting into the abyss. This summer, witness the breakthrough that proves if you overcomplicate walking enough, you can turn a graceful quadruped into a vibrating heap of indecision.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered.

title: Contrastive Imitation Learning for Language-guided Multi-Task Robotic Manipulation
abstract: Developing robots capable of executing various manipulation tasks, guided by natural language instructions and visual observations of intricate real-world environments, remains a significant challenge in robotics. Such robot agents need to understand linguistic commands and  distinguish between the requirements of different tasks. In this work, we present $\mathtt{\Sigma\mbox{-}agent}$, an end-to-end imitation learning agent for multi-task robotic manipulation. $\mathtt{\Sigma\mbox{-}agent}$ incorporates contrastive Imitation Learning (contrastive IL) modules to strengthen vision-language and current-future representations. An effective and efficient multi-view querying Transformer (MVQ-Former) for aggregating representative semantic information is introduced. $\mathtt{\Sigma\mbox{-}agent}$ shows substantial improvement over state-of-the-art methods under diverse settings in 18 RLBench tasks, surpassing RVT by an average of 5.2% and 5.9% in 10 and 100 demonstration training, respectively. $\mathtt{\Sigma\mbox{-}agent}$ also achieves 62% success rate with a single policy in 5 real-world manipulation tasks. The code will be released upon acceptance.
author: Teli Ma;Jiaming Zhou;Zifan Wang;Ronghe Qiu;Junwei Liang
aff: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou);the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology（Guangzhou);Hong Kong University of Science and Technology;Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
position: PhD student;PhD student;MS student;MS student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to make a robot that understands verbal commands by having it play a high-tech game of 'spot the difference' between its current view and its goal. While it technically performs better than its predecessors, it still fails its real-world chores nearly 40% of the time.
why_bad: Marginal Gains: A 5% improvement over the previous model is the academic equivalent of adding a sprig of parsley to a frozen dinner and calling it 'haute cuisine.'
Mediocre Reliability: Bragging about a 62% success rate in the real world is bold, considering most people prefer their robots not to fail four out of every ten attempts at setting the table.
The 'Trust Me' Protocol: Promising to release the code only 'upon acceptance' is a classic red flag that the results might be as fragile as the robot's grip on a wine glass.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show contestant who claims they’ve revolutionized the omelet by inventing a 'Multi-View Querying Spatula.' They spend forty minutes explaining the aerodynamics of the flip (MVQ-Former) and how they 'contrast' the raw egg with the cooked egg to achieve synergy. When it’s time to serve, the omelet is only edible 62% of the time, and it tastes almost exactly like the one the previous contestant made with a regular fork. When the judges ask to see the secret recipe, the chef says they’ll only hand it over if they win the $100,000 grand prize first.

title: Learning Robotic Manipulation Policies from Point Clouds with Conditional Flow Matching
abstract:
Learning from expert demonstrations is a popular approach to train
robotic manipulation policies from limited data. However, imitation learning
algorithms require a number of design choices ranging from the input modality,
training objective, and 6-DoF end-effector pose representation. Diffusion-based
methods have gained popularity as they allow to predict long horizon trajectories
and handle multimodal action distributions. Recently, Conditional Flow Matching
(CFM) (or Rectified Flow) has been proposed as a more flexible generalization
of diffusion models. In this paper we investigate the application of CFM in the
context of robotic policy learning, and specifically study the interplay with the
other design choices required to build an imitation learning algorithm. We show
that CFM gives the best performance when combined with point cloud input
observations. Additionally, we study the feasibility of a CFM formulation on
the SO(3) manifold and evaluate its suitability with a simplified example. We
perform extensive experiments on RLBench which demonstrate that our proposed
PointFlowMatch approach achieves a state-of-the-art average success rate of 67.8%
over eight tasks, double the performance of the next best method.
author: Eugenio Chisari;Nick Heppert;Max Argus;Tim Welschehold;Thomas Brox;Abhinav Valada
aff: Universität Freiburg;University of Freiburg, Albert-Ludwigs-Universität Freiburg;Universität Freiburg;University of Freiburg;University of Freiburg;University of Freiburg, Albert-Ludwigs-Universität Freiburg

roast.laymen_summary: We taught a robot how to move by showing it 3D 'dot clouds' and using a new math trick to smooth out its pathing. While the authors claim this makes the robot twice as capable as before, it’s mostly just a fancier way of connecting the dots in a simulation.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: Swapping Diffusion for Conditional Flow Matching is a lateral architectural move that feels more like a hardware upgrade than a new scientific discovery.
Theoretical Hand-waving: Applying the math to 3D rotations was only tested on a 'simplified example,' which is researcher-speak for 'it broke when we tried it on the hard stuff.'
Benchmark Suspicion: Doubling the performance of existing methods on a standard benchmark usually suggests the 'old' methods weren't optimized properly rather than a miracle breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Overfit,' where today we’re ditching the traditional whisk for a laser-guided, 3D-mapped emulsion system. Instead of just stirring the sauce, we are 'flow matching' every individual molecule of oil into the vinegar. The chef claims this makes the vinaigrette 67% more 'stable,' but the judges noticed he only practiced on a bowl of plastic beads in a vacuum. It’s a beautifully engineered solution for a salad that no one is actually allowed to eat, delivered with the confidence of a man who’s never had to wash his own dishes.

title: Tag Map: A Text-Based Map for Spatial Reasoning and Navigation with Large Language Models
abstract: Large Language Models (LLM) have emerged as a tool for robots to generate task plans using common sense reasoning. For the LLM to generate actionable plans, scene context must be provided, often through a map. Recent works have shifted from explicit maps with fixed semantic classes to implicit open vocabulary maps based on queryable embeddings capable of representing any semantic class. However, embeddings cannot directly report the scene context as they are implicit, requiring further processing for LLM integration. To address this, we propose an explicit text-based map that can represent thousands of semantic classes while easily integrating with LLMs due to their text-based nature by building upon large-scale image recognition models. We study how entities in our map can be localized and show through evaluations that our text-based map localizations perform comparably to those from open vocabulary maps while using two to four orders of magnitude less memory. Real-robot experiments demonstrate the grounding of an LLM with the text-based map to solve user tasks.
author: Mike Zhang;Kaixian Qu;Vaishakh Patil;Cesar Cadena;Marco Hutter
aff: ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to help robots navigate by replacing complex 3D maps with a giant, glorified grocery list of objects found in a room. They argue that feeding an LLM a massive text file is smarter than using actual spatial data because it saves memory and speaks the AI's native language.
why_bad: Spatial amnesia: By flattening a 3D world into a text list, the robot loses the ability to understand where things are relative to each other, effectively turning a navigator into a confused librarian.
The 'Wall of Text' problem: Scaling this to a real-world building would result in a text prompt so long that the LLM would run out of 'memory' before it even found the bathroom.

Oversimplified benchmarks: The paper claims victory by comparing its text list to high-end systems in a way that ignores the fact that a list of words cannot actually represent physical depth or geometry.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Post-it Note
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense would have you believe that their client can navigate a pitch-black labyrinth using nothing but a list of nouns. Imagine a witness testifying about a crime scene; instead of describing the room, they hand the jury a 40-foot-long CVS receipt listing every item in the building and shout, 'Figure it out yourself!' They claim this is 'efficient' because a receipt weighs less than a blueprint. But when the robot—I mean, the defendant—trips over the very 'sofa' it just read about, it becomes clear that knowing the name of a thing is not the same as knowing where not to hit it. The prosecution rests: you cannot substitute a dictionary for a GPS and call it 'spatial reasoning'.

title: Learning Robotic Locomotion Affordances and Photorealistic Simulators from Human-Captured Data
abstract: Learning reliable affordance models which satisfy human preferences is often hindered by a lack of high-quality training data. Similarly, learning visuomotor policies in simulation can be challenging due to the high cost of photo-realistic rendering. We present PAWS: a comprehensive robot learning framework that uses a novel portable data capture rig and processing pipeline to collect long-horizon trajectories that include camera poses, foot poses, terrain meshes, and 3D radiance fields. We also contribute PAWS-Data: an extensive dataset gathered with PAWS containing over 10 hours of indoor and outdoor trajectories spanning a variety of scenes. With PAWS-Data we leverage radiance fields' photo-realistic rendering to generate tens of thousands of viewpoint-augmented images, then produce pixel affordance labels by identifying semantically similar regions to those traversed by the user. On this data we finetune a navigation affordance model from a pretrained backbone, and perform detailed ablations. Additionally, We open source PAWS-Sim, a high-speed photo-realistic simulator which integrates PAWS-Data with IsaacSim, enabling research for visuomotor policy learning. We evaluate the utility of the affordance model on a quadrupedal robot, which plans through affordances to follow pathways and sidewalks, and avoid human collisions. Project resources are available on the [website](https://pawslocomotion.com).
author: Alejandro Escontrela;Justin Kerr;Kyle Stachowicz;Pieter Abbeel
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Covariant

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a high-tech backpack that records humans walking so a robot dog can learn to copy their paths. They used these recordings to create a fancy 3D video game world where the robot can practice walking without ever leaving the lab.
why_bad: The 'novel' data capture rig is essentially an over-engineered selfie-stick for feet, offering more of a hardware DIY project than a fundamental algorithmic breakthrough.
The affordance model relies on the shaky assumption that wherever a human chooses to walk is the only 'correct' path, which ignores the robot's unique physical capabilities and constraints.

While the radiance field simulator produces beautiful 'photo-realistic' pictures, it lacks the actual physical dynamics required to prove the robot won't faceplant the moment it hits a real-world sidewalk.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Expensive Stalker-Bot
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents Exhibit A: a robot that claims to 'understand the world' but is actually just a glorified shadow-boxer. The defense argues that by filming a human's afternoon stroll, they have unlocked the secrets of robotic navigation. I ask you: if I film myself walking through a minefield, has the camera 'learned' to avoid mines, or is it just a witness to my survival? The 'photo-realistic' evidence they’ve submitted is nothing more than a high-resolution deepfake of competence. They’ve built a $100,000 mirror and called it a window to the future. I move to strike this 'PAWS' framework from the record on the grounds that recording a human walking and telling a robot to 'do that' isn't artificial intelligence—it's just creepy.

title: GraspSplats: Efficient Manipulation with 3D Feature Splatting
abstract:
The ability for robots to perform efficient and zero-shot grasping of object parts is crucial for practical applications and is becoming prevalent with recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs). To bridge the 2D-to-3D gap for representations to support such a capability, existing methods rely on neural fields (NeRFs) via differentiable rendering or point-based projection methods. However, we demonstrate that NeRFs are inappropriate for scene changes due to its implicitness and point-based methods are inaccurate for part localization without rendering-based optimization. To amend these issues, we propose GraspSplats. Using depth supervision and a novel reference feature computation method, GraspSplats can generate high-quality scene representations under 60 seconds. We further validate the advantages of Gaussian-based representation by showing that the explicit and optimized geometry in GraspSplats is sufficient to natively support (1) real-time grasp sampling and (2) dynamic and articulated object manipulation with point trackers.
With extensive experiments on a Franka robot, we demonstrate that GraspSplats significantly outperforms existing methods under diverse task settings. In particular, GraspSplats outperforms NeRF-based methods like F3RM and LERF-TOGO, and 2D detection methods. The code will be released.
author: Mazeyu Ji;Ri-Zhao Qiu;Xueyan Zou;Xiaolong Wang
aff: University of California, San Diego;University of California, San Diego;University of Wisconsin - Madison;University of California, San Diego
aff_unique_norm: University of California, San Diego;University of Wisconsin-Madison

roast.laymen_summary: We built a system that lets robots see and grab objects in 3D using 'Gaussian Splats'—essentially high-tech digital glitter—which is way faster than the old, sluggish AI methods. It creates a map of the room in under a minute so the robot can stop guessing where the handle is and actually pick up your mug.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just taking an existing 3D rendering trick and bolting it onto a robot arm without enough original sauce to justify a full paper.
It relies heavily on 'depth supervision,' which is academic speak for 'it only works if you have a perfect 3D camera and a lab-grade setup with zero shadows or reflections.'
The experimental results feel like a 'home game'—the comparisons against older methods like F3RM are convenient, but it ducks more modern, robust competitors that don't need a 60-second prep time.
roast.analogy_name: The Instant Influencer's Interior Design App
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re pitching an app called 'SplatDecor.' Instead of a professional architect taking weeks to measure your house (that's NeRF), our app has an influencer walk into the room, spin around for 59 seconds taking 200 selfies, and—BOOM—a 3D model. It looks stunning on a smartphone screen, and you can 'virtually' move the couch instantly. But here’s the catch: the app only works if the lighting is studio-perfect, and if you actually move a physical chair, the AI gets confused because it’s still staring at the 'glitter' of where the chair used to be. It’s a 'vibes-based' reconstruction that’s fast enough for a TikTok demo, but maybe don't trust it to operate heavy machinery in a messy kitchen just yet.

title: Learning Differentiable Tensegrity Dynamics using Graph Neural Networks
abstract: Tensegrity robots are composed of rigid struts and flexible cables. They constitute an emerging class of hybrid rigid-soft robotic systems and are promising systems for a wide array of applications, ranging from locomotion to assembly. They are difficult to control and model accurately, however, due to their compliance and high number of degrees of freedom. To address this issue, prior work has introduced a differentiable physics engine designed for tensegrity robots based on first principles. In contrast, this work proposes the use of graph neural networks to model contact dynamics over a graph representation of tensegrity robots, which leverages their natural graph-like cable connec- tivity between end caps of rigid rods. This learned simulator can accurately model 3-bar and 6-bar tensegrity robot dynamics in simulation-to-simulation experiments where MuJoCo is used as the ground truth. It can also achieve higher accuracy than the previous differentiable engine for a real 3-bar tensegrity robot, for which the robot state is only partially observable. When compared against direct applications of recent mesh-based graph neural network simulators, the proposed approach is computationally more efficient, both for training and inference, while achieving higher accuracy. Code and data are available at https://github.com/nchen9191/tensegrity_gnn_simulator_public
author: Nelson Chen;Kun Wang;William R. Johson III;Rebecca Kramer-Bottiglio;Kostas Bekris;Mridul Aanjaneya
aff: Rutgers University;;;Yale University;Rutgers University;Rutgers University, New Brunswick

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a computer how to predict the movements of 'tensegrity' robots—which are basically bundles of sticks held together by glorified rubber bands—using a trendy AI shortcut called a Graph Neural Network. The idea was to stop doing difficult physics math and instead let the AI 'vibe' its way through the robot's clumsy gymnastics.
why_bad: Generalization issues: The model works on 3-bar and 6-bar robots, but it’s a 'one-trick pony' that likely collapses if you add a single extra stick or change the tension.

Incremental novelty: Applying a Graph Neural Network to a structure that is literally a physical graph is the 'putting cheese on a burger' of AI—it’s the most obvious move possible and hardly qualifies as a breakthrough.

Sim-to-real gap: Comparing your new simulator to an old simulator (MuJoCo) is just grading your own homework, and the 'real world' testing was too thin to prove this wouldn't fail the moment it left the lab.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're on a high-stakes cooking show. Instead of learning how gravity and heat actually affect a soufflé, you decide to train a golden retriever to bark whenever it *thinks* the cake might fall over. You call this 'Differentiable Dessert Dynamics.' You show the judges that the dog is 90% accurate at predicting the collapse of a 3-layer cake, but only if the cake is made of plastic and the room has no wind. When you finally try it with a real cake, the dog gets distracted by a squirrel, and the whole thing ends up as a sticky puddle on the floor. You’re asking for a Michelin star for 'innovative barking,' but the judges are still hungry because you never actually learned how to bake.

title: OrbitGrasp: SE(3)-Equivariant Grasp Learning
abstract: While grasp detection is an important part of any robotic manipulation pipeline, reliable and accurate grasp detection in $\\mathrm{SE}(3)$ remains a research challenge. Many robotics applications in unstructured environments such as the home or warehouse would benefit a lot from better grasp performance. This paper proposes a novel framework for detecting $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ grasp poses based on point cloud input. Our main contribution is to propose an $\mathrm{SE}(3)$-equivariant model that maps each point in the cloud to a continuous grasp quality function over the 2-sphere $S^2$ using a spherical harmonic basis. Compared with reasoning about a finite set of samples, this formulation improves the accuracy and efficiency of our model when a large number of samples would otherwise be needed. In order to accomplish this, we propose a novel variation on EquiFormerV2 that leverages a UNet-style backbone to enlarge the number of points the model can handle. Our resulting method, which we name OrbitGrasp, significantly outperforms baselines in both simulation and physical experiments.
author: Boce Hu;Xupeng Zhu;Dian Wang;Zihao Dong;Haojie Huang;Chenghao Wang;Robin Walters;Robert Platt
aff: Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University ;Northeastern University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot brain that uses complex 3D geometry to figure out exactly how to grab objects from any angle. They basically used high-level physics math to ensure the robot doesn't get confused if you tilt a mug sideways before it tries to pick it up.
why_bad: Mathematical overkill: Using spherical harmonics to describe a simple 'pinch' gesture is like using a supercomputer to calculate the optimal way to hold a taco.
The 'UNet-EquiFormer' mashup is a computational gas-guzzler that likely runs too slowly for any real-world robot that isn't moving in slow motion.
The benchmarks are cherry-picked; outperforming old baselines is easy when your model has ten times the parameters and a fraction of the inference speed.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Our chef today didn't just want to flip a burger; they insisted on calculating the thermal equilibrium of the patty using a 50-piece orchestra and a liquid nitrogen chiller. They're calling it 'OrbitFlip.' It’s a beautiful performance, but the 'novel' spatula they built is so heavy it crushed the grill, and by the time the math finished calculating the exact rotation of the sesame seeds, the customer had already died of old age. It’s technically impressive work, Chef, but I asked for a cheeseburger, not a doctoral thesis on the fluid dynamics of ketchup.

title: View-Invariant Policy Learning via Zero-Shot Novel View Synthesis
abstract:
Large-scale visuomotor policy learning is a promising approach toward developing generalizable manipulation systems. Yet, policies that can be deployed on diverse embodiments, environments, and observational modalities remain elusive. 
In this work, we investigate how knowledge from large-scale visual data of the world may be used to address one axis of variation for generalizable manipulation: observational viewpoint. Specifically, we study single-image novel view synthesis models, which learn 3D-aware scene-level priors by rendering images of the same scene from alternate camera viewpoints given a single input image.
For practical application to diverse robotic data, these models must operate *zero-shot*, performing view synthesis on unseen tasks and environments. We empirically analyze view synthesis models within a simple data-augmentation scheme that we call View Synthesis Augmentation (VISTA) to understand their capabilities for learning viewpoint-invariant policies from single-viewpoint demonstration data. Upon evaluating the robustness of policies trained with our method to out-of-distribution camera viewpoints, we find that they outperform baselines in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.
author: Stephen Tian;Blake Wulfe;Kyle Sargent;Katherine Liu;Sergey Zakharov;Vitor Campagnolo Guizilini;Jiajun Wu
aff: Stanford University;;Computer Science Department, Stanford University;Toyota Research Institute;Toyota Research Institute;Toyota Research Institute;Stanford University
position: PhD student;;PhD student;Researcher;Researcher;Staff Research Scientist;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop robots from losing their minds every time a camera moves an inch by using AI to 'hallucinate' what the room looks like from the side. Essentially, we’re feeding the robot fake, AI-generated photos of its chores so it doesn't freak out when it sees a spatula from a slightly different angle.
why_bad: The AI-generated images often suffer from 'visual fever dreams,' where the objects look like melting wax, leading the robot to learn physics that don't exist.
The computational cost is astronomical; you're essentially using a supercomputer to generate thousands of selfies for a robot that still struggles to pick up a block.
The performance gains are marginal compared to just rotating the camera a little bit manually, making the whole 'zero-shot generative' complexity feel like using a chainsaw to cut a grape.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where robots are so fragile that moving their chair two inches causes a total existential breakdown... one team of scientists has a plan. They won't teach the robot about 3D space. They won't teach it common sense. Instead, they will force it to stare at a trillion AI-generated deepfakes of a kitchen counter until its brain turns into digital mush. This summer, witness the high-stakes drama of a $100,000 robotic arm trying to grab a coffee mug while hallucinating that the entire universe is actually a shimmering, low-resolution JPEG. It’s 'The Matrix,' but for toasters, and the Architect is a generative model that can't quite draw fingers correctly.
roast.tone_check: Lean, mean, and scientifically grounded.

title: FetchBench: A Simulation Benchmark for Robot Fetching
abstract: Fetching, which includes approaching, grasping, and retrieving, is a critical challenge for robot manipulation tasks. Existing methods primarily focus on table-top scenarios, which do not adequately capture the complexities of environments where both grasping and planning are essential. To address this gap, we propose a new benchmark FetchBench, featuring diverse procedural scenes that integrate both grasping and motion planning challenges. Additionally, FetchBench includes a data generation pipeline that collects successful fetch trajectories for use in imitation learning methods. We implement multiple baselines from the traditional sense-plan-act pipeline to end-to-end behavior models. Our empirical analysis reveals that these methods achieve a maximum success rate of only 20%, indicating substantial room for improvement. Additionally, we identify key bottlenecks within the sense-plan-act pipeline and make recommendations based on the systematic analysis.
author: Beining Han;Meenal Parakh;Derek Geng;Jack A Defay;Gan Luyang;Jia Deng
aff: Department of Computer Science, Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University
position: PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;MS student;Undergrad student;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a virtual obstacle course to teach robots how to stop being clumsy and actually fetch items in a messy house. Unfortunately, even the 'smartest' AI they tested failed 80% of the time, proving that robots are still basically toddlers in a world of glass vases.
why_bad: Lack of Novelty: The academic world already has a dozen 'Robot Fetching' simulators; adding a few extra procedural chairs doesn't make it a breakthrough contribution.

The Sim-to-Real Delusion: The paper ignores that simulated physics are essentially a 'polite' version of reality, meaning a robot trained here would likely vibrate into a million pieces the second it touched a real-world spoon.
Strawman Baselines: They tested outdated AI models to make their benchmark look 'challenging,' which is like bragging that a toddler failed a Bar Exam you designed for infants.
roast.analogy_name: Peloton for Roomba: The Metaverse Edition
roast.analogy_description: Imagine I'm pitching you 'Fetch-r,' a revolutionary startup that builds high-end virtual obstacle courses for robots. We don't actually build the robots—that's too hard—and our virtual physics engine treats gravity as more of a 'suggestion' than a law. We’ve discovered that when we put a digital robot in a digital room, it fails to find the digital beer 80% of the time. Is it a failure? No, it's a 'benchmarking opportunity!' We’re disrupting the industry by proving that our simulated robots are just as incompetent as everyone feared, but now in 4K resolution. Don't worry about the real world; we'll fix that in a Series B update once we figure out how friction works.

title: Event3DGS: Event-Based 3D Gaussian Splatting for High-Speed Robot Egomotion
abstract:
By combining differentiable rendering with explicit point-based scene representations, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated breakthrough 3D reconstruction capabilities. 
    However, to date 3DGS has had limited impact on robotics, where high-speed egomotion is pervasive: Egomotion introduces motion blur and leads to artifacts in existing frame-based 3DGS reconstruction methods. 
    To address this challenge, we introduce Event3DGS, an event-based 3DGS framework.
    By exploiting the exceptional temporal resolution of event cameras, Event3GDS can reconstruct high-fidelity 3D structure and appearance under high-speed egomotion. 
    Extensive experiments on multiple synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of Event3DGS compared with existing event-based dense 3D scene reconstruction frameworks; Event3DGS substantially improves reconstruction quality (+3dB) while reducing computational costs by 95\%. 
    Our framework also allows one to incorporate a few motion-blurred frame-based measurements into the reconstruction process to further improve appearance fidelity without loss of structural accuracy.
author: Tianyi Xiong;Jiayi Wu;Botao He;Cornelia Fermuller;Yiannis Aloimonos;Heng Huang;Christopher Metzler
aff: University of Maryland, College Park;University of Maryland, College Park;University of Maryland, College Park;University of Maryland, College Park;University of Maryland, College Park;Department of Computer Science, University of Maryland, College Park;University of Maryland, College Park
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Research Scientist;Full Professor;Full Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Maryland;University of Maryland, College Park

roast.laymen_summary: Standard cameras turn into a blurry mess when robots move fast, making 3D mapping nearly impossible. This paper tries to fix that by using 'event cameras'—which act like high-speed motion sensors—to create sharp 3D models even when the robot is driving like a getaway driver.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just standard 3D Gaussian Splatting with a fresh coat of event-data paint; it's an incremental step being sold as a giant leap.
The 95% computational reduction claim feels like creative accounting, likely ignoring the massive 'hidden' cost of processing the raw event stream before the splatting even begins.
The 'high-speed' experiments look more like a grad student caffeinatedly waving a camera in a lab rather than actual high-velocity robotics in complex, messy environments.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every robot moves like a Jason Bourne fight scene, the footage is a smeary disaster. Enter the protagonist: a camera that doesn't take pictures, but instead 'feels' the light. It promises to paint a photorealistic masterpiece while strapped to a centrifuge. But the critics are calling 'cut!' because the artist is just tracing over someone else's drawing and claiming they invented the pencil. Coming this summer: A movie that’s 95% shorter, 3 decibels louder, and still somehow misses the point.

title: Perceive With Confidence: Statistical Safety Assurances for Navigation with Learning-Based Perception
abstract: Rapid advances in perception have enabled large pre-trained models to be used out of the box for transforming high-dimensional, noisy, and partial observations of the world into rich occupancy representations. However, the reliability of these models and consequently their safe integration onto robots remains unknown when deployed in environments unseen during training. In this work, we address this challenge by rigorously quantifying the uncertainty of pre-trained perception systems for object detection via a novel calibration technique based on conformal prediction. Crucially, this procedure guarantees robustness to distribution shifts in states when perceptual outputs are used in conjunction with a planner. As a result, the calibrated perception system can be used in combination with any safe planner to provide an end-to-end statistical assurance on safety in unseen environments. We evaluate the resulting approach, Perceive with Confidence (PwC), with experiments in simulation and on hardware where a quadruped robot navigates through previously unseen indoor, static environments. These experiments validate the safety assurances for obstacle avoidance provided by PwC and demonstrate up to 40% improvements in empirical safety compared to baselines.
author: Anushri Dixit;Zhiting Mei;Meghan Booker;Mariko Storey-Matsutani;Allen Z. Ren;Anirudha Majumdar
aff: Princeton University;Princeton University;Princeton University;;Google DeepMind;Princeton University

roast.laymen_summary: We gave a robot dog a pre-trained brain but realized it hallucinates when it sees a hallway it wasn't trained on, so we added a math-based 'BS detector' to keep it from crashing. It’s essentially teaching a robot to say 'I’m 95% sure that’s a wall' before it decides not to walk through it.
why_bad: The 'novel' calibration technique is just Conformal Prediction with a fresh coat of paint, offering little theoretical innovation for the heavy-duty math crowd.
The 'safety guarantees' are so conservative that the robot likely moves with the urgency of a tectonic plate, making it practically useless in the real world.
Testing in 'static indoor environments' is the robotics equivalent of bragging you can walk through an empty hallway without tripping; it ignores the chaos of moving objects and dynamic shifts.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a kicker who’s studied every NFL game ever played but refuses to swing his leg until his personal actuary calculates a 99% statistical certainty that he won't stub his toe. He’s standing on the 20-yard line, the crowd is screaming, and he’s checking his spreadsheets while the play clock hits zero. Sure, he hasn't missed a field goal yet because he literally hasn't kicked the ball, but the coach is losing his mind. He’s 'mathematically safe,' but he’s basically just an expensive lawn ornament in a jersey.

title: Progressive Multi-Modal Fusion for Robust 3D Object Detection
abstract:
Multi-sensor fusion is crucial for accurate 3D object detection in autonomous driving, with cameras and LiDAR being the most commonly used sensors. However, existing methods perform sensor fusion in a single view by projecting features from both modalities either in Bird's Eye View (BEV) or Perspective View (PV), thus sacrificing complementary information such as height or geometric proportions.
To address this limitation, we propose ProFusion3D, a progressive fusion framework that combines features in both BEV and PV at both intermediate and object query levels. Our architecture hierarchically fuses local and global features, enhancing the robustness of 3D object detection. Additionally, we introduce a self-supervised mask modeling pre-training strategy to improve multi-modal representation learning and data efficiency through three novel objectives. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and Argoverse2 datasets conclusively demonstrate the efficacy of ProFusion3D. Moreover, ProFusion3D is robust to sensor failure, showing strong performance when only one modality is available.
author: Rohit Mohan;Daniele Cattaneo;Florian Drews;Abhinav Valada
aff: Albert-Ludwigs-Universität Freiburg;Universität Freiburg;;University of Freiburg

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to stop self-driving cars from being 'one-perspective' idiots by forcing them to look at the world through 2D photos and 3D maps simultaneously. It’s basically giving the car's brain a set of bifocals and a fill-in-the-blanks coloring book to help it learn faster.
why_bad: The 'significant' performance gains are likely marginal improvements that look impressive only because the authors chose the specific metrics where their over-engineered model didn't fail.
The computational complexity is a nightmare; you'd need a server farm in the trunk of the car just to process a single left turn in real-time.
The 'novel' self-supervised pre-training is essentially a collection of last year's buzzwords rebranded as 'objectives' without any proof they actually solve the underlying data scarcity problem.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re a VC. We’re launching 'RetinaMax.' Most people just use their eyes to walk. Boring! Our headset projects a 3D topographic map onto your left eye and a 4K livestream of your own feet onto your right eye, then asks you to solve a crossword puzzle to 'pre-train' your brain. It’s 'Multi-Modal Synergy.' Sure, the headset weighs 40 pounds and you’ll walk into a pole because of the processing lag, but think of the data richness! We aren't just walking; we're progressively fusing the sidewalk experience. Pre-seed starts at $50M, compute costs not included.

title: RobotKeyframing: Learning Locomotion with High-Level Objectives via Mixture of Dense and Sparse Rewards
abstract: This paper presents a novel learning-based control framework that uses keyframing to incorporate high-level objectives in natural locomotion for legged robots. These high-level objectives are specified as a variable number of partial or complete pose targets that are spaced arbitrarily in time. Our proposed framework utilizes a multi-critic reinforcement learning algorithm to effectively handle the mixture of dense and sparse rewards. Additionally, it employs a transformer-based encoder to accommodate a variable number of input targets, each associated with specific time-to-arrivals. Throughout simulation and hardware experiments, we demonstrate that our framework can effectively satisfy the target keyframe sequence at the required times. In the experiments, the multi-critic method significantly reduces the effort of hyperparameter tuning compared to the standard single-critic alternative. Moreover, the proposed transformer-based architecture enables robots to anticipate future goals, which results in quantitative improvements in their ability to reach their targets.
author: Fatemeh Zargarbashi;Jin Cheng;Dongho Kang;Robert Sumner;Stelian Coros
aff: Disney Research|Studios;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;Disney Research, Disney Research;ETHZ - ETH Zurich
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Principal Researcher;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot dog to hit specific poses at specific times, like a stop-motion puppet that moves itself. They used a complex AI brain to balance 'general walking' with 'striking a pose,' hoping it would learn to anticipate its next move.
why_bad: The 'Multi-Critic' approach is essentially just hyperparameter tuning in a trench coat, offering marginal gains for significant architectural bloat.
Using a Transformer to process a handful of pose targets is the definition of 'over-engineering,' akin to using a chainsaw to slice a single grape.
The paper fails to prove why this method is better than traditional control theory, which has been hitting 'keyframe' targets without the help of a neural network since the 1970s.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Over-Encoded Quadruped
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution contends that the defendant—a four-legged robot—is guilty of grand larceny for stealing compute cycles. The defense claims the robot 'anticipates the future,' but the evidence shows it’s merely looking at its own itinerary and sweating. We have a 'Multi-Critic' system where two internal voices argue over whether the robot is walking well or just hitting its marks, creating a legal deadlock that results in a gait resembling a folding chair falling down a flight of stairs. To use a Transformer for such a trivial sequence of events is a clear violation of Occam’s Razor; you don't need a Supreme Court ruling to tell a dog how to sit by 3:00 PM. I move to strike the entire methodology from the record.

title: LLARVA: Vision-Action Instruction Tuning Enhances Robot Learning
abstract: In recent years, instruction-tuned Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have been successful at several tasks, including image captioning and visual question answering; yet leveraging these models remains an open question for robotics. Prior LMMs for robotics applications have been extensively trained on language and action data, but their ability to generalize in different settings has often been less than desired. To address this, we introduce LLARVA, a model trained with a novel instruction tuning method that leverages structured prompts to unify a range of robotic learning tasks, scenarios, and environments. Additionally, we show that predicting intermediate 2-D representations, which we refer to as *visual traces*, can help further align vision and action spaces for robot learning. We generate 8.5M image-visual trace pairs from the Open X-Embodiment dataset in order to pre-train our model, and we evaluate on 12 different tasks in the RLBench simulator as well as a physical Franka Emika Panda 7-DoF robot. Our experiments yield strong performance, demonstrating that LLARVA — using 2-D and language representations — performs well compared to several contemporary baselines, and can generalize across various robot environments and configurations.
author: Dantong Niu;Yuvan Sharma;Giscard Biamby;Jerome Quenum;Yutong Bai;Baifeng Shi;Trevor Darrell;Roei Herzig
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Johns Hopkins University;NVIDIA;University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department
position: Undergrad student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Research Intern;Postdoc;PhD student;Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of California, Berkeley;Johns Hopkins University;NVIDIA;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots to follow directions by showing them 8.5 million 'connect-the-dots' pictures of movement, hoping it would make them less clumsy in new environments. Ultimately, they slapped a fancy 'visual trace' label on some 2D lines and claimed the robot could now understand the world, even though it mostly just got good at playing a video game version of reality.
why_bad: The 'visual traces' are essentially just 2D heatmaps rebranded with a fancy name, offering very little actual innovation over existing spatial prompting methods.

The performance gains in the RLBench simulator don't convincingly translate to the real world, suggesting the model is just 'overfitting to the vibes' of the dataset rather than learning true physics.
Despite the massive 8.5M image dataset, the model fails to outperform simpler, less-bloated baselines in meaningful ways, making the high computational cost hard to justify.
roast.analogy_name: The Madden Pro in the Real NFL
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a guy who is the absolute undisputed king of playing Madden on his couch. He’s analyzed 8 million frames of gameplay, he knows every button combo for a 'visual trace' juke move, and he’s convinced he’s ready for the big leagues because he dominated the 'Pro' difficulty setting. But the moment he steps onto a real grass field with a 300-pound lineman charging at him, he realizes that 'predicting 2D representations' of a tackle is a lot different than actually getting hit. LLARVA is that gamer—it looks like a Hall of Famer in the simulator's highlight reel, but when it’s time to actually move dirt in the physical world, it’s still looking for the 'X' button to pick up a coffee cup. It’s got the stats, it’s got the playbook, but it’s playing a different sport than reality.

title: Mobile ALOHA: Learning Bimanual Mobile Manipulation using Low-Cost Whole-Body Teleoperation
abstract: Imitation learning from human demonstrations has shown impressive performance in robotics. However, most results focus on table-top manipulation, lacking the mobility and dexterity necessary for generally useful tasks. In this work, we develop a system for imitating mobile manipulation tasks that are bimanual and require whole-body control. We first present Mobile ALOHA, a low-cost and whole-body teleoperation system for data collection. It augments the ALOHA system with a mobile base, and a whole-body teleoperation interface. Using data collected with Mobile ALOHA, we then perform supervised behavior cloning and find that co-training with existing static ALOHA datasets boosts performance on mobile manipulation tasks. With 50 demonstrations for each task, co-training can increase success rates by up to 90\%, allowing Mobile ALOHA to autonomously complete complex mobile manipulation tasks such as sauteing and serving a piece of shrimp, opening a two-door wall cabinet to store heavy cooking pots, calling and entering an elevator, and lightly rinsing a used pan using a kitchen faucet.  We will open-source all the hardware and software implementations upon publication.
author: Zipeng Fu;Tony Z. Zhao;Chelsea Finn
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Google

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers strapped two robot arms to a motorized base and taught it to do chores by playing a high-stakes game of 'Follow the Leader.' It’s essentially a $30,000 remote-controlled puppet that can sauté shrimp, provided a human shows it exactly how to do it fifty times first.
why_bad: The system relies on behavioral cloning, meaning it lacks actual reasoning; if the shrimp moves two inches to the left, the robot will happily sauté the empty air.
The 'low-cost' hardware results in significant mechanical slop, forcing the software to work overtime just to compensate for the robot's own wobbliness.
