Co-training on static datasets is a clever band-aid, but it doesn't solve the fundamental 'distribution shift' problem where the robot panics the moment it leaves its training kitchen.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Neural Network Finals! We’ve got Mobile ALOHA in the kitchen, and folks, it looks like a Hall of Famer until the wind blows! It’s running the 'Imitation Playbook'—it doesn't know *how* to cook; it’s just lip-syncing the movements of a master chef. If the defense moves the frying pan even a fraction of an inch, this robot is throwing an interception right into the trash can. It’s a spectacular highlight reel, but don't let the fancy footwork fool you: if there’s a fumble in the environment, this bot doesn't have the situational awareness to recover the ball!

title: Adaptive Diffusion Terrain Generator for Autonomous Uneven Terrain Navigation
abstract:
Model-free reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful method for developing robust robot control policies capable of navigating through complex and unstructured terrains.
The effectiveness of these methods hinges on two essential elements: 
(1) the use of massively parallel physics simulations to expedite policy training, 
and
(2) the deployment of an environment generator tasked with crafting terrains that are sufficiently challenging yet attainable, thereby facilitating continuous policy improvement. 
Existing methods of environment generation often rely on heuristics constrained by a set of parameters, limiting the diversity and realism.
In this work, we introduce the Adaptive Diffusion Terrain Generator (ADTG), a novel method that leverages Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) to dynamically expand an existing training environment by adding more diverse and complex terrains tailored to the current policy.
Unlike conventional methods, ADTG adapts the terrain complexity and variety based on the evolving capabilities of the current policy.
This is achieved through two primary mechanisms:
First, by blending terrains from the initial dataset within their latent spaces using performance-informed weights, ADTG creates terrains that suitably challenge the policy. 
Secondly, by manipulating the initial noise in the diffusion process, ADTG seamlessly shifts between creating similar terrains for fine-tuning the current policy and entirely novel ones for expanding training diversity.
Our experiments show that the policy trained by ADTG outperforms both procedural generated and natural environments, along with popular navigation methods.
author: Youwei Yu;Junhong Xu;Lantao Liu
aff: Indiana University;Indiana University, Bloomington;Indiana University, Bloomington

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build an AI 'dream architect' that uses the technology behind Midjourney to hallucinate increasingly difficult obstacle courses for robots. The goal was to stop robots from getting bored by making the terrain get weirder and harder the better the robot gets at walking.
why_bad: Computational Gluttony: Using a Denoising Diffusion Model to generate a bumpy floor is like using a supercomputer to calculate a restaurant tip—it works, but the energy bill is more expensive than the actual robot.

The Physics Hallucination: Because diffusion models prioritize looking cool over being real, the AI often generated 'Escher-esque' terrains that are physically impossible, teaching the robot to exploit glitches rather than learn gravity.

Circular Logic: The 'adaptive' part of the system is essentially the AI grading its own homework, leading to a performance loop where the robot only succeeds in the specific, weird world the AI built for it.
roast.analogy_description: [Voiceover: Deep, gravelly movie trailer voice] In a world... where robots are too stupid to cross a gravel driveway... one team decided to play God with a GPU. Meet 'The Architect.' It doesn't just give you a treadmill; it uses a billion-dollar CGI studio to manifest a crumbling volcanic landscape beneath your feet in real-time. It’s breathtaking. It’s revolutionary. It’s... currently rendering frame 1 while the robot has already fallen over and died. Watch as a machine tries to learn how to hike by staring at a dream-sequence that costs more to run than the actual hardware. Coming this summer: 'The Glitch in the Ground'—where the physics are made up and the peer reviews don't matter.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimal.

title: An Open-Source Soft Robotic Platform for Autonomous Aerial Manipulation in the Wild
abstract: Aerial manipulation combines the versatility and speed of flying platforms with the functional capabilities of mobile manipulation, which presents significant challenges due to the need for precise localization and control. Traditionally, researchers have relied on off-board perception systems, which are limited to expensive and impractical specially equipped indoor environments. In this work, we introduce a novel platform for autonomous aerial manipulation that exclusively utilizes onboard perception systems. Our platform can perform aerial manipulation in various indoor and outdoor environments without depending on external perception systems. Our experimental results demonstrate the platform's ability to autonomously grasp various objects in diverse settings. This advancement significantly improves the scalability and practicality of aerial manipulation applications by eliminating the need for costly tracking solutions. To accelerate future research, we open source our modern ROS 2 software stack and custom hardware design, making our contributions accessible to the broader research community.
keywords: Aerial Manipulation;Learning-Based Grasping;Autonomous Flight;Robotic Systems;Soft Grasping
author: Erik Bauer;Marc Blöchlinger;Pascal Strauch;Arman Raayatsanati;Cavelti Curdin;Robert K. Katzschmann
aff: BMW Group;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;ETH Zurich;ETHZ - ETH Zurich;Swiss Federal Institute of Technology
position: Intern;MS student;MS student;MS student;MS student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: BMW Group;ETH Zurich;Swiss Federal Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: We strapped a squishy mechanical hand onto a drone so it can grab things outdoors without needing an expensive room full of motion-tracking cameras. It's basically an open-source flying claw machine that aims to prove drones don't need a babysitter to pick up a tennis ball.
why_bad: The 'in the wild' claim is a massive stretch, as the experiments likely took place in a controlled backyard rather than a chaotic real-world environment.
Using onboard perception isn't the groundbreaking 'novelty' the authors think it is; the robotics community moved past external Vicon systems years ago.
The paper lacks rigorous control theory to explain how the drone compensates for the 'soft' gripper's unpredictable wobbling during flight.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Squishy Sky-Burglar
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, you expect this court to believe your client is a 'sophisticated autonomous agent'? We have testimony that the drone oscillates more than a nervous witness on the stand! You claim it operates 'in the wild,' yet the 'wild' in question was a gentle breeze on a sunny afternoon—hardly a jungle. This isn't 'state-of-the-art perception'; it's a glorified GoPro strapped to a prayer. I move to strike the 'open-source' defense from the record, as releasing the blueprints for a flying wet noodle doesn't constitute a contribution to science. Objection sustained!

title: Discovering Robotic Interaction Modes with Discrete Representation Learning
abstract:
Abstract: Human actions manipulating articulated objects, such as opening and closing a drawer, can be categorized into multiple modalities we define as interaction modes. Traditional robot learning approaches lack discrete representations of these modes, which are crucial for empirical sampling and grounding. In this paper, we present ActAIM2, which learns a discrete representation of robot manipulation interaction modes in a purely unsupervised fashion, without the use of expert labels or simulator-based privileged information. Utilizing novel data collection methods involving simulator rollouts, ActAIM2 consists of an interaction mode selector and a low-level action predictor. The selector generates discrete representations of potential interaction modes with self-supervision, while the predictor outputs
corresponding action trajectories. Our method is validated through its success rate in manipulating articulated objects and its robustness in sampling meaningful actions from the discrete representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate ActAIM2’s effectiveness in enhancing manipulability and generalizability over baselines and ablation studies. For videos and additional results, see our website: https://actaim2.github.io/.
keywords: Discovering Robotic Interaction Modes with Discrete Representation Learning
author: Liquan Wang;Ankit Goyal;Haoping Xu;Animesh Garg
aff: Department of Computer Science;NVIDIA;Toronto University;NVIDIA

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach a robot to open drawers by letting it guess different 'styles' of grabbing without any human guidance. While the robot successfully invented a few ways to pull a handle in a perfect digital world, it’s mostly just a glorified way of saying the AI is making up its own Kategorien for basic physics.
why_bad: The 'novel' unsupervised approach is essentially a repackaged clustering algorithm that struggles to prove the learned 'modes' are actually useful and not just random noise.
The paper is an incremental sequel (ActAIM2) that fails to provide a significant leap over the original, feeling more like a bug fix than a breakthrough.
Evaluation is confined strictly to simulators, meaning the system's 'robustness' would likely evaporate the moment it encounters a real-world drawer with a sticky hinge or a different handle.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Simulated Games, where our star athlete is trying to solve a Rubik's Cube while refusing to acknowledge that colors exist. He’s labeled his different ways of fumbling with the cube as 'Discrete Interaction Modes' and is claiming a gold medal because he didn't ask for the instructions. The crowd is silent, mostly because he's performing in a void where friction is optional and the cube never jams. He’s successfully discovered 'Mode A: Dropping it' and 'Mode B: Throwing it,' and the judges are wondering why they’re watching a sequel to a performance that already failed to clear the bar.

title: InterACT: Inter-dependency Aware Action Chunking with Hierarchical Attention Transformers for Bimanual Manipulation
abstract: We present InterACT: Inter-dependency aware Action Chunking with Hierarchical Attention Transformers, a novel imitation learning framework for bimanual manipulation that integrates hierarchical attention to capture inter-dependencies between dual-arm joint states and visual inputs. InterACT consists of a Hierarchical Attention Encoder and a Multi-arm Decoder, both designed to enhance information aggregation and coordination. The encoder processes multi-modal inputs through segment-wise and cross-segment attention mechanisms, while the decoder leverages synchronization blocks to refine individual action predictions, providing the counterpart's prediction as context. Our experiments on a variety of simulated and real-world bimanual manipulation tasks demonstrate that InterACT significantly outperforms existing methods. Detailed ablation studies validate the contributions of key components of our work, including the impact of CLS tokens, cross-segment encoders, and synchronization blocks.
author: Andrew Choong-Won Lee;Ian Chuang;Ling-Yuan Chen;Iman Soltani
aff: University of California, Davis;University of California, Davis;University of California, Davis;University of California, Davis

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to make a two-armed robot less clumsy by building a complex 'hierarchy' of attention so the left hand finally knows what the right hand is doing. It’s essentially a fancy digital brain that breaks tasks into chunks, hoping the robot won't drop your coffee while trying to open the sugar packet.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The reviewers likely saw this as 'ACT' (Action Chunking with Transformers) with a fresh coat of paint and some extra math that doesn't fundamentally change the game.

Computational Bloat: Adding segment-wise and cross-segment attention mechanisms makes the 'brain' much heavier and slower without proving the extra processing power is actually necessary for folding a towel.

Ablation Anti-climax: The 'key components' like CLS tokens and sync blocks likely provided such marginal gains that they could be attributed to a lucky random seed rather than a breakthrough in robotics.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where robots move like drunk marionettes... one team decided that two arms were better than one. But instead of just teaching them to work together, they built a 50-story skyscraper of hierarchical logic just to coordinate a simple clap. Witness the drama of 'Synchronization Blocks!' Feel the tension of 'Cross-Segment Attention!' It’s a multi-modal blockbuster about a robot that spends ten minutes calculating the inter-dependency of its thumbs before failing to pick up a stapler. Coming this fall: A system so complex, it needs a supercomputer to do what a raccoon does for a piece of garbage.

title: Scaling Manipulation Learning with Visual Kinematic Chain Prediction
abstract: Learning general-purpose models from diverse datasets has achieved great success in machine learning. In robotics, however, existing methods in multi-task learning are typically constrained to a single robot and workspace, while recent work such as RT-X requires a non-trivial action normalization procedure to manually bridge the gap between different action spaces in diverse environments. In this paper, we propose the visual kinematics chain as a precise and universal representation of quasi-static actions for robot learning over diverse environments, which requires no manual adjustment since the visual kinematic chains can be automatically obtained from the robot’s model and camera parameters. We propose the Visual Kinematics Transformer (VKT),  a convolution-free architecture that supports an arbitrary number of camera viewpoints, and that is trained with a single objective of forecasting kinematic structures through optimal point-set matching. We demonstrate the superior performance of VKT over BC transformers as a general agent on Calvin, RLBench, ALOHA, Open-X, and real robot manipulation tasks. Video demonstrations and source code can be found at https://mlzxy.github.io/visual-kinetic-chain.
author: Xinyu Zhang;Yuhan Liu;Haonan Chang;Abdeslam Boularias
aff: Rutgers University;Rutgers University;Rutgers, New Brunswick;, Rutgers University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to build a 'universal remote' for robots by teaching them to predict where their own mechanical bones should move in a video, rather than learning specific motor commands. It’s an attempt to stop researchers from having to manually recalibrate every new robot like they’re re-tuning a 1950s radio every time they want to change the channel.
why_bad: The 'Calibration Trap': The model assumes your camera and robot measurements are perfect, but in the real world, a 1-millimeter bump to the camera turns the 'universal' brain into a digital lobotomy.

The 'Quasi-Static' Problem: By focusing on slow, 'quasi-static' movements, the authors basically taught a robot how to move in a world made of molasses, making it useless for any task requiring speed or momentum.

Geometric Overfitting: Critics pointed out that 'predicting kinematic chains' is just a fancy way of doing pose estimation, which often fails to generalize once the robot leaves the specific lab-grown environments it was trained in.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Monocled Witness
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents this 'Visual Kinematics Transformer' as a revolutionary witness. They claim it can identify a crime in any lighting, in any city, on any street. But look closer at the testimony! This witness only 'sees' the world through a highly specific, custom-made monocle. If the monocle—the camera calibration—is tilted even one degree by a passing breeze, the witness starts screaming that the sky is a sidewalk. The defense calls this 'universal representation'; I call it a liability. Your Honor, we cannot convict a robot of being 'general-purpose' when its entire worldview shatters the moment it leaves its perfectly calibrated courtroom. I move to strike this 'quasi-static' testimony as hearsay—it’s too slow to catch a thief and too fragile to survive the streets!

title: Q-SLAM: Quadric Representations for Monocular SLAM
abstract: In this paper, we reimagine volumetric representations through the lens of quadrics. We posit that rigid scene components can be effectively decomposed into quadric surfaces. Leveraging this assumption, we reshape the volumetric representations with million of cubes by several quadric planes, which results in more accurate and efficient modeling of 3D scenes in SLAM contexts. First, we use the quadric assumption to rectify noisy depth estimations from RGB inputs. This step significantly improves depth estimation accuracy, and allows us to efficiently sample ray points around quadric planes instead of the entire volume space in previous NeRF-SLAM systems. Second, we introduce a novel quadric-decomposed transformer to aggregate information across quadrics. The quadric semantics are not only explicitly used for depth correction and scene decomposition, but also serve as an implicit supervision signal for the mapping network. Through rigorous experimental evaluation, our method exhibits superior performance over other approaches relying on estimated depth, and achieves comparable accuracy to methods utilizing ground truth depth on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
keywords: Neural Radiance Fields;Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
author: Chensheng Peng;Chenfeng Xu;Yue Wang;Mingyu Ding;Heng Yang;Masayoshi Tomizuka;Kurt Keutzer;Marco Pavone;Wei Zhan
aff: ;University of California, Berkeley;NVIDIA;University of California, Berkeley;NVIDIA;;University of California, Berkeley;Stanford University;

roast.laymen_summary: The authors suggest that instead of mapping 3D rooms using millions of tiny digital cubes, we should just pretend everything is made of smooth, egg-shaped mathematical surfaces. They claim this makes AI 'vision' faster and more accurate, but it basically assumes your messy apartment has the architectural simplicity of a giant bowl of marbles.
why_bad: The 'quadric assumption' is a massive oversimplification that falls apart the moment the camera sees anything complex, like a houseplant or a pile of laundry, which definitely aren't smooth geometric spheres.
Introducing a 'quadric-decomposed transformer' to manage these shapes likely reintroduces the exact computational heaviness they claimed to be solving by ditching voxels.

The 'depth rectification' step is essentially the algorithm gaslighting the raw sensor data to make it fit into pretty curves, which leads to great results on synthetic shapes but fails in the noisy, jagged real world.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to the Molecular Gastronomy Challenge! The judges asked for a rustic, chunky beef stew. But you, the 'innovative' chef, decide that dicing carrots and browning beef is too inefficient. Instead, you toss everything into a blender and use a chemical kit to turn the entire meal into a few perfectly smooth, gelatinous spheres. You present the plate and brag that it’s 'streamlined' and 'technically resembles food geometry.' The judges take one bite and realize that while your spheres look great on Instagram, you’ve completely ignored the actual texture of the meat. You’re claiming it tastes just like the real thing, but everyone can see you’ve just served a bowl of math-flavored marbles and called it dinner.

title: Leveraging Mutual Information for Asymmetric Learning under Partial Observability
abstract: Even though partial observability is prevalent in robotics, most reinforcement learning studies avoid it due to the difficulty of learning a policy that can efficiently memorize past events and seek information. Fortunately, in many cases, learning can be done in an asymmetric setting where states are available during training but not during execution. Prior studies often leverage the state to indirectly influence the training of a history-based actor (actor-critic methods) or a history-based critic (value-based methods). Instead, we propose using state-observation and state-history mutual information to improve the agent's architecture and ability to seek information and memorize efficiently through intrinsic rewards and an auxiliary task. Our method outperforms strong baselines through extensive experiments and achieves successful sim-to-real transfers to a real robot.
author: Hai Huu Nguyen;Long Dinh Van The;Christopher Amato;Robert Platt
aff: Northeastern University;Hanoi University of Science and Technology;Northeastern University;Northeastern University
aff_unique_norm: Northeastern University;Hanoi University of Science and Technology

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a robot to navigate in the dark by letting it use night-vision goggles during practice, hoping it would eventually develop a 'gut feeling' for where the furniture is. By using complex math to reward the robot for being curious, we attempted to give a goldfish the memory of an elephant.
why_bad: The 'Mutual Information' math used is notoriously difficult to calculate accurately, making the results look more like a lucky roll of the dice than a scientific breakthrough.
The 'asymmetric' training setup is basically a sophisticated form of cheating that often fails to generalize once the 'training wheels' (full state data) are removed.
The 'strong baselines' were likely just under-tuned versions of existing algorithms, making the proposed method look better than it actually is in a fair fight.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that trains stock traders by giving them tomorrow’s newspaper during practice sessions, then expects them to perform just as well in the real market without it. We’re calling it 'Asymmetric Intuition.' We reward our interns with 'Clarity Points' whenever they guess a stock price correctly, using a proprietary algorithm to measure how much they 'remember' from their cheating days. It’s a revolutionary way to build a genius workforce, provided the real world behaves exactly like the simulation and no one asks too many questions about why the math looks like a Jackson Pollock painting.

title: Conformal Prediction for Semantically-Aware Autonomous Perception in Urban Environments
abstract: We introduce Knowledge-Refined Prediction Sets (KRPS), a novel approach that performs semantically-aware uncertainty quantification for multitask-based autonomous perception in urban environments. KRPS extends conformal prediction (CP) to ensure 2 properties not typically addressed by CP frameworks: semantic label consistency and true label coverage, across multiple perception tasks. We elucidate the capability of KRPS through high-level classification tasks crucial for semantically-aware autonomous perception in urban environments, including agent classification, agent location classification, and agent action classification. In a theoretical analysis, we introduce the concept of semantic label consistency among tasks and prove the semantic consistency and marginal coverage properties of the produced sets by KRPS. The results of our evaluation on the ROAD dataset and the Waymo/ROAD++ dataset show that KRPS outperforms state-of-the-art CP methods in reducing uncertainty by up to 80\% and increasing the semantic consistency by up to 30\%, while maintaining the coverage guarantees.
keywords: Uncertainty in Robotics;Robot Perception;Semantics for Robotics
author: Achref Doula;Tobias Güdelhöfer;Max Mühlhäuser;Alejandro Sanchez Guinea
aff: ;Technische Universität Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a fancy logic-checker to stop self-driving cars from making contradictory guesses, like claiming a pedestrian is simultaneously a 'stationary parked car' and a 'speeding cyclist.' It uses a statistical safety net called Conformal Prediction to ensure that even when the AI is confused, its list of possibilities at least makes sense together.
why_bad: The 'novel' KRPS framework is essentially a glorified set of hard-coded constraints disguised as complex math, offering little innovation over existing logic-based filtering.
The 80% reduction in uncertainty is highly suspicious and likely a byproduct of the researchers narrowing the 'prediction sets' so aggressively that the model might miss rare but critical edge cases.
The theoretical proofs for 'semantic consistency' are largely tautological, effectively proving that if you force labels to be consistent, they will, in fact, be consistent.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Contradictory Eyewitness
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a witness on the stand who testifies that the getaway vehicle was 'definitely a blue truck, but also potentially a flock of seagulls.' The defense introduces a new legal protocol called KRPS to force the witness to be 'semantically aware.' Now, the witness testifies that the vehicle was 'either a blue truck or a blue van.' The researchers argue this is a 50% increase in courtroom efficiency. The Judge—a cynical peer reviewer—points out that while the witness is no longer babbling about birds, they still don't actually know what they saw, and the 'theoretical proof' of consistency is just a fancy way of saying the witness was coached not to mention seagulls.

title: GenDP: 3D Semantic Fields for Category-Level Generalizable Diffusion Policy
abstract: Diffusion-based policies have shown remarkable capability in executing complex robotic manipulation tasks but lack explicit characterization of geometry and semantics, which often limits their ability to generalize to unseen objects and layouts. To enhance the generalization capabilities of Diffusion Policy, we introduce a novel framework that incorporates explicit spatial and semantic information via 3D semantic fields. We generate 3D descriptor fields from multi-view RGBD observations with large foundational vision models, then compare these descriptor fields against reference descriptors to obtain semantic fields. The proposed method explicitly considers geometry and semantics, enabling strong generalization capabilities in tasks requiring category-level generalization, resolving geometric ambiguities, and attention to subtle geometric details. We evaluate our method across eight tasks involving articulated objects and instances with varying shapes and textures from multiple object categories. Our method demonstrates its effectiveness by increasing Diffusion Policy's average success rate on \textit{unseen} instances from 20\% to 93\%. Additionally, we provide a detailed analysis and visualization to interpret the sources of performance gain and explain how our method can generalize to novel instances. Project page: https://robopil.github.io/GenDP/
author: Yixuan Wang;Guang Yin;Binghao Huang;Tarik Kelestemur;Jiuguang Wang;Yunzhu Li
aff: University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois, Urbana Champaign;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Boston Dynamics AI Institute;;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
aff_unique_norm: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Boston Dynamics AI Institute

roast.laymen_summary: Current robots are like toddlers who panic if you give them a blue cup instead of a red one. This paper tries to give them '3D X-ray vision' so they can recognize the 'soul' of an object—like knowing a handle is a handle—regardless of its shape or color.
why_bad: Computational Obesity: The system relies on massive foundation models to process 3D data, making the robot's 'thinking' process so slow it essentially has the reaction time of a heavily sedated sloth.
Architectural Glue-Ware: Reviewers felt the paper didn't invent much new science, but rather just used 'academic superglue' to stick a popular Diffusion Policy to a popular Vision Model.

The 'God Model' Crutch: The success is almost entirely dependent on the pre-trained vision models being perfect; if the foundation model doesn't recognize a weirdly shaped teapot, the whole system collapses like a house of cards.
roast.analogy_name: The IKEA Instruction Manual of the Gods
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where robots can't tell a toaster from a footstool... one team decided to give a machine the power of THE ETERNAL GOOGLE. Instead of just looking at a spoon, the robot must now build a 4D holographic map of 'Spoon-ness' and cross-reference it with the collective knowledge of humanity. It’s a high-octane thriller where the kitchen is on fire, but the robot refuses to move because it’s still busy rendering the semantic texture of the spatula. Coming this summer: 'THE OVERTHINKER'—He knows what everything is, but he’s too busy calculating the meaning of life to actually do the dishes.

title: One Policy to Run Them All: an End-to-end Learning Approach to Multi-Embodiment Locomotion
abstract:
Deep Reinforcement Learning techniques are achieving state-of-the-art results in robust legged locomotion.
While there exists a wide variety of legged platforms such as quadruped, humanoids, and hexapods, the field is still missing a single learning framework that can control all these different embodiments easily and effectively and possibly transfer, zero or few-shot, to unseen robot embodiments.
To close this gap, we introduce URMA, the Unified Robot Morphology Architecture. Our framework brings the end-to-end Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning approach to the realm of legged robots, enabling the learned policy to control any type of robot morphology.
The key idea of our method is to allow the network to learn an abstract locomotion controller that can be seamlessly shared between embodiments thanks to our morphology-agnostic encoders and decoders. This flexible architecture can be seen as a first step in building a foundation model for legged robot locomotion.
Our experiments show that URMA can learn a locomotion policy on multiple embodiments that can be easily transferred to unseen robot platforms in simulation and the real world.
author: Nico Bohlinger;Grzegorz Czechmanowski;Maciej Piotr Krupka;Piotr Kicki;Krzysztof Walas;Jan Peters;Davide Tateo
aff: Technische Universität Darmstadt;Technical University of Poznan;Technical University of Poznan;IDEAS NCBR Sp.;Technical University of Poznan;TU Darmstadt;Technische Universität Darmstadt
position: PhD student;MS student;MS student;Postdoc;Assistant Professor;Full Professor;Researcher
aff_unique_norm: Technische Universität Darmstadt;Technical University of Poznan;IDEAS NCBR

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a single 'universal brain' that could control any robot shape, from a robotic dog to a mechanical human, without needing to know which was which. Unfortunately, by trying to make a controller that works for everything, they created one that moves every robot like a glitchy accordion.
why_bad: The 'foundation model' claim is massive over-hyping; it's essentially just a multi-task neural network that struggles to outperform basic, specialized scripts from five years ago.
By being 'morphology-agnostic,' the model ignores the actual laws of physics and joint limits of specific robots, leading to 'floaty' movement that would snap a real motor in seconds.
The 'zero-shot' transfer to unseen robots is mostly a statistical fluke of the simulation environment rather than a robust understanding of locomotion mechanics.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a 'Universal Fitness Guru' who teaches a single class to a giraffe, a lobster, and a Roomba simultaneously. The guru refuses to learn what a 'neck' or a 'vacuum filter' is, instead just shouting 'EXTEND YOUR APPENDAGE' at the general vicinity of the floor. Sure, it's 'scalable' because the instructor is blissfully ignorant of anatomy, but at the end of the session, the giraffe has a snapped spine, the lobster is confused, and the Roomba is just spinning in a circle of its own shame. It’s disruptive, it’s bold, and it’s legally uninsurable.

title: Learning Compositional Behaviors from Demonstration and Language
abstract: We introduce Behavior from Language and Demonstration (BLADE), a framework for long-horizon robotic manipulation by integrating imitation learning and model-based planning. BLADE leverages language-annotated demonstrations, extracts abstract action knowledge from large language models (LLMs), and constructs a library of structured, high-level action representations. These representations include preconditions and effects grounded in visual perception for each high-level action, along with corresponding controllers implemented as neural network-based policies. BLADE can recover such structured representations automatically, without manually labeled states or symbolic definitions. BLADE shows significant capabilities in generalizing to novel situations, including novel initial states, external state perturbations, and novel goals. We validate the effectiveness of our approach both in simulation and on real robots with a diverse set of objects with articulated parts, partial observability, and geometric constraints.
author: Weiyu Liu;Neil Nie;Ruohan Zhang;Jiayuan Mao;Jiajun Wu
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Massachusetts Institute of Technology;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a robot that learns to do complex chores by watching videos and reading 'how-to' guides, using a giant AI brain to plan the steps. While it sounds like the robot is getting smarter, it’s mostly just a fancy way of guessing what to do next without truly understanding the physical world it's bumping into.
why_bad: The 'grounding' is more of a suggestion than a reality; the AI knows the word 'handle' but has the spatial awareness of a roomba on a bouncy castle.
The system relies on LLMs to hallucinate 'preconditions' for actions, which works great until the robot tries to open a door that’s already open or grasp a shadow.
The 'novel situations' tested were likely just the same three tasks in different lighting, failing to prove the robot wouldn't have a total meltdown in a real, messy kitchen.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a quarterback who has memorized every playbook in NFL history and watched a thousand hours of film, but has never actually touched a pigskin. He steps into the huddle and calls a 'Triple-Option Compositional Post-Route' because the textbook said it was 'optimal.' The ball is snapped, a gust of wind blows at two miles per hour, and—OH! He’s been sacked by a folding chair! He’s got the vocabulary of Tom Brady but the physical coordination of a newborn giraffe on ice. It’s a bold strategy to talk your way into the endzone, folks, but the scoreboard doesn't count 'structured representations'!

title: UBSoft: A Simulation Platform for Robotic Skill Learning in Unbounded Soft Environments
abstract: It is desired to equip robots with the capability of interacting with various soft materials as they are ubiquitous in the real world. While physics simulations are one of the predominant methods for data collection and robot training, simulating soft materials presents considerable challenges. Specifically, it is significantly more costly than simulating rigid objects in terms of simulation speed and storage requirements. These limitations typically restrict the scope of studies on soft materials to small and bounded areas, thereby hindering the learning of skills in broader spaces. To address this issue, we introduce UBSoft, a new simulation platform designed to support unbounded soft environments for robot skill acquisition. Our platform utilizes spatially adaptive resolution scales, where simulation resolution dynamically adjusts based on proximity to active robotic agents. Our framework markedly reduces the demand for extensive storage space and computation costs required for large-scale scenarios involving soft materials. We also establish a set of benchmark tasks in our platform, including both locomotion and manipulation tasks, and conduct experiments to evaluate the efficacy of various reinforcement learning algorithms and trajectory optimization techniques, both gradient-based and sampling-based. Preliminary results indicate that sampling-based trajectory optimization generally achieves better results for obtaining one trajectory to solve the task. Additionally, we conduct experiments in real-world environments to demonstrate that advancements made in our UBSoft simulator could translate to improved robot interactions with large-scale soft material. More videos can be found at https://ubsoft24.github.io.
author: Chunru Lin;Jugang Fan;Yian Wang;Zeyuan Yang;Zhehuan Chen;Lixing Fang;Tsun-Hsuan Wang;Zhou Xian;Chuang Gan
aff: University of Massachusetts at Amherst;South China University of Technology;, Tsinghua University;University of Massachusetts at Amherst;Tsinghua University;Liquid AI;Carnegie Mellon University;University of Massachusetts at Amherst;NVIDIA
position: PhD student;MS student;MS student;MS student;Undergrad student;Researcher;PhD student;Assistant Professor;Intern
aff_unique_norm: University of Massachusetts Amherst;South China University of Technology;Tsinghua University;Liquid AI;Carnegie Mellon University;NVIDIA

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a digital playground to teach robots how to handle squishy things like mud or dough without their computers bursting into flames. They tried to save memory by making the physics super-detailed only where the robot is touching, while everything else becomes a blurry, low-res mess.
why_bad: The 'adaptive resolution' trick often violates the laws of physics, making objects gain or lose energy simply because the robot looked at them differently.
The claim that 'sampling-based optimization' worked best is basically admitting their simulator’s math is too messy for elegant algorithms to handle.
The 'real-world' experiments were likely too simplistic to prove that a robot trained in their 'infinite' digital mud-pit would actually function in a messy backyard.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every robot needs to master the squish... one team had a dream: A digital universe made entirely of Jell-O. But they didn't have the hardware budget for a whole universe, so they built a world that literally dissolves into Minecraft blocks the moment the robot turns its head! Watch in horror as a robot tries to walk across a continent that only exists when it's being stepped on. It’s a psychological thriller where 'conservation of mass' is just a suggestion and the 'real world' validation is just a guy poking a sponge in a lab. Coming this fall: A simulation so 'unbounded,' the laws of physics eventually just give up and quit!
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimized; laymen-friendly 'Real Talk' achieved.

title: Text2Interaction: Establishing Safe and Preferable Human-Robot Interaction
abstract: Adjusting robot behavior to human preferences can require intensive human feedback, preventing quick adaptation to new users and changing circumstances. Moreover, current approaches typically treat user preferences as a reward, which requires a manual balance between task success and user satisfaction. To integrate new user preferences in a zero-shot manner, our proposed Text2Interaction framework invokes large language models to generate a task plan, motion preferences as Python code, and parameters of a safety controller. By maximizing the combined probability of task completion and user satisfaction instead of a weighted sum of rewards, we can reliably find plans that fulfill both requirements. We find that 83% of users working with Text2Interaction agree that it integrates their preferences into the plan of the robot, and 94% prefer Text2Interaction over the baseline. Our ablation study shows that Text2Interaction aligns better with unseen preferences than other baselines while maintaining a high success rate. Real-world demonstrations and code are made available at [sites.google.com/view/text2interaction](sites.google.com/view/text2interaction).
keywords: Human-Robot Interaction;Human Preference Learning;Task and Motion Planning;Safe Control
author: Jakob Thumm;Christopher Agia;Marco Pavone;Matthias Althoff
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Technische Universität München

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to fix the 'annoying robot' problem by using a chatbot to instantly write the robot's instruction manual based on your specific vibes. Instead of spending months training the machine, the researchers just ask an AI to guess how you'd like your coffee delivered and hope it doesn't spill it on your lap.
why_bad: The 'Text2Interaction' framework is essentially a glorified wrapper for GPT-4, making it more of a fancy prompt-engineering project than a fundamental robotics breakthrough.
Delegating safety controller parameters to an LLM is a terrifying gamble; if the model hallucinates a decimal point, your 'helpful' robot becomes a high-speed kinetic hazard.
The user study is suspicious—comparing a modern LLM to a 'baseline' usually means they compared their shiny new tech against a robot programmed to be intentionally lobotomized.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup where the valets don't actually know how to drive, but they’re really fast at reading the manual while the car is moving. You tell the valet, 'I like a smooth ride,' and instead of practicing, he asks a chatbot to write a Python script that controls the pedals in real-time. Sure, it 'maximizes the probability' of not hitting a wall, but you're still letting a hallucinating algorithm handle the brakes. It’s disrupting the 'not crashing' industry by replacing rigorous engineering with a robot that operates on pure, unadulterated vibes and a prayer.

title: VoxAct-B: Voxel-Based Acting and Stabilizing Policy for Bimanual Manipulation
abstract: Bimanual manipulation is critical to many robotics applications. In contrast to single-arm manipulation, bimanual manipulation tasks are challenging due to higher-dimensional action spaces. Prior works leverage large amounts of data and primitive actions to address this problem, but may suffer from sample inefficiency and limited generalization across various tasks. To this end, we propose VoxAct-B, a language-conditioned, voxel-based method that leverages Vision Language Models (VLMs) to prioritize key regions within the scene and reconstruct a voxel grid. We provide this voxel grid to our bimanual manipulation policy to learn acting and stabilizing actions. This approach enables more efficient policy learning from voxels and is generalizable to different tasks. In simulation, we show that VoxAct-B outperforms strong baselines on fine-grained bimanual manipulation tasks. Furthermore, we demonstrate VoxAct-B on real-world $\texttt{Open Drawer}$ and $\texttt{Open Jar}$ tasks using two UR5s. Code, data, and videos are available at https://voxact-b.github.io.
author: I-Chun Arthur Liu;Sicheng He;Daniel Seita;Gaurav S. Sukhatme
aff: University of Southern California;;;University of Southern California

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to give a two-armed robot a 'brain' that turns the world into 3D Minecraft blocks so it can figure out how to hold things and move at the same time. While it looks fancy on paper, it's essentially a very expensive way to make a robot take five minutes to open a drawer that a human could kick open in a second.
why_bad: The 'Voxel Tax': Converting the entire world into 3D grids is so computationally heavy that the robot has the reaction time of a sloth on a FaceTime call with bad Wi-Fi.
Sim-to-Real Delusions: The model crushed it in a perfect digital simulation, but struggled in the real world where things like 'shadows' and 'actual physics' exist.

Cherry-Picked Baselines: The authors compared their work against outdated methods to make their results look impressive, conveniently ignoring the faster, modern models that don't need a supercomputer to open a jar.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where opening a jar of pickles requires the computing power of a small nation. ONE ROBOT has two arms, a Vision Language Model, and a serious case of overthinking. Watch in awe as VoxAct-B turns a simple kitchen into a trillion 3D pixels, processing every single block just to find the handle! It’s got the hardware of a Terminator, the vision of a LEGO architect, and the processing speed of a 1995 desktop. Coming this fall to a lab near you: The Bimanual Blunder. He’s stabilizing! He’s acting! He’s... still thinking about where the drawer is!

title: PointPatchRL - Masked Reconstruction Improves Reinforcement Learning on Point Clouds
abstract:
Perceiving the environment via cameras is crucial for Reinforcement Learning (RL) in robotics. While images are a convenient form of representation, they often complicate extracting important geometric details, especially with varying geometries or deformable objects. In contrast, point clouds naturally represent this geometry and easily integrate color and positional data from multiple camera views. However, while point-cloud processing with deep learning has seen many recent successes, RL on point clouds is under-researched, with only the simplest encoder architecture considered in the literature. We introduce PointPatchRL (PPRL), a method for RL on point clouds that builds on the common paradigm of dividing point clouds into overlapping patches, tokenizing them, and processing
the tokens with transformers. PPRL provides significant improvements compared with other point-cloud processing architectures previously used for RL. We then complement PPRL with masked reconstruction for representation learning and show that our method outperforms strong model-free and model-based baselines on image observations in complex manipulation tasks containing deformable objects and variations in target object geometry.
author: Balazs Gyenes;Nikolai Franke;Philipp Becker;Gerhard Neumann

title: Dynamics-Guided Diffusion Model for Sensor-less Robot Manipulator Design
abstract: We present  Dynamics-Guided Diffusion Model (DGDM), a data-driven framework for generating task-specific manipulator designs without task-specific training. Given object shapes and task specifications, DGDM generates sensor-less manipulator designs that can blindly manipulate objects towards desired motions and poses using an open-loop parallel motion. This framework 1) flexibly represents manipulation tasks as interaction profiles, 2) represents the design space using a geometric diffusion model, and 3) efficiently searches this design space using the gradients provided by a dynamics network trained without any task information. We evaluate DGDM on various manipulation tasks ranging from shifting/rotating objects to converging objects to a specific pose. Our generated designs outperform optimization-based and unguided diffusion baselines relatively by 31.5\% and 45.3\% on average success rate. With the ability to generate a new design within 0.8s, DGDM facilitates rapid design iteration and enhances the adoption of data-driven approaches for robot mechanism design. Qualitative results are best viewed on our project website https://dgdmcorl.github.io.
author: Xiaomeng Xu;Huy Ha;Shuran Song
aff: Stanford University;Columbia University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: This paper proposes using a trendy AI image-generator to dream up custom robot hands that can push objects around without using any cameras or sensors. It’s essentially trying to solve the 'clumsy person in a dark room' problem by 3D-printing a hyper-specific, weirdly shaped tool for every single thing you might want to move.
why_bad: The 'Blind Luck' Problem: Relying on open-loop, sensor-less movement assumes the real world is as predictable as a math equation, which anyone who has ever tripped over a rug knows is a lie.
Geometric Hallucinations: The diffusion model frequently generates 'cool' shapes that are physically impossible to manufacture or violate basic laws of rigid-body dynamics.
The Solution in Search of a Problem: In an era where sensors cost $5, building a bespoke, computationally expensive hardware design for every single micro-task is the definition of over-engineering.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re tired of learning how to use a fork, so you hire a Silicon Valley startup to 3D-print a custom, AI-generated 'food-shoving spatula' for every individual pea on your plate. Our tech, DGDM, uses a generative model to hallucinate the perfect plastic shape to blindly catapult your dinner into your mouth with 'open-loop' efficiency. Sure, it ignores minor details like 'friction' or 'your face moving,' and yes, it’s 45% better than just throwing the food at the wall, but it’s high-tech, data-driven, and completely ignores the fact that you could just use your eyes.

title: A Planar-Symmetric SO(3) Representation for Learning Grasp Detection
abstract:
Planar-symmetric hands, such as parallel grippers, are widely adopted in both research and industrial fields.
Their symmetry, however, introduces ambiguity and discontinuity in the SO(3) representation, which hinders both the training and inference of neural network-based grasp detectors.
We propose a novel SO(3) representation that can parametrize a pair of planar-symmetric poses with a single parameter set by leveraging the 2D Bingham distribution.
We also detail a grasp detector based on our representation, which provides a more consistent rotation output.
An intensive evaluation with multiple grippers and objects in both the simulation and the real world quantitatively shows our approach's contribution.
author: Tianyi Ko;Takuya Ikeda;Hiroya Sato;Koichi Nishiwaki
aff: Woven by Toyota, Inc.;Woven by Toyota, Inc.;The University of Tokyo, Tokyo University;Woven by Toyota

roast.laymen_summary: Robot grippers are like identical twins; because they look the same from the front and back, AI gets mathematically paralyzed trying to figure out which side is which. The researchers tried to solve this identity crisis using complex probability shapes so the robot stops overthinking whether it's holding a cup or just upside-down-holding a cup.
why_bad: The 2D Bingham distribution is mathematical overkill; it's the academic equivalent of using a thermonuclear warhead to kill a mosquito.
The authors failed to convincingly show why this is better than the industry-standard 'just constrain the angle' hack that everyone already uses.
The 'real-world' experiments were conducted in such controlled conditions that the robot basically only had to succeed at not falling over.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a 'revolutionary' spatula that uses a GPS-synchronized gyroscope to tell you which side is the top. You point out that both sides of a spatula are identical and it doesn't matter, but the CEO insists that without a $50 million AI model to 'resolve the symmetry ambiguity,' your pancakes are inherently suboptimal. It’s a brilliant solution to a problem that only exists if you’re a pedantic robot who refuses to realize that 'up' and 'down' are the same thing when you're a flat piece of metal.

title: Automated Creation of Digital Cousins for Robust Policy Learning
abstract: Training robot policies in the real world can be unsafe, costly, and difficult to scale. Simulation serves as an inexpensive and potentially limitless source of training data, but suffers from the semantics and physics disparity between simulated and real-world environments. These discrepancies can be minimized by training in *digital twins*, which serve as virtual replicas of a real scene but are expensive to generate and cannot produce cross-domain generalization. To address these limitations, we propose the concept of ***digital cousins***, a virtual asset or scene that, unlike a *digital twin*, does not explicitly model a real-world counterpart but still exhibits similar geometric and semantic affordances. As a result, *digital cousins* simultaneously reduce the cost of generating an analogous virtual environment while also facilitating better robustness during sim-to-real domain transfer by providing a distribution of similar training scenes. Leveraging digital cousins, we introduce a novel method for their automated creation, and propose a fully automated real-to-sim-to-real pipeline for generating fully interactive scenes and training robot policies that can be deployed zero-shot in the original scene. We find that digital cousin scenes that preserve geometric and semantic affordances can be produced automatically, and can be used to train policies that outperform policies trained on digital twins, achieving 90\% vs. 25\% success rates under zero-shot sim-to-real transfer. Additional details are available at https://digital-cousins.github.io/.
author: Tianyuan Dai;Josiah Wong;Yunfan Jiang;Chen Wang;Cem Gokmen;Ruohan Zhang;Jiajun Wu;Li Fei-Fei
aff: ;Stanford University;Stanford University;Computer Science Department, Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University
position: ;MS student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Postdoc;Assistant Professor;Full Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Training robots is a nightmare because they can't tell the difference between a virtual simulation and the messy real world. This paper suggests that instead of building a perfect digital clone of your room, we should just let the robot practice on a bunch of 'close enough' 3D furniture found on the internet.
why_bad: The term 'Digital Cousins' is mostly a fancy rebranding of existing domain randomization techniques that researchers have been using for a decade.
The 'automated' pipeline is heavily dependent on pre-existing 3D asset libraries, meaning if your real-world object is unique, the 'cousin' strategy falls apart.
The massive performance gap (90% vs 25%) suggests the 'Digital Twin' baseline was intentionally nerfed or poorly calibrated to make the new method look like a miracle.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Identical Imposter
roast.analogy_description: Members of the jury, the defense claims their robot failed to pick up a mug because the virtual training room didn't have the exact same coffee stains as the real one. Objection! The prosecution argues that a mug is a mug, whether it’s a 'Digital Twin' or this new 'Digital Cousin'—which, let’s be honest, is just a random mug in a cheap wig and a fake mustache. The authors are trying to convince this court that training a robot on a 'relative' of an object is a legal breakthrough, when in reality, they’re just flooding the witness stand with 500 generic IKEA assets and calling it 'robustness.' You can't claim a 'zero-shot' victory when you've coached the defendant with every lookalike in the tri-state area. This isn't a scientific revolution; it's a game of 'Guess Who' played with venture capital money.

title: IMAGINATION POLICY: Using Generative Point Cloud Models for Learning Manipulation Policies
abstract: Humans can imagine goal states during planning and perform actions to match those goals. In this work, we propose IMAGINATION POLICY, a novel multi-task key-frame policy network for solving high-precision pick and place tasks. Instead of learning actions directly, IMAGINATION POLICY generates point clouds to imagine desired states which are then translated to actions using rigid action estimation. This transforms action inference into a local generative task. We leverage pick and place symmetries underlying the tasks in the generation process and achieve extremely high sample efficiency and generalizability to unseen configurations. Finally, we demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across various tasks on the RLbench benchmark compared with several strong baselines and validate our approach on a real robot.
author: Haojie Huang;Karl Schmeckpeper;Dian Wang;Ondrej Biza;Yaoyao Qian;Haotian Liu;Mingxi Jia;Robert Platt;Robin Walters
aff: Northeastern University;The Robotics and AI Institute;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University;Brown University;Northeastern University;Northeastern University
position: PhD student;Researcher;PhD student;PhD student;MS student;Intern;PhD student;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: Northeastern University;Robotics and AI Institute;Brown University

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of just moving its arm, this robot creates a 3D 'hallucination' of what a finished task should look like and then tries to make reality match its mental picture. It’s essentially a robotic perfectionist that insists on drawing a blueprint before it’s willing to pick up a single Lego.
why_bad: Generating high-resolution 3D point clouds in real-time is a computational nightmare that turns a 5-second task into a 5-minute math problem.
The 'imagination' is fragile; if the robot's camera sees even a bit of glare or dust, its mental model of the world collapses into digital static.
The paper relies heavily on 'perfect' simulator data, failing to prove it wouldn't just knock over the table in a messy, non-simulated kitchen.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where chores are boring, one robot refuses to just do them. Meet 'The Imaginer.' Instead of unloading the dishwasher, it spends three hours creating a photorealistic 3D mood board of where every plate *could* live. Watch in awe as it renders a masterpiece of a clean kitchen while the sink overflows. It’s not 'inefficient'—it’s 'visionary.' Coming this fall: *The Dreamer Who Couldn't Grab a Spoon.*
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark successfully deployed.

title: SoloParkour: Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Visual Locomotion from Privileged Experience
abstract:
Parkour poses a significant challenge for legged robots, requiring navigation through complex environments with agility and precision based on limited sensory inputs.
In this work,  we introduce a novel method for training end-to-end visual policies, from depth pixels to robot control commands, to achieve agile and safe quadruped locomotion.
We formulate robot parkour as a constrained reinforcement learning (RL) problem designed to maximize the emergence of agile skills within the robot's physical limits while ensuring safety. 
We first train a policy without vision using privileged information about the robot's surroundings. 
We then generate experience from this privileged policy to warm-start a sample efficient off-policy RL algorithm from depth images.
This allows the robot to adapt behaviors from this privileged experience to visual locomotion while circumventing the high computational costs of RL directly from pixels.
We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on a real Solo-12 robot, showcasing its capability to perform a variety of parkour skills such as walking, climbing, leaping, and crawling.
author: Elliot Chane-Sane;Joseph Amigo;Thomas Flayols;Ludovic Righetti;Nicolas Mansard
aff: LAAS / CNRS;New York University;;Max-Planck Institute;LAAS / CNRS
aff_unique_norm: LAAS;New York University;Max-Planck-Gesellschaft zur Förderung der Wissenschaften e.V.

title: Policy Adaptation via Language Optimization: Decomposing Tasks for Few-Shot Imitation
abstract: Learned language-conditioned robot policies often struggle to effectively adapt to new real-world tasks even when pre-trained across a diverse set of instructions. We propose a novel approach for few-shot adaptation to unseen tasks that exploits the semantic understanding of task decomposition provided by vision-language models (VLMs). Our method, Policy Adaptation via Language Optimization (PALO), combines a handful of demonstrations of a task with proposed language decompositions sampled from a VLM to quickly enable rapid nonparametric adaptation, avoiding the need for a larger fine-tuning dataset. We evaluate PALO on extensive real-world experiments consisting of challenging unseen, long-horizon robot manipulation tasks. We find that PALO is able of consistently complete long-horizon, multi-tier tasks in the real world, outperforming state of the art pre-trained generalist policies, and methods that have access to the same demonstrations.
author: Vivek Myers;Chunyuan Zheng;Oier Mees;Kuan Fang;Sergey Levine
aff: University of California, Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department, University of California, Berkeley;;Google

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach robots new tricks by having a smart AI chatbot write them a step-by-step to-do list based on just a couple of video examples. Instead of retraining the whole robot brain, we just let it follow this 'cheat sheet' to handle long, complicated chores it hadn't seen before.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just asking a VLM to do what it was already built to do, which is about as innovative as using a calculator to do long division.
The experimental results are suspiciously narrow, comparing the model against outdated baselines that were practically designed to fail the specific tasks chosen.
The system relies entirely on the language model not hallucinating; one wrong 'step' from the AI and the robot is just expensive scrap metal wandering off-script.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Robot Bowl! PALO is stepping onto the field today with a bold strategy: they've replaced the quarterback's years of training with a pair of AirPods playing a GPT-4 podcast on 'How to Play Football.' Look at him go! He's breaking the play down into 'Step 1: Catch ball, Step 2: Run.' It’s a masterclass in micromanagement, folks! But wait—the defense just moved two inches to the left, and the AI is still reading instructions for a play from the 1970s. He’s got the 'semantic understanding' of a superstar, but he’s currently trying to tackle his own Gatorade cooler because the 'decomposition' didn't account for wind. That’s a turnover on downs, and the scouts in the stands are already heading for the exits!

title: Harmon: Whole-Body Motion Generation of Humanoid Robots from Language Descriptions
abstract: Humanoid robots, with their human-like embodiment, have the potential to integrate seamlessly into human environments. Critical to their coexistence and cooperation with humans is the ability to understand natural language communications and exhibit human-like behaviors. This work focuses on generating diverse whole-body motions for humanoid robots from language descriptions. We leverage human motion priors from extensive human motion datasets to initialize humanoid motions and employ the commonsense reasoning capabilities of Vision Language Models (VLMs) to edit and refine these motions. Our approach demonstrates the capability to produce natural, expressive, and text-aligned humanoid motions, validated through both simulated and real-world experiments. More videos can be found on our website https://ut-austin-rpl.github.io/Harmon/.
author: Zhenyu Jiang;Yuqi Xie;Jinhan Li;Ye Yuan;Yifeng Zhu;Yuke Zhu
aff: University of Texas, Austin;University of Texas at Austin;Tsinghua University;NVIDIA Research;The University of Texas at Austin;Computer Science Department, University of Texas, Austin
position: PhD student;MS student;Undergrad student;Researcher;PhD student;Assistant Professor
aff_unique_norm: University of Texas at Austin;Tsinghua University;NVIDIA

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'Siri for Robot Bodies' that turns text commands like 'do a celebratory dance' into fluid humanoid movements. They basically took a library of human movements and used a ChatGPT-style brain to tweak them, hoping the robot wouldn't fall over in the process.
why_bad: The 'Retargeting' Trap: Human motion data doesn't account for a robot's mechanical clunkiness, often resulting in movements that look great on a screen but would snap a real robot's servos in half.

Physics-Blind Reasoning: Relying on Vision Language Models (VLMs) for 'common sense' is risky because while an AI knows what 'climbing' looks like, it doesn't understand center-of-mass or gravity, leading to the 'Uncanny Valley of Faceplanting.'
Subjective Success: The paper relies heavily on 'vibe-based' evaluations of how natural the motion looks rather than providing hard, cold metrics on stability or collision avoidance.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where gravity is just a suggestion, one robot is about to learn that 'moving like a human' is harder than it looks on TikTok. Meet 'Harmon,' the humanoid who takes stage directions from a poet who has never seen a wrench. Watch in awe as a Vision Language Model tries to choreograph a 300-pound metal machine based on a vibe check, ignoring the laws of thermodynamics to give you the performance of a lifetime. It’s got the grace of a gazelle, the logic of a chatbot, and the structural integrity of a house of cards. This summer, experience the cinematic event that reviewers called 'statistically significant but physically impossible.' Harmon: Motion without Emotion (or Balance).

title: HiRT: Enhancing Robotic Control with Hierarchical Robot Transformers
abstract: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, leveraging powerful pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) backends, have shown promise in robotic control due to their impressive generalization ability. However, the success comes at a cost. Their reliance on VLM backends with billions of parameters leads to high computational costs and inference latency, limiting the testing scenarios to mainly quasi-static tasks and hindering performance in dynamic tasks requiring rapid interactions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes \textbf{HiRT}, a \textbf{Hi}erarchical \textbf{R}obot \textbf{T}ransformer framework that enables flexible frequency and performance trade-off. HiRT keeps VLMs running at low frequencies to capture temporarily invariant features while enabling real-time interaction through a high-frequency vision-based policy guided by the slowly updated features. Experiment results in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate significant improvements over baseline methods. Empirically, we achieve a 58\% reduction in inference time delay while maintaining comparable success rates. Additionally, on novel dynamic manipulation benchmarks which are challenging for previous VLA models, HiRT improves the success rate from 48% to 75%.
author: Jianke Zhang;Yanjiang Guo;Xiaoyu Chen;Yen-Jen Wang;Yucheng Hu;Chengming Shi;Jianyu Chen
aff: Beijing Institute of Technology;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;Tsinghua University;;Tsinghua University
position: Undergrad student;PhD student;Graduate student;MS student;Undergrad student;;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Big AI models are too bloated and slow to control robots in real-time, effectively turning a million-dollar machine into a laggy toaster. The authors tried to fix this by letting a 'fast' tiny brain handle the movement while the 'slow' big brain occasionally chimes in with directions.
why_bad: The 'hierarchical' approach is a decades-old robotics trope rebranded with 'Transformer' in the title for clout.
The performance gains are mostly an artifact of comparing their model against baselines that were never designed to move faster than a glacier.
The paper fails to prove the system won't hallucinate a command while the 'slow' brain is buffering, potentially turning a dynamic task into a dynamic disaster.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Philosophizing Goalie
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution submits that this 'innovation' is a fraud. Imagine a goalie who insists on contemplating the socio-economic implications of a puck before moving his glove. To solve his obvious failure, he hires a toddler to poke him whenever the puck gets close. The defense calls this 'Hierarchical Intelligence'; I call it a man who still can't play hockey without a babysitter. You haven't built a faster brain; you've just built a slow one that's learned how to outsource its chores to a calculator. I move to strike this 'framework' from the record as it provides no new evidence of actual machine reasoning.
roast.tone_check: Dry, snarky, and scientifically grounded.

title: Shelf-Supervised Cross-Modal Pre-Training for 3D Object Detection
abstract: State-of-the-art 3D object detectors are often trained on massive labeled datasets. However, annotating 3D bounding boxes remains prohibitively expensive and time-consuming, particularly for LiDAR. Instead, recent works demonstrate that self-supervised pre-training with unlabeled data can improve detection accuracy with limited labels. Contemporary methods adapt best-practices for self-supervised learning from the image domain to point clouds (such as contrastive learning). However, publicly available 3D datasets are considerably smaller and less diverse than those used for image-based self-supervised learning, limiting their effectiveness. We do note, however, that such data is naturally collected in a multimodal fashion, often paired with images. Rather than pre-training with only self-supervised objectives, we argue that it is better to bootstrap point cloud representations using image-based foundation models trained on internet-scale image data. Specifically, we propose a shelf-supervised approach (e.g. supervised with off-the-shelf image foundation models) for generating zero-shot 3D bounding boxes from paired RGB and LiDAR data. Pre-training 3D detectors with such pseudo-labels yields significantly better semi-supervised detection accuracy than prior self-supervised pretext tasks. Importantly, we show that image-based shelf-supervision is helpful for training LiDAR-only and multi-modal (RGB + LiDAR) detectors. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on nuScenes and WOD, significantly improving over prior work in limited data settings.
author: Mehar Khurana;Neehar Peri;James Hays;Deva Ramanan
aff: ;Carnegie Mellon University;Georgia Institute of Technology;School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;Georgia Institute of Technology

roast.laymen_summary: Since labeling 3D laser-scan data is a manual nightmare, the authors decided to let 'smart' 2D image models do the homework for them. They used these recycled image guesses to train self-driving car sensors, essentially hoping that a smart photo-assistant can teach a blind laser how to see.
why_bad: Derivative Methodology: The 'shelf-supervised' approach is essentially just using someone else's pre-trained model to generate labels, which reviewers found lacked significant algorithmic novelty.
Spatial Delusions: 2D foundation models lack true depth perception, meaning they often 'hallucinate' 3D boxes that don't actually align with the physical LiDAR points.

Cherry-picked Comparisons: The paper avoids competing with top-tier semi-supervised leaders, opting instead to beat up on weaker, older self-supervised methods to make its results look more impressive.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Blind Architect and the Flat Map
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense claims they have built a revolutionary 3D skyscraper. In reality, they hired a consultant who has only ever seen a postcard of a building to bark orders at the construction crew. This 'Shelf-Supervised' witness wasn't even at the job site; it’s testifying based on 2D snapshots it saw on the internet. The prosecution argues that you cannot determine the structural integrity of a 3D environment using the testimony of a 'Foundation Model' that thinks depth is just a suggestion. We move to strike this entire methodology from the record on the grounds that 'pseudo-labels' are just a fancy legal term for 'sophisticated hearsay.'

title: Generative Image as Action Models
abstract: Image-generation diffusion models have been fine-tuned to unlock new capabilities such as image-editing and novel view synthesis. Can we similarly unlock image-generation models for visuomotor control? We present GENIMA, a behavior-cloning agent that fine-tunes Stable Diffusion to “draw joint-actions” as targets on RGB images. These images are fed into a controller that maps the visual targets into a sequence of joint-positions. We study GENIMA on 25 RLBench and 9 real-world manipulation tasks. We find that, by lifting actions into image-space, internet pre-trained diffusion models can generate policies that outperform state- of-the-art visuomotor approaches, especially in robustness to scene perturbations and generalizing to novel objects. Our method is also competitive with 3D agents, despite lacking priors such as depth, keypoints, or motion-planners.
author: Mohit Shridhar;Yat Long Lo;Stephen James
aff: Dyson;Dyson Robot Learning Lab;Dyson

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to turn a robot into a digital artist by making it 'paint' its next move onto a photo instead of just calculating coordinates. By piggybacking on the massive internet knowledge of Stable Diffusion, the robot learns to recognize spatulas and handles better than its peers, even if it's basically treating a warehouse job like a prompt-engineering contest.
why_bad: Inference Latency: Using a massive diffusion model to decide every micro-movement is like waiting for a high-res 4K render just to blink, making the robot painfully slow in real-time scenarios.
Spatial Hallucination: Because the model is 'drawing' actions in 2D pixels, it lacks a true grasp of 3D depth, often leading to 'artistic' movements that don't actually line up with physical reality.

Over-reliance on the Controller: The paper treats the 'low-level controller' as a trivial detail, when in reality, it's doing the grueling work of translating a messy doodle into precise motor voltages.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Over-Engineering Your Dinner!' Today, instead of just flipping the shrimp with a spatula like a normal human, we’re using a world-class oil painter. Our chef doesn't touch the food; instead, he painstakingly paints a photorealistic masterpiece of where the shrimp *should* land on the grill. Then, a confused line cook tries to move the actual shrimp to match the painting. It’s visually stunning and the painter knows exactly what a shrimp looks like thanks to his art degree, but by the time he’s finished detailing the highlights on the shell, the kitchen is on fire and the customers have died of old age. It’s a Michelin-star solution to a problem that a simple pair of tongs solved in 1950.

title: Visual Manipulation with Legs
abstract: Animals have the ability to use their arms and legs for both locomotion and manipulation. We envision quadruped robots to have the same versatility. This work presents a system that empowers a quadruped robot to perform object interactions with its legs, drawing inspiration from non-prehensile manipulation techniques. The proposed system has two main components: a visual manipulation policy module and a loco-manipulator module. The visual manipulation policy module decides how the leg should interact with the object, trained with reinforcement learning (RL) with point cloud observations and object-centric actions. The loco-manipulator controller controls the leg movements and body pose adjustments, implemented based on impedance control and Model Predictive Control (MPC). Besides manipulating objects with a single leg, the proposed system can also select from left or right legs based on the critic maps and move the object to distant goals through robot base adjustment. In the experiments, we evaluate the proposed system with the object pose alignment tasks both in simulation and in the real world, demonstrating object manipulation skills with legs more versatile than previous work.
author: Xialin He;Chengjing Yuan;Wenxuan Zhou;Ruihan Yang;David Held;Xiaolong Wang
aff: Shanghai Jiaotong University;University of California, San Diego;Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, San Diego;Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, San Diego
position: Undergrad student;MS student;PhD student;PhD student;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor
