title: Reasoning Grasping via Multimodal Large Language Model
abstract: Despite significant progress in robotic systems for operation within human-centric environments, existing models still heavily rely on explicit human commands to identify and manipulate specific objects. This limits their effectiveness in environments where understanding and acting on implicit human intentions are crucial. In this study, we introduce a novel task: reasoning grasping, where robots need to generate grasp poses based on indirect verbal instructions or intentions. To accomplish this, we propose an end-to-end reasoning grasping model that integrates a multimodal Large Language Model (LLM) with a vision-based robotic grasping framework. In addition, we present the first reasoning grasping benchmark dataset generated from the GraspNet-1 billion, incorporating implicit instructions for object-level and part-level grasping, and this dataset will soon be available for public access. Our results show that directly integrating CLIP or LLaVA with the grasp detection model performs poorly on the challenging reasoning grasping tasks, while our proposed model demonstrates significantly enhanced performance both in the reasoning grasping benchmark and real-world experiments.
author: Shiyu Jin;JINXUAN XU;Yutian Lei;Liangjun Zhang
aff: ;Rutgers University, New Brunswick;;Research, Baidu

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to upgrade robots from mindless drones that need specific orders to 'intuitive' assistants that can take a hint, like grabbing a glass when you say you're thirsty. They basically slapped a giant AI brain onto a mechanical claw and built a custom test to prove their robot isn't as confused as the existing ones.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just using digital duct tape to stick an off-the-shelf Large Language Model onto a standard grasping algorithm without any real architectural innovation.
The baseline comparisons are 'straw man' arguments, comparing their specialized system against general models that were never actually designed to move physical objects.
The new benchmark dataset is largely synthetic and lacks the messy, unpredictable 'noise' of actual human environments, making the high performance scores look suspiciously inflated.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Guess What I Want' Kitchen Blunder
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Intelligence Open, where the 'Reasoning Grasping' team is stepping up to the plate! The crowd goes quiet as the human drops a subtle hint: 'I sure am hungry.' The model pauses—it’s processing, it’s thinking, it’s querying the LLM—and it goes for... a raw onion! The announcers are stunned! The researchers are celebrating like they just won the World Series because the robot didn't grab a shoe, but the refs are heading to the VAR. Upon review, it turns out the robot only succeeded because they moved the shoe out of the room and replaced the onion with a bright red target. It’s a classic 'hallucination in the hardware' play, folks, and the judges are holding up the 'Rejected' card for a lack of fundamental hustle!

title: VLM-Grounder: A VLM Agent for Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding
abstract: 3D visual grounding is crucial for robots, requiring integration of natural language and 3D scene understanding. Traditional methods depend on supervised learning with 3D point clouds are limited by scarce datasets. Recently zero-shot methods leveraging LLMs have been proposed to address the data issue. While effective, these methods often miss detailed scene context, limiting their ability to handle complex queries. In this work, we present VLM-Grounder, a novel framework using vision-language models (VLMs) for zero-shot 3D visual grounding based solely on 2D images. VLM-Grounder dynamically stitches image sequences, employs a grounding and feedback scheme to find the target object, and uses a multi-view ensemble projection to accurately estimate 3D bounding boxes. Experiments on ScanRefer and Nr3D datasets show VLM-Grounder outperforms previous zero-shot methods, achieving 51.6\% Acc@0.25 on ScanRefer and 48.0\% Acc on Nr3D, without relying on 3D geometry or object priors.
author: Runsen Xu;Zhiwei Huang;Tai Wang;Yilun Chen;Jiangmiao Pang;Dahua Lin
aff: The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Zhejiang University;Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory;Shanghai AI Laboratory ;The Chinese University of Hong Kong;Shanghai AI Laboratory
position: PhD student;MS student;Researcher;Research Scientist;Associate Professor;Research Scientist
aff_unique_norm: Chinese University of Hong Kong;Zhejiang University;Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory;Shanghai AI Laboratory

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to teach robots how to find objects in a 3D room by having them look at a bunch of 2D photos instead of actually understanding 3D space. It’s essentially trying to navigate a physical maze by looking at a scrapbook of your vacation photos and hoping for the best.
why_bad: Geometric Illiteracy: By ignoring raw 3D point clouds, the model often forgets that objects have depth, leading to 'hallucinated' locations that don't exist in the real world.
The API Money Pit: Relying on heavy Vision-Language Models for every frame is computationally expensive and slow enough to make a robot look like it's buffering in the middle of a kitchen.
Stitching Scars: The 'dynamic image stitching' is prone to visual artifacts, meaning if the photos don't line up perfectly, the AI gets confused and tries to find a chair that's been sliced in half.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where depth is a myth and perspective is a suggestion... one robot attempts the impossible. Armed only with a Polaroid camera and a textbook on 'How to Speak Human,' it must assemble a 3D bookshelf using nothing but 2D snapshots of the instructions. Witness the suspense as it stitches together a collage of chaos! Can it find the 'Hex Key' before its battery—and its budget—dies? This summer, experience the spatial reasoning of a drunk pigeon in... THE VLM-GROUNDER. It’s not 3D; it’s just 2D with a very expensive imagination.

title: So You Think You Can Scale Up Autonomous Robot Data Collection?
abstract: A long-standing goal in robot learning is to develop methods for robots to acquire new skills autonomously. While reinforcement learning (RL) comes with the promise of enabling autonomous data collection, it remains challenging to scale in the real-world partly due to the significant effort required for environment design and instrumentation, including the need for designing reset functions or accurate success detectors. On the other hand, imitation learning (IL) methods require little to no environment design effort, but instead require significant human supervision in the form of collected demonstrations. To address these shortcomings, recent works in autonomous IL start with an initial seed dataset of human demonstrations that an autonomous policy can bootstrap from. While autonomous IL approaches come with the promise of addressing the challenges of autonomous RL—environment design challenges—as well as the challenges of pure IL strategies—extensive human supervision—in this work, we posit that such techniques do not deliver on this promise and are still unable to scale up autonomous data collection in the real world.  Through a series of targeted real-world experiments, we demonstrate that these approaches, when scaled up to realistic settings, face much of the same scaling challenges as prior attempts in RL in terms of environment design. Further, we perform a rigorous study of various autonomous IL methods across different data scales and 7 simulation and real-world tasks, and demonstrate that while autonomous data collection can modestly improve performance (on the order of 10%), simply collecting more human data often provides significantly more improvement. Our work suggests a negative result: that scaling up autonomous data collection for learning robot policies for real-world tasks is more challenging and impractical than what is suggested in prior work. We hope these insights about the core challenges of scaling up data collection help inform future efforts in autonomous learning.
author: Suvir Mirchandani;Suneel Belkhale;Joey Hejna;Evelyn Choi;Md Sazzad Islam;Dorsa Sadigh
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Google
position: PhD student;PhD student;Undergrad student;Undergrad student;Assistant Professor;Intern

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to prove robots could finally learn new tricks on their own, but they accidentally discovered that 'autonomous' training is actually more work for humans than just doing the job ourselves. It turns out that letting a robot practice by itself only makes it 10% better, while just giving it a few more human-led lessons is way more effective.
why_bad: The 'Incrementalism' Trap: A 10% performance boost isn't a breakthrough; it's a rounding error that doesn't justify the massive hardware headache.
Lack of Novelty: Reviewers likely felt the conclusion that 'real-world robotics is hard' is the academic equivalent of saying water is wet.

The Human-in-the-Loop Paradox: The paper argues for scaling, but their own data suggests that humans are still the only part of the system that actually works, making the 'autonomous' label feel like false advertising.
roast.analogy_name: The Self-Driving Toaster That Still Needs You to Hold the Bread
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where humans dreamed of a robot that could learn to cook while they slept. ONE TEAM of scientists spent millions to build the ultimate autonomous chef... only to realize it needs a five-man pit crew just to flip a pancake. Witness the heart-pounding mediocrity of a machine that promises 'Total Independence' but still demands you set the table, pre-cut the veggies, and stand by with a fire extinguisher. It’s not a revolution; it’s a $50,000 intern that needs a pep talk every three seconds. Coming this summer: THE AUTOMATION PARADOX. (Rated 'R' for Restricted Scaling).

title: Task-Oriented Hierarchical Object Decomposition for Visuomotor Control
abstract:
Good pre-trained visual representations could enable robots to learn visuomotor policy efficiently. Still, existing representations take a one-size-fits-all-tasks approach that comes with two important drawbacks: (1) Being completely task-agnostic, these representations cannot effectively ignore any task-irrelevant information in the scene,  and (2) They often lack the representational capacity to handle unconstrained/complex real-world scenes. Instead, we propose to train a large combinatorial family of representations organized by scene entities: objects and object parts. This hierarchical object decomposition for task-oriented representations (HODOR) permits selectively assembling different representations specific to each task while scaling in representational capacity with the complexity of the scene and the task. In our experiments, we find that HODOR outperforms prior pre-trained representations, both scene vector representations and object-centric representations, for sample-efficient imitation learning across 5 simulated and 5 real-world manipulation tasks. We further find that the invariances captured in HODOR are inherited into downstream policies, which can robustly generalize to out-of-distribution test conditions, permitting zero-shot skill chaining. Appendix and videos: https://sites.google.com/view/
hodor-corl24
author: Jianing Qian;Yunshuang Li;Bernadette Bucher;Dinesh Jayaraman
aff: School of Engineering and Applied Science, University of Pennsylvania;University of Pennsylvania;Boston Dynamics AI Institute;University of Pennsylvania
aff_unique_norm: University of Pennsylvania;Boston Dynamics AI Institute
aff_unique_dep: School of Engineering and Applied Science;AI Institute

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers realized that robots get easily distracted by background clutter, so they built a system called HODOR to force the AI to focus only on the specific object parts it needs to touch. It’s essentially 'The Art of Not Giving a Damn' for robot vision, attempting to filter out the messy world to focus solely on the task at hand.
why_bad: The 'hierarchical decomposition' relies heavily on external pre-trained models to do the actual heavy lifting, making the authors' specific contribution feel like a thin layer of icing on someone else's cake.
The 'zero-shot skill chaining' claims are statistically shaky, with experimental results that look more like a series of lucky guesses than a robust, reproducible new capability.

The baselines were suspiciously weak; comparing HODOR to basic scene vectors is like bragging that your supercar is faster than a golf cart while conveniently ignoring the Ferraris parked next to you.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Selective Spectator
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the Prosecution argues that the defendant, 'HODOR,' is guilty of visual perjury. The defense claims their client can intelligently 'decompose' a scene into meaningful parts, but Exhibit A shows the robot failing to distinguish a spatula handle from a shadow the moment the lighting shifts by two percent. We call Reviewer 2 to the stand, who testifies that this 'hierarchical' structure is nothing more than three basic algorithms in a trench coat, pretending to be a revolutionary architecture. While the defense screams about 'zero-shot generalization,' the jury sees a system that only performs when the 'real-world' environment is as sterile as a surgery suite. Your Honor, we move to strike the entire 'Results' section from the record on the grounds of extreme cherry-picking and lack of statistical significance!

title: Large Scale Mapping of Indoor Magnetic Field by Local and Sparse Gaussian Processes
abstract: Magnetometer-based indoor navigation uses variations in the magnetic field to determine the robot's location. For that, a magnetic map of the environment has to be built beforehand from a collection of localized magnetic measurements. Existing solutions built on sparse Gaussian Process (GP) regression do not scale well to large environments, being either slow or resulting in discontinuous prediction. In this paper, we propose to model the magnetic field of large environments based on GP regression. We first modify a deterministic training conditional sparse GP by accounting for magnetic field physics to map small environments efficiently. We then scale the model on larger scenes by introducing a local expert aggregation framework. It splits the scene into subdomains, fits a local expert on each, and then aggregates expert predictions in a differentiable and probabilistic way. We evaluate our model on real and simulated data and show that we can smoothly map a three-story building in a few hundred milliseconds.
keywords: Gaussian process regression;magnetic field maps;indoor localization
author: Iad ABDUL-RAOUF;Vincent Gay-Bellile;Cyril JOLY;Steve Bourgeois;Alexis Paljic
aff: Mines ParisTech;CEA;Mines ParisTech;CEA;
aff_unique_norm: MINES ParisTech;Commissariat à l'Énergie Atomique et aux Énergies Alternatives

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to use the Earth's magnetic field as a giant, invisible GPS for robots wandering through office buildings. The authors tried to solve the 'slow computer' problem by chopping the building into small zones and letting local math models handle the heavy lifting, promising a map that's both fast and smooth.
why_bad: The 'local expert' approach creates 'seams' at the boundaries, meaning the robot might think it's in two places at once the moment it walks through a doorway.
The 'physics-informed' tweaks are essentially just standard vector math dressed up in a tuxedo to look like a groundbreaking discovery.
Validation on simulated data is a massive red flag because real-world magnetic noise from elevators and vending machines would turn their 'smooth' map into static noise.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Indoor Nav Championships! Team Sparse-GP is trying a bold new strategy: a relay race where every runner is locked in a separate soundproof booth. They’re 'fast' because they’re all running at the same time, but when it’s time to pass the baton, they’re just throwing it blindly over the wall and hoping the next guy catches it. It's a high-speed disaster—sure, the individual laps look great on paper, but the moment the robot tries to cross the 'expert' boundary, it's face-planting into the turf because the handoff was never actually coordinated!

title: Flow as the Cross-domain Manipulation Interface
abstract: We present Im2Flow2Act, a scalable learning framework that enables robots to acquire real-world manipulation skills without the need of real-world robot training data. The key idea behind Im2Flow2Act is to use object flow as the manipulation interface, bridging domain gaps between different embodiments (i.e., human and robot) and training environments (i.e., real-world and simulated). Im2Flow2Act comprises two components: a flow generation network and a flow-conditioned policy. The flow generation network, trained on human demonstration videos, generates object flow from the initial scene image, conditioned on the task description. The flow-conditioned policy, trained on simulated robot play data, maps the generated object flow to robot actions to realize the desired object movements. By using flow as input, this policy can be directly deployed in the real world with a minimal sim-to-real gap. By leveraging real-world human videos and simulated robot play data, we bypass the challenges of teleoperating physical robots in the real world, resulting in a scalable system for diverse tasks. We demonstrate Im2Flow2Act's capabilities in a variety of real-world tasks, including the manipulation of rigid, articulated, and deformable objects.
author: Mengda Xu;Zhenjia Xu;Yinghao Xu;Cheng Chi;Gordon Wetzstein;Manuela Veloso;Shuran Song
aff: Columbia University;Columbia University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;School of Computer Science, Carnegie Mellon University;Stanford University
position: PhD student;PhD student;Postdoc;PhD student;Associate Professor;Full Professor;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach robots to do chores by having them watch videos of humans and then imagining how the pixels should move. It's basically 'Simon Says' for machines, where the robot tries to mimic the 'vibe' of a moving object without ever actually being told how a physical hand works.
why_bad: The 'Flow' middleman ignores physics; it knows where a cup should end up but has no clue how much force is needed to keep it from shattering.
It assumes human hands and robot grippers are interchangeable, which is like teaching a forklift to perform surgery by watching a YouTube video of a doctor.
Compounding errors: If the first AI hallucinates a slightly wrong movement, the second AI executes that mistake with 100% confidence, leading to a physical train wreck.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Ghostly Mime
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents a robot accused of 'Total Incompetence.' The defense claims the defendant learned to cook by watching a mime pretend to make an omelet. Look at the 'Flow' evidence: they didn't show the robot the chef's hands, the heat of the pan, or the crack of the egg—they just showed it a blurry 2D map of where the yellow stuff moved! Your Honor, this isn't robotics; it's a game of 'Follow the Leader' played by a leader who is invisible and a follower who has no sense of touch. You cannot convict a robot of 'Skill' when its entire training manual is just a collection of moving shadows and 'trust me, bro' simulations. I move to dismiss on the grounds that 'Optical Flow' is not a substitute for 'Actual Physics!'

title: Contrast Sets for Evaluating Language-Guided Robot Policies
abstract: Robot evaluations in language-guided, real world settings are time-consuming and often sample only a small space of potential instructions across complex scenes. In this work, we introduce contrast sets for robotics as an approach to make small, but specific, perturbations to otherwise independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) test instances. We investigate the relationship between experimenter effort to carry out an evaluation and the resulting estimated test performance as well as the insights that can be drawn from performance on perturbed instances. We use contrast sets to characterize policies at reduced experimenter effort in both a simulated manipulation task and a physical robot vision-and-language navigation task. We encourage the use of contrast set evaluations as a more informative alternative to small scale, i.i.d. demonstrations on physical robots, and as a scalable alternative to industry-scale real world evaluations.
author: Abrar Anwar;Rohan Gupta;Jesse Thomason
aff: University of Southern California;University of Southern California;Amazon

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers proposed testing robots by giving them nearly identical instructions with one tiny change—like switching 'red' to 'blue'—to see if they actually understand what they're doing. The goal was to find a cheap shortcut to prove a robot is smart without having to run thousands of expensive, real-world experiments.
why_bad: The concept of 'contrast sets' was basically copy-pasted from the natural language processing (NLP) field, offering very little theoretical novelty for the robotics community.
The claim that this method 'reduces experimenter effort' is highly debatable, as meticulously setting up specific, tiny variations in a physical room can be just as tedious as traditional testing.
The evaluation metrics were too thin, failing to prove that passing these 'tiny tweak' tests actually correlates with a robot's ability to handle the chaotic mess of the real world.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Our authors are basically telling Gordon Ramsay that instead of tasting a chef's entire five-course menu, we should just taste their omelet, and then taste it again with slightly more chives. While this certainly saves the judges from a stomach ache, it doesn't actually tell you if the chef will melt down when the kitchen gets busy or if they'll accidentally serve a raw chicken. It’s a clever shortcut for a lazy Tuesday, but you aren't winning any Michelin stars by proving you can handle 'more salt' vs 'less salt' while the rest of the restaurant is on fire.

title: Sparsh: Self-supervised touch representations for vision-based tactile sensing
abstract: In this work, we introduce general purpose touch representations for the increasingly accessible class of vision-based tactile sensors. Such sensors have led to many recent advances in robot manipulation as they markedly complement vision, yet solutions today often rely on task and sensor specific handcrafted perception models. Collecting real data at scale with task centric ground truth labels, like contact forces and slip, is a challenge further compounded by sensors of various form factor differing in aspects like lighting and gel markings. To tackle this, we turn to self-supervised learning (SSL) that has demonstrated remarkable performance in computer vision. We present Sparsh, a family of SSL models that can support various vision-based tactile sensors, alleviating the need for custom labels through pre-training on 460k+ tactile images with masking and self-distillation in pixel and latent spaces. We also build TacBench, to facilitate standardized benchmarking across sensors and models, comprising of six tasks ranging from comprehending tactile properties to enabling physical perception and manipulation planning. In evaluations, we find that SSL pre-training for touch representation outperforms task and sensor-specific end-to-end training by 95.1% on average over TacBench, and Sparsh (DINO) and Sparsh (IJEPA) are the most competitive, indicating the merits of learning in latent space for tactile images. Project page: https://sparsh-ssl.github.io
author: Carolina Higuera;Akash Sharma;Chaithanya Krishna Bodduluri;Taosha Fan;Patrick Lancaster;Mrinal Kalakrishnan;Michael Kaess;Byron Boots;Mike Lambeta;Tingfan Wu;Mustafa Mukadam
aff: University of Washington;Carnegie Mellon University;Meta Facebook;;Meta;Meta;Carnegie Mellon University;;Meta;;Meta AI

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of teaching every individual robot finger how to feel things from scratch, these researchers tried to build a 'Master Sense of Touch' by having an AI stare at 460,000 photos of squished rubber. It's essentially trying to give robots universal 'finger-tip intuition' so they don't have to relearn what a slippery surface looks like every time they get a hardware upgrade.
why_bad: The 'general purpose' claim is a stretch because the model is mostly just becoming a connoisseur of specific gel-based sensor artifacts rather than actually understanding the universal physics of contact.
Performing well on a digital benchmark (TacBench) is essentially 'armchair athletics'—it doesn't prove the robot won't still crush a strawberry into jam during a real-world manipulation task.

The reviewers likely felt the paper was just 'Computer Vision: Recycled,' applying standard image-masking tricks to tactile data without addressing the unique, messy noise and calibration issues that make touch actually hard.
roast.analogy_name: The Finger-Painting Prodigy Who Can’t Hold a Brush
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where robot hands have the grace of a drunk toddler wearing oven mitts, one team of scientists decided to show an AI half a million pictures of people poking rubber. They called it: SPARSH. It studied the shadows. It mastered the squish. It dominated the leaderboards. But when the lights come up and it's time to actually grab a wine glass, the critics realized: knowing what a squeeze *looks* like isn't the same as *feeling* the slide. Coming this summer: A robot that can describe a texture in 4K latent space but still drops your phone in the toilet.

title: Exploring Under Constraints with Model-Based Actor-Critic and Safety Filters
abstract: Applying reinforcement learning (RL) to learn effective policies on physical robots without supervision remains challenging when it comes to tasks where safe exploration is critical. Constrained model-based RL (CMBRL) presents a promising approach to this problem. These methods are designed to learn constraint-adhering policies through constrained optimization approaches. Yet, such policies often fail to meet stringent safety requirements during learning and exploration. Our solution ``CASE'' aims to reduce the instances where constraints are breached during the learning phase. Specifically, CASE integrates techniques for optimizing constrained policies and employs planning-based safety filters as backup policies, effectively lowering constraint violations during learning and making it a more reliable option than other recent constrained model-based policy optimization methods.
author: Ahmed Agha;Baris Kayalibay;Atanas Mirchev;Patrick van der Smagt;Justin Bayer
aff: Volkswagen Group;Data Lab, Volkswagen Group;Machine Learning Research Lab, Volkswagen Group;Machine Learning Research Lab; Volkswagen Group;VW Group
position: Research Assistant;PhD student;PhD student;Full Professor;research scientist

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'don't-break-the-robot' system that uses a backup plan to stop AI from making expensive mistakes during training. However, they essentially just put a digital leash on the robot that's so tight it can't actually learn how to do anything useful without the leash holding its hand.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The paper basically duct-taped a standard safety filter onto an existing algorithm and acted like they discovered fire.
The 'Safe but Useless' Problem: The safety constraints are so aggressive that the robot barely explores, resulting in a policy that is safe only because it's too scared to move.
Evaluation Gaps: The experiments were likely conducted in a sterilized simulation where gravity is a suggestion, failing to prove the method wouldn't cause a literal mechanical fire on a real robot.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the track, folks! We’re watching the CASE team’s entry, and—oh boy—it looks like they’ve entered a Formula 1 race with training wheels and a governor that caps the speed at 5 miles per hour. The driver wants to take the corner, but the 'Safety Filter' mom is in the passenger seat screaming and slamming the dual-control brakes every time a butterfly flutters nearby. Sure, the car doesn't have a scratch on it, but it’s currently being overtaken by a literal turtle. The judges are shaking their heads; you can’t claim to be the fastest learner on the track when you’re too terrified to even find the gas pedal!

title: Learning Transparent Reward Models via Unsupervised Feature Selection
abstract: In complex real-world tasks such as robotic manipulation and autonomous driving, collecting expert demonstrations is often more straightforward than specifying precise learning objectives and task descriptions. Learning from expert data can be achieved through behavioral cloning or by learning a reward function, i.e., inverse reinforcement learning. The latter allows for training with additional data outside the training distribution, guided by the inferred reward function. We propose a novel approach to construct compact and interpretable reward models from automatically selected state features. These inferred rewards have an explicit form and enable the learning of policies that closely match expert behavior by training standard reinforcement learning algorithms from scratch. We validate our method's performance in various robotic environments with continuous and high-dimensional state spaces.
keywords: Inverse reinforcement learning;Reinforcement learning;Imitation learning;Robots;Reward learning;Robot learning
author: Daulet Baimukashev;Gokhan Alcan;Kevin Sebastian Luck;Ville Kyrki
aff: Aalto University;Aalto University;Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam;Aalto University

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of writing a manual for robots, these researchers tried to let the AI watch a pro and write its own 'simple' rulebook for success. They claim the AI can pick out the most important bits on its own, but in reality, they just built a very fancy way for a robot to guess what it's supposed to be doing.
why_bad: The 'interpretability' claim is a total reach; just because a reward function is short doesn't mean a human can actually understand why the AI is choosing to do a backflip.
The 'unsupervised' feature selection is a gamble that usually ends up with the AI focusing on irrelevant background noise instead of the actual task-critical movements.
The 'complex' environments used for testing are actually standard toy benchmarks that haven't been impressive to the research community since 2018.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Kitchen Stadium, folks! Our rookie chef today is trying the 'Transparent Tactic'—he’s watching a Michelin-star pro cook a lobster thermidor through a soundproof window. He’s not asking for the recipe; no, he’s 'unsupervised,' baby! He’s picking out 'compact features' like the color of the chef’s socks and the way the steam hits the ceiling. He’s serving up a plate of boiled water and calling it 'Interpretable Soup' because you can see right through it! The judges are looking for flavor, but all they’re getting is a minimalist performance piece. That’s a 15-yard penalty for Lack of Substance, and he’s headed straight for the walk-in freezer of academic rejection!

title: ReKep: Spatio-Temporal Reasoning of Relational Keypoint Constraints for Robotic Manipulation
abstract: Representing robotic manipulation tasks as constraints that associate the robot and the environment is a promising way to encode desired robot behaviors. However, it remains unclear how to formulate the constraints such that they are 1) versatile to diverse tasks, 2) free of manual labeling, and 3) optimizable by off-the-shelf solvers to produce robot actions in real-time. In this work, we introduce Relational Keypoint Constraints (ReKep), a visually-grounded representation for constraints in robotic manipulation. Specifically, ReKep are expressed as Python functions mapping a set of 3D keypoints in the environment to a numerical cost. We demonstrate that by representing a manipulation task as a sequence of Relational Keypoint Constraints, we can employ a hierarchical optimization procedure to solve for robot actions (represented by a sequence of end-effector poses in SE(3)) with a perception-action loop at a real-time frequency. Furthermore, in order to circumvent the need for manual specification of ReKep for each new task, we devise an automated procedure that leverages large vision models and vision-language models to produce ReKep from free-form language instructions and RGB-D observation. We present system implementations on a mobile single-arm platform and a stationary dual-arm platform that can perform a large variety of manipulation tasks, featuring multi-stage, in-the-wild, bimanual, and reactive behaviors, all without task-specific data or environment models.
author: Wenlong Huang;Chen Wang;Yunzhu Li;Ruohan Zhang;Li Fei-Fei
aff: NVIDIA;Computer Science Department, Stanford University;University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign;Stanford University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to get robots to perform chores by having an AI look at a room, pick out 'important dots,' and write a math equation for how those dots should move. It’s essentially trying to turn 'putting away groceries' into a real-time geometry homework assignment solved by a chatbot.
why_bad: The system relies on Vision-Language Models to generate Python code on the fly, which is basically asking a hallucinating intern to write safety-critical flight software.

Claiming 'real-time' performance while running a hierarchical optimization loop on complex 3D constraints is a bold-faced lie unless your definition of real-time includes awkward five-second pauses between every movement.

The reliance on 3D keypoints is incredibly brittle; if the lighting shifts or a cat walks by, the 'relational constraints' crumble and the robot starts trying to rotate the table instead of the coffee mug.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the chef refuses to use his hands or a recipe. Instead, he stares at a raw slab of meat and shouts vague instructions at a smart speaker, which then tries to write a computer program to move a set of robotic tweezers. The chef claims this is 'versatile and labeling-free,' but halfway through, the smart speaker forgets what a 'crust' is, the tweezers get stuck in an infinite loop of poking the dough, and the audience is left starving while the chef insists the 'perception-action loop' is technically functioning perfectly. It’s an over-engineered solution to a problem that a simple 'if-then' statement or a human with a spatula solved decades ago.

title: UMI-on-Legs: Making Manipulation Policies Mobile with Manipulation-Centric Whole-body Controllers
abstract: We introduce UMI-on-Legs, a new framework that combines real-world and simulation data for quadruped manipulation systems.  We scale task-centric data collection in the real world using a handheld gripper (UMI), providing a cheap way to demonstrate task-relevant manipulation skills without a robot. Simultaneously, we scale robot-centric data in simulation by training a whole-body controller.  The interface between these two policies are end-effector trajectories in the task-frame, which are inferred by the manipulation policy and passed to the whole-body controller for tracking. We evaluate UMI-on-Legs on prehensile, non-prehensile, and dynamic manipulation tasks, and report over 70% success rate for all tasks. Lastly, we also demonstrate the zero-shot cross-embodiment deployment of a pre-trained manipulation policy checkpoint from a prior work, originally intended for a fixed-base robot arm, on our quadruped system. We believe this framework provides a scalable path towards learning expressive manipulation skills on dynamic robot embodiments.
author: Huy Ha;Yihuai Gao;Zipeng Fu;Jie Tan;Shuran Song
aff: Columbia University;Stanford University;Stanford University;Google;Stanford University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Research Scientist;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to turn a robot dog into a high-tech butler by training it with videos of people using handheld pincers and a physics simulator. They hoped the 'legs' and the 'arm' would coordinate perfectly, but ended up with a system that misses its target nearly a third of the time.
why_bad: A 70% success rate in a controlled lab setting is essentially 'it works on my machine'—it's too unreliable for any actual real-world application.
The 'whole-body' coordination is largely an illusion; the arm and legs aren't truly communicating, leading to the robot fighting its own momentum during complex tasks.
The data collection method (UMI) ignores the 'bouncy chassis' problem, where the robot's walking vibrations confuse the arm's precision in ways a steady human hand never would.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where high-stakes robotics meets total lack of coordination... ONE ROBOT DOG must learn to use a fork. He’s got the legs of a champion and the hands of a surgeon, but they haven't spoken in years. Witness the cinematic tension of a machine trying to open a cabinet while its back legs are accidentally doing the Macarena. It’s a heart-pounding race against a 30% failure rate. This summer, 'The Uncoordinated Waiter' reminds us that having four legs doesn't mean you have a single clue what your hand is doing.

title: Steering Your Generalists: Improving Robotic Foundation Models via Value Guidance
abstract: Large, general-purpose robotic policies trained on diverse demonstration datasets have been shown to be remarkably effective both for controlling a variety of robots in a range of different scenes, and for acquiring broad repertoires of manipulation skills. However, the data that such policies are trained on is generally of mixed quality -- not only are human-collected demonstrations unlikely to perform the task perfectly, but the larger the dataset is, the harder it is to curate only the highest quality examples. It also remains unclear how optimal data from one embodiment is for training on another embodiment. In this paper, we present a general and broadly applicable approach that enhances the performance of such generalist robot policies at deployment time by re-ranking their actions according to a value function learned via offline RL. This approach, which we call Value-Guided Policy Steering (V-GPS), is compatible with a wide range of different generalist policies, without needing to fine-tune or even access the weights of the policy. We show that the same value function can improve the performance of five different state-of-the-art policies with different architectures, even though they were trained on distinct datasets, attaining consistent performance improvement on multiple robotic platforms across a total of 12 tasks. Code and videos can be found at: https://nakamotoo.github.io/V-GPS
author: Mitsuhiko Nakamoto;Oier Mees;Aviral Kumar;Sergey Levine
aff: University of California, Berkeley;Electrical Engineering & Computer Science Department, University of California, Berkeley;Google DeepMind;Google

roast.laymen_summary: Big AI models for robots are often trained on sloppy human data, leading to 'foundation models' that are about as coordinated as a toddler on espresso. This paper proposes a 'V-GPS' system that doesn't fix the robot's brain, but instead acts as a picky supervisor that chooses the least-dumb move from a list of options.
why_bad: Inference Latency: Reviewers pointed out that asking a model to generate dozens of actions and then ranking them all in real-time makes the robot move with the agonizing slowness of a dial-up modem.

The Oracle Paradox: The paper assumes you can train a perfect 'Value Function' to judge actions, but critics argued that if you had enough high-quality data to do that, you wouldn't need this complicated steering setup in the first place.

Scope and Baselines: The 'plug-and-play' nature was seen as a bit of a gimmick, with reviewers noting that simple fine-tuning—actually teaching the robot better habits—often outperforms this high-maintenance chaperone approach.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a witness stand where the defendant—a general-purpose robot—is being asked how to fold a shirt. Instead of one coherent answer, the robot blabs out fifty different responses at once, half of which involve setting the shirt on fire or eating it. The 'Value Function' is a frantic, high-priced defense attorney standing next to the witness. Every time the robot opens its mouth, the attorney shouts 'Objection!' and points to the one specific sentence that doesn't lead to an immediate conviction. While this 'steering' technically keeps the robot out of jail, the Judge (the reviewer) is unimpressed because the trial is now taking six months, the attorney is doing all the heavy lifting, and the defendant is clearly still a liability who hasn't learned a single thing.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark levels at 98% capacity.

title: Neural Inverse Source Problem
abstract: Reconstructing unknown external source functions is an important perception capability for a large range of robotics domains including manipulation, aerial, and underwater robotics. In this work, we propose a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) based approach for solving the inverse source problems in robotics, jointly identifying unknown source functions and the complete state of a system given partial and noisy observations.  Our approach demonstrates several advantages over prior works (Finite Element Methods (FEM) and data-driven approaches): it offers flexibility in integrating diverse constraints and boundary conditions; eliminates the need for complex discretizations (e.g., meshing); easily accommodates gradients from real measurements; and does not limit performance based on the diversity and quality of training data. We validate our method across three simulation and real-world scenarios involving up to 4th order partial differential equations (PDEs), constraints such as Signorini and Dirichlet, and various regression losses including Chamfer distance and L2 norm.
keywords: Inverse source problem;Physics informed neural network
author: Youngsun Wi;Jayjun Lee;Miquel Oller;Nima Fazeli
aff: University of Michigan;;University of Michigan - Ann Arbor;University of Michigan

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a brain for robots that can guess what’s pushing or pulling them—like wind or a magnetic field—just by watching how they move. They used 'Physics-Informed Neural Networks' to skip the tedious math grids usually required, hoping to make robots smarter at sensing their environment without needing massive amounts of training data.
why_bad: The 'PINN' approach is notoriously slow and unstable for the complex 4th-order equations they tackled, often taking longer to converge than the robot would take to actually crash.
The paper lacks a 'fair fight' baseline, comparing their fancy neural network against outdated traditional methods rather than modern, optimized solvers that would likely run circles around them.

The 'Inverse Problem' is mathematically 'ill-posed,' meaning a dozen different causes could produce the same result, and the authors failed to prove their AI isn't just hallucinating one of the wrong ones.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a referee trying to call a foul from the locker room just by listening to the sound of the crowd. Our authors are claiming they've built an AI 'super-ref' that doesn't need to actually watch the game; it just feels the 'vibrations' of the stadium. But look at the replay, folks! While the AI is busy calculating the molecular friction of the grass blades to determine who tripped whom, the game ended three hours ago. They’re celebrating a 'precision call' in a simulation where the players were static statues, but put this ref on a real pitch and it’s going to red-card a corner flag. It's a classic rookie mistake: bringing a supercomputer to a street fight and forgetting that in the real world, the physics don't wait for your gradient descent to finish its coffee break!

title: Unpacking Failure Modes of Generative Policies: Runtime Monitoring of Consistency and Progress
abstract: Robot behavior policies trained via imitation learning are prone to failure under conditions that deviate from their training data. Thus, algorithms that monitor learned policies at test time and provide early warnings of failure are necessary to facilitate scalable deployment. We propose Sentinel, a runtime monitoring framework that splits the detection of failures into two complementary categories: 1) Erratic failures, which we detect using statistical measures of temporal action consistency, and 2) task progression failures, where we use Vision Language Models (VLMs) to detect when the policy confidently and consistently takes actions that do not solve the task. Our approach has two key strengths. First, because learned policies exhibit diverse failure modes, combining complementary detectors leads to significantly higher accuracy at failure detection. Second, using a statistical temporal action consistency measure ensures that we quickly detect when multimodal, generative policies exhibit erratic behavior at negligible computational cost. In contrast, we only use VLMs to detect modes that are less time-sensitive. We demonstrate our approach in the context of diffusion policies trained on robotic mobile manipulation domains in both simulation and the real world. By unifying temporal consistency detection and VLM runtime monitoring, Sentinel detects 18% more failures than using either of the two detectors alone and significantly outperforms baselines, thus highlighting the importance of assigning specialized detectors to complementary categories of failure. Qualitative results are made available at sites.google.com/stanford.edu/sentinel.
author: Christopher Agia;Rohan Sinha;Jingyun Yang;Ziang Cao;Rika Antonova;Marco Pavone;Jeannette Bohg
aff: Stanford University;Stanford University;Stanford University;;;Stanford University;Stanford University
position: PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;;;Associate Professor;Assistant Professor

roast.laymen_summary: We built a specialized 'nanny' system to watch robots and scream when they either start twitching uncontrollably or confidently fail at their jobs. It uses basic math to catch the jitters and an expensive AI 'eye' to realize when the robot is just staring at a wall instead of folding laundry.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially a glorified IF-THEN statement that glues a standard variance check to a slow, expensive Vision Language Model.
Using a VLM for 'runtime monitoring' is like hiring a philosopher to watch a Formula 1 race; by the time the AI realizes the car has crashed, the wreckage is already cold.
The 18% performance gain is a suspiciously modest margin that likely collapses the moment the robot leaves the highly controlled 'lab' setting.
roast.analogy_name: The Twitchy Rookie and the Zoom-Call Umpire
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a baseball player who occasionally suffers from a vibrating seizure mid-swing or, worse, stands perfectly still and insists he just hit a grand slam. The researchers' solution? A two-man crew. First, there's a guy with a stopwatch who flags the player if his hands shake too much—that's the 'temporal consistency' check. Then, there's an umpire watching the game via a laggy Zoom call from three time zones away to decide if the player is actually playing baseball. By the time the remote umpire 'confidently' confirms the player is just eating dirt, the fans have gone home and the stadium lights are out. It’s a bold strategy to fix a broken robot, but the reviewers aren't buying tickets to this circus.

title: Not All Errors Are Made Equal: A Regret Metric for Detecting System-level Trajectory Prediction Failures
abstract:
Robot decision-making increasingly relies on data-driven human prediction models when operating around people. While these models are known to mispredict in out-of-distribution interactions, only a subset of prediction errors impact downstream robot performance.  
We propose characterizing such ``system-level'' prediction failures via the mathematical notion of regret: high-regret interactions are precisely those in which mispredictions degraded closed-loop robot performance. 
We further introduce a probabilistic generalization of regret that calibrates failure detection across disparate deployment contexts and renders regret compatible with reward-based and reward-free (e.g., generative) planners.  
In simulated autonomous driving interactions, we showcase that our system-level failure metric can automatically mine for closed-loop human-robot interactions that state-of-the-art generative human predictors and robot planners struggle with. 
We further find that the very presence of high-regret data during human predictor fine-tuning is highly predictive of robot re-deployment performance improvements. 
Furthermore, fine-tuning with the informative but significantly smaller high-regret data (23% of deployment data) is competitive with fine-tuning on the full deployment dataset, indicating a promising avenue for efficiently mitigating system-level human-robot interaction failures.
author: Kensuke Nakamura;Thomas Tian;Andrea Bajcsy
aff: Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, Berkeley;Carnegie Mellon University
aff_unique_norm: Carnegie Mellon University;University of California, Berkeley

roast.laymen_summary: We realized that robots don't need to be perfect psychics; they just need to know which of their wrong guesses will actually cause a car crash. By focusing only on the 'oopsies' that lead to disaster, we can train AI much faster using only a quarter of the usual mountain of data.
why_bad: The 'Novelty' Nuisance: Reviewers noted that using 'regret' to measure performance is like 'discovering' that fire is hot—it's a foundational concept in decision theory that the authors tried to pass off as a brand-new invention.

The Simulation Stigma: The math looks great in a sterile computer simulation where humans act like NPCs, but reviewers suspect it would suffer a total nervous breakdown the moment it encounters a real-world cyclist or a distracted toddler.

Baseline Boredom: The paper failed to prove that their complex 'Probabilistic Regret' actually works better than much simpler, existing methods, making the extra math feel like putting a spoiler on a minivan.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Pedantic Valet
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the Prosecution presents Exhibit A: An AI that thinks guessing a pedestrian’s gait is as important as noticing a semi-truck. The Defense argues their new 'Regret Metric' is a breakthrough, but let’s look at the facts. It’s like a valet who expects a standing ovation for not driving your Porsche into the harbor, while simultaneously filing a 40-page report because he parked three inches too far from the curb. The jury finds that while the math is technically 'legal,' the defendant has failed to prove this isn't just common sense dressed up in a three-piece suit. We move to strike this paper from the record until it can handle a real-world street corner without a mathematical existential crisis!

title: EquiBot: SIM(3)-Equivariant Diffusion Policy for Generalizable and Data Efficient Learning
abstract: Building effective imitation learning methods that enable robots to learn from limited data and still generalize across diverse real-world environments is a long-standing problem in robot learning. We propose EquiBot, a robust, data-efficient, and generalizable approach for robot manipulation task learning. Our approach combines SIM(3)-equivariant neural network architectures with diffusion models. This ensures that our learned policies are invariant to changes in scale, rotation, and translation, enhancing their applicability to unseen environments while retaining the benefits of diffusion-based policy learning such as multi-modality and robustness. We show on a suite of 6 simulation tasks that our proposed method reduces the data requirements and improves generalization to novel scenarios. In the real world, with 10 variations of 6 mobile manipulation tasks, we show that our method can easily generalize to novel objects and scenes after learning from just 5 minutes of human demonstrations in each task.
author: Jingyun Yang;Ziang Cao;Congyue Deng;Rika Antonova;Shuran Song;Jeannette Bohg
aff: Stanford University;;Stanford University;;Stanford University;Stanford University

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to give a robot 'spatial common sense' so it understands that a cup is still a cup even if it's rotated, moved, or resized. By using fancy geometry math, they hoped the robot could learn complex chores from just five minutes of human footage instead of a lifetime of training data.
why_bad: Over-engineered Math: Including 'scale-invariance' (the S in SIM(3)) is useless for real-world physics because objects don't spontaneously change size unless the robot is operating in a Pixar movie.
The 'Lab-Grown' Generalization: The 'novel scenarios' were likely just the same table with a slightly different tablecloth, failing to prove the bot wouldn't choke in a real, messy apartment.

Computational Gluttony: Equivariant layers are mathematically beautiful but computationally expensive, meaning the robot probably thinks at the speed of a 1990s dial-up modem while trying to decide if a spoon is still a spoon.
roast.analogy_name: The Master Chef of Identical Potatoes
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley,' where our contestant, EquiBot, claims they can cook a 5-star meal in any kitchen after watching one 5-minute YouTube clip. EquiBot spends three hours mathematically proving that a whisk still whisks even if you hold it at a 42-degree angle or if the whisk magically grows to the size of a surfboard. It’s a geometric masterpiece! But the moment a real guest asks for pepper—which wasn't in the 5-minute training video—the bot stands frozen, over-analyzing the rotational symmetry of the salt shaker while the soufflé collapses and the kitchen catches fire. It’s a lot of PhD-level math just to avoid learning how to actually use a spatula.

title: Continuous Control with Coarse-to-fine Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Despite recent advances in improving the sample-efficiency of reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms, designing an RL algorithm that can be practically deployed in real-world environments remains a challenge. In this paper, we present Coarse-to-fine Reinforcement Learning (CRL), a framework that trains RL agents to zoom-into a continuous action space in a coarse-to-fine manner, enabling the use of stable, sample-efficient value-based RL algorithms for fine-grained continuous control tasks. Our key idea is to train agents that output actions by iterating the procedure of (i) discretizing the continuous action space into multiple intervals and (ii) selecting the interval with the highest Q-value to further discretize at the next level. We then introduce a concrete, value-based algorithm within the CRL framework called Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN). Our experiments demonstrate that CQN significantly outperforms RL and behavior cloning baselines on 20 sparsely-rewarded RLBench manipulation tasks with a modest number of environment interactions and expert demonstrations. We also show that CQN robustly learns to solve real-world manipulation tasks within a few minutes of online training.
author: Younggyo Seo;Jafar Uruç;Stephen James
aff: Dyson;London Dyson Robot Learning Lab;Dyson

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of teaching a robot to move smoothly like a human, the authors treat the world like a game of 'Hot or Cold' where the robot guesses a general area and then frantically zooms in until it finds the target. It’s basically trying to turn a high-end robot arm into an old-school arcade claw machine that thinks in chunks instead of curves.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is thinner than a wafer; recursively discretizing space is just a fancy way of saying 'we built a decision tree,' a concept that’s been around since the disco era.
Computational overhead is a nightmare because the agent has to run multiple inference passes just to figure out where to move its pinky finger, making it 'sample-efficient' but 'CPU-expensive.'
The approach falls apart in high-dimensional spaces; while it works for a simple gripper, trying this on a full humanoid would result in a search-tree explosion that would melt a server rack.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup called 'Teleport-ish.' Instead of driving to work, you look at a map of the world and pick the Northern Hemisphere. Then you look at a map of the Hemisphere and pick your city. Then you pick your street. Then your desk. You’re not 'driving'; you’re just making a series of increasingly narrow guesses about where you should be. It’s great because you don’t need to know how to steer a car, but it’s terrible because if you miscalculate the first 'coarse' step, you end up perfectly 'fine-tuning' your position at the bottom of the Atlantic Ocean. Investors, we've replaced 'smooth motion' with 'aggressive squinting'!

title: Dreamitate: Real-World Visuomotor Policy Learning via Video Generation
abstract: A key challenge in manipulation is learning a policy that can robustly generalize to diverse visual environments. A promising mechanism for learning robust policies is to leverage video generative models, which are pretrained on large-scale datasets of internet videos. In this paper, we propose a visuomotor policy learning framework that fine-tunes a video diffusion model on human demonstrations of a given task. At test time, we generate an example of an execution of the task conditioned on images of a novel scene, and use this synthesized execution directly to control the robot. Our key insight is that using common tools allows us to effortlessly bridge the embodiment gap between the human hand and the robot manipulator. We evaluate our approach on 4 tasks of increasing complexity and demonstrate that capitalizing on internet-scale generative models allows the learned policy to achieve a significantly higher degree of generalization than existing behavior cloning approaches.
author: Junbang Liang;Ruoshi Liu;Ege Ozguroglu;Sruthi Sudhakar;Achal Dave;Pavel Tokmakov;Shuran Song;Carl Vondrick
aff: Columbia University;Columbia University;Columbia University;Columbia University;Toyota Research Institute;Toyota Research Institute;Stanford University;Columbia University
position: MS student;PhD student;PhD student;PhD student;Researcher;Research Scientist;Assistant Professor;Associate Professor

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to skip the hard work of teaching robots physics by letting them 'daydream' a video of themselves completing a task and then blindly following the footage. It's essentially attempting to navigate reality by watching a movie of someone else doing it and hoping the world doesn't change while you're buffering.
why_bad: Temporal Hallucinations: Video models are notorious for ignoring the laws of physics, leading the robot to attempt movements that involve teleporting or phased-array clipping through solid matter.
Glacial Inference: Generating a high-resolution dream of the future takes so long that the robot would be stationary for minutes between every half-second of actual movement.
The Embodiment Gap: Pretending a human hand in a video is the same as a multi-jointed metallic claw is like trying to use a map of Mars to find a Starbucks in midtown Manhattan.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where physics is merely a suggestion... one machine refuses to look at the road. Witness the cinematic event of the year: a robot that navigates traffic not by driving, but by rendering a 4K Pixar-style movie of itself arriving safely at its destination. See the drama unfold as the car attempts to merge into a lane that only exists in its own imagination! Experience the suspense of 5-minute wait times for every single turn! It’s got the visuals of a blockbuster, the logic of a fever dream, and the real-world utility of a screen door on a submarine. Coming soon to a lab where 'looking right' is more important than 'actually working.'
